Another decade of western economic malaise – or, God forbid, another financial crisis – is likely to see more radical solutions and politicians emerging. Here's Nick Clegg's Christmas card. David Cameron has been tweeting from South Africa. There are two polls around this morning. For the record, here are the figures. ICM for the Guardian Labour: 37% (down 1 point from ICM last month) Conservatives: 32% (up 2) Lib Dems: 12% (down 1) Ukip: 9% (down 1) Labour lead: 5 points (down 3) According to Electoral Calculus, this would give Labour a majority of 64. YouGov for the Sun Labour: 38% (down 1 point from YouGov in the Sunday Times) Conservatives: 33% (down 1) Ukip: 13% (up 2) Lib Dems: 10% (no change) Government approval: -30 (down 5) Labour lead: 5 points (no change) According to Electoral Calculus, this would give Labour a majority of 56. I'm afraid we're not exactly at the centre of the news cyclone today. You may want to take a look at Matthew Weaver's Guardian live blog of the Mandela memorial service. For those of you loyal enough to stick with Westminster, I can promise you ... well, not much. The diary is empty. But that should leave me plenty of time to go through the papers and scour the internet. And you never know what will turn up. Here's the agenda for the day. 11am: A report from the Commission on Civil Society and Democratic Engagement is launched at an event in the House of Commons. The commission is an alliance of charities, and the report (pdf) explains how the lobbying bill threatens their ability to campaign. 11.30am: Danny Alexander, chief secretary to the Treasury, takes Treasury questions in the Commons. Alexander is leading for the government because George Osborne is abroad. 12.30pm: The advisory council on the misuse of drugs publishes its report on ketamine. It will recommend reclassifying it from a Class C drug to a Class B drug. 2pm: Labour hosts a summit on A&E services. 2.45pm: Mark Sedwill, permanent secretary at the Home Office, gives evidence to the Commons home affairs committee. As usual, I’ll also be covering all the breaking political news as well as looking at the papers and bringing you the best politics from the web. I’ll post a summary at about 1pm and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I’m on @AndrewSparrow.

headline: Labour conference - Ed Balls's speech: Politics live blog
trail_text: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of the Labour conference in Brighton, including Ed Balls' speech
body_text: That's it from us for the night. Andrew Sparrow will be back in charge tomorrow morning. Have a nice evening. Ed Balls seemed notably relaxed and confident during that interview – perhaps suggesting a newfound confidence in the Labour frontbench. Here are the key points: • Balls said “a really hard and tough look” needed to be taken into the High Speed 2 rail scheme. "I think it's about time we injected a bit of hard thinking into this debate, and if at the end of that you go ahead, good," he said, although his comments suggested he was sceptical that it was worth the £50bn price tag. • Balls suggested strongly that health spending would remain ringfenced under a future Labour government. • Balls said Labour was no longer advocating a cut in VAT because it no longer suited the economic circumstances: “Clearly since June the recovery has begun.” • He said he agreed wholeheartedly with Ed Miliband’s comments that any shadow minister briefing against a colleague should be sacked, and said he had never done that himself. The Damian McBride era was “a different place – it really feels like quite a long time ago,” he said. • He said the government’s help to buy scheme was set too high, but he could see the case for a smaller scheme. But building houses was more important, he said. In a final question, Richards asks about Balls's piano lessons and whether that was the only time his mind was clear of politics. Yes, he says. It's really hard to do one thing with one hand and one thing with the other, he says, so it takes "absolute total concentration". It's fabulous, he says. Richards tries to get him to pre-announce Ed Miliband's policy announcements planned for his speech tomorrow, but of course he won't. And with that it finishes. Balls is asked to make more of a distinction between Labour and the other parties. The questioner suggests funding Sure Start children's centres. Balls says these are one of Labour's greatest achievement and it's terrible to see them being closed and having services scaled back. "I'm with you. This will be a very important part of what we do in a Labour government." He jokes that he'll tell them if they promise not to tell anyone. (I've never seen him this relaxed.) Balls is asked to name some more policies for people to take home. He says he could do his whole speech from memory. It's over 4,000 words. He says the biggest thing he was worried about was whether his towel joke would work. But Harriet Harman said: "Fabulous towel joke. I wish I'd thought of it." He lists a number of policies he mentioned in the speech. Tomorrow you'll get "certainly two more manifesto commitments which are there and good and strong - but that's off the record!" Richards asks if Labour is going to reverse the rise in VAT. Balls says he made that case in 2011 for 2011 - but it would not be right for 2015 as the recovery begins. "Clearly since June the recovery has begun. We're not advocating a temporary VAT cut now." Balls suggests Nick Clegg got off scot-free when he announced his free school meals policy without announcing how it would be funded. "Nick Clegg got away with it... Well, not really because people didn't take it that seriously because they think, 'It's Nick Clegg ... '" Balls has a good joke himself, about how John Prescott used to sit at the back of his battle bus cold-calling random people to ask if they are going to vote Labour. "Once he asked Gordon to join him ... " Balls is asked to give out a list of the policies he announced today to help members make the case to voters. Balls says he will do something similar by Saturday. The questioner makes a good joke about Balls getting a standing ovation and then returning the mic: "Maybe we've all been to too many rock concerts ... but I was expecting two more policies!" Everyone laughs and the questioner says he has been working on that joke all day. "It was very good," says Balls sweetly. Should the ringfencing of health spending continue? He says he doesn’t want to make a promise now but he says it’s very likely. Balls is asked about Miliband's promises that any shadow ministers who brief against other shadow ministers will be sacked. What does he think and has he ever done that? Balls says he agrees with Miliband wholeheartedly and he has never briefed or sanctioned briefing against colleagues. Asked about the help to buy scheme, Balls says £600,000 is a very high figure for a taxpayer guarantee for mortgages. He can see the case for a smaller scheme. But again he returns to the idea of acting on the supply of housing first. He says Osborne thinks helping first-time buyers is a good thing politically, but he doesn't want to build houses because "he's locked into the fiscal path". Balls is asked whether the green message is a vote winner for Labour. Yes, he says. The government through incoherence and lack of vision has united the CBI and Greenpeace on green issues. Let's have a 2030 renewables goal in law, Balls says - and that is a response to a demand from the CBI and Greenpeace. Cameron has "totally ducked this policy agenda in government" - despite widespread support. "I think it's a big opportunity for us." Balls is asked about land tax. Sometimes there are complexities in the tax system that mean people we don't want to pay have to pay, he says. In the end it is about the supply of homes rather than the price of land, he says. Richards opens it up to questions. "I hope this is interesting for all of you. It feels a bit like In the Psychiatrist's Chair," Balls muses (if only). Balls looks back at the summer of 1996 and recalls the Skinner/Baddiel/Lightning Seeds song Three Lions, with its famous refrain "football's coming home" (and I said there was going to be no culture tonight...). Blair and Brown had independently written their conference speeches to end with the line: "Labour's coming home." A row ensued. Gordon grumpily gave way, even though he liked football more than Blair. How could the two of them get to the day before conference without having seen each other's speeches, asks Balls. Miliband showed him his speech for this year's conference two weeks ago. Richards brings up Damian McBride's revelations and asks what Balls reflects on that era. Balls says he was "horrified" by McBride's revelations. But it allows us to look back on this "different place - it really feels like quite a long time ago," he says. Richards notes that, unusually in recent times, Balls and Miliband are an opposition duo with much experience of power. But he suggests Balls and Miliband do not want to push this because "the recent past became so toxic". Balls says he is happy to have a debate about Labour's time in government and the lessons we have learned. People are more worried about where things are going than where they've come from, he says. He suggests it would be ridiculous if he started every interview about reminiscing about what he did in 2006 "like some pub bore". But he acknowledges Labour's frontbench are a group of 40-somethings who are "veterans". "There's a lot of track record and experience," he says. The 1964 Wilson government was similar, he suggests. A more recent parallel is that Margaret Thatcher had been a cabinet minister before becoming PM, he says. "I don't think a responsible chancellor should be the cheerleader for an infrastructure project on this scale without being hardheaded about value for money," he says. He suggests George Osborne would much rather be a political strategist than a chancellor and he acts like it. "I don't think George Osborne is doing his job properly on HS2." That gets some applause. Richards suggests it sends out an incoherent message to oppose the biggest capital spending project in the country and yet push for more capital spending generally. Balls says Labour has had no discussions about announcing it is opposing HS2. "I think it's about time we injected a bit of hard thinking into this debate, and if at the end of that you go ahead, good," Balls says. Money is fungible, he says - every billion you spend on HS2 is not spent on other things. Is it the best project to spend £50bn on, he says. You don't sound very keen on it, says Richards. Balls says he is a Leeds Wakefield MP - you can see some benefits. But journey times alone are not enough, he says. "I'm not meaning to sound un-keen," says Balls. Balls recalls that the Lib Dems changed their policy on the deficit in order to go into coalition with the Tories. We want to build houses, create jobs and keep the economy moving, and we'll be disciplined doing that, he says. Richards asks about High Speed 2. Balls says this is something we have got to really know will work and will give value for money. We supported this, he says, but now costs have gone up and up. There are also questions about the benefits, he says. This is about the use of £50bn of public money, he says. We need to know the costs are properly under control, the benefits are there, and this is the best way to spend £50bn. It needs "a really hard and tough look" - after that they may approve it, he says. Has he already made up his mind? No, says Balls. But the costs have gone up by billions and billions in the last few months, he says. He compares it to the Olympics - which was "fiscally small" compared to HS2. Richards asks if Balls has changed his mind from his 2010 Keynesian views on the economy and the deficit to today's much more conservative approach. Balls suggests Keynes would have approached the 2010 economy by prescribing more spending, but the 2015 case will be very different, because of the mistakes the Coalition has made. "They should have taken our advice in 2010 and 2011, and they didn't." Richards asks if his Keynesian instincts got ahead of his political instincts. Balls says "if you don't make what you think is the right argument, you are letting people down". Balls says he and Ed Miliband, 18 months from the election, have set out more detail of tax and spending manifesto commitments that Cameron did before the 2010 election or Blair did before the 1997 vote. He talks about meeting mothers who said the amount of free childcare they were currently getting was not enough. Richards asks if Balls is trying to take "tax and spend" out of the election campaign. He says it's very hard for an opposition party to win that argument. Balls says if that was his idea he would be drawing the OBR into party politics, which he says he is not. There is a brief outbreak of jargon as Balls hits Richards with a reference to "Barnett consequentials". I'll draw a veil over that. Richards begins by asking Balls about whether the Office for Budget Responsibility will agree to analyse Labour spending policies. Balls says Robert Chote of the OBR supports the idea. He says there is no desire to draw the OBR into party politics. He says the OBR is established enough to take on this new role. He wants exactly the same certification the government gets for its measures. We can change the OBR's charter so it says "governments and prospective governments", but he admits it may need a change in the law. "I've not had a legal opinion on that." Balls says he is surprised that George Osborne has rejected the idea. (Sorry for spelling Steve Richards's name wrong in that tweet below. Very sloppy.) Hi, this is Paul Owen taking over from Andy for the evening. I’m here in a disappointingly bland and windowless conference room in the Metropole hotel – a far cry, unfortunately, from the seedy glamour conjured up by its memorable cameo appearance in The Waste Land. But that’s enough culture. I’m here to see shadow chancellor Ed Balls interviewed by the Independent’s Steve Richards, a perceptive interviewer and commentator who can usually wring some new information out of his victims. This has been Balls’s day at the Labour conference, so fingers crossed Richards will manage to catch him off guard tonight. I’ll live-blog the whole thing here. • Labour delegates have demanded an end to the public sector pay freeze. They applauded Dave Prentis, the Unison general secretary,when he put the demand at the heart of his speech to the conference. More than anything, our people need hope. They need to know now that when elected, our party will end the pay cap which is blighting so many lives. It's not rocket science. If this is a cost of living conference, then the pay freeze must end. No ifs, no buts - a clear commitment to end the Tory pay freeze. And they overwhelmingly backed an emergency motion, passed without opposition, saying the next Labour manifesto should include "a strategy to end the public sector pay cap to help support economic growth, improve services and tackle the cost of living crisis". Afterwards a party spokesman played down the suggestion that this meant the party was committed to lifting the pay cap. Of course Labour supports a strategy to return to growth. As public sector pay caps are currently set until 2016, it is right that this motion was amended before it was debated. Labour Party policy is made via the National Policy Forum. In his speech Prentis also called for the value of the national minimum wage to be lifted so it matches that of the living wage. What is so wrong with a clear, unambigous Labour promise to turn a statutory minimum wage into a statutory living wage. • Sajid Javid, a Conservative Treasury minister, has claimed that Ed Balls' speech contains "debt-funded spending commitments". There was nothing new in Ed Balls’ speech – just more debt-funded spending commitments, breaking his own promise to show ‘iron discipline’ on public spending. Coming after this week’s revelation that there is a £27.9bn black hole in Labour’s spending plans, it’s no wonder nobody trusts Ed Balls or Ed Miliband to take the tough decisions needed to secure a recovery for everyone. • Charlie Whelan, Gordon Brown's former spin doctor, has said that Brown would be "shocked" to learn what Damian McBride had been doing on his behalf. This is what he told the Daily Politics. I had no idea what Damian was up to and neither did Gordon Brown. I do genuinely think that Gordon Brown would be more shocked than anybody by some of the allegations that have been made in his book. • Jim Murphy, the shadow defence secretary, has said that Labour should do more to show that it understands ordinary people, particularly in the south of England, who go to church and like the royal family. (See 4.08pm.) • Vernon Coaker, the shadow Northern Ireland secretary, has said that nationalists must accept that the Good Friday agreement means Northern Ireland remains within the UK until a majority say otherwise. That's all from me for today. My colleague Paul Owen will post an update from the fringe. On her Reality Check blog Mona Chalabi says she thinks Labour's childcare pledge could cost £2bn, and that the £800m bank levy that is supposed to fund it won't cover the full cost. Here's a short afternoon reading list. • Alastair Campbell on his blog condemns Damian McBride. I honestly believe – and I know that this is a big if given how politics and politicians are – that if the big beasts of New Labour had all more or less stuck together, if Gordon had understood the value of the broader team and not just his own, if some of his darker people had been sacked early – or better still never been near the place – there is a chance we would still be there and this wretched coalition government would not be undoing so much of the good that Tony, Gordon and their colleagues did. That is the real crime of the horrible, nasty, vindictive politics and internal backstabbing that McBride, after all the years of GB denials,now admits to, so forgive me if I refuse to bow down before this attempt at Catholic redemption. • Siobhan Kenny at Comment is free says Damian McBride almost cost her her job. My colleagues tell me the problem with the comments is affecting our whole website. They are on the case at Guardian HQ. At a fringe meeting at lunchtime Jim Murphy, the shadow defence secretary, said Labour must do more to understand people living in the south of England. He said that in Scotland it had become unusual for people to vote Conservative, and he said he hoped that people in the south of England, and affluent voters, never felt the same way about supporting Labour. We have to ensure that it never becomes counter-cultural among optimistic and contented voters to ever consider voting Labour. The Conservatives can win an election without Scotland. It is harder for us to win an election without winning a substantial share of votes in some of the 209 constituencies south .... The Labour Party has sometimes talked in the past about respecting diversity and sometimes that's been, rightly, about the extension of civil rights for those with disabilities and the LGBT (lesbian, gay, bisexual and transgender). I think there needs to be a new language about respecting diversity that is about some of those voters who live their lives in a way that is entirely straightforward. They go to church on Sunday and they have an immeasurable sense of patriotism and a real affection for the royal family and much else besides. We have got to have a genuine understanding and attachment to that set of voters. Effectively he was saying Labour should make more of an effort to understand and appreciate those who go to church and like the royal family. The Telegraph's Jon Swaine has found Gordon Brown at the United Nations in New York and asked him about Damian McBride. He did not get an answer. The comments don't seem to be working at the moment. I don't know why, but I'm trying to get that sorted. On her Reality Check blog, Mona Chalabi says that the Conservative costings for Labour's jobs guarantee seem rather selective. And while we're on the subject of Len McCluskey, the Unite general secretary, he seemed to be threatening some kind of civil disobedience when he was speaking at a fringe meeting at lunchtime. He said this. If this government continues to attack us, and continues to try and reduce our rights even further, let me warn them this. If you push us further outside of the law, then be it on your own heads what the consequences are, because we’ve got no intention in Unite or in this movement of allowing laws introduced by the Bullingdon boys to restrict the legitimate and international rights we have to protect our workers ... Conference, if we’re pushed outside of the law then so be it. McCluskey has made this kind of comment before, talking about the prospect of using civil disobedience to resist any potential attempt to restrict labour rights. Earlier this year Len McCluskey, the Unite general secretary, suggested that Liam Byrne, the shadow work and pensions secretary, should be sacked. Since then there has been constant speculation about Byrne's future, and so it is hard to avoid seeing his speech to the conference just now in that context. As I said earlier, it sounded uncharacteristically leftish and combative (partly because he was quite shouty), But it went down well. Here are the main points. • Byrne said the Tories should be ashamed of their record. Once upon a time the Tories told us they cared: all those speeches in Easterhouse. And people gave them the benefit of the doubt. We were promised a Tory party that cared about the poor. We were promised a welfare revolution. We were promised we’re all in this together. Three years on I tell you the jury is in. A cost of living crisis. A million young people out of work. Long term unemployment at record highs. Disabled people living in fear. Child poverty rising. Living standards hammered. A promise that started in Easterhouse has ended with the spectacle of a Tory Minister, Michael Gove, blaming the poor for the temerity to turn up at a food bank. He should be ashamed. • He said Iain Duncan Smith, the work and pensions secretary, was incompetent as well as prejudiced. These Tories have let their prejudice destroy their policies. And just as bad as the prejudice is the incompetence. They say to err is human. But if you want someone to really screw it up you send for Iain Duncan Smith. • He said Labour was inviting Jenny Macklin, an Australian politician, to help investigate how to integrate health and social care. But let’s take Andy Burnham’s idea of whole person care and ask why not bring together health, social care - and the back to work system into one comprehensive service. That’s what Labour did in Australia. Let’s see if we can learn from that here. I’m delighted to announce that Jenny Macklin, a fine Labour politician and the architect of the system down under, is going to help us figure out how. In his speech Ed Balls also mentioned the Andy Burnham plan. He said this. Can we improve care and save money, as Andy Burnham has proposed, by pooling health and social care as a single service, with a single budget and joint management? That was significant because at one stage Balls was known to be sceptical about the idea. Taken together, these two speeches suggest that Labour is more committed to the proposal than when Burnham first floated it, slightly tentatively, in a speech. • Byrne said he said he was setting up a committee to look at ways of rescuing universal credit. The Tories’ system may prove dead on arrival. So we need a better way. So, today we announce our Universal Credit Rescue Committee. And I’m grateful to Kieran Quinn, leader of Tameside, the first pathfinder, for his offer to drive our work. • In the conference hall delegates are debating two composite motions on the cost of living. One of them calls for Labour to abandon the public sector pay cap. The session was opened by Liam Byrne, the shadow work and pensions secretary, who delivered an uncharacteristically leftish and combative speech. I'll post some excerpts shortly. Labour has had an opinion poll lead pretty much since the end of 2010, but as Paul Owen has been hearing, that lead is somewhat “fragile”. There were three reasons for this, said Ipsos Mori’s Gideon Skinner at a fringe event at the elegant Hotel du Vin just back from the Brighton seafront: Labour is losing ground on the electorate’s No 1 issue, the economy, with the Tory lead on this issue increasing to 18 points recently and people’s optimism about the economy rising too; Ed Miliband is “still not convincing the public”; and Labour is relying riskily on former Lib Dems switching to their party, when former Tories making the leap will surely be needed too. Skinner mapped Miliband’s approval ratings – which are not impressive – against those of failed Tory leaders Iain Duncan Smith and William Hague. “His highs have been better than Hague’s and IDS’s but his lows are fairly similar,” he said. He pointed out that Margaret Thatcher had worse ratings as a leader on some measures than opposition leaders James Callaghan and Neil Kinnock so these findings were “not the be-all and end-all … but I think there is something there to be concerned about”. Michael Dugher, shadow minister without portfolio, pointed out that a Lord Ashcroft poll recently had found Labour 14 points ahead in the 40 most marginal seats. And he said that in last year’s US election “[Mitt] Romney was ahead of [Barack] Obama on the economy but Obama was ahead of Romney in terms of standing up for ordinary people” – there was a “very similar trend here,” he said. Dugher claimed that “whenever Ed has exposure his ratings go up. We want more Ed Miliband, not less.” As the election approaches, Miliband would get more such exposure, he said. Skinner had shown the crowd a slide showing that 51% of people said they did not know what Miliband stood for, compared to 33% for Cameron, and Sophy Ridge of Sky, also on the panel, said this was “a troubling statistic”. She agreed that it was dangerous for Labour to rely on Lib Dems switching without persuading any Tories to do so – noting that in byelections since 2010 “the Lib Dems have held up pretty well”. Andrew Harrop of the Fabian Society agreed that “in every key marginal seat we need a few hundred Tories to vote Labour for us to be in a winning position”, adding that “on top of that we need to turn out people who didn’t vote last time” – young people, new voters, and people who have been switched off politics”. He was surprisingly confident that Labour “probably will be the largest party” in parliament after the next election, although it might not win a majority – but interestingly he warned that disputes over union funding of the party could be “a car crash this next six months … it could be so distracting”. Here's some reaction to the Ed Balls speech. From Simon Walker, director general of the Institute of Directors Politicians must understand the central role of business in creating wealth, prosperity and employment. I remain concerned by Labour’s proposals to increase the minimum wage in specific sectors, and requiring firms to take on an apprentice for every non-EU worker they hire is a recipe for disaster. Having said that, Ed Balls engaged with several issues vital to business leaders in his speech. We share his concerns about the extended Help to Buy scheme, which risks pushing up house prices while doing little to increase the supply of homes. Planning reform is the vital missing element here. There are still too many flaws in the system that hold up home-building. It was also significant that he questioned whether HS2 was the best way to spend £50bn. The IoD agrees with Labour that infrastructure investment is essential, but governments must be clear that they are getting value for money. Mr Balls’s commitment to discipline in the public finances is welcome, but will require Labour to clamp down on additional spending pledges. Their rhetoric must be matched by actions. He should be very wary of attempting to achieve deficit reduction through tax rises, which would be deeply damaging to the recovery. From John Longworth, director general of the British Chambers of Commerce Ed Balls identified many issues of concern to business in his conference speech, including infrastructure and growth finance. Yet Labour's policy alternatives on the key long-term economic issues remain embryonic at best. Many policy reviews have been undertaken, but Labour's renewed credibility with business rests on swiftly setting out concrete and clear measures on the key issues facing companies across the UK. And Labour must address worrying hints that they are starting to lose their nerve on transformative investment projects, including HS2, in favour of current spending. From Robin Hillman, a spokesman for the Robin Hood Tax campaign Our public services have been slashed to pay for the financial sector's mistakes, so it is good news that Labour is asking banks to make a greater contribution. But Labour's proposal lacks ambition – the bank levy and one-off bonus tax are little more than small change for the richest sector in Britain. Banks can afford to be paying billions more in tax, money that is desperately needed to shore up public services and create more jobs. From Craig Bennett, Friends of the Earth's director of policy and campaigns The shadow chancellor understands that the only long-term plan which works for Britain has low-carbon growth as its cornerstone. Mr Balls’s promise to let the Green Investment Bank borrow to invest and bring in a 2030 target for clean energy is exactly what we need to restore battered investor confidence. • Ed Balls, the shadow chancellor, has announced that a Labour government would increase the bank levy by £800m to fund the expansion of free childcare for three- and four-year-olds from 15 hours a week to 25 hours a week. Alison Garnham, chief executive of Child Poverty Action Group, welcomed the announcement. This proposal is great news for hard-pressed families struggling with the cost of childcare. Childcare costs are cripplingly high, and while tax credits give important help, most UK parents are still footing the lion's share of the bill. The more hours of childcare parents can get for free, the more it will help with the squeeze on living standards they are facing. • Balls has questioned the wisdom of spending £50bn on the HS2 high-speed rail project, creating speculation that Labour could abandon its support for the scheme before the 2015 general election. • A Treasury minister, Sajid Javid, has said the government will not change the remit of the Office for Budget Responsibility to allow it to audit Labour's spending plans. He was responding to Balls's call for the OBR to be allowed to assess the accuracy of Labour's claims. But Robert Chote, the head of the OBR, said he thought it would be a good idea to have opposition spending policies independently audited. I think the idea of having independent scrutiny of party manifestos in the runup to an election is generally a good idea. That would provide a more informed public debate, and would, for example, help potentially with coming up with coalition agreements afterwards. Certainly, with my former hat on with the Institute for Fiscal Studies, we were involved in trying to do that ahead of the last two general elections. Chote said that the OBR was taking legal advice to see whether changing its remit would require primary legislation. He also said that in the Netherlands the equivalent of the OBR publishes its assessment of opposition plans 12 weeks before the election. For the same to happen in the UK, parties would have to publish their manifestos much earlier than they do now, he said. • Jim Murphy, the shadow defence secretary, has said that Labour would make attacking a soldier a specific criminal offence. • Chuka Umunna, the shadow business secretary, has said Labour would hold an inquiry into blacklisting in the construction industry if the government does not order one. (See 11.56am.) • Alan Milburn, the former Labour cabinet minister, has said that Labour needs to find some "beef" to bolster its policies and should not be conniving with Tory attempts to reduce immigration. As Rowena Mason reports, Milburn said he was really worried about the direction of the party on immigration, shortly after Ed Miliband said Labour would cut the number of foreign workers coming to the UK. We seem to be moving towards a nice cosy consensus that I'm really worried about … our future relies on more immigration not less. If we've got an ageing population, with fewer domestically produced workers, the gap can only be closed if are prepared in a sensible, managed and rational way to encourage immigration not close it down. • David Lammy, the former minister, has told the World at One that Ed Miliband has not yet persuaded voters that he is a prime minister in waiting. You’ll get a constituency of people who switch on to the bedroom tax and welfare issues like that, but large segments of the country who own their own home and want to know how you’re going to run the economy because they don’t want interest rates to go up, or they want their kids to be able to get on the housing ladder ... Today, Ed Balls confronted that head-on, and delivered a lengthy, explicit answer. Gordon Brown was fond of using lists in his speeches and Balls used the same device at least twice, when he was reminding the audience what the last Labour government achieved and when he told them, right at the end, what Labour would do in 2015. It was a checklist for activists, ideal for campaigners who need to be able to answer the "why vote Labour" question on the doorstep. There is a lot of interest, too, in what he had to say about HS2. He has been using the no "blank cheque" line for some time, but to put that up in headlights in a conference speech signals, more clearly than before, that Labour is threatening to pull the plug on the project. (If you want to know why, it helps to look at the polls. Public support for HS2 is falling, more people are now opposed than in favour, and Labour supporters are particularly hostile to the idea.) I am not for a moment pretending there are not some who have suggested we should countenance a referendum. That is pretty different from the Conservative party, where, within the ranks of the cabinet, you have got people like Michael Gove and Philip Hammond falling over themselves on the way to the television studios to be the first to say 'if there was a referendum tomorrow I'd vote no'. On the subject of the Office for Budget Responsibility auditing Labour's spending plans, it is worth remembering what Robert Chote, the head of the OBR, has said about this. I interviewed him last year and asked him if this would be a good idea. He made it clear that in principle he was in favour, but he also said that he did not expect parliament to change the OBR's remit to allow it to audit opposition proposals any time soon. Here's the key quote. It's entirely reasonable for parliament to want us to establish our credibility and trustworthiness with a narrower mandate before you extend it in that sort of way. I think at the end of the day that having independent rigorous analysis of all the political parties' policies is a good thing for them, it's a good thing for public debate, it's a good thing for democracy. Glenis Wilmott, the leader of the Labour MEPs, is winning the soundbite of the day award so far. She has just spoken in the Britain's global role debate and she reminded delegates of David Cameron's comment about going to Brussels ready for an ambush. "In this town you have to be ready for an ambush at any time, and that means lock and load and have one up the spout," he said. After quoting Cameron, she described him as "disturbing cocktail of John Wayne and Alan Partridge". I'll post more from the speech when I've read the text. Justine Thornton, Ed Miliband's wife, is more than just a dress. She said so herself at a Young Labour event last night. I wanted to come along tonight to say a few words because I thought, 'If I don't say anything to you guys tonight probably all you'll know about me this year is the make of the dress I wear for Ed's speech on Tuesday. That's a side of me the media tend to focus on and I though it was quite important to reassure you that I am in fact more than a dress. She talked about how her relationship with Miliband developed. In May 2005, Ed was the prospective parliamentary candidate for Doncaster North. I was his friend. So I went to Doncaster to help him campaign and I was moving chairs around in a hall getting ready for a campaign meeting and I became aware there were three long-standing party members watching me. After a while one of them said to me who was I, so I explained that I was a friend of Ed's and I had come from London to help him campaign. There was a pause and then the lady said to me, 'That's a very long way to come to move chairs, are you sure you're just friends?'. She was clearly very astute and realised before we did I think. And she explained why she backed his decision to stand as for the Labour leadership. The key moment came when she saw him visit a working men's club in Plymouth, she said. As I listened to Ed's answers I realised firstly he knew and understood about the lives of the people in that room and secondly that he cared about creating a society that wouldn't feel like it felt to me that day, which was the people there and the bankers and the lawyers around me in London were living in two separate countries. That was it for me. That was the day when I understood why Ed had decided to stand to be leader of the Labour party, difficult a decision though that was for many reasons, and it was the day when psychologically the day I threw my lot in with Ed and the Labour party. So, there it is, that's why I'll be on the barricades in 2015 wearing whatever dress the media deem necessary to get us through. There was plenty of substance in the various Ed Balls interviews this morning. He wanted to talk about childcare, but the broadcasters wanted to talk about Damian McBride too. Here are the main points. I've taken the quotes from the Press Association and PoliticsHome. • Ed Balls has denied a story in the Times (paywall) saying that Labour is considering a plan to cut child benefit payments to parents who do not ensure their children have the MMR vaccine. He said this: There is no question of a Labour government ever taking child benefit away or punishing parents for choices they make on vaccinations. I’ve no idea where that’s come from. It’s not our policy. It’s on the front of the papers, but it’s just not true. The Times story said the idea was being considered by Jon Cruddas, Labour's policy chief. Here's an excerpt. Under plans being considered by Jon Cruddas, Labour’s policy chief, child benefit would be paid only to parents who could prove that their children’s vaccination records were up to date. The policy is already in place in Australia and Mr Cruddas, MP for Dagenham & Rainham, is championing it as a way of attaching “conditionality” to benefits and services provided by the state. “This is an example of the sort of measure which we want to see that ties public goods to how people behave as citizens,” said a senior source. • Balls has accused the Conservatives of refusing to let the Office for Budget Responsibility audit Labour's spending plans for party political reasons. After the proposal was announced last night, Sajid Javid, a Treasury minister, claimed the idea was "just a stunt" to distract attention from "the fact that Labour have been found out for making unfunded commitments". (Why inviting the OBR to say whether or not the commitments are unfunded should distract attention from this issue is not clear.) But Andrew Tyrie, the Conservative chair of the Commons Treasury committee, said the proposal could "improve the quality of public debate in an election". Balls said that Tyrie was right. I not only want it to happen, I think it will happen. So because the Conservative party will play party politics with trying to give the public reassurance that all parties manifestos add up, to rebuild trust in politics, then it won’t happen? That’s their choice, but I don’t think that’ s what they’ll do. There is currently a consensus about the independent OBR’s role. The chair of the Treasury Select Committee, who is a Conservative, has said he supports this and he doesn’t think it requires legislation. I’ve spoken to the OBR, and I think the OBR are absolutely up for doing this. But it’s parliament’s decision. I hope that without any legislation we can do this. The OBR did this job for the government for months before the law was even passed. If George Osborne can agree with me, the OBR can do this now. • He said that he had never briefed against colleagues in the way that Damian McBride did. This is what he said when he was asked if he could categorically say he had never been involved in a negative briefing against a government colleague. Yes. That’s not something I’ve ever done. Damian McBride has come out and said he did some of those things. It was despicable. • He also denied knowing that McBride was involved in negative briefings. This is what he said when he was asked if he had ever urged Gordon Brown to sack McBride (as Ed Miliband and Douglas Alexander have both said they did). This is what Balls said. To be honest nobody ever came to me and complained about Damian McBride. I didn’t pass on those complaints to Gordon Brown. I didn’t complain about Damian McBride, because I don’t think until we saw the revelations in this book, we didn’t know what was going on. I didn’t know that Damian McBride was doing personal briefings against ministers. The first time I’d found out was when I saw the text of that hideous email in 2009, and I said to Damian ‘what have you done? How could it come to this?’ On the Today programme, Nick Robinson, the BBC's political editor, said that this claim would be greeted with "deep scepticism" in Brighton. He suggested that "collective eyebrows hit the ceiling" when Balls said he did not know what McBride was up to. Here's some examples of journalists – how shall we put this politely – expressing some doubts about this. Miliband said in his interview with Andrew Marr yesterday that he asked Brown to sack McBride. Douglas Alexander told a fringe meeting last night that he did the same. He made the point when asked about McBride's claim that Alexander told Brown to get rid of his sister Wendy as Labour's leader in Scotland. This is what Alexander said. I did urge Gordon to dismiss somebody. But it was Damian McBride. That might explain the way that he briefed against me then and writes against me now. I was always a supporter of my sister and I was never a supporter of Damian. • Balls denied using McBride to undermine his rivals in the Labour leadership contest. If I ever did, it didn't work very well, did it, because I didn't become the party leader. No, it's utter nonsense. There's always going to be people who will in an off-the-record smearing way make allegations, but it's not true and there's no evidence for that at all. It's not something I've ever done, it's not something I will ever do. • Balls said that he did not agree with Tessa Jowell about wanting Gordon Brown to say what he knew about McBride's activities. To be honest, I don’t think the world is helped by Gordon Brown saying anything about this. It’s in the past. It’s irrelevant. • Balls said that Labour had not yet decided whether it would increase capital spending. Labour would match the coalition's plans for day-to-day spending in 2015, while making different choices within the spending envelope. Asked if he would increase spending on capital spending, he said: "On capital, we won't make that decision until we see where we are on the economy in a year-and-a-half's time." • He said that he was happy to describe himself as socialist – but not his economic programme. Of course, I’ve always used the word socialism, I’ve no problem about that. We for decades have talked about socialism, about our values. It’s not a use of the word which would apply to an economic programme, it’s not sort of the kind of German Democratic Socialist Republic kind of socialism. It’s a socialism which is rooted in the struggles of working people in the 19th century to get fairness, proper rights at work, to have a health service which works for them, education available for all. It’s about an economy and a society which is not only for the privileged few. • Balls said he was not worried that some business groups had reacted negatively to Labour's plan to force big firms to hire an apprentice every time they employed a skilled foreign worker. The fact you don't always get a 100% reaction from some businesses or from some banks or from some energy companies to a Labour policy probably tells you that Labour is setting out clear, detailed, tough policies which will work for working families, many of whom will feel they have had a pretty bad deal from banks and energy companies and some businesses. It's Ed Balls' big day at the Labour conference and already we've had two stories from the speech. Last night it was revealed that Balls will use his speech to say that he wants the Office for Budget Responsibility, the government's spending watchdog, to audit Labour's spending plans before the general election. And this morning Labour has released excerpts from the speech saying Balls will propose extending free childcare from 15 to 25 hours a week, saying he can fund the measure by raising the bank levy. This morning Balls has been giving a round of interviews. I'll post the highlights soon. Here's the agenda for the day. 9am: Conference opens. Conference arrangements committee report. 9.35am: Gareth Thomas, chair of the Co-operative party, gives a speech. 9.40am: Debate on Britain's global role, with speeches from Jim Murphy, the shadow defence secretary, Ivan Lewis, the shadow international development secretary, and Douglas Alexander, the shadow foreign secretary. 11am: Debate on work, including a speech from Chuka Umunna, the shadow business secretary, and a vote on an emergency motion saying Labour should set up a commission on employment rights as a matter of urgency. 12pm: Ed Balls, the shadow chancellor, delivers his speech. 1pm: Fringe meetings, including Len McCluskey, the Unite general secretary, on union rights, Liam Byrne, the shadow work and pensions secretary, on Labour's approach to poverty, Chuka Umunna on responsible capitalism and Jim Murphy on how to win the election. 2.15pm: Vernon Coaker, the shadow Northern Ireland secretary, gives a speech. 2.25pm: Debate on work and business, including speeches from Liam Byrne and Rachel Reeves, the shadow chief secretary to the Treasury, and a vote on an emergency motion saying a Labour government should end the public sector pay cap. I'll be covering all the news from the Labour conference, including looking at the papers and bringing you the best comment from the web. I'll post a summary at 1pm and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I'm on @AndrewSparrow

headline: M&S and the gender politics of quilts
trail_text: Letters: In my experience, infant girls go for pink and frilly, and boys pick gun and dinosaur patterns. Sorry
body_text: Perhaps Martin Newnham (Letters, 21 September), anxious to dragoon his grandchildren into the right career pathways, should try allowing them to choose their own quilt covers. In my experience, infant girls go for pink and frilly, and boys pick gun and dinosaur patterns. Sorry, but that is generally how it appears to be. Maybe M&S know this. Richard Wilson Oxford • When my dad took me to buy a quilt cover back in the 70s he was asked: "Is it for a boy or a girl?" He answered: "No, it's for a bed." It seems that attitudes haven't changed much. Helen Fowweather Thame, Oxfordshire • Why didn't Mr Newnham just buy a boy's quilt and save himself 60p postage complaining to M&S? He should not worry so much, women are very capable in spite of M&S. Hugh Scullion London • I couldn't help making the connection between the grandfather finding pink quilts labelled for girls and space and dinosaur-themed quilts labelled for boys in Marks & Spencer, and a favourite aunt (A Letter to..., Family, 21 September) buying her nephew Lego, pens and books while her niece gets "pretty scarves, nail varnish and sweet necklaces". Because boys need "food, love and exercise" while girls just want "pretty knick-knacks and girly chats". And we wonder why more girls don't aspire to be scientists and engineers. Janet Hooper Ivybridge, Devon

headline: Politics as it happened
trail_text: MPs bicker over national security and public funding
body_text: Sir Humphrey seems the right point at which we part ways. Let's do the evening summary. Today, in federal politics: Two issues dominated: national security and a deal between the major parties about election funding and new thresholds for political donations. The Coalition is retaining pressure on the government over proper resourcing for Australia's spy agencies; and it's linking that issue to asylum boats. Shadow Attorney-General George Brandis used a private briefing from ASIO as a means of accusing the Prime Minister of misleading parliament. The Prime Minister had downplayed a Four Corners report on Monday night which suggested ASIO had been the subject of a hack - Senator Brandis suggested Ms Gillard's comment was incorrect based on his information from ASIO. Labor then blasted Senator Brandis for talking out of school. Opposition leader Tony Abbott gathered the cameras to record a meeting he and his team convened with a former ASIO chief, Paul O'Sullivan, on the subject of Australia's security. The Prime Minister then accused Mr Abbott of cheap politics and grubby theatrics. A deal between the major parties on public funding for elections and new thresholds for the disclosure of donations looked to be coming unstuck in the late afternoon as a backlash increased. Liberals were breaking ranks on the package. One MP declared it would be withdrawn before it reached the House of Representatives. The parliamentary independents criticised the package as a major party fix and a breach of faith with the voters. Labor man Martin Ferguson meanwhile announced he would retire from politics at the September poll. It was an emotional valedictory. Mr Abbott choked up as he paid tribute to Mr Ferguson. It was that kind of day - nothing held back. Anywhere. Thanks so much to Mike Bowers, who shot like a dream today in the chamber and elsewhere. Thanks to Sydney colleagues: Madhvi, Mary and Christian. We'll do it all again tomorrow. Vintage Sir Humphrey. Courageous means this will lose you the election. Labor's Joel Fitzgibbon, meanwhile, has gone all Sir Humphrey Appleby on the package. Mr Broadbent says its just untenable for the major parties to give themselves a big lick of taxpayers money. When you can't get money for an infrastructure project for your electorate, you can't go home and tell your voters that you've just voted for a package to give more money to political parties. Back now to this funding and donations deal between the government and the Coalition. It seems to be blowing up, big time. Victorian Liberal MP Russell Broadbent has just told me there is serious disquiet within the Coalition about this proposal. Mr Broadbent: I will not support this bill. There has been no public discussion about this. It's been dumped on us out of the blue. This bill has not been through the shadow cabinet or the party-room. Mr Broadbent won't go as far to say that he will cross the floor. He thinks this proposal is dead in the water. I expect the bill to be withdrawn before it gets to the House. A number of years ago I wrote a feature for The Age on the Fergusons and the uranium debate within the ALP. If you are interested in more particulars about this family, and one of Martin Ferguson's signature policy issues - convincing the ALP to comes to terms with uranium mining and exports - you can have a read of it here. If you click through and look at the page you'll see a great picture of Jack Ferguson and his sons. Here's a excerpt: The story of the Ferguson family tells much about Labor's internal struggles with uranium. Jack Ferguson — father of federal Labor MPs Martin and Laurie, and trade unionist Andrew — spoke passionately against softening Labor's stance at the conference of 1982. And 24 years after that time, his sons remain divided. Martin supports change, Andrew doesn't, and Laurie thinks the current policy is stupid, but this doesn't mean he's comfortable with where the party is going on the issue. A reader has emailed to chide me for failing to tell the story of Martin Ferguson's father Jack. I said father Jack was a NSW politician. My correspondent: Father Jack rose from being a bricklayer to Deputy Premier of NSW. Noted. And of course, that is absolutely true. Here's another section. On Labor values, the achievements since 2007, and the importance of remaining true to the principles of market economics. Mr Ferguson: My friends, we have had 21 years of growth in this country and we should never forget that as a nation. When other countries faced recession during the global financial crisis, Australia continued to grow. In my opinion, this, along with the apology to the Stolen Generation, are major achievements of Labor in government since 2007. I am also delighted that we are now finally moving towards a national disability scheme, a further chance to look after the less privileged in our society. Australia’s recognition as a member of the G20 is also a fitting recognition of the constructive role we play internationally. I think it’s fair to say many try to demonise the resources industry, but we should not forget that it accounts for 60 per cent of our export wealth – wealth that has meant we can take better care of our community, improve the lives of Australians and give opportunities to those who never thought that they could do the things they now do in that sector. As Minister for Energy, I am proud of the microeconomic reforms process I have continued to deliver in the energy sector – competition reforms established under the Keating Government. I am proud that I have stayed true to the market principles underpinning the sector to deliver an efficient and reliable essential service to the community. Let's press on now. I've sought a text of Mr Ferguson's speech. Here are some of the key passages. Mr Ferguson: When I look back on my career, firstly at the Miscellaneous Worker’s Union, then as ACTU President, and finally as a Member of Parliament, my main motivation has been to get Australians into decent, well-paying jobs. This is what the Labor Party means to me: helping those less fortunate in life by providing new jobs and opportunities to achieve a better quality of life. Creating opportunities by working with business is not the same thing as pointless class rhetoric. In essence we need to grow the pie to share it. As I said in my Maiden speech to Parliament on the 2nd of May 1996, “The people of the electorate are working people whose priorities first are their families, better job opportunities, better housing opportunities, better educational opportunities, better health opportunities and support for the aged”. During the 1980s and 1990s, this involved some genuinely tough decisions with people like Simon Crean, Bill Kelty, and of course Bob Hawke and Paul Keating. We knew the economy had to be restructured, which would involve closing down old industries, creating efficiencies and new opportunities. This meant that jobs were lost in many industries with members that I and other unionists had represented for decades. Colleagues, they were hard decisions to make, but they were taken in the knowledge that they would provide the basis for a better Australia for all of us. Let me tell you: standing at the factory gates with people who had just lost their jobs, all for the greater good of reforming our economy was not easy! Mr Dreyfus insists that ASIO is properly resourced. None of the delays in the construction of the ASIO building relate to any breach of security he says. I'm just going to pop round to Sky to talk to David myself about the events of the day. Back with you all in a jiffy. Attorney-General Mark Dreyfus is out now to attack Senator George Brandis for revealing details of his private ASIO briefing. He's on Sky News, with political editor David Speers. Mr Dreyfus says the Prime Minister was correct to say there were inaccuracies in the Four Corners report. She was completely right to say so. He then declines to detail what the inaccuracies were. He's back to Senator Brandis being an outrage. While I've been watching Question Time, my colleague Lenore Taylor has been chasing down Coalition people. Just prior to Question Time, it was becoming clear that there is a fair amount of disquiet around the Coalition about the public funding of political parties deal. The Opposition leader's office is saying only that the party will respond after it sees details of the legislative package, which is supposed to be introduced tomorrow. Some MPs are unhappy that the Liberal Party has signed on to this arrangement with the ALP. Mr Abbott composes himself sufficiently to say farewell. Mr Ferguson looks chuffed. Notwithstanding Mr Ferguson's general disaffection - his concerns with Labor's political strategy, his support for Kevin Rudd, his general alienation from things - I think it's amazing he's going. Martin Ferguson is a workaholic. He lives, breathes, eats and sleeps politics, and Labor politics. He was born to, and for, this life. Hard to imagine him moving on. Bit of love too from Kevin Rudd. Some quick context and background for readers who don't follow politics closely. Mr Ferguson was a Cabinet minister up until earlier this year. A supporter of Kevin Rudd, Mr Ferguson went to the backbench after the botched leadership spill in March. Before parliament, Mr Ferguson was a trade union official. The Fergusons are a significant family in Labor history. Father Jack was a NSW politician. Mr Ferguson's brother Laurie is in federal parliament, and brother Andrew is a union leader. Mr Ferguson has been critical of Labor's class war rhetoric in this term. His remarks on that subject in the farewell speech this afternoon were another slap at the Treasurer Wayne Swan. Mr Ferguson's pragmatic political style, and his commitment to free market economics, has won him friends across the aisle. Mr Abbott's emotional reaction to the news is a barometer of that respect. A lot of Coalition MPs like the member for Batman. As Mr Abbott said: I salute an honourable opponent and a great Australian. Mr Ferguson has been a trenchant critic of the Greens. I doubt there will be tears this afternoon from Greens leader Christine Milne. Here was Mr Ferguson, in the chamber, saying farewell, just a few moments ago. The Prime Minister says farewell to Mr Ferguson. Lovely frame from Mike Bowers. Mr Ferguson thanks the parliamentary staff, the parliamentary library and parliamentary committees "where friendships are formed across the political divide." He thanks his former departmental officials, his personal staff. My success has been your success. Mr Ferguson chokes up as he mentions his parents, his wife and his children. Life has never been easy with me due to long hours and lengthy periods away from home. I thank Trisha, Ben and Clare for your encouragement and support. Without that I would never have been part of the achievement of Labor and the labour movement. Parliament breaks into applause. Ferguson is much respected in this place. Now the Prime Minister is seeking to speak on indulgence. It's been a privilege to sit and listen to him. Ms Gillard says in our own Labor way we will honour his commitment. I know personally the sacrifices he has made. I understand the toll that's taken. The Prime Minister says from Labor's point of view, he can leave this parliament knowing hes been a pivotal part of great policy achievements. The Leader of the Opposition contributes. Mr Abbott says its a sad day for the parliament to see the exit of a person of such stature. He is steeped in this parliament. He is steeped in the tradition of service. The Member for Batman is Labor royalty. He respects the party he has served, we too respect the party he has served, the Labor Party at its best has been a nation building party. I regret that he felt unable to remain in the current parliament and in the current government. Now Mr Abbott is crying. Labor's Martin Ferguson has used indulgence to announce he will quit parliament at the next election. Mr Ferguson is clearly emotional. He says he's advised the Prime Minister of his intentions. It's a wide-ranging farewell speech. Mr Ferguson remarks that false class warfare does no-one any credit. He says we have to grow the pie to share the pie. He's paying tribute to colleagues including Simon Crean and Bob Hawke. He says reforms in that era have been vital to Australia prosperity, but explaining that to the workers at the factory gate who had just lost their jobs was not easy. In this parliament, Mr Ferguson reflects on his work as resources minister. Many seek to demonise the resources industry, he says, but we should never forget it provides export wealth. The Prime Minister has ended Question Time. Mr Pyne is unhappy. Ms Gillard has ended proceedings before 3pm. Outrageous. Speaker Anna Burke has just advised the Member for Herbert not to be a dibber dobber. Stephen Jones sets a challenge. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison has bowled up two questions to the Prime Minister concerning NSW police seeking information about asylum seekers. Mr Morrison says the government has refused to provide the information being sought by the NSW Police Commissioner. Mr Morrison is quoting evidence to senate estimates. Ms Gillard: If our authorities and agencies get legitimate requests from agencies, then of course they are responded to. That question from Bronwyn Bishop in the making. Liberal frontbencher Bronwyn Bishop to Ms Gillard. Given the Prime Minister's assertion that she takes national security seriously, why then, when she was deputy prime minister, did she send her bodyguard to meetings of the national security committee of Cabinet? The Prime Minister: The Leader of the Opposition in sanctioning that question is showing why he's unfit to lead. Another national security question - asylum seekers released without security checks. The Prime Minister objects again. They (the Opposition) gone down the low road of trying to provoke fear in the community. It is grossly unacceptable. The Prime Minister gets a border protection question. Ms Gillard counsels the Opposition against pursuing questions for base political motives. Hmm. Don't be political in Question Time? There's a new concept. Nobody does eye roll better than Speaker Anna Burke. The Prime Minister is asked whether she stands by comments she made to parliament yesterday concerning the Four Corners report on the hack of the ASIO building. The Prime MInister says she's not endorsing the partial quote from Liberal frontbencher Michael Keenan, but she's standing by her complete answer yesterday. I stand by the statement I made in full. Well done Anthony. Mr Byrne is congratulated by his good friend Kevin Rudd. Labor's Anthony Byrne in the House. The man of the moment. Mr Pyne is back now, asking the Prime Minister about Mr Byrne's answer. Given he's sticking by his criticism, do you still say he's wrong? The Prime Minister remarks that Mr Byrne has provided a very elegant answer to his previous question. Mr Pyne objects. He's now been warned by the Speaker. The Prime Minister sticks by her answer. The facts about ASIO are these. Since coming to government in 2007, the government has increased funding for ASIO by 27 per cent. This is a campaign being pursued for base political reasons, not the national interest. The Manager of Opposition Business Christopher Pyne is posing a question to Labor backbencher Anthony Byrne - the man who kicked off this national security debate by raising concerns about the impact of the efficiency dividend on Australia's spy agencies. This is highly unusual, and House of Representative Speaker Anna Burke rules the question out of order. Mr Pyne objects, at length. Speaker Burke holds her ground. The question is out of order. Mr Pyne points out that Mr Byrne is happy to answer the question. He does look happy enough. Mr Albanese stands up to back the Speaker. Mr Pyne is then asked to withdraw an interjection. He withdraws, but complains that there has been a false statement. Speaker Burke asks Mr Pyne to rephrase his question. Mr Albanese says hang on, why is he getting another go? Speaker Burke ignores Mr Albanese and says re-phrase the question. Mr Pyne re-phrases the question. Mr Albanese objects again. The question is out of order. Speaker Burke says part of it is. Mr Byrne is on his feet. Mr Byrne: I have nothing further to add to the statement I have made. Mr Byrne then criticises the Leader of the Opposition. He says the report from the intelligence committee was a bipartisan report. He blasts Mr Abbott for throwing national security around like a political football. He says Mr Abbott is the least qualified person to comment on national security since Mark Latham. Mark Latham! He would of course be a former Labor leader. I wish readers of #politicslive could have heard the roar of laughter that just echoed up the corridor of the parliamentary press gallery in Canberra. Gold. Here's Question Time. Mr Abbott opens on national security and ASIO. Does the Prime MInister accept evidence given to a senate committee that ASIO has reduced its overseas operations? The Prime Minister: I refer the Leader of the Opposition to my answers yesterday. Over the life of this government funding has increased by 27 per cent. The facts are the facts, and they are exactly as I've outlined them. Mr Abbott comes back with two supplementaries quoting evidence to senate committees. Ms Gillard sticks by her statement that ASIO's funding has increased. Labor MP Stephen Jones, who has lead a backbench push to crack down on live odds being broadcast during sport, has made a quick statement to parliament. He's welcomed the Prime Minister's response unveiled on Sunday. But he's indicating the matter is not yet concluded. Mr Jones wanted stronger measures. It may well not be the last word on the matter. Stirring in Liberal ranks? ABCNews24 is broadcasting an interview with Gary Gray, the former Special Minister of State, on election funding. Political editor Lyndal Curtis is asking Mr Gray can he guarantee that the extra public funding won't go to political advertising for the looming campaign. Mr Gray says the money will go to administrative funding, not to ads. Lyndal doesn't seem convinced. She's also wondering why Labor rolled over on the reporting threshold for political donations. Labor wanted to reduce the threshold to $1,000. The ALP shook hands with the Liberals on $5,000. Why, when you had the numbers to get $1,000 through the parliament with the cross benchers, did you backflip, Lyndal inquires. Mr Gray says its best to reach agreement on such matters between the "parties of government" - not the minors and micros. Not sure how that's become conventional wisdom, but it has over the past 48 hours. The Liberals, it must be said, are noticeably quiet on this deal. I don't think I've heard anyone out defending it. Have I missed something? Question Time coming up at 2pm, stay tuned. Speaking of 100 days to go, election campaigns always mean an assault on the letterboxes of the voters of Australia. This leaflet looks particularly charming. Some video just in of the stoush we pointed you to earlier today between National senator Barnaby Joyce and Independent Tony Windsor - slugging it out in September for the seat of New England. Recapping: Mr Windsor alleged Senator Joyce was the lucky beneficiary of a donation from mining magnate Gina Rinehart. Senator Joyce: To the best of my knowledge, I've received not so much as a dollar. Do you accept Senator Joyce at his word Mr Windsor? No, I don't. Don't worry - only 100 days to go, give or take. Professor Lowe is talking about the downsides of work intensification on well being. (Anyone know what he's talking about? No, didn't think so, el desko readers.) Now he's pondering the strategy of promoting economic growth at all costs. Professor Lowe says we are failing miserably by looking at just one blunt measure. How about sustainability? How about biodiversity? Health, happiness, wisdom. Watching the sunset, listening to the birds. We need to think about what we value and how we get back to improving our quality of life. And we need to be globosapiens (that's wise citizens of the earth). There's only one planet. No plan B. Consumption should not be an end in itself. The future is not somewhere we are going, it's something we are creating. The National Press Club is playing host today to Ian Lowe of the Australian Conservation Foundation. The theme is ecological security, and spending to promote it. A lovely scene from the building from Mike Bowers. Senator Brandis strolls through the Mural Hall with colleague, Wyatt Roy. Now speaking of around the building, I'm informed the Shadow Treasurer Joe Hockey and former Prime Minister Kevin Rudd are enjoying a cappucino together in the autumn sunshine. Aussies courtyard. A place of peace love and harmony. Let's take stock. Seven things about federal politics you need to know this lunchtime. National security is still dominating the political news cycle. Shadow-Attorney General Geroge Brandis has accused the Prime Minister of misleading parliament yesterday over comments she made about an "inaccurate" Four Corners story about a hack on the ASIO building. Senator Brandis' comments followed a private security briefing with ASIO late yesterday. Senator Brandis has been given a slap by Transport Minister Anthony Albanese for speaking publicly about an intelligence briefing. Not the done thing, Mr Albanese says. Mr Abbott is keeping the spotlight firmly on this issue, inviting a former ASIO chief, Paul O'Sullivan, in for a chat about security issues in general. Remarked Mr Abbott at said briefing: "These are difficult and testing times for our security agencies. We are determined as a Coalition, should responsibility fall on our shoulders, to make sure that our agencies have the resources to do the job that is necessary." The deal between the ALP and the Liberal Party covering public funding for the election and donation thresholds has prompted all the parliamentary independents to wring their hands and warn of a voter backlash. Senator Xenophon, in continuation. The outrage about the major party agreement on public funding and donation thresholds continues to echo around the parliament today. We've had Rob Oakeshott, and Tony Windsor, and the Greens out bagging this deal this morning. South Australian Independent Nick Xenophon is the latest person to find a microphone. This is about an escalating arms race in terms of advertising, and this helps incumbents. I think there will be a backlash. If so inclined, you can listen-in to Mr Abbott's remarks at the beginning of his meeting with his national security team and Mr O'Sullivan this morning. Speaking of bi-partisanship, rallying for cancer. Health Minister Tanya Plibersek, and her shadow, Peter Dutton. Important to point out for the record that staged photo opportunities is very much multi-partisan practice in this place. Funny isn't it though? It feels very analogue in the digital world. Excuse me. Could we have some privacy? Greens Senator Hanson-Young has ventured out to one of the parliamentary courtyards to comment on a story from the ABC that police in effect inflamed tensions on Christmas Island in the lead-up to a riot by detainees in March. She's not sure if a senate committee was misled by officials giving evidence about this incident. But Senator Hanson-Young wants the Gillard Government to overturn its asylum policy, particularly the no-advantage test ensuring that asylum seekers can't improve their circumstances by paying a people smuggler. It is a recipe for disaster. We need to scrap the no-advantage rule. My colleague Lenore Taylor has kicked off her new analytical blog this morning with a column about the post truth election. Politicians have always tried to paint their opponents in an unflattering way and cast doubt upon their promises and credibility. But increasingly the “narrative”, the story politicians tell about themselves and their opponents, bears scant relationship to the actual policies on offer. Journalism, and the new trend towards "fact checking" units, is supposed to separate the policies from the stories. But as Mitt Romney’s pollster Neil Newhouse famously declared during the US presidential election, “We’re not going to let our campaign be dictated by fact checkers.” The challenge is to make sure that come polling day, voters can still make a decision on the basis of facts. Interested of course in thoughts from readers. You can join in on the blog, or on Twitter, using the hashtag #politicslive I thought yesterday was a blast from 2001. Political tragics might enjoy this wander down memory lane. Here's John Howard's election policy speech from 2001 - the speech that famously declared we decide who comes here, and the circumstances in which they come. The more things change, and all that. Amusingly I gather, Mr Abbott asked the snappers to leave the organised picture opportunity because "we need some privacy." No use pondering manifest ironies in politics. The quickest route to madness - that sort of thing. Meanwhile, Michelle Grattan from The Conversation wrote a piece this morning analysing the return of national security to the political stage. She reasons national security is never a great issue for Labor. Question Time yesterday was dominated by what we once called the "tin hat" themes: appropriate resourcing for security agencies, and boat arrivals. Tony Abbott's political attack was straight out of John Howard's playbook. Here's an excerpt from Michelle: National security issues don’t play well for Labor, so to have two controversies erupting simultaneously is a fresh blow for a government already on the ropes. Four Corners' allegation that Chinese hackers have obtained the blueprint of Canberra’s huge new ASIO building was separate from complaints on Monday from Anthony Byrne, Labor chairman of the parliamentary committee that oversights the intelligence organisations, that agencies are struggling under belt tightening. But the two have been easily conflated by the opposition, which also drew a link between the funding argument and the large numbers of boat people, whom it said are in the community without adequate security checks. And in the background is a heightened awareness of danger, with chief of the Australian Defence Force David Hurley sending ADF members a warning to be vigilant, in the wake of the appalling murder in London of the British soldier, and a high-profile arrest in Sydney this week by officers of the counter-terrorism team of a man who allegedly threatened a public official. Now for some alternative government optics. Photographers were invited down to record a meeting between the leader of the Opposition, Mr Abbott, his national security team, and the former head of ASIO, Paul O'Sullivan. Mr Abbott's spokesman says Mr O'Sullivan was invited in this morning to brief the Opposition on a range of national security issues. Has the budget emergency been overtaken by the national security emergency? And this one. Mr Albanese was asked about party reform because of a story we covered yesterday on the blog: a motion from Victorian backbencher Steve Gibbons to get rid of the practice of the leader appointing the front bench. Kevin Rudd introduced this practice as leader, overturning a longstanding tradition where the factional bosses selected the ministry. Mr Gibbons, just for your background, is no fan of Mr Rudd. Rather amusingly, Mr Rudd was quoted in a newspaper this morning indicating he would support the Gibbons motion. Mr Gibbons took to Twitter for his reaction. If so inclined, you can listen to this short grab from Senator Brandis on Sky Television earlier. He says the Prime Minister in Question Time yesterday dismissed the Four Corners report as inaccurate. That claim, by the Prime Minister, is false. The Shadow Attorney-General George Brandis has been out this morning telling reporters that the Prime Minister misled parliament yesterday when she suggested a Four Corners report about a hack on the ASIO building was "inaccurate." Senator Brandis was briefed by the head of ASIO late yesterday concerning the allegations aired in the Four Corners program. Mr Albanese is asked to comment on whether Ms Gillard misled the parliament. Senator Brandis was briefed by ASIO last night, a reporter begins. Mr Albanese: How do you know that? That was a joke. You want me to comment on an interview between Senator Brandis and ASIO? Get serious. I'm not going to comment on an intelligence briefing. I pretty surprised the Shadow Attorney-General is commenting on a briefing from ASIO. Then he's asked about the electoral funding and donations package. Do these changes have any merit? There are absolute merits to this reform. Such as? Mr Albanese says political donations of $5,000 or more will now be transparent. He says it is always desirable as a point of principle with electoral reform to proceed on a bipartisan basis - to seek major party agreement. Then we are onto ALP reform. Should the caucus select the ALP frontbench, or should the leader select the frontbench? Mr Albanese says the party needs reform. He wants democratisation of the party from the grass roots up. He says party members should have a say in the ALP leadership. Now, is Kevin Rudd causing trouble again? Kevin is continuing to do his job. The Transport Minister Anthony Albanese is out in one of the chilly parliament courtyards addressing reporters. Mr Albanese says he's cranky with the Coalition for voting against legislation last night "standing up for Australian jobs." Mr Albanese then finds himself being asked about Ford. He's sad about Ford leaving Australia, and the job losses at Geelong and Broadmeadows. Then he's asked about the fate of the Parramatta to Epping rail link. (We mentioned this story in our wrap of the political news this morning.) Why has this project been shelved? We didn't defer it, the state government has refused to go ahead with this project. We can't force a state government to build an extension to a state government rail line. On the subject of mining squllionaires, my ever diligent colleague from ABC online, Simon Cullen, has this update about Clive Palmer and his PUP. In addition to the national security theme, MPs are continuing their huffing and puffing over a deal the ALP and the Liberal Party have struck about political donations and public funding for elections.
