The Independents, Tony Windsor and Rob Oakeshott, think the major parties have done the wrong thing. Mr Oakeshott is suggesting this is a breach of the agreement he reached with the ALP allowing the ALP to form government after the 2010 poll. And just to stir the pot on political donations a bit more, Mr Windsor is out and about declaring that his opponent in the seat of New England, the Nationals' Barnaby Joyce, has been the lucky recipient of help from mining billionaire Gina Rinehart. Senator Joyce is personally close to the reclusive Ms Rinehart. Not so, says Senator Joyce. He's been returning in kind. Mr Windsor, he says, is the multi-millionaire, courtesy of the sale of his property to a coal company. It's nasty up there: New England will be one of the most interesting contests of the 2013 election in my view. Good morning and welcome to our live coverage of politics from the national capital. Our gorgeous opening blog picture from Mike Bowers seems absolutely appropriate given Canberra seems to have swerved briefly into a spy thriller. A testy political debate over spies and terror has carried over from yesterday into the morning in politics. Lets begin this morning with a run through the main political stories in the newspapers. Climate The Clean Energy Finance Corporation is planning to write up to $800 million in green loans before the election, defying the Coalition's call for the agency not to sign contracts before September 14 because Tony Abbott has vowed to scrap it. The CEFC has revealed it is in "active discussions" with 50 projects seeking $2 billion and that an additional 119 project proponents have presented proposals that are seeking finance worth $3.3bn. The figures are contained in an email from the CEFC to the opposition pleading its case not to be scrapped if the Coalition wins the election. (Paywalled) Public funding of elections With unusual bipartisanship, Labor and the Coalition argue their agreement to award political parties another $58m in public funding is good for democracy. But the Greens and independents beg to differ. Despite a fiery debate in Labor’s caucus, in which long-time campaigners for donation reform said they were “ashamed” of the agreement, the government formally announced details of the changes to be rushed through before parliament rises for the September election. The attorney general, Mark Dreyfus, confirmed they would award around $1 per vote in each house (33 cents per vote per year of a parliamentary term) to all parties and independents to help with the costs of party administration and free them from the need to rely entirely on private donations. The payment is on top of the $2.47 per vote the parties are already entitled to. Terror An Australian-born man being investigated for alleged terrorism offences threatened to slit the throat of a commonwealth official during a telephone conversation between the two, a court has heard. Milad Bin Ahmad Shah al-Ahmadzai was arrested at his home in Sydney's west on Monday night by officers from the Joint Counter Terrorism Team, which works closely with ASIO and includes staff from the Australian Federal Police. (Paywalled) Paid parental leave Tony Abbott has sought to silence growing internal dissent over his controversial paid parental leave scheme by asserting the decision to proceed with the policy was set in stone and insisting most women, including farmer’s wives, would benefit. Mr Abbott put his foot down in Tuesday’s joint Coalition party room meeting as the dissent among the Nationals grew louder following the Coalition’s decision to support Labor's budget saving to axe the baby bonus for stay-at-home mothers. Mr Abbott also faced unrest as the party room endorsed the shadow cabinet decision to support another savings measure which would trim the 30 per cent private health insurance rebate by $700 million over four years. Eight MPs spoke out on this. Paid parental leave was discussed at Nationals’ own party room meeting on Monday night. Informally, some Nationals are threatening to vote against the measure when in government; one, who did not want to be identified, told colleagues he had eaten his fair share of “shit sandwiches” over the years but “I’m not going to eat this one”. (Payawlled) Rudd mischief Kevin Rudd backers have again been accused of engaging in a final attempt to destabilise Prime Minister Julia Gillard before the election. With 108 days to go before the poll, tensions in the Labor caucus spilled over yesterday, with the PM coming under attack on a number of fronts from within her own ranks. Rudd supporters have been accused of trying to inflame a sledging match in caucus after Senator John Faulkner condemned new laws which give political parties an extra $58 million in taxpayer-subsidised campaign funds and go soft on disclosure rules. Mr Faulkner called the laws a "disgrace". MPs were also taken by surprise when retiring MP Steve Gibbons, a fan of neither Ms Gillard or Mr Rudd, put a motion to the caucus calling for the PM to be stripped of her powers to choose her Cabinet - powers first granted to Mr Rudd in 2007. Marginal-seat MP Laura Smythe, a Rudd supporter, also weighed in, raising the ongoing debacle of asylum seeker policy. Adding fuel to the fire, Mr Rudd yesterday backed Mr Gibbons' call, claiming he would support the reforms. A spokesman said: "Following his statements of February, 2012, of course Mr Rudd would support this change as part of a package of larger reforms to the Australian Labor Party, including reforms which Mr Rudd has spoken of in the past." Transport Rail commuters and motorists will wait longer for vital infrastructure after the Gillard government quietly shifted $2 billion earmarked for the Parramatta-Epping line into a fund for projects not due to be built until after 2019. The decision puts a gaping hole in Tony Abbott's $6 billion promise to redirect the money to duplicate the Pacific Highway if the Coalition wins government in September. In addition to following my live updates throughout the day, you can also follow me on Twitter @murpharoo and join in the conversation there by using the hashtag #politicslive

headline: Energy bills plan announced and Cameron in China: Politics live blog
trail_text: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of all the day's political developments as they happen, including the full details of the government's plans to cut energy bills, energy firms announcing cuts in response, and David Cameron in China
body_text: • Caroline Flint, the shadow energy secretary, has complained that energy companies will not have to contribute a penny towards the measures announced today to curb fuel price rises. She was speaking in the Commons, where she dismissed Ed Davey's measures as ineffective. She told the energy secretary: Last month, you appeared to agree with our criticisms of the way energy companies have put up their prices when you said they were treating their customers like, and I quote, 'cash cows'. In light of what you have said, can you confirm that in what you have said today there is not a single measure that will cost the energy companies a single penny? [There are] hundreds of millions of pounds of tax-payers' money being spent, energy companies helping fewer households with their energy efficiency and yet people's bills will still be higher this winter than last, and the energy companies are still allowed to carry on over-charging people. Whatever you say here today, if you genuinely want to get bills down, nothing less than a price freeze, and action to stop the energy companies over-charging, will do. The Green party has also come out against them. This is from Caroline Lucas, the Green MP. It is extraordinarily irresponsible, as well as counterproductive, for the government to claim it is reducing energy bills by slashing the scheme designed to bring them down. Watering down our energy efficiency commitments at precisely the time their most needed would mean more cold homes, more winter deaths, and job losses in the construction industry. Only a massive programme of home insulation and other energy efficiency measures will bring energy bills down for good. • Dominic Grieve, the attorney general, has accused the European Union of over-stepping its legal powers. He is making the argument in a speech in Brussels. This is what he said about the speech in advance. This won't just be the usual Euro bashing by a British minister. I am going to Brussels to speak - partly in French - as a lawyer, to lawyers and spell out what lies at the base of Britain's and other member states' frustrations with the EU. I'm going to talk about some very specific proposals where we think there's been some woolly thinking and try and get across to these key EU legal officials and judges that it's this sort of thing that really riles citizens in Britain and across Europe and needs to be changed if the EU is to meet the challenge of securing prosperity for people across the continent. And here's an extract from the speech released in advance. Overstepping the boundaries under the treaties undermines the very legitimacy of EU action. To put it straightforwardly, the EU should only be doing what member states signed up to. And if this doesn't happen, its legitimacy in the eyes of the public is undermined. As the PM said in January, 'democratic consent for the EU in Britain is now wafer thin'. That's all from me for today. Thanks for the comments. Bob Stewart, a Conservative, asks what would happen if the government told the European Union that it was not going to collect VAT on fuel. Davey says that would be an unwise idea. The lawyers would not allow it, he says. Mark Pritchard, a Conservative, asks what the government is doing to help people who use oil to heat their homes. Davey says one of his ministerial colleagues has been doing a lot of work on this. For example, the government has been promoting a "buy early" scheme that can help. Philip Davies, a Conservative, says Davey is reciting "the same kind of claptrap" that Ed Miliband used to produce when he was energy secretary. His constituents want the cheapest energy, not the greenest energy, Davies says. Davey says he does not accept that. Labour's John Healey says people will see a government failing to stand up to the energy companies. Davey says he does not accept that. If they look at Labour, people will see a a government not acting in the national interest, because its plans would cut investment. Labour's David Anderson says the energy companies have got off "scot free". Davey says he does not accept that. Christopher Chope, a Conservative, asks why Davey did not just get rid of VAT on fuel bills. That would have been illegal, Davey says. Labour's David Winnick says an increasing number of people are "terrified" to use their heating. We are returning to the fuel poverty that existed under the last Labour government, he says. As Tory MP s jeer, he corrects himself; under the last Tory government, he says. Ed Davey says the last Labour energy secretary (Ed Miliband) said alarming people about energy bills was not a mature way to conduct politics. He should have kept his word, Davey says. Davey is responding to Flint. He says she did not welcome this price cut. He looked at the energy freeze proposal, he says. But it would not work. Firms would put their prices up before and afterwards. He says the government told the Big Six that it expected them to pass cuts on. That is what will happen. The Big Six were created by Labour, he says. In 2000 there were 17 suppliers in the market. Labour took that down to six. Davey says the government is consulting. And when it publishes its consultation paper, it will publish its impact assessment. He says he is keen to keep people in employment. As for moving the costs on to taxation, that fuel poverty campaign groups campaigned for this, because it is more progressive, he says. Caroline Flint, the shadow energy secretary, is responding. Does Davey accept that the energy firms will still be allowed to raise prices this winter? Can Davey confirm that, if the average increase this winter is £120, his £50 cut will still lead to customers paying £70 more. One supplier has said it will not pass on cuts until March, she says. What will the government do to ensure cuts are passed on immediately. Flint says the Energy Company Obligation is badly designed. How many fewer households will receive energy saving measures? If ECO is running for four years, not two, doesn't that mean half as many people will be helped every year? What will be the impact on jobs? And can Davey confirm the burden is just being shifted to taxation? Davey said a few weeks ago energy firms were treating their customers like cash cows. Can he confirm there is nothing in this that will cost them a penny? Nothing less than a price freeze will do, she says. Davey explains why role the green levies play. But these measures are paid for directly by consumers. So the government should keep them under review. The government has to act responsibly, he says. It has reviewed the "cost profile" of these measures, he says. And that is why it has announced changes. Average bills in 2014 will be lower than they would otherwise have been, he says. And he says the energy companies have said there will be no need for price rises in 2014, unless there is a significant change to wholesale costs. Ed Davey, the energy secretary, is speaking now. The latest price rises announced by the energy companies were particularly unwelcome, he says. Rising wholesale prices were particularly to blame. But the energy firms need to be more transparent about their prices, he says. Ed Davey, the energy secretary, is about to make a statement in the Commons about energy bills. I would be surprised if he has anything much new to say. Full details of the proposals were released this morning. But the reaction could be interesting. More on Hutton-gate. Here's a short energy bills reading list. • YouGov's Peter Kellner says that voters do not trust what politicians are saying about energy prices. Our political leaders, then, are right to detect the political sensitivity of fuel prices. But they should beware of interpreting public opinion too simply. Yes, voters like any help they can get coping with the cost of living. But what they want even more is competent, clear-sighted political leadership. A pound a week off our fuel bills, like the promise of a 17-month post-election freeze, is all very well. But it will attract electoral dividends only if it can be presented as part of a responsible and lasting approach to the problem. Which is why the choice of immediate policy is only part of the challenge. Restoring trust is the bigger task, and the leaderships of all three main parties have a huge amount to do. • Mat Hope at the Carbon Brief blog says today's announcement "looks like it's going to have a small, short-term impact on household energy bills - announced just in time for Christmas". But energy bills will still rise in the New Year, he says. • Trewin Restorick at Huffington Post suggests four measures that could make green levies more palatable to politicians and voters. In China, in a television interview, David Cameron rejected suggestions that there was an inconsistency between his conduct in Sri Lanka, where he made human rights the focus of his visit, and his conduct in China, where he has played down the human rights agenda. I think I am totally consistent. I come to China and I don't believe there is a choice between raising growth and investment issues or raising human rights issues. I raise them both. That's what a policy of engagement is all about. There are some huge opportunities here in China for British jobs, British growth, British investment. I want to make the most of them and that is right for our country to compete and succeed in the global race. But I also raised concerns about human rights and we have agreed on this visit to restart the Britain/China human rights dialogue which will start again next year, so that is an important achievement too. Asked whether he regretted meeting the Dalai Lama (which offended the Chinese, leading to a trip planned for earlier this year being cancelled), Cameron replied: The British policy towards Tibet is unchanged. I have met the Dalai Lama as leader of the opposition, I have met him as prime minister. I don't have plans to meet him again. But my diary is for me to decide. Jeremy Hunt, the health secretary, is accompanying David Cameron in China. Labour doesn't think he should be there. This is from Andrew Gwynne, a shadow health minister. With A&Es bursting at the seams, the NHS stands on the verge of a winter crisis yet our Health Secretary has decided his priority is jetting off to China. Jeremy Hunt should be focusing on clearing up the mess he and David Cameron have made of the health service not running thousands of miles in the other direction. It proves you can't trust them with the NHS. • The six major energy companies have said gas and electricity bills will rise by around £50 less than expected this winter, after the government published full details of his plans to curb energy bill increases. You can read details of the government's announcements at 8.53am and 9.20am, and the energy companies' response are at 9.39am, at 10.08am and at 11.34am. Ed Miliband has said that, despite what the government has unveiled, bills are still going up. (See 11.02am.) And environmentalists have criticised the move. This is from Sophie Neuburg, a Friends of the Earth energy campaigner. The government has crumbled in response to pressure from the Big Six, leaving the fuel poor and the environment to pick up the bill. The effect of all the measures announced today is that funding for energy efficiency has fallen by over £700 million, condemning thousands of people to shiver in heat-leaking homes. If ministers were serious about tackling fuel bills they would introduce a comprehensive energy efficiency programme and take urgent steps to wean our economy off increasingly costly fossil fuels – the real driving force behind rocketing fuel bills. • Michael Fallon, the energy minister, has confirmed that the government wants to change the definition of fuel poverty. This is what he told the World at One. The old definition wasn’t very helpful – it included relatively well-off people who happened to have very large, drafty houses, and you don’t want taxpayer help for them. And also, the population that we were helping through the old definition moved around a bit, and it was different people all the time. So we’ve been working with the voluntary organisations and we’ve had an independent review under Professor Hills to come up with a better definition of those who not only have higher costs but have less income. We haven’t finalised the definition yet – we’ve simply taken power in the energy bill, which is going to become law shortly, to set this new definition, and we are consulting all the voluntary organisations on it. • David Cameron has said Britain will act as China's strongest advocate in the west. At the start of a three-day trip to the country, he also promised to lead a "dialogue of mutual respect and understanding". • Downing Street has protested to the Chinese authorities about a "completely inappropriate" decision to bar a British journalist from a press conference in Beijing with David Cameron and his Chinese counterpart, Li Keqiang. • Cameron has distanced himself from Boris Johnson after the London mayor mocked people with low IQs and hailed the benefits of greed and inequality in spurring economic growth. • Downing Street has rejected claims that Cameron's visit amounts to a "crony trip" because of the number of close associates he is taking with him. Asked about this claim, a Number 10 spokesman said: "I don't accept that at all." He went on: "The delegation list is drawn together by UKTI. It is determined on the basis of the very best business opportunities. It is all about getting the very best business opportunities for the UK. • The Office for National Statistics has released figures showing Britain's wage-earners have taken a £5,000 pay cut in the past five years. • The Bank of England has admitted that the supply of credit to small businesses is still constrained even though the amount advanced in the third quarter was the highest since the flagship funding for lending (FLS) scheme was introduced in summer 2012. • The TUC has published research claiming that "the government’s General Anti-Abuse Rule (GAAR) is so poorly designed that it will allow 99 per cent of tax avoidance to continue". • A consortium has been formed to save the Gay Hussar, the celebrated political eatery in London's Soho which is due to be sold this week. As the Press Association reports, The Gay Hussar has been the haunt of politicians and journalists for decades and the consortium includes Lord Kinnock, the former Labour leader, and Lord Ashcroft, former deputy chairman of the Conservative Party. Other prominent supporters and backers include former Tory leadership challenger David Davis, Labour MP John Cryer and Neil and Christine Hamilton. There are more details on the consortium's website. My colleague Karl Mathiesen is investigating whether the government is right to say that the energy price measures announced today are really carbon neutral. If you have got any thoughts on this, please post them on his Eco Audit blog. In a new book of essays about foreign policy edited by the shadow foreign secretary, Douglas Alexander, and Ian Kearns, Influencing Tomorrow, Mark Leonard argues that Britain has been rather naive about the prospects of getting China to become more liberal. The hard truth is that Western countries have relied on an overly optimistic view that engaging China and supporting its development would inevitably end up transforming its political system, economy and foreign policy. The experience of the last five years has revealed this as a faith-based approach with nothing to show for it. What happened to Bloomberg's Rob Hutton in China seems to make this point quite well. And so with that in mind (and not just because lobby journalists love writing about themselves - although they do) here's a round up of Twitter comment on Hutton-gate. From Iain Dale, the blogger and publisher From the Daily Mail's Tim Shipman From the Labour blogger Hopi Sen You can read Conquest's three laws here. From the broadcaster Julia Hartley-Brewer From the Telegraph's Peter Foster Hutton has recently published a very good book about journalese called Romps, Tots and Boffins. (On Twitter I called it a mini classic.) This has prompted some predictable jokes. Declaration of interest: Hutton is based in the Guardian's office in the House of Commons, and his desk is next to mine. He's a friend. And, on his China trip, David Cameron has also distanced himself from Boris Johnson's comments suggesting those with a low IQ are destined to fail in life. Here's Nicholas Watt's story on the Bloomberg journalist Rob Hutton being banned from a press conference in China. And here's how it starts. Downing Street has protested to the Chinese authorities over the "completely inappropriate" decision to bar a British journalist from attending a press conference in Beijing with David Cameron and his Chinese counterpart, Li Keqiang. No 10 raised its "deep concerns" on two occasions with Chinese officials after the foreign ministry decided to exclude Robert Hutton, a political journalist with the US wire service Bloomberg, from the event at the Great Hall of the People on Monday. British officials in Beijing informed Hutton, a member of the British parliamentary lobby who is accompanying the prime minister to China, that he would not be admitted to the press conference ... The Bloomberg website is blocked in China after it ran stories on the wealth of families of senior leaders including relatives of the president, Xi Jinping. The US news organisation last month denied killing a similar sensitive story following a New York Times report that said editors had been concerned its ability to report from China would be compromised if it ran the piece. Bloomberg said the piece was still in preparation. "Press conference" is probably not quite the right phrase. As Nick explains in his copy, this was a "no questions" press conference - which means it was not really a press conference at all. Did you know we have a National Fraud Authority? Well, we won't for much longer. Theresa May, the home secretary, is abolishing it. She's announced this in a written statement. Here's an extract. As part of the government’s reforms to policing and the fight against serious and organised crime, I have decided to close the National Fraud Authority and realign its responsibilities to reflect the creation of the National Crime Agency. The National Crime Agency, with its Economic Crime Command, will bring a single national focus to cutting economic crime and will lead and coordinate the national fight against fraud, working with law enforcement agencies, regulators, government and the public, private and voluntary sectors. While the National Fraud Authority has been successful in raising awareness of fraud and improving co-ordination, the focus should now be on cutting economic crime. The National Fraud Authority will close by 31 March. Labour has sharpened its attack on the coalition over energy prices. This looks interesting. Rob Hutton is a Bloomberg political correspondent and a member of the Westminster lobby travelling with David Cameron. I'll post more when I get the details. You can read all today's Guardian politics stories here. And all the politics stories filed yesterday, including some in today's papers, are here. As for the rest of the papers, here's the PoliticsHome list of top 10 must reads, here's the ConservativeHome round-up of today's political stories and here's the New Statesman's list of top 10 comment articles. And here are three articles I found particularly interesting. • Andrew Grice in the Independent says George Osborne wants departmental spending cuts to fund the giveaways in the autumn statement. George Osborne is demanding a round of last-minute spending cuts from Whitehall departments to fund more than £2bn of giveaways in his Autumn Statement on Thursday. The Chancellor has angered Conservative and Liberal Democrat ministers by asking for emergency savings on the eve of his statement so that he can balance the books. The departments under pressure include the Home Office, Justice, Defence, Business and Work and Pensions. Mr Osborne’s move has reopened a Cabinet debate over the previous decision to ring-fence the NHS, schools and overseas aid budgets, meaning any extra savings must be found from the non-protected departments that have already suffered cuts announced since 2010. • Elizabeth Rigby and Tanya Powley in the Financial Times (subscription) say industrialists are complaining that green taxes are pushing them to "crisis point". British industry has warned that green taxes are pushing it to “crisis point” even as George Osborne prepares to cut environmental levies on household energy bills in this week’s Autumn Statement. Leading industrialists from companies such as Tata Steel and Ineos told the Financial Times that green taxes were putting their British plants at a competitive disadvantage relative to those plants’ European competitors. They urged David Cameron and his chancellor to extend their pledge to “roll back” the levies on households to manufacturing plants. British industry has warned that green taxes are pushing it to “crisis point” even as George Osborne prepares to cut environmental levies on household energy bills in this week’s Autumn Statement. Leading industrialists from companies such as Tata Steel and Ineos told the Financial Times that green taxes were putting their British plants at a competitive disadvantage relative to those plants’ European competitors. They urged David Cameron and his chancellor to extend their pledge to “roll back” the levies on households to manufacturing plants. • Tom Bawden in the Independent says the government is changing the definition of fuel poverty. The Government is attempting to manipulate official figures to bring down fuel poverty, it is claimed today. A clause in the Energy Bill will change the definition of the key poverty indicator, reducing the number of English households counted as “fuel-poor” from 3.2 million to 2.4 million overnight. The new definition, which could come into force before Christmas, will instantly reduce the percentage of fuel-poor households in England by nearly a third, from 15 per cent to 11 per cent, according to calculations by MPs on the cross-party Environmental Audit Committee (EAC). “The Government is shifting the goalposts on fuel poverty so that official statistics record far fewer households as fuel-poor,” said the committee’s chair, Joan Walley MP. “The changes to the fuel-poverty definition and target should be stopped unless the Government is prepared to make a public commitment to end fuel poverty altogether.” ScottishPower has now become the last of the big six energy companies to put out a statement responding to the government's fuel bills announcement. It says it hopes to avoid further price rises in 2014. ScottishPower welcomes the Government’s announcement of proposals to reduce the impact of the Energy Company Obligation, an introduction of a universal rebate and other measures. Once implemented early in the New Year, these initiatives will lead to real savings for customers in 2014 and ScottishPower agrees with Government estimates which suggest total savings for households of around £50 for a typical dual fuel customer. Neil Clitheroe, CEO of Retail and Generation at ScottishPower, said: “We are acutely aware of the burden that higher gas and electricity bills place on households and have been working with Government to ease the strain. We are pleased the Government has recognised the impact of green and social levies on rising consumer energy bills and we have worked collaboratively with them to identify a number of individual savings not only to these programmes, but also other rising input costs. “We look forward to working with Government on the detail of these proposals. We will continue to be as open as possible about consumer bills, explaining the impact of input costs on a line by line basis. As always, we will continue to try and avoid further price rises in 2014, but this will depend on whether there are increases in wholesale energy prices or other costs outside our control." The Office for National Statistics has published a report on what has happened to average household incomes over the last 35 years. Here's the summary and here's the full report (pdf). It says that since the start of the downturn median household income has fallen by 3.8%, allowing for inflation. But there is a big gap between what's happened to pensioner households and non-pensioner households. However, when looking separately at non-retired and retired households, the median income for non-retired households fell by 6.4% between 2007/08 and 2011/12, while the median income for retired households grew by 5.1%. While David Cameron has been tweeting about his meeting with the Chinese president. Here's Ed Miliband on the energy bills announcement. David Cameron has been meeting the Chinese president, Xi Jinping. Here's what the Press Association has filed about how it started. The two men shook hands for the cameras in front of their nations' flags before going into talks with small teams of advisers. Xi thanked Cameron for the congratulations he sent after he was elected general secretary of the Chinese Communist Party and President of China a year ago. And he asked the PM to pass on his "cordial greetings and best wishes to Her Majesty the Queen". Cameron responded: "I will certainly pass your regards to Her Majesty. I know that she too wants to see very strong relations between Britain and China and also looks forward to future potential visits between our two countries." Xi reminded Cameron that they first met in 2007, during his visit to Beijing as leader of the opposition, when he expressed the hope that he would return one day as PM. The Chinese president added: "On several occasions, you have expressed a positive attitude of valuing the role of China and your commitment to enhancing the UK's relations with China. I appreciate your position." And he told Cameron: "We have come to a crucial moment in global economic recovery and change in the international landscape. "Both China and UK are major economies in the world and permanent members of the UN Security Council. The relation between our two countries not only concerns our respective development, but also will exert major global impact." Cameron said that his decision to bring six government ministers and a large business delegation with him was "a signal of the importance I attach to the success of China and, I hope, the success of the China-British relationship." Here is is some more reaction to the announcement. From Paul King, chief executive of the UK Green Building Council Make no mistake, this is bad news for people who cannot afford to heat their homes, especially if they live in solid walled properties, and bad news for thousands of construction industry workers who may well be joining the dole queue this Christmas. It remains perverse that the prime minister is attempting to reduce energy bills by slashing the very scheme that is designed to bring them down for good. We have to recognise that some in the coalition have fought hard for a package of incentives to sweeten the pill. Encouraging households to take up energy efficiency measures when moving into a property through a ‘Stamp Duty rebate’ is something we have repeatedly called for. From Richard Lloyd, executive director at Which?, the consumer organisation It's about time the government started getting the cost of energy under control and this will be a welcome step in the right direction for consumers who are struggling with the increased cost of living. It is right to refocus the Energy Company Obligation so that it gives greater priority to those most in need of help with lower cost measures such as loft and cavity wall insulation. But in return for more time to get this right, the suppliers must now commit to greater transparency and to getting their costs down, fast. From Cathy Debenham at YouGen, a renewable energy advice website While we are pleased to see that the elements of the Energy Companies Obligation aimed at low income and vulnerable households have been maintained, it is disappointing that the Carbon Emissions Reduction Obligation is being reduced. This offers help for people living in homes with solid walls or hard-to-treat cavities. They often have the coldest and most expensive to heat homes, and very few people will be able to insulate without financial help. People are already finding it very difficult to get ECO for solid wall insulation, and we believe that help needs to be strengthened, rather than reduced. Just as the feed-in tariff has helped the solar industry to develop and grow - with more people installing solar panels and massive price reductions as a result - we believe that the solid wall industry needs similar support to grow. This should encourage innovation and economies of scale so that it eventually becomes affordable without subsidy. Here's Caroline Flint, the shadow energy secretary, on the government's energy bills announcement. Even after these changes to levies, energy bills are still rising and the average household will still be paying £70 more for their energy than last winter. Any help is better than none, but you can judge this government by who they’re asking to pick up the tab – the taxpayer. The energy companies have got off scot-free. This shows why nothing less than a price freeze and action to reset the market to stop the energy companies overcharging again in the future will do. Energy UK, the trade body representing energy firms, has welcomed the government's energy bills announcement. Here's its statement. This is a good outcome for consumers. The changes will bring a welcome set of reductions to the policy costs which are a significant part of the household bill, have been rising and were set to rise further. Costs are spread in a fairer manner and household bills are reduced. The industry will continue to insulate homes. The parts of ECO that target fuel poverty have been increased and the complicated solid wall installation has a more practical timescale and is made less complex. Companies will be making their own announcements individually about what it means for their customers. Labour is unimpressed by today's energy bills announcement. Here's Alex Belardinelli, Ed Balls's press spokesman. The Tory Treasury twitter feed (an official account) has hit back. Robert Peston's BBC blog on the energy bills announcement explains the changes very clearly. EDF has now responded to the government's fuel bills announcement. It says the move vindicates its decision to announce a 3.9% price increase (which was based on the assumption that the government was going to cut the cost of green levies). The Carbon Brief blog has posted a graph on Twitter putting the cuts to fuel bills generated by today's announcement in perspective. Ed Davey, the energy secretary, has been giving interviews this morning about today's announcement. Here are the main points. • Davey said that the government was saving households £50 a year on average while at the same time "protecting the fuel poor". • He said that energy bills would have been higher if Labour had been in office. For example, under Ed Miliband, they were going to put something called the renewable heat incentive onto energy bills – that would have cost an extra £179. From day one, we took off energy charges from the bill that Labour had wanted, so under Labour energy bills would be much higher than they are now. • He said Labour's plan for a price freeze would hurt the fuel poor because it would lead to a cut in investment. It could also lead to small suppliers going out of business, he said. One of the reasons why Labour’s proposal is economic illiteracy is that if wholesale prices went up during their price freeze, what would happen is the smaller companies - who wouldn’t be able to trade for a loss unlike the bigger companies - would go out of business, and that would reduce competition. I've taken the quotes from PoliticsHome. Four of the big six energy companies have now responded to the government's energy bills announcement. Here is what they are saying. Statement from British Gas British Gas is reducing household gas and electricity prices by an average of 3.2%, which is equivalent to £41 on average off the annual dual fuel bill1. An additional £12 customer rebate for the Government’s Warm Home Discount (WHD) scheme brings a total annual saving of £53 to the average dual fuel customer. Today’s announcement follows the Government’s proposed decision to make changes to the Energy Company Obligation and to introduce a rebate to customers to cover the cost of the WHD scheme. It also takes into account the agreement between the Government and electricity network companies to defer an element of electricity distribution costs in the period from April 2014 to March 2015. British Gas is passing on the benefit of all these changes to its customers in full. The price reduction will take effect from 1 January 2014, and will apply to all customers, whether on variable or fixed tariffs. Chris Weston, the Managing Director of British Gas, said: “British Gas is pleased to be cutting energy bills by an average of £53 from 1 January. We’ve been able to do this because the Government has committed to making changes to the environmental and social obligations that are paid for through energy bills. These changes will now allow us to help more people at a lower cost. Two weeks ago British Gas announced it was increasing electricity bills by 10.4%, and gas bills by 8.4%. Statement from SSE Following the announcement by the UK government that it is to consult on proposals to reduce the future costs associated with the Energy Company Obligation and to facilitate other measures to reduce the cost of electricity and gas for customers, SSE can confirm that it will pass any resulting savings on to customers before the end of the current financial year. On the basis of today's announcement and the planned consultation, SSE plans to reduce its household energy prices before the end of its financial year. The overall impact of the proposed changes, including the rebate which is expected to apply in Autumn 2014, should lead to a typical dual fuel customer benefiting by around 4%. This should equate to a saving of around £50 for a typical dual fuel customer. These changes should remove a significant amount of upward pressures from energy bills. SSE plans to extend its current Autumn 2014 price cap at the new lower levels to Spring 2015, subject to there being no marked and sustained increase in wholesale energy costs, network costs or new policy-related costs. William Morris, Managing Director, Retail, said: "We promised our customers that they would benefit from any cost savings and although there is some detail still to be worked out, we are able to commit to lowering our prices. If the proposals set out today are implemented, it will mean energy costs for a typical dual fuel customer can be cut by around 4%. "We continue to believe that policy costs should be paid through general taxation, not energy bills. The Warm Homes Discount changes are therefore a step in the right direction" Statement from Paul Massara, CEO at RWE npower We welcome today's announcement as an important step in cutting energy costs for our domestic customers. As a result of this announcement we will reduce our prices. We are currently calculating how large this reduction will be, and can assure our customers that it will fully reflect the reduction in the costs to our business. "In addition, in order to give our customers greater certainty with their household budgets, we don’t plan to increase energy prices before Spring 2015, unless there are increases in wholesale energy costs or network charges. Statement from E.ON. Here's an extract. E.ON has not announced a price rise since December 2012 and once again is the only major company to hold off for so long into the winter. The result of these changes [announced by the government today] should mean our customers will pay less next year than otherwise would have been the case. We are working through the details and will provide an update in due course. E.ON can also provide customers with the assurance that it doesn't expect to have to raise prices as a result of social or environmental obligations in the next 18 months. There remains a risk, however, that increases in network charges or wholesale energy costs for example could force a price increase but the company (E.ON) very much hopes that situation does not occur. The news release from the Department of Energy and Climate Change (DECC) about energy bills is unusually detailed. I have mentioned the key points already. (See 8.53am.) Here are nine points from the small print worth flagging up. 1. The £50 a year per household saving is an average. "The value of the benefit will vary between companies," DECC says. 2. The £50 figure applies to England, Scotland and Wales. Separate arrangements apply to Northern Ireland. 3. Mostly the cut is being funded by regulatory changes announced by the government. But firms are also taking "voluntary action" to cut distribution costs, contributing around £5 on average to the value of the cuts. 4. The overall package is carbon neutral. Watering down the Energy Company Obligation (ECO), by giving companies an extra two years to meet its targets (the deadline has been extended from March 2015 to March 2017), actually increases emissions. By the government has announced various energy saving measures, for home buyers, landlords and public sector buildings, to offset this impact. These measures will cost £540m over three years. 5. Around 60,000 homes will benefit from the scheme allowing people to get £1,000 to spend on energy saving measures when they buy a new home. 6. Funding for Green Deal Communities is being extended from £20m to £80m. 7. The Green Deal is being "strengthened, streamlined and reformed". There are more details here, in a separate news release. You could read this as an admission that so far it has not been a glittering success. 8. Energy firms will have become "more transparent" about how much they spend on social and environmental measures. This may require changes to the law. 9. Measures to prevent fraud, particularly relating to loft and cavity wall insulation, are being introduced. Is this an admission that there is a potential problem? This morning the government has announced the full details of its plan to curb energy bill increases. The broad outline of the plans, which will cut bills from what they would otherwise have been by £50 on average, have been known for some days, but this morning all the components of the plan have been set out in a news release from the Department of Energy and Climate Change. Here's an extract: Today the Government confirms it will reduce the current cost of levies by: Establishing a rebate saving the average customer £12 on their bill, for the next two years, worth a total of £600 million. The Warm Homes Discount will continue to help millions of vulnerable households receive a £135 rebate off their energy bill. Reducing the cost of the Energy Company Obligation (ECO), an insulation scheme delivered by major energy suppliers. This will result in £30-£35 off bills, on average, next year. The existing dedicated support in ECO for low income and vulnerable households will be maintained and extended from March 2015 until March 2017. In addition, electricity distribution network companies are willing to take voluntary action to reduce network costs in 2014/15. This would allow a further one-off reduction of an average of around £5 on electricity bills, which energy suppliers will be able to pass on to their customers as well. And the energy firms have been reacting. • British Gas is to cut dual fuel energy bills by an average of £53 from January 1. • Npower says it will reduce bills and that "we don't plan to increase energy prices before spring 2015, unless there are increases in wholesale energy costs or network charges." • Scottish & Southern Energy said it expects a saving for the typical dual-fuel customer of around 4% before the end of March, equivalent to a saving of around £50 for a typical dual-fuel customer. Ed Davey, the energy secretary, has been giving interviews about his announcement this morning. I will post the highlights soon, as well as more detail about the announcement, and more reaction to it, from the energy companies and others. Otherwise, the main event today is David Cameron's "Don't mention the Dalai Lama" tour of China. My colleague Nicholas Watt is travelling with the prime minister. Here's his latest story. The political news diary at home is threadbare. As usual, I’ll also be covering all the breaking political news as well as looking at the papers and bringing you the best politics from the web. I’ll post a lunchtime summary at about 1pm and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I’m on @AndrewSparrow.

headline: Politics as it happened
trail_text: Crisis talks after reports of exposure to asbestos in the NBN rollout
body_text: That was quite a day. Let's take stock with the evening summary. The day dawned with asbestos and the NBN roll out dominating the political news cycle. There was a brief diversion into a bipartisan interlude on the republic between Wayne Swan and Malcolm Turnbull; and on a new administrative headquarters for DisabilityCare to be located in Geelong - but within moments, it was back to a brutal day in national affairs. Workplace Minister Bill Shorten and Communications Minister Stephen Conroy gathered a group of stakeholders for a meeting on the asbestos problems in parliament at lunchtime in order to get to the bottom of which contractors or residents could have been exposed to asbestos during the NBN rollout. Telstra chief David Thodey took responsibility, and the group resolved to set up an independent taskforce to examine the issues. The day rolled forward to Question Time. There were short sharp blows on asbestos. The Coalition sought to pressure the government over the management of the whole mess by asking whether it had pursued this issue before media reports surfaced last week of asbestos-related incidents. Shorten said he had, and produced some letters to back the claim up. The government then returned fire by raising some unfortunate comments Tony Abbott made about asbestos campaigner Bernie Banton - and also highlighting that deputy Liberal leader Julie Bishop had once worked as a lawyer for James Hardie. The Coalition changed course and then went on boat arrivals, and national security, raising the case of an Egyptian jihadist held in a low security detention facility in South Australia. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison used the case to suspend the standing orders - he was seeking an independent inquiry into the case. Morrison said allowing a known jihadist into the country, and allowing him to be held in a low security immigration processing facility, raised profound questions about whether Australia's current border measures and security assessments were adequate, given the strain the whole detention system is under with an upswing in irregular boat arrivals. Morrison only just lost this vote, which was resolved 68-69. Such motions require an absolute majority to proceed. The government ran interference in this attack by highlighting comments from Julie Bishop suggesting the Indonesians would in fact turn people smugglers' boats around, even though they are saying publicly they have no intention of doing so. Here, incidentally, is a tear sheet of one of Shorten's letters to Telstra which we chased up after Question Time. Anyway, that was about the sum of the parts. Thanks very much indeed to Mike Bowers for his fine work today, and to readers of #politicslive Let's do it all again tomorrow. If you've been following this issue and our coverage today, and are interested, I'd encourage you to read Kath Viner's blog post in full. You can do so here. Here's the bottom line: It was a long interview. We journalists and editors need to be careful in making sure selections don't distort the overall intent. But we believe we have represented Bishop's comments accurately. Kath Viner: Since all reports of long interviews necessarily select the parts regarded as newsworthy, we think the key issue was not that a selection had been made but rather whether the selected parts distorted the interview on the point selected, or were otherwise presented in a misleading way. Having double-checked with Bishop on Sunday in light of the ambassador's remarks, and having changed the headline in recognition of the subtle but diplomatically significant distinction between an agreement and an expectation of co-operation, the Guardian believes that it has accurately conveyed the substance of Bishop's comments on this issue in the interview. Our editor-in-chief, Kath Viner, has just posted a blog about Lenore Taylor's interview with Julie Bishop today, which has been the subject of controversy around the place today. Kath's blog contains the relevant extract from the transcript of Lenore Taylor's interview with Julie Bishop. I'll post that first, then give you another excerpt from Kath's piece. Ms Bishop tells Lenore that based on her conversations with senior officials, she's convinced the Coalition can work with Indonesia to achieve the policy of turning back the boats. The transcript: Lenore Taylor: “On Indonesia, you always seem to say that you have had lots of discussions with Jakarta and you think you could manage the issue of turning back boats. Why do you think you could manage the issue of turning back boats given what Indonesian officials always say publicly? What do you know that we don't know about how about that could be managed?” Julie Bishop: “Well I am not at liberty to tell you. I have had a number of conversations with high-ranking Indonesian ministers and officials, as has [immigration spokesman] Scott Morrison as has [Coalition leader] Tony Abbott and I am convinced we can work in cooperation with Indonesia to achieve our policy aim.” Lenore Taylor: “So they would take boats back?” Julie Bishop: “I am confident we would be able to achieve what we did in the past. The fact is they are Indonesian boats with Indonesian crew and I am sure we can work cooperatively with them and Lenore, one thing you understand about diplomacy and others do as well is the professional diplomats are paid to present, aah, particular views but what goes on behind the scenes can be quite different – what people say privately can be different to what they say publicly, that’s why I am devoting my time to quiet consistent diplomatic messaging and relationships.” Sky TV is just broadcasting some vision from the press conference earlier today from the Telstra CEO David Thodey. I wasn't able to get down there. It is clear that Mr Thodey is taking responsibility for the asbestos issues. Thodey: We take ownership and responsibility. First chance I've had to share Mike Bowers wonderful frame of the chamber today. Your Bear Pit at work. Turnbull continues: They can't just say this is all Telstra's problem. Telstra is doing this work for, and on behalf of the NBN Co. The attempt to push this all off to Telstra is, frankly, a lawyer's argument. If you are the government, Turnbull says, responsible for occupational health and safety, then you have to be all over it. Turnbull says the correspondence Shorten has released raises more questions than it answers. Shadow Communications Minister Malcolm Turnbull is out now with the media on asbestos. Turnbull: We welcome the establishment of a register, and we welcome the announcement by Telstra that they will undertake an audit. He says there remains a number of questions about the government's knowledge of this matter. We know the commercial deal was done with NBN Co in 2011. The issue of disturbance of Telstra's infrastructure was therefore going to happen on a grand scale. Shorten's concern demonstrates that he knew asbestos was going to be a big issue. He raised concerns - but why didn't he raise his concerns with the Communications Minister? That's a bit odd isn't it? Want the definition of tough day at the office? Mike Bowers captures it. Is there a rough number of incidents, Shorten is asked? He says there have been thirty reported incidents of asbestos breaches since 1996, the vast majority in the past few weeks. Will the NBN roll out slow down, Conroy is asked? June targets? No. September targets? No. The work is ongoing, he says. If there is a material change, NBN Co will advise of that. Did Telstra just fob you off, The Conversation's Michelle Grattan asks Shorten? They wrote back to me, and said they were on top of it. Minister Conroy is insisting that NBN Co are not responsible for any of this during a mildly combative exchange with David Crowe, journalist from The Australian. In response to a question seeking metrics - how many pits, where are these problems precisely - Shorten suggests there is some information about where the problems currently lie, but the government is still seeking further particulars. We need the best possible information Shorten says. For me, he says, it's an unsatisfactory state of affairs that we can't tell residents the full story. Shorten's questioned about a fund. It was discussed but not resolved definitively. He points to a new register tracking exposure to asbestos. This is a very important advance when it comes to future claims. Shorten is defending his actions based on his contact with Telstra in 2009. He says he was reassured by Telstra that all was safe. Should you have pressed harder, Shorten is asked? When Telstra write back to you not once, not twice, but three times .. Does anyone think the Opposition would be tackling asbestos if it wasn't for the politics of the NBN Co? We contacted Telstra. They gave promises. We are the ones driving this process. Workplace Minister Bill Shorten and Communications Minister Stephen Conroy are now reporting back after the talks with Telstra and others earlier today. Shorten says: The group has agreed to support an independent taskforce to examine these issues. Telstra has committed to provide transparent information to people who feel they might have been exposed to asbestos. It will report back to the Commonwealth in a speedy fashion about how it intends to remove asbestos from their pits. Shorten: Everyone has a responsibility in our community to make sure people are safe from the scourge of asbestos. Minister Conroy says NBN Co fully supports the outcomes of the morning. He says there have been a couple of instances where sub contractors to NBN Co have not followed proper procedures. He says there should be no shortcuts when it comes to the handling of asbestos. Conroy is asked whether NBN Co has done enough and whether he should take responsibility. Conroy: I am satisfied that Telstra has put up their hand and taken full responsibility for their work. Should I take responsibility? Well, Telstra has taken full responsibility. Julia Gillard and Craig Emerson, catching up during that division. That's Question Time, over and out. Shadow foreign minister Julie Bishop seeks leave to make a personal explanation. This concerns the interview with Lenore Taylor that we've referred to throughout today. Bishop: At no stage did I say there was an agreement with the (Indonesian government) Bishop is objecting to a headline on the story, and notes that has been amended. Bishop has been under attack from Labor on this story for most of today - and the static has cut across the Coalition's political attack on the border protection theme. And then it was done. The Morrison motion was not carried by the required absolute majority required in such cases. The House is dividing on the motion now. While we wait, here's Morrison, launching the suspension of the standing orders. Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor is on his feet to respond. The Prime Minister has remained in the House. Normally she would leave during such procedural skirmishes. Not today. O'Connor says this person has committed no offences while in Australia. He says this man has never left detention. O'Connor says he is happy to provide a security briefing to the Opposition - but when they did that last week, the details were released publicly. This is not on, O'Connor says. Those briefings have to remain in-camera. The person is now in high security detention. The reason why this motion is being brought on today is (they) are trying to scare the Australian people in a way that is really outrageous. O'Connor says the Coalition is long on rhetoric but short on substance. The Opposition has not cooperated with efforts in parliament to slow down the flow of irregular arrivals. He says Abbott pretends he has an agreement with Indonesia to turn back boats when that clearly isn't the case, but he refuses to deal with the Malaysia Solution. It doesn't make sense. Malaysia wants to assist and Abbott won't allow it. I oppose the motion because the Opposition are disingenuous on this issue. They don't want a compact. They want to get in the way of policies recommended by experts. They want to create fear within our community. Morrison telegraphed his intention late last week. In a statement released on Friday, he demanded that the Immigration Minister Brendan O’Connor immediately establish an independent inquiry into the mishandling of the detention of a convicted jihadist terrorist by his department, placed in low security family accommodation for asylum seekers at Inverbrackie in South Australia. The Morrison statement: The Government was grossly negligent in keeping a known and convicted jihadist terrorist in low security asylum seeker family accommodation at Inverbrackie for five months, a facility secured by a perimeter pool fence and located adjacent to a defence force facility. The inquiry must receive full cooperation and access and take evidence from all relevant agencies, ministers and their offices, with usual protocols to apply to sensitive information. The subject of this case is a convicted terrorist whose crimes include premeditated murder, firearm and explosives possession and destruction of property. He was a member of the terrorist organisation Egyptian Islamic Jihad, which merged with Al Qaeda. Morrison is using parliamentary procedure to push for an independent inquiry by a select committee of the parliament into these events. The government, Morrison says, has sat on its hands while a convicted terrorist came into this country. Morrison: That is where the bar has been set. We need an inquiry because this government does not learn from its mistakes. They are light touch on our borders, they are a light touch on national security, and this minister is a light touch. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison is pursuing the case of the suspected Egyptian jihadist detained in a low security immigration detention facility in South Australia despite being the subject of an Interpol alert. We covered this case on the live blog last week. Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor says the person concerned: has not been out of detention. O'Connor suggests this person has also been monitored by security agencies. He says his department is working with intelligence agencies to ensure the security assessment system works. The agencies did not fail us. Mr Morrison is pressing for more particulars. Mr O'Connor says he has no advice that this person has been through any process leading towards him being granted a protection visa. There are situations where a person could be determined a refugee but not released into the community due to offences that have been previously committed. Mr Morrison is now attempting to suspend the standing orders to discuss the Gillard Government's failings on border protection and national security. Tony Abbott switches the attack to boat arrivals, and the government losing control of the borders. The Prime Minister swivels the attack around using today's Guardian Australia's interview with Julie Bishop. Gillard declares Bishop has embarrassed Indonesia by suggesting there's a deal on turning boats back, when there is no such deal. Trade Minister Craig Emerson is now backing that point in with a Dorothy Dixer. (Much hollering from the Opposition bench.) Emerson says the Indonesians have made it clear they will not tow back boats. They will not. Climate Minister Greg Combet has been wheeled in to support Shorten on asbestos. He's now shouting about Julie Bishop representing James Hardie before she entered politics. National Luke Hartsuyker asks Shorten how many NBN contractors have so far undertaken asbestos removal? What training have they undertaken? Shorten isn't engaging with specifics. He takes the political riposte. Shorten: I've never represented the companies in court who made the absestos; and I've never insulted the victims of asbestos and campaigners. (By this he means Abbott and his deputy, Julie Bishop.) Willing down there today. Manager of Opposition business, Christopher Pyne, directing tactical traffic. Turnbull is back with the contents of that 2009 letter tabled by Shorten. GIven you clearly knew there were risks associated with asbestos, why didn't you raise it with Telstra between March 2009 and this week? Shorten doesn't engage with that allegation directly. A supplementary now from Turnbull to Shorten. Will you confirm you made no contact with Telstra or NBN Co since 2009 despite being aware of the risks? Shorten responds that he wrote to Telstra on August 4, then November 2 - Telstra wrote back with a detailed response. Of Turnbull's pursuit? Shorten: Tawdry. Workplace Minister Bill Shorten. An apt summary by Crikey correspondent Bernard Keane of a Dorothy Dixer Shorten just asked himself to update the House about today's asbestos talks. Independent Rob Oakeshott wants particulars of the Debates Commission - a new body to conduct election debates for September. Does the Prime Minister support this concept? Julia Gillard: We certainly are committed to a leader's debate commission. Turnbull is back at the dispatch box. He'd like the 2009 letter. Given this letter was written well before Telstra's agreement with NBN Co, what was this letter about? Was it about pits and ducts and asbestos? In what capacity was it written? Shorten ignores the specifics of the question. Shorten: Asbestos should not be a political football. The Workplace Minister has now tabled the letter, written in March 2009. Here's Garrett a few moments ago on indulgence. A Dorothy Dixer now allowing Treasurer Wayne Swan to have a shout about Queensland Premier Campbell Newman not signing on to Gonski. Shouting is ensuing. Shadow Communications Minister Malcolm Turnbull is asking a question of Bill Shorten. Can he inform the House how often he has met with Telstra in relation to asbestos risks and the NBN before the news reports last week? After all, everyone knew there were risks. Mr Shorten says he wrote first to Telstra in 2009 and Telstra indicated it had the issue under control. Shorten says he's met with a group of stakeholders today. Telstra made it very clear at this meeting that they accept they have to do more. I'm pleased to inform the House ... that they all agreed about the outcomes of the meeting we have just had. We saw Telstra accepted responsibility for end-to-end training throughout the system. Telstra also accepted the need to do much better in accepting the anxieties of the community. Turnbull would like Shorten to table the 2009 letter. House of Representatives Speaker Anna Burke says she can't do that. She can only request that of papers the minister is reading from. Opposition leader Tony Abbott opens on the NBN. Does the Prime Minister agree that millions spent on advertising for the NBN would have been better spent on keeping people safe from asbestos? The Prime Minister returns in kind. I suppose we shouldn't be surprised given this is the same man who insulted Bernie Banton on his death bed. (Mr Banton, as asbestos campaigner, died of an asbestos related illness. Mr Abbott once said of Banton, who was pressuring the Howard Government to take action on asbestos-related diseases: "Just because a person is sick doesn't necessarily mean that he is pure of heart in all things." Abbott later apologised for the remark.) The Prime MInister says Telstra, not the government, is responsible for pits and ducts. It being the scheduled hour of Question Time, it is Question Time. Schools Minister Peter Garrett is on his feet on a matter of indulgence. Garrett is clearly emotional. He speaks of the death of Mr Yunupingu. The music of Yothu Yindi spoke to Australians, Garrett says, with urgent political intent. He dreamed that Australians could be better, and he demanded that Australian politics be better, and so it became. This is a day of sorrow and also a day of pride. For now, he's gone, and like so many Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people, gone too young. Liberal Ken Wyatt echoes the words of Garrett. Wyatt says Mr Yunupingu was not only a musician but an educator. He brought Australians together, he participated in public debates about education, he had insight into learning which contributed to innovative approaches to education. To me, and so many others who knew him personally, he was an inspiration. Mr Yunupingu, thank you for making our lives so much richer. Here are the two ministers - Bill Shorten and Stephen Conroy - on the way in to the asbestos talks. Through the crack in the doorway. The asbestos talks. Telstra is believed to be outlining the steps it has taken to contain the situation regarding asbestos at the NBN sites. Man in fedora. As discussed. Oh dear. Now the monarchists are cranky. The Australian Monarchist League has fired off this missive to Malcolm Turnbull, (who, as we've noted, was out this morning, fanning those flames of republicanism) - a letter copied to hundreds of their closest friends in the media. 3 June 2013 Hon. Malcolm Turnbull MP PO Box 545 EDGECLIFF NSW 2027 Dear Mr Turnbull, On this morning's ABC News Breakfast show, you said, in reference to comments made in 1999, that the 'monarchists lied'. The Australian Monarchist League is Australia's largest member-based monarchist organisation and both media and the general public refer to us as 'monarchists'. I would point out that the Australian Monarchist League has never intentionally lied to anyone at any time and has always acted in a truthful and open manner. We would therefore appreciate you issuing a public notice that your comments did not refer to the Australian Monarchist League. Thanking you Yours sincerely, Philip Benwell National Chair Australian Monarchist League Crisis talks on asbestos. Mike Bowers records the opening. Perhaps it's Ms Hanson's tendency to refer to herself in the first person? In the ABC footage of Hanson, a chap in a check fedora and sunglasses is watching on with intense interest. And over in Sydney, another lady with red hair, Pauline Hanson, is maintaining her rage: the voice is characteristically querulous, the vibrato outrage undimmed. (And she's announcing she'll have yet another go at trying to get a spot in parliament this September.) Hanson: The cost of living has become unaffordable. Over at The Conversation, political editor Michelle Grattan has been speaking to Brian Howe, the former member for Batman and a former Deputy Prime Minister. The battle for Batman (the Victorian seat vacated by the retiring Labor man Martin Ferguson last week) is the contest everybody is talking about. And I mean everybody. Cats have views on this pre-selection, and they don't mind sharing them. But I digress. Mr Howe has told Michelle Julia Gillard (who is backing a bloke for this seat, factional mover and shaker David Feeney) should just shut up. Here's a bit from The Conversation: Former deputy prime minister Brian Howe, who held Batman from two decades, has strongly backed local woman Mary-Anne Thomas in the bitter preselection battle for the seat. Howe said Labor should keep its promise to deliver women candidates in a fair share of safe seats, but it was important as well to have as candidate a credible and active local resident in an area where the Green vote was increasing. He also said he was “very disappointed to see the Prime Minister involved” in the preselection. “It’s quite counterproductive for her to be involved. This is not about the election – this is about the future.” Let's stand still and work out where politics has touched down this lunchtime. Asbestos and the NBN rollout has been front and centre in the political discussion. Workplace Relations Minister Bill Shorten and Communications Minister Stephen Conroy are sitting down as we speak with representatives from Telstra, NBN contractors, unions and asbestos campaigners to work out what to do after recent incidences where workers or residents could have been exposed to asbestos. The two ministers will address the media on the sum of those talks after Question Time today - around 3.30pm. The Prime Minister has confirmed that the national heaquarters of DisabilityCare will be in Geelong - the location of two Labor-held seats in trouble in September. There's been a small outbreak of bipartisanship on the republic - Wayne Swan and Malcolm Turnbull have been out promoting a renewed discussion about constitutional reform, pushing through the defeat of the referendum in 1999. Boats, Indonesia and asylum policy is also kicking around courtesy of the Coalition wanting to keep political focus on this issue; and some comments from Shadow Foreign Minister Julie Bishop which appeared somewhat at odds with what Indonesia is saying publicly about the Coalition's policy of turning back people smuggler's boats. The Fair Work Commission has given the lowest paid a weekly increase of $15. Let's try - all the breaking news notwithstanding - and circle back to turning back people smuggler's boats, and whether the Coalition can achieve an agreement with Indonesia on this front Julie Bishop has put out a statement concerning this morning's Guardian Australia story. There is not an agreement with the Indonesians - that's not possible - Bishop asserts she's simply confident that all will be well. I am confident that, if elected to government, the Coalition will be able to work constructively with the Indonesian government on people smuggling issues. However, it is not possible for the Coalition, from Opposition, to negotiate agreements publicly or privately with foreign governments. This is what Bishop said to Lenore in the interview - she suggested the Indoensians understood the Coalition policy, and had been affirmative about it behind the scenes. Professional diplomats are paid to present particular views but what goes on behind the scenes can be quite different – what people say privately can be different to what they say publicly. And we have had wide ranging discussions at the highest levels on this issue. The new minimum wage is $622 per week. The weekly increase is just over $15. The president of the FWC Justice Iain Ross notes in this decision that minimum incomes are falling in relative terms in this country. But he argues that increasing the minimum wage is a blunt instrument in addressing the needs of the low paid. He argues he tax-transfer system can apply more targeted assistance to households. Just to add to the briskness of today, the Fair Work Commission is now handing down its minimum wage decision in Melbourne. The minimum wage will be increased by 2.6 per cent. The president of the FWC says inflation is contained, increases in superannuation are on the way, the carbon price impact on households is not as great as first thought, therefore the minimum wage increase is a bit lower than people may have thought. The Opposition leader Tony Abbott was out briefly on the doors of parliament a little while ago. He was asked about how he intended to implement the Coalition's policy of turning back people smugglers' boats when it is safe to do so. (This issue became sticky for the Coalition last Friday when the Indonesian Ambassador to Australia indicated there was no agreement to turn boats back. Defence officials have already queried whether this pledge is workable in practice.) In an interview with Guardian Australia political editor, Lenore Taylor, Shadow Foreign Minister Julie Bishop counsels that all will be well, whatever the Indonesians might be saying to the contrary. 'It's the relationship stupid.' Here's Lenore's piece. This is an excerpt: High-ranking Indonesian ministers and officials have indicated privately that Indonesia would cooperate with a Coalition government to turn back people smuggler boats insists Julie Bishop, the foreign affairs spokeswoman and deputy Liberal leader, despite the country's ambassador claiming publicly that “no such collaboration will happen.” “I have had a number of conversations with high-ranking Indonesian ministers and officials, as has [immigration spokesman] Scott Morrison as has [Coalition leader] Tony Abbott and I am convinced we can work in cooperation with Indonesia to achieve our policy aim,” Bishop said in a wide-ranging foreign policy interview with Guardian Australia. Tony Abbott is also on the 'all will be well' theme. The Coalition has a strong and constructive relationship with Indonesia, Abbott says. Here's the relevant sections from his press conference this morning. QUESTION: Mr Abbott are you prepared to defy the Indonesian Government to turn boats back there even though their Ambassador on Friday made it clear that they don’t want to accept them? TONY ABBOTT: Look, we’re very confident that we can have a strong and constructive relationship with the Indonesian Government. I’ve had several meetings with the President. I’m met with Foreign Minister Natalegawa. I have a good and strong relationship with Ambassador Nadjib. Julie Bishop obviously spends an enormous amount of time working with the Indonesians, as does Scott Morrison. So we are very confident that an incoming Coalition government won’t repeat the kind of mistakes that we’ve seen from the current government of which the absolute worst was the suspension of the live cattle trade in a state of panic over a TV programme. QUESTION: You’re talking about the willingness to turn boats around now. Is that different to the reality of turning boats around? Will you actually turn around any boats? TONY ABBOTT: The Howard Government turned boats around. It wasn’t a large number, but it was enough to send an absolutely crystal clear signal to the people smugglers and their clients that the game was up and that the Australian Government and people would no longer be played for mugs. QUESTION: How long do you think it will take to stop the boats? TONY ABBOTT: We will make a difference from day one, from day one we will make a difference and obviously we will be judged on our results – should we win this election – at the subsequent one. Garrett has now switched modes to the Gonski school funding reforms. He (like Swan was earlier) is asked about Premier Campbell Newman, saying no. Or no most likely. Garrett has advised Newman to take a leaf out of New South Wales Premier Barry O'Farrell's book. (NSW has signed on to Gonski). Garrett says he doesn't know whether Newman has been pressured by his Coalition colleagues in Canberra: Tony Abbott and Shadow Schools Minister Christopher Pyne. But he knows he should get on with doing a deal. Garrett: It's time Premier Newman put down the cudgels and picked up the phone to begin negotiations. We are absolutely confident we can reach agreement by the end of the month. Schools Minister Peter Garrett is out now paying tribute to Mr Yunupingu, with his ministerial colleague Warren Snowdon. Garrett: I'm wanting to pay tribute to someone who was a beacon for their people. This is someone we will sorely miss. Mr Garrett (who readers will remember had a rock career with Midnight Oil before entering politics) says he knows he speaks for colleagues in the music industry in this public tribute. He says there are still considerable health challenges facing indigenous communities. Snowdon says Mr Yunupingu taught him to have faith. Not the religious kind of faith, but faith in actions being able to make a difference. Garrett is asked about Treaty. Yothu Yindi sang about it. isn't it time to have one? It was an important song. Garrett treads carefully here. The policy of the Gillard Government is to recognise indigenous people in the constitution - that's one step back from a treaty. Garrett backs government policy and inches forward one jot - we need recognition and any other settlements that might be appropriate. He's asked about his favourite memory of the singer. He nominates sitting backstage sitting with the boys dressed up and painted. Mr Yunupingu spoke about culture and reconciliation.
