Liberal Senator Cory Bernardi - described by host Tony Jones on last night's ABC TV Q&A program as a 'social media enthusiast' - wants folks to know he's overhauled his website. As this audio makes clear, he wants the new site to be a meeting place for conservatives. Those who caught Q&A last night will recall Senator Bernardi described himself not as a social media enthusiast (perhaps that was a given) but a healer in the Liberal Party. This contribution is by far my favourite caption for Mike Bowers' lovely opening still this morning. Thanks for playing, Lee Skelton. If you think you can do better, you can leave a comment here on the blog, or join the Twitter conversation using the hashtag #politicslive @murpharoo Mike is @mpbowers Greens deputy leader. Powered by Ladyhawke. (My Delirium not inappropriate theme music for this sitting period). "Leaked internal polling" generally points to an agenda of some kind. Let's stick with the Newspoll for now. The Opposition leader Tony Abbott has picked up three points in this morning's survey on the preferred prime minister measure - and the Prime Minister has lost four points. The Coalition's primary vote is 49 per cent, and Labor's has slipped to 30 per cent. Newswire AAP is calling that a 35 seat loss for Labor if the election was held today. The Abbott Government would take The Lodge with an 8 per cent swing. The poll confirms a trend evident for many months. Issues in play over the past couple of weeks include the botched public funding agreement which collapsed last week - and of course the reports of asbestos and the NBN rollout were prominent as Newspoll went into the field. Fitzgibbon, in continuation. DAVID KOCH: Is there a push to take the party in a new direction now after figures like these? JOEL FITZGIBBON: No, Kochie, that little battle has been done and dusted and the die has been cast and again, we just need to bind together as a party and get on with it. Bind together as a party? I'll leave that thought with you. You can see that vision of Fitzgibbon giving it to the official line if you click on this link. Or here is an audio clip if you want to listen. Not everyone it seems is as intent as the Prime Minister in changing the conversation this morning. Labor backbencher Joel Fitzgibbon (Fitzgibbon went to the backbench after his contribution to Labor's botched leadership coup in March) has appeared on a popular breakfast television program this morning. Helping. Actually, not so much. The host David Koch wants Fitzgibbon's reaction to the bad opinion polls today. Here's the relevant excerpt: DAVID KOCH: Joel, Joel, Joel, are you shattered by this? And can you, are you still going to go to the election with this Prime Minister? JOEL FITZGIBBON: Hang on Kochie, I just brought the manual with me. I’ll see what it says. It says I should say, “polls come and go, but the only poll that matters is on election day”. A bit of translation: the line Fitzgibbon used on TV this morning is the line that is generally issued to MPs by the central communications command in the event of bad polls. It's what they are told to say. Fair to say Fitzgibbon just gave that whole process the bird on national television. Faced with a forest of bad headlines this morning, the Prime Minister is intent early on a change of subject. Let's talk about school improvement. Overnight, Julia GIllard has written to Queensland Premier Campbell Newman in the following terms - c'mon Campbell, what's going on with Gonski? Surely you aren't putting Tony Abbott's political interests ahead of the kiddies of Queensland? There's even a PS. Here's the Prime Minister's letter: Dear Premier Newman, Your state is one of the biggest winners from the National Plan for School Improvement (NPSI). Queensland schools will gain $3.8 billion – around $2.2 million on average per school, over six years. As you know, $2.5 billion of that extra cash would come from the Australian Government and $1.3 billion from your state. A two-for-one deal that will bring huge benefits to Queensland schools. Parents, principals, teachers and the broader community will wonder why you persist in refusing this extra money for their schools. It will be extremely disappointing to Queenslanders if you do not put extra money for schools in tomorrow’s Budget. It cannot be ignorance since you know how much Queensland schools – government and non-government alike – will gain from the NPSI. Your officials have been provided with detailed modelling, and updates, on five separate occasions. Your officials have also had 56 bi-lateral and multi-lateral meetings with my government’s officials. Despite this, you have not responded properly to the offer, and you have not authorised your officials to give accurate information about how you will fund schools next year and beyond. Perhaps it is simply that you do not want to guarantee your schools real funding growth in your Budget. As you know, we’re asking all states to provide indexation of at least 3%. That guarantee is essential. It ensures that schools get the full benefit of new money and prevents your government from using the new federal money to cover up your spending cuts. Perhaps it is because the Leader of the Opposition or members of his team are demanding you put their political interests ahead of the interests of Queensland children. Whatever the reason for your refusal to agree to $3.8 billion extra for Queensland schools, there can be no excuse for your government’s wilful misrepresentation of the scale of investment that is available, and surely there will be no forgiveness for you turning your back on Queensland’s kids. Yours, Julia Gillard Prime Minister PS: Reform is essential. Queensland schools were outperformed by NSW in every NAPLAN measure across every year level in 2012. Good morning and welcome to our live coverage of politics from Canberra. A gorgeous frame from Mike Bowers to open on the blog this morning - a golden parliament. Sometimes a little less golden than this image suggests. Let's get cracking. Unsurprisingly, the NBN rollout and asbestos is still a dominant political story this morning, but it’s been joined overnight by a batch of new opinion polls portending a wipeout for the Gillard Government in September. The coverage of the asbestos story cuts both ways in the papers and digital sites today - there are negative stories for the government and negative stories for Tony Abbott harking back to his days as Health Minister in the Howard Government. The opinion polls lean just one way: goodbye Labor. On our selection. Let’s run through the main stories. Asbestos Labor is fighting off new safety fears over its National Broadband Network as more cases of asbestos exposure during its construction fuel a growing political brawl over the management and cost of the $37.4 billion project. The health risks spread to all mainland states yesterday after the Queensland safety regulator revealed three new incidents and federal authority Comcare warned of an "alarming" spate of asbestos safety breaches by contractors. The Australian (paywalled) Telstra has admitted that up to one in five of its suburban communications pits contains asbestos, fuelling fears the National Broadband Network rollout could expose a big number of workers and households to the deadly fibre. The danger led the Federal Government to call an emergency meeting yesterday with Telstra and NBN officials to discuss the threat. It also invited asbestos advocates, union leaders and Australia's chief health officer to help co-ordinate a national response to public anxiety. The West Australian The NBN asbestos affair is, apart from being a major concern in itself, particularly bad for the government because it triggers memories of the mishandled pink batts scheme. That’s why the opposition is going all out to attempt to pin some blame on Workplace Minister Bill Shorten, despite Telstra accepting general responsibility. The Conversation Telstra tried to fast-track compensation arrangements for its employees exposed to asbestos but was rebuffed by Tony Abbott's own department back in 2001. With the opposition on Monday leading a parliamentary attack on the government over its alleged lack of urgency in addressing asbestos discovered in Telstra junction pits being handed over to the NBN, Fairfax Media has learnt the giant telco wanted to create an independent body to accelerate compensation and sought approval from the Department of Workplace Relations. However, the department, then under the ministerial leadership of Mr Abbott, rejected the plan. Fairfax Media Back in October 2007, when Tony Abbott was Health Minister, he denied asbestos disease sufferers access to subsidised medicine for their illness. Naturally, the asbestos disease sufferers were not happy with the decision. And they organised a petition, imploring Abbott to place the costly drug, Alimta, on the Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme. Some 17,000 people signed it. It was arranged that it would be delivered to Abbott’s Sydney office by Bernie Banton, 61, who was suffering terminal mesothelioma. But when Banton showed up, in his wheelchair, with his oxygen tube, trailed by media, Abbott had done a bunk. Banton was devastated and told the media so. He said Abbott had known he was coming. He called the then-Health Minister, now-Opposition Leader and probable future Prime Minister a “gutless creep” and a “disgraceful human being”. The Global Mail Bad polls Julia Gillard's personal support has dropped to a three-month low and Tony Abbott has extended his lead as the nation's preferred prime minister after public outrage over plans to give the Labor and Liberal parties $10 million each in taxpayer funds for electioneering. Satisfaction with the way the Prime Minister is doing her job has fallen three points to 28 per cent in the past two weeks and dissatisfaction has risen from 59 per cent to 62 per cent, the highest since the end of March. And Mr Abbott has stretched his lead as preferred prime minister to eight points. Ms Gillard faced a revolt from her Labor MPs and a public outcry at attempts to quietly agree to public funding changes for political parties last week. The Australian (paywalled) Julia Gillard's home state has abandoned her with as many as six seats in Victoria now at risk of falling to the Coalition, including that of her Attorney General Mark Dreyfus. And Tony Abbott has surged ahead nationally as a better potential Prime Minister, with three separate polls now confirming a worsening electoral crisis looming for Labor as it battles to contain the latest NBN asbestos debacle and more boat arrivals. In what will be a shock for the Prime Minister, an exclusive JWS/Herald Sun poll reveals a 15.4 per cent swing against Mr Dreyfus in the once-safe seat of Isaacs, enough to remove him. If repeated in neighbouring seats, the PM's hopes of retaining the furniture in her home state could be dashed. (news.com) Deputy Prime Minister Wayne Swan looks set to lose his Brisbane seat of Lilley, with internal polling suggesting Labor will struggle to retain any Queensland seats at the September 14 federal election. In a result even worse than the 1996 ''baseball bats'' election, when Labor was reduced to two of the then 26 seats in Queensland, Labor may retain only one MP - former prime minister Kevin Rudd. The Queensland polling, taken in recent weeks in Mr Swan's seat of Lilley, is believed to show Mr Swan's primary vote has collapsed to just 28 per cent, compared with 41 per cent at the last election. Fairfax Media Don’t speak out of school Shadow Treasurer Joe Hockey has issued a gag order on Coalition MPs and candidates talking to the Parliamentary Budget Office over fears that new laws will expose the Coalition to greater scrutiny. But in a bid to crack down on ill-discipline in Coalition ranks, he has banned any candidate or MP speaking about election policy without prior approval from the leader's office or the campaign head office. Fearing that changes to the powers of the PBO, expected before the September election, will put individual members under PBO scrutiny over the total cost of Coalition policies, Mr Hockey has written to all MPs and candidates warning them about straying from official policy line. He has demanded that if any candidate or MP is contacted by the PBO, then the contact must be immediately referred to Mr Hockey's office. (news.com) Asylum boats There was almost no warning. The boat had stopped about 10 minutes earlier. Since then it had rocked gently in the swell, settling lower in the water. Its Indonesian crew shouted to one another, increasingly agitated. On the roof of the open wooden outsize fishing boat, Mohammed Ishaq was shaken awake by another refugee. “Get up, the boat is sinking,” he was told. But even as he stood, the 31-year-old Afghan-born Pakistani felt the deck tilting sharply under his feet. He slid, fell and hit the water. It was 24 June 2012. The boat was 107 nautical miles from the nearest land. Of the 204 refugees aboard, almost all from Afghanistan or Pakistan, 94 would die. (Guardian Australia) You can follow live updates throughout the day here on the live blog, or join the conversation on Twitter using the hashtag #politicslive You can also follow me on @murpharoo

headline: Politics live: readers' edition - Friday 30 August
trail_text: Share breaking news, leave links to interesting articles online and chat about the day's political events in our open thread
body_text: I'm not writing my usual Politics Live blog today, but, as an alternative, here's Politics Live: readers' edition. It's intended to be a place where you can catch up with the latest news and find links to good politics blogs and articles on the web. Please feel free to use this as somewhere you can comment on any of the day's political stories - just as you do when I'm writing the daily blog. It would be particularly useful for readers to flag up new material in the comments - breaking news or blogposts or tweets that are worth passing on because someone is going to find them interesting. A lot of what I do on my blog is aggregation - finding the good stuff and passing it on - and you can do this, too (as I know, because it happens every day when I'm blogging). All of today's Guardian politics stories are here, and all the politics stories filed on Thursday including some in today's paper, are here.

headline: Delhi's new chief rejects security on mission to clean up Indian politics
trail_text: Arvind Kejriwal, whose party is made up of all first-time candidates, vows to fight corruption after shock results at polls
body_text: The former tax inspector and activist who is to be Delhi's new chief minister, the top elected official in the metropolis, has refused police protection, saying that his "biggest security" is God. Arvind Kejriwal, leader of a new populist political party "dedicated to improving the lot of the common man", announced on Monday that he would form a government to run the sprawling, troubled and increasingly wealthy city of 15 million people. The 45-year-old novice politician, who has vowed to end the "VIP culture" of the capital, also said that new ministers would refuse to accept the large government bungalows that are customary perks of such posts as well as cars that use flashing red beacons to force their way through traffic. Even minor dignitaries in the capital travel with a large police escort, adding to the already acute congestion and enraging ordinary citizens. Kejriwal's Aam Admi (Common Man) party stunned political analysts and established parties when it won 28 out of 70 seats in local assembly elections in Delhi earlier this month. The newcomer to the capital's cutthroat machinations, who launched his party a year ago, beat the former chief minster of the city, a veteran of the ruling Congress party who had dismissed his challenge as "not even on our radar". Congress suffered a catastrophic defeat, being reduced to eight seats. Almost all the candidates of the AAP were political debutants and included a rickshaw driver, a lawyer and a TV actor. Their key pledge was to clean up politics and the endemic corruption that has crippled the provision of public service for the many millions who cannot afford to pay for private healthcare, schooling or even basics such as water. The party's message and symbol – a broom – proved popular with urban voters also struggling with runaway inflation, chronic youth underemployment and slowing economic growth. Kerjiwal, who called the party's victory a historic win, had initially said it would not form a minority government. But after lengthy negotiations in recent days, Congress has now decided to support the AAP in the Delhi local assembly. "It's a great day, a very exciting time," said Aathishi Marlena, a senior AAP activist and policy adviser. Marlena said the challenges were daunting but that the party would meet them. "Tackling the rampant corruption, the lack of transparency and accountability, the problems faced by the common people is a matter of political will and that's what we bring to this." The main opposition Bharatiya Janata party won 32 seats but no other political group was willing to offer it support. Sachin Pilot, a Congress minister, described the emergence of a new political outfit – the AAP in Delhi – as "absolutely astonishing". "For my party of course it has been a setback but generally speaking the fact that indiviudals can come together in a very difficult space and find themselves relevant and meaningful has been a new phenomenon. In the last three or four decades it hasn't happened at all. That's something one has to recognise reconcile and then deal with," Pilot told the Guardian, speaking shortly after the poll earlier this month. Senior Congress officials told reporters that the party's support for the AAP in Delhi would not be unconditional and would depend on the party's performance. The election in Delhi was one of four state elections in the last month, which saw Congress lose heavily. The polls were dubbed a semi-final for the general election due next spring, by analysts. Though India's population remains largely rural, the proportion of seats in the national assembly determined by urban voters has steadily risen in recent years. Some observers see the new middle classes in cities and, particularly, tens of thousands of small towns, as determining what is likely to be a tough battle between the Congress party and the BJP next year. Also crucial are likely to be between 120 million – 150 million first time voters. The AAP has said it now wants to focus efforts on expanding into the huge northern state of Uttar Pradesh, which has a population fo 180 million, and Bihar, another poor northern state. It hopes to become a national party however. Kejriwal's announcement ended two weeks of speculation and fears of a hung assembly in Delhi, a city which has become much richer in recent years but faces huge problems of crime, sanitation, housing and pollution . Though the outgoing Congress government in Delhi had been praised for successfully building a metro system and holding the Commonwealth Games in the Indian capital in 2010, it had also been hit by graft charges and suffered from the general unpopularity of the Congress-led coalition government at a national level. The new government will be sworn in on Thursday. The BJP called the AAP's decision a betrayal. "The AAP accused the Congress of being the most corrupt. Today they have compromised on their principles. This is gross betrayal," said Dr Harsh Vardhan, the BJP's chief ministerial candidate.

headline: Oh Lord, can't we keep religion out of politics?
trail_text: Teresa Wiltz: America's founders insisted on church-state separation. Yet now we have senators and justices prattling about the devil. Save us
body_text: The devil is making a comeback. Or maybe, if we're to believe a certain group of politicos, he's been hiding in plain sight all along. First, we had Representative Michele Bachmann (Republican, Minneosota) asserting that President Obama's decision to help a segment of the Syrian rebels is proof positive that the four horsemen of the apocalypse would be showing up any day now. Because terrorists. "Rather than seeing this as a negative, we need to rejoice, Maranatha, come Lord Jesus, His day is at hand," she said in an interview with a Christian radio station. Senator Rand Paul (Republican, Kentucky), speaking at the Values Voter Summit over the weekend, declared that there was a "worldwide war against Christianity" – and the president, he said, isn't doing a thing to stop it. And then we had Senator Ted "Shutdown" Cruz (Republican, Texas), at the same conference, using a line from the movie The Usual Suspects – "the greatest trick the devil ever played was to convince the world he didn't exist" – to make a clumsy analogy to liberals. Because leftwingers have "convinced conservatives that we cannot win". Never mind those plummeting Republican approval ratings, senator, Satan made the pollsters do it. Sadly, I've come to expect such "us v them" histrionics from Bachmann et al. They are, after all, playing to their base. So I'd be tempted to dismiss this all as political theater – played-out political theater – if it weren't for that crazy times interview that US supreme court Justice Antonin Scalia did with New York magazine. In it, he talked about the devil. At great length. With no apparent irony: And then You know, it is curious. In the Gospels, the devil is doing all sorts of things. He's making pigs run off cliffs, he's possessing people and whatnot. And that doesn't happen very much anymore … It's because he's smart … What he's doing now is getting people not to believe in him or in God. He's much more successful that way. And then, he told the reporter that since she didn't hold the same belief system, she'd be going to hell, too. Let's hope he was kidding about that. Now, I'm all about freedom of religion. (My own beliefs tend to veer towards agnosticism, with a healthy side of Buddhism.) And I certainly respect the good justice's right to believe in whatever the hell he wants to believe in, even if that means a fervent belief in Beelzebub. But given that Scalia's about to weigh in on a case about public prayer in government meetings, I really wish he'd kept his lips zipped. Religion has no place in politics – and it certainly has no place in the US supreme court. Religion, or the lack thereof, is such a deeply, deeply personal thing. People who think they've got a personal pipeline to the divine make me nervous. Scratch that. People in power who think they've got a personal pipeline to the divine make me nervous. Really nervous. Because religion has been used in this country to justify all sorts of things – slavery being at the top of that list. It encourages revisionist thinking, like the Texas Board of Education's reshaping American history in its textbooks. And unless you've pulled some mystical magic trick, died, crossed over to the great beyond and come back again, how do you really know what God's thinking? If, that is, She exists at all. So, I don't like the role that religion – to be specific, Christianity – plays too often in American politics. It made me cringe during the 2008 presidential election, watching Barack Obama tie himself into knots trying to establish his Christian bonafides. Why should it matter? What if he were, let's say, a devout adherent to the Baha'i faith? Or if he were a committed atheist? No, we weren't created to be a Christian nation, even though the Founding Fathers had Christians in their ranks. When he was president, Thomas Jefferson, a fervent theist, told a group of Baptist ministers government had no business meddling in religion. Moreover, he said, there needed to be a "wall of separation between church and state". What happened to the wall? I like the wall. We need the wall. In fact, let's toss a few more bricks on top of the wall while we're at it – the better to keep the devil from meddling in our political affairs.

headline: Labour conference - Ed Miliband's speech: Politics live blog
trail_text: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of the Labour conference in Brighton, including Ed Miliband's speech
body_text: Here is a summary of that fringe event. • The lights could go out if Labour introduces its 20-month freeze on energy prices, Ian Peters of British Gas said. “If we have no ability to control what we do in the retail prices” and wholesale prices suddenly go up within a single year “that will threaten energy security,” he said. Asked if that meant the lights would go out, he replied: “I think that is a risk.” • Tom Greatrex, the junior shadow energy minister, rejected this. He said: “I do not accept that that [the price freeze] is likely to produce blackouts at all … I don’t see that as a realistic prospect, as much as it might be a good line for a news bulletin and a good headline.” • Greatrex was asked if Labour would cut the companies some slack if there was a sudden increase in wholesale prices. Greatrex said they would not, and pointed out that when wholesale prices have come down in the past that had not been passed on to consumers. That’s it from us for today. Join Andrew Sparrow tomorrow morning for the final day of the Labour conference. Reg Platt of the IPPR says he does not believe there will be a supply problem brought on by the Labour policy and he says that if the energy companies threaten that, they will deserve a harsh response from the public. Tom Greatrex is asked about this idea that the lights might go out. Ian Peters interrupts to say he was talking about “in year market volatility” rather than long-term increases. Greatrex says: I don’t accept that the process of ensuring you have transparency and clarity in the market is going to lead to blackouts … In terms of the fixed period, the price freeze, that is because we would need that period to get that framework in place. I do not accept that that is likely to produce blackouts at all … I don’t see that as a realistic prospect, as much as it might be a good line for a news bulletin and a good headline. Gary Smith of the GMB says he does not agree that the lights will go out because of Labour’s announcement. British Gas’s Ian Peters is asked what he thinks of the Labour announcement. “If we have no ability to control what we do in the retail prices” and wholesale prices go up “that will threaten energy security,” Peters says. Does he mean the lights will go out? “I think that is a risk,” he says. Nick Pearce of the IPPR asks what happens if there is a big increase in wholesale prices. Greatrex says from 2015 we would have to put a short piece of legislation through to establish the cap, and then until 2017 the costs will be frozen. When wholesale costs have come down in the past, Greatrex says, this has not been reflected in consumers’ bills. “During that period [2015-17] it is right to be able to have that freeze,” he says. He says “it’s not about a short-term fix”. Tom Greatrex, the shadow junior energy minister, is up next. He says all the big six energy companies know they have "lost trust with their customers". It’s “vital” that trust is rebuilt, he says. There are significant levels of profit from companies in the generation part of the energy sector rather than the retail part – and these are often the same companies, he points out. Greatrex responds to criticisms that Labour’s policy would threaten investment. Investment recently has “partly fallen away” because of “mixed messages” and “false debates” from the government on shale gas, renewables and nuclear energy, he says. Reg Platt of the IPPR thinktank speaks first, saying wholesale gas costs are largely responsible for high energy prices. Platt says: “It’s very difficult to know what those profits are that are being made, and what you might deem appropriate in the sector.” He predicted Labour’s cap would be “a very popular policy with the public”. Ian Peters, managing director of residential energy at British Gas, speaks next. He says the vast majority of a gas or electricity bill is made up of things his company cannot control – such as wholesale costs, delivery to the home, and taxes. “Energy prices reflect the cost of energy,” he says. Profits are a small proportion, he says. We’re a strong British country creating jobs and investing in Britain’s future, Peters says. Gary Smith of the GMB union goes next. He says energy bills could double over the next few years and “that is a crisis that is facing people in how they’re going to meet that”. Smith says large chunks of the UK’s gas and electricity infrastructure are owned by Chinese companies, and he doesn’t believe they have British interests at heart. He backs Peters on his contention that the main drivers of increased prices are out of the hands of energy companies. Hi, Paul Owen here to take the reins for the next hour or so. I came here to this a conference room on the top floor – more or less on the roof – of the Metropole hotel to hear Caroline Flint, the shadow energy secretary, hopefully talk about Labour’s most eye-catching policy for years: Ed Miliband’s promise to impose a 20-month price freeze on all energy companies. Unfortunately Flint has pulled out and will be replaced by her junior shadow minister Tom Greatrex. But I think it will probably be worth hearing what he has to say. Miliband announced the new policy in his conference speech today, saying: “The companies won't like it because it will cost them money. But they have been overcharging people for so long because the market does not work. And we need to press the reset button." To my mind this policy has the potential to have real “cut-through” with voters and may even have some impact on the outcome of the 2015 election. Labour's promise to increase the number of hours of free childcare is the same, in my opinion. • Ed Miliband has delighted his party with a well-delivered conference speech that contained a promise to freeze gas and electricity bills for almost two years if Labour wins the general election. It was the one announcement in the speech not trailed in advance, and it is the probably most eye-catching example of his desire to tame "irresponsible" capitalism through robust regulation. For Labour activists who have been looking for populist policies to sell on the doorstep, it is a godsend. But it's a risky move too. Energy companies claim that it is unfair, and that it will curb investment. They may, of course, be exaggerating. (The plan will cost the industry around £4bn. Utility companies complained bitterly about the £5bn windfall tax proposed by Labour before the 1997 election, but in the event it was imposed with little fuss). But there are other dangers too. What happens if the Tories respond with fuel payments targeted at those on low and middle-incomes (which would allow David Cameron to criticise a price freeze benefiting the rich)? And what happens if energy prices start to fall, undermining Labour's key election offer? • Ed Davey, the Lib Dem energy secretary, has suggested that Miliband's policy could lead to "widespread blackouts". Energy UK, the trade body for the industry, has also suggested that energy companies could stop investing in Britain in response to Miliband's threat. That's all from me for today. Thanks for the comments. My colleague Paul Owen will post an update from the fringe later. Here are the best quotes from Ed Miliband's speech. • Miliband said his main theme was that Britain could do better. I want to start today with the simplest of thoughts. An idea that has inspired change for generations. The belief that helped drive us out of the Second World War and into that great reforming government of 1945. An ambition that is more important now than it has been for decades. An emotion that is felt across our country at kitchen tables every night. A feeling that is so threatening to those who want to keep things as they are. Words that are so basic and yet so powerful, so modest and yet so hard to believe. Six simple words that say: Britain can do better than this. Britain can do better than this; we are Britain, we are better than this. • He said he believed that listening to others, and understanding their problems, was the most important thing a leader could do. [Miliband's mother] is the most patient, generous person I have met in my whole life. And she taught me never to be contemptuous of others, never to be dismissive of their struggle. Now she was teaching me a lesson of life. And some people will say, ah yeah but you have to leave decency behind when it comes to politics. Well I say they are wrong, because only if you reach out and listen can you do the most important thing a leader can do, the most important qualification in my view for being Prime Minister. Only then will you have the ability to walk in the shoes of others and know who to fight for, whoever your opponent, however powerful they are, guided by the only thing that matters: your sense of what is right. This is what I believe, this is where I stand, this is the leadership Britain needs. • He said the key problem for Britain was that ordinary people were no longer benefiting from growth. Now what I’m about to tell you is the most important thing I’m going to say today about what needs to change about our country. For generations in Britain when the economy grew the majority got better off. And then somewhere along the way that vital link between the growing wealth of the country and your family finances was broken. This is, this goes beyond one party or one government. It is more important to you than which party is in power, even more important than that ... They used to say a rising tide lifts all boats, now the rising tide just seems to lift the yachts. Now I say this to the people of Britain. If I were you I wouldn’t even take a second look at a political party unless they make this their central defining purpose because your future depends on it. Your children’s future depends on it. Britain’s future depends on it. I say we are Britain we can do better than this. • He said this problem was linked to David Cameron's approach to what Cameron calls "the global race". The cost of living crisis isn’t an accident of David Cameron’s economic policy it is in his economic policy. Let me explain why. You see he believes in this thing called the global race, but what he doesn’t tell you is that he thinks for Britain to win the global race you have to lose, lower wages, worse terms and conditions, fewer rights at work. But Britain can’t win a race for the lowest wages against countries where wages rates are pennies an hour and the more we try the worse things will get for you. Britain can’t win a race for the fewest rights at work against the sweat shops of the world and the more we try the worse things will get for you. And Britain can’t win a race for the lowest skilled jobs against countries where kids leave school at the age of 11. And the more we try the worse things will get for you. It is a race to the bottom. Britain cannot and should not win that race. • He said the Tories were only interested in those at the top. So when they tell you the economy is healing, that everything is fixed, just remember, they are not talking about your life, they are talking about their friends at the top. That is who they are talking about; it is high hopes for them. And every so often you know the mask slips doesn’t it. The other day a man they call Lord Howell, he was I think their advisor on fracking at one point… There is nothing funny about that. He said it was wrong to frack in some areas but it was ok in others, it was ok in the North East of England because he said, and I quote ‘it was full of desolate and uninhabited areas.’ In one casual aside dismissing one who le region of the country. Let’s tell these Tories about the North East of England and every other part of Britain. People go out to work. They love their kids. They bring up their families. They care for their neighbours. They look out for each other. They are proud of their communities. They are proud of their communities. They hope for the future. The Tories call them inhabitants of desolate areas. We call them our friends, our neighbours, the heroes of our country. They are fed up of a government that doesn’t understand their lives and a Prime Minister who cannot walk in their shoes. We are Britain, we are better than this. • He said Labour would freeze gas bills. Take the gas and electricity companies. We need successful energy companies, in Britain. We need them to invest for the future. But you need to get a fair deal and frankly, there will never be public consent for that investment unless you do get a fair deal. And the system is broken and we are going to fix it. If we win the election 2015 the next Labour government will freeze gas and electricity prices until the start of 2017. Your bills will not rise. It will benefit millions of families and millions of businesses. That’s what I mean by a government that fights for you. That’s what I mean when I say Britain can do better than this. • He said Labour would get house building up to 200,000 a year by 2020. There are 9 million people in this country renting a home, many of whom who would want to buy. 9 million people - we don’t just have a cost of living crisis, we have a housing crisis too. In 2010 when we left office there was a problem. There were one million too few homes in Britain. If we carry on as we are, by 2020 there will be two million too few homes in Britain. That is the equivalent of two cities the size of Birmingham. Wave got to do something about it and the next Labour government will. So we’ll say to private developers, you can’t just sit on land and refuse to build. We will give them a very clear message - either use the land or lose the land, that is what the next Labour government will do. We’ll say to local authorities that they have a right to grow, and neighbouring authorities can’t just stop them. We’ll identify new towns and garden cities and we’ll have a clear aim that by the end of the parliament Britain will be building 200,000 homes a year, more than at any time in a generation. That’s how we make Britain better than this. • He said Labour did rescue the NHS. When we came to office there were waiting time targets of 18 months that were not being met, when we left office there were waiting time targets of 18 weeks that were being met. When we came to office there was an annual winter A&E crisis, when we left office the people had A&E services they could rely on. When we came to office there were fewer doctors and nurses, we when left office more doctors and nurses than ever before. And when we came to office people said well the health service, it was a good idea in previous generations but I don’t really believe it will be there in the next, and we left office with the highest public satisfaction in the history of the health services. Yes friends, we did rescue the National Health Service. • He said he would happily fight David Cameron on the question of leadership and character. But the next election isn’t just going to be about policy. It is going to be about how we lead and the character we show. I have got a message for the Tories today: if they want to have a debate about leadership and character, be my guest. And if you want to know the difference between me and David Cameron, here’s an easy way to remember it. When it was Murdoch versus the McCanns, he took the side of Murdoch. When it was the tobacco lobby versus the cancer charities, he took the side of the tobacco lobby. When it was the millionaires who wanted a tax cut versus people paying the bedroom tax, he took the side of the millionaires. Come to think of it, here is an even easier way to remember it: David Cameron was the Pr ime Minister who introduced the bedroom tax, I’ll be the Prime Minister who repeals the bedroom tax. You see here is the thing about David Cameron. He may be strong at standing up to the weak, but he is always weak when it comes to standing up against the strong. That is the difference between me and David Cameron, so let’s have that debate about leadership and character, and I relish that debate. Here's the full text of Ed Miliband's speech. I'll post the best quotes in a moment. Angela Knight, chief executive of Energy UK, the trade association for the energy body, told Sky News a few minutes ago that Ed Miliband's proposal could lead to energy companies pulling investment out of the UK. Possible the first thing that might happen is a bit more serious in some respects. Much of the money that is required to invest in energy in the UK comes from overseas. They will be looking at these sorts of statements and thinking do I go and invest this money in a country where I don't know if I'm going to get a return because there are price freezes around, or do I go and invest it in a country which perhaps thinks in a rather different manner. Nigel Farage, the Ukip leader, has responded to Ed Miliband's speech. (He didn't like it.) The Labour leader’s speech was an extraordinary confection promising Britain a return to the divided, bankrupt country of the late seventies. Economically, environmentally and constitutionally inept and destructive, at over an hour, it may the longest political valedictory speech in our history .... [His plans] to freeze gas prices until 2017, without saying how he could possibly achieve this in a free market while adhering to the carbon cutting craze that is forcing energy prices up by channelling vast amounts of money into unreliable, inefficient green technologies such as wind. Labour’s idea to create an Energy Security Board does little more than bring back memories of candles after dark and burning braziers at street corners. Here's Guardian Comment is free panel verdict, with contributions from Diane Abbott, Jackie Ashley, John McDonnell, Melissa Kite, Hopi Sen and David Clark. From Craig Bennett, Friends of the Earth's policy and campaigns director Freezing energy bills will certainly be welcomed by cash-strapped households, who face rocketing prices due to the soaring cost of gas and bumper energy company profits. The only way to deliver an energy system we can all afford is by urgently investing in an ambitious and comprehensive energy efficiency programme and weaning the nation off increasingly costly fossil fuels - and this must be a priority for an incoming Labour Government. Ed Miliband was right to put the environment at the heart of his conference speech - he must now put it firmly at the centre of Labour's policy-making to realise his goal of one million new green jobs. Ed Davey, the Lib Dem energy secretary, has put out a statement about Ed Miliband's plan to freeze energy bills. He suggests that it could lead to power cuts. Everyone wants to help with the cost of rising bills, which is why Liberal Democrats have cut income tax by £700 for working people. But Labour’s plan is a promise that won’t work. When they tried to fix prices in California it resulted in an electricity crisis and widespread blackouts. We can’t risk the lights going out here too. Fixing prices in this way risks blackouts, jeopardises jobs and puts investment in clean, green technology in doubt. The Institute of Directors does not like the speech. This is from Simon Walker, the IoD's director general, on the plan to penalise developers if they do not build on land with planning permission. The obstacle to house building is invariably the bureaucratic nightmare of trying to get a project off the ground. Taxing developers who are not building is no substitute for simply making it easier to build. This approach risks making it even less attractive for developers to build, whilst ignoring the urgent need for planning liberalisation. And this is what he says about the proposal to freeze energy bills. We should think very, very carefully before piling more distortion on an already grossly distorted energy market. Price controls only add greater uncertainty to companies who we need to take the financial risks of energy investment. It was also a great shame not to hear anything about the potential benefits of domestic shale gas exploration, which must feature as part of the UK’s long-term energy strategy. From Frances O'Grady, general secretary of the TUC This was a defining speech that rightly focussed on the living standards crisis and went on to offer hope. At its heart was a clear break from the view that Britain succeeds by reducing the rights, pay and prospects of people at work. More importantly the clear pledges made today and during the rest of this week showed that there will be a real choice at the next election – no-one can now say that the parties are all the same. The right were never going to like this speech. Here is reaction from two groups on the libertarian fringe of politics. From Matthew Sinclair, chief executive of the TaxPayers' Alliance When the Government fixes prices it always ends in a disaster for consumers. Ed Miliband is sticking by the green taxes and expensive subsidies that drive up the price of energy, so at best this new policy would just store up massive price hikes for another day. At worst it could create a crisis and force the Government to bail out the sector. If he really wants to help families struggling with their bills, he should promise that the next Labour Government would scrap the taxes and regulations that will otherwise keep making energy much more expensive for British consumers. From Philip Booth, editorial director at the Institute for Economic Affairs Ed Miliband clearly does not understand the root cause of rising living costs. His speech set out an array of government interventions. It is precisely these kind of interventions which have seen the price of food, energy and housing reach such burdensome levels. His tinkering policies will do little for families struggling to make ends meet. Unfortunately the Labour Party has not recognised that the most effective way to reduce living costs and unleash economic growth is to reduce taxes and ease regulation. In his speech Ed Milband defended England's union with Scotland with a story about a Labour delegate from Glasgow having to receive emergency hospital treatment in Liverpool. The SNP's Angus Robertson thinks trying to use the NHS to make a unionist point will backfire. For Ed Miliband to use the NHS as a reason to attack independence shows an astonishing lack of knowledge both about the National Health Service and about Scotland. The reality is that Scotland is already independent in terms of health policy, and it is precisely because NHS Scotland comes under the remit of the Scottish Parliament, rather than Westminster, that we are able to protect and develop the founding ethos of our health service, in stark contrast to the privatisation and fragmentation happening south of the border. This was a real blunder by Mr Miliband which plays right into the hands of the case for Yes, because it demonstrates that the best decisions for Scotland are decisions made in Scotland. And as experts have confirmed, exactly the same cross-border healthcare arrangements that NHS Scotland is obliged to have now with counterparts in the rest of the UK and overseas would apply in an independent Scotland. And here's some more union reaction. From Paul Kenny, leader of the GMB It was a very polished performance, with the welcome theme that Britain can do better. Millions of people know that life can be better. That was a completely different Labour leader to the one we saw three years ago. No prompts, no plastic performance - he has grown into being a prime minister. From Steve Murphy, general secretary of construction union Ucatt Our current housing crisis is a result of a failure to build a sufficient number of homes especially council housing for decades. This is the first time that a political party is seriously addressing this crisis which is affecting Britain socially and economically. From Lord Kinnock, the former Labour leader A brilliant speech, wonderfully delivered and, most importantly, I thought it was fundamental, practical patriotism that will resonate in the country and it's really what Ed means. From Dave Prentis, the Unison general secretary [Miliband] showed he understands what people are going through. He offered firm commitments to help hard-pressed families. He gave a vision for the many, not the few. From Joss Garman, deputy political director of Greenpeace As energy secretary, it was Ed Miliband who introduced new pollution rules for power stations and pushed for more ambition at the global climate talks in Copenhagen, so it was good to see him once again putting climate change back at the heart of his pitch to the country. His promise that his government would completely remove carbon pollution from the energy system and freeze energy bills will mean he has to face down the powerful energy companies, but public opinion is on his side. Together with his pledge to create a million new green jobs, he has set a new bar for the coalition parties to meet. From John Longworth, director general of the British Chambers of Commerce Ed Miliband is right to say that Britain should be in a 'race to the top'. While it was refreshing to hear a leader speak about major economic issues, he did not set out a clear vision to move Britain's economy from good to great. What's more, his speech contained more sticks than carrots for business. The way to have a high-wage, high-value economy is to encourage business investment and to build an enterprise-friendly environment. While some of his proposals pass this test, many others do not. What's more, he has made significant spending commitments, but it is not clear how the country will create the wealth required to pay for them. From Peter Hain, the Labour former cabinet minister It was a barnstorming speech, a virtuoso performance. I don't know any other politician in Britain who can deliver without a note that kind of rousing speech of that length of time and tell a story about what Ed Miliband's Britain will be like - a fairer Britain and more prosperous Britain for everybody. From curbing electricity and gas prices to cutting business rates to small businesses to abolishing the reviled 'bedroom tax', all of these policies - building hundreds of thousands of more houses - will resonate with every one of the concerns in every household in Britain. From John Cridland, the CBI director general Businesses will view the proposals on tax and energy as a setback for Labour's pro-enterprise credentials. Rising energy bills are tough on families and businesses, but the proposed energy price freeze will deter much-needed investment and is at odds with Labour's pledge to decarbonise the economy and create a million green jobs. The main reasons that bills are going up is the combination of rising wholesale prices, the cost of policies needed to keep the lights on and the move to a low-carbon economy. Here's Len McCluskey, the Unite leader, on the speech. People at home urgently wanting to know what a Labour government will do for them will certainly have a better idea today. They will know that this is a leader and a party that is on their side. Ed Miliband should never be afraid to articulate a courageous vision for this nation because that is what these extraordinary, fearful times urgently need. Voters are hungry for something better for Britain than foodbanks, tattered wages and Wonga-nomics. They want to hear about homes, that their children will have a future in this country, about a fair day's wage for a fair day's work. They need Labour to stand for a better life for the people of this country than the worry and insecurity this miserable government offers. It was good to hear Ed mention the role of unions positively in delivering the minimum wage, challenging in-work poverty. This is what we do day-in, day-out. Here are the key points from the speech. • Miliband says Labour would freeze gas and electricity bills for 20 months after 2015 general election. • 16 and 17-year-olds would be given the vote. • By 2020 200,000 new homes would be built a year. • Business rates would be cut for small businesses. • All primary schools would have breakfast clubs or after-school clubs. • Minimum wage would be strengthened. Here is some Twitter reaction from journalists and commentators. The energy companies have been reacting to the speech already. Snap Verdict: Labour delegates wanted a biggie and they got one. Many policies announcements have little "cut through" with the public, but the energy price freeze surprise at the heart of the speech will probably do the trick. Even if the Conservatives retaliate somehow (as they surely will), it will appeal to voters, and it will signal a determination to use the power of the state to strong-arm corporate Britain in a way the coalition (or New Labour) could not or would not match. Miliband was not using notes, and the speech was more conversational than a conventional leader's conference. But that worked, and may impress people who have doubts about Miliband's power as a communicator. The speech was also rich in hope and optimism (unlike much of what has been heard from the conference platform this week), and his character challenge to David Cameron was the most effective he has delivered. More later. But first I will round up some other reaction .... Our sentiment tracker showed a steady climb over the course of Ed Miliband's speech, with just one major dip into negative territory, at around 2.45pm, possibly around the time Miliband said Labour would not be able to spend money it does not have. PO We had a few internet problems here, and I may have missed some, but I think Ed Miliband only said three of our Milibingo terms: one nation, cuts and unions. I didn't hear any of the others: infighting, recovery, credibility, unemployment, macho or coalition. Let me know in the comments if I'm wrong. PO Miliband says that when people said at the dawn of the industrial revolution that Britain could be better than this, they were right. Those that said at the darkest hour, during the war, that Britain could be better than this, and they did. And the same applied when the battle for equality was being fought at the end of the last century. Britain can be better than this, he says. He will lead a government for Britain. Miliband says there is going to be a big fight in the run up to the general election. Think about the people, he says. I don't want to win this fight for Labour. I want to win it for Britain. Miliband condemns the Home Office for sending the "go home" vans to areas where immigrant families have lived for years. We are better than that, Miliband says. Miliband has a message for the Tories. If they want a debate about leadership and character, "be my guest". When there was a choice between Murdoch and the McCanns, Cameron sided with Murdoch. He sided with the tobacco industry. And he cut taxes for millionaires. And he was the PM who introduced the bedroom tax. Miliband will be the PM who abolishes it. Cameron may be strong at standing up to the weak. But he is weak at standing up to the strong. Miliband has a story about Scotland. Cathy Murphy lives in Glasgow. She was ill. But she came to the Labour conference in Liverpool in 2011. She fell ill, and had to have a serious operation. She had it at a world-leading hospital in Liverpool. She has to return every six months for a check-up. The doctors do not worry that she is from Scotland. They care for her anyway. He invites the audience to applaud. And he says he wants the Liverpool hospital to carry on treating patients like Cathy. Miliband turns to devolution. Devolution works, he says. Carwyn Jones is a brilliant first minister of Wales. And Johann Lamont is doing a brilliant job fighting the SNP. Miliband says he would like to hear more from young people. It was gay and lesbian young people who led the fight for gay marriage. And young women are saying the battle for equality is not won. They are not satisfied that 30% of Labour MPs are women. They want it to be 50%. And they are right. And they are not satisfied that women are still subject to violence and harassment. And they are right. Let's give the vote to 16 and 17-year-olds, he says. Miliband says he wants ordinary workers to be at the heart of the party. Leadership matters. But in the end change comes from the bottom. He wants to make Labour a mass membership party. Let's make ourselves truly the people's party again. Unions: Labour’s best frenemies. PO Miliband says he has explained how Labour can rescue the economy and society. Now he wants to talk about rescuing politics. It is the bit they have all been looking forward to - party reform. Change is difficult, he says. But Labour cannot just be a party of 200,000. It should be a party of 500,000, or 600,000. It is possible because of the unique link with the unions. Miliband says he does not want to end that link. He wants to mend that link. Miliband says Labour really did rescue the NHS. The government is responsible for what is wrong with it now. And Labour has to rescue the NHS all over again. (This gets what seems to be the largest round of applause of the speech.) Miliband says Labour does not believe in a race to the bottom. It believes in a race to the top. (Another glitch. I've missed a bit.) Miliband says the next Labour government should deal with the scourge of low pay. The minimum wage has been falling in values. Labour must do something about this. Some sectors, like banking, pay the living wage. But other sectors need to pay more. That's why government will strengthen the minimum wage. Miliband says firms will have to provide apprenticeships if they want to hire skilled foreign workers. And companies that train their workers will be able to "call time" on their competitors who do not do this. Miliband says to win the race to the top Britain has to support small businesses. New jobs will come from small firms. In the future most of the new jobs will come from firms that don't yet exist. The government has cut taxes for big businesses, but put them up for big ones. He says that's not right. Labour would take money from big business to cut taxes for small businesses. It will cut business rates for them in 2015, and freeze them for the next year. One nation Labour stands for small businesses. Cuts: Ed will cut small business rates, he says. PO Miliband turns to the environment. In 20 years' time his children will be asking if his was the last generation not to get climate change, or the first to get it. George Osborne says it's a matter of what you can afford. He's right. Britain cannot afford not to take climate change seriously. Miliband says Britain should be in a race to the top. But Labour is not going to be able to do this easily. You think opposition is tough? Government is harder. Labour will not be able to spend money it does not have. And, if he said it would, people would not believe him anyway. Miliband says it is not the low achievements of the Tories that annoy him. It is their low aspirations for Britons. The Tories are only interested in their friends at the top. Think of Lord Howell, the Tory adviser on fracking. He said it was wrong to frack in some places, but okay in others, like the north east, which is "desolate". Let's tell people about the north east. People love their kids there, they go out to work, they are proud of their communities, they look after their neighbours. The Tories call them desolate. We call them friends, and the heroes of our country. Miliband says he would advise the British public not to even take a second look at a party unless they acknowledge this. We can do better than this. Do the Tories get it? He invites the audience to respond. They shout no. Miliband says when he was growing up living standards were rising. People could afford to go on holiday for the first time. They used to say a rising tide lifts all boats. Now a rising tide just seems to lift the yachts. We need to understand how we got here. This bit is the most important thing he will say today, he says. For generations, as the economy grew, everyone benefited. But then the link between growth and the majority broke. This is more important to people even than who is in power, he says. (I've missed a bit, because my computer crashed, but I'm up now.) Miliband has just told stories of meeting people who like Britain, but who believe Britain can do better than this. Britain can do better than this, he says (twice). And Britain will be better than this, he says (again, twice). Mililband is now on to the political challenge. Britain can do better than this ... We're Britain, we're better than this. Are you satisfied with a country where people are working harder for less? People listen only to the powerful. Miliband is not satisfied. We're Britain.We're better than this. One nation: he stole it from a Tory, and he’s never giving it back. It’s Miliband’s catchphrase: one nation. PO She said Ed was attractive. He was not geeky, she said. And she said he was dressed casually "and had style". Miliband was pleased with this. But then he found out she was concussed. Anyway, he says to her: "You've made my year." Ed Miliband starts by thanking someone from the bottom of his heart. Not Justine - though he does want to thank her too. And not his mum. But a woman called Ella Phillips. She rode past him on his bike. She fell off. He helped her. And she said he was "an action hero." Ed Miliband has arrived in the hall. On the BBC Andrew Neil asks Nick Robinson asks if Ed Miliband will announce a 20-month energy freeze. Robinson says he has been told various things on an embargoed basis. But there will be an announcement about energy, he says. A random selection of Labour tweets from the hall ... Lord Ashcroft, the former Tory deputy chairman, is in the hall to watch the speech. (He has been speaking at a fringe meeting.) He's been tweeting. You can watch the speech live at the top of this page - and register what you think of it by pressing plus and minus on our "sentiment tracker". Just refresh the page to see them both. Ed Miliband will be speaking shortly. He is expected on stage at around 2.15pm. He is speaking without notes, and the party is not releasing a text in advance. That means that I will have to blog it in reported speech. Overnight Labour briefed two announcements from the speech, the house building plans and the business rate cuts for small firms. But we're expecting something more. The BBC's Andrew Neil has just posted this on Twitter. • Lord Adonis, the Labour former transport secretary who is leading Ed Miliband's growth review, has urged the party not to abandon the HS2 high-speed rail project. This is what he said at a Progress fringe event. We cannot as a party preach long-termism and not practice it ourselves. We have got to be very clear about that. We are the party that started HS2. I published the plan three and a half years ago. We set the whole thing out, we set out the rationale, including capacity. You have got to in politics, you cannot say that your principles and all that apply to other people but they don't apply to you when short term political advantage might rear its head. We have got to stick with this. It's very important. • Organisations representing large businesses have criticised Miliband's plan to cut business rates for small firms by abandoning a planned corporation tax cut for large firms. The CBI said the proposal undermined Labour's pro-enterprise credentials and the Institute for Directors said it was "a dangerous move for Labour to risk our business-friendly environment". But the Federation of Small Businesses and the British Retail Consortium welcomed the move. • Maria Eagle, the shadow transport secretary, has said Labour would put the interests of rail passengers before profit to "end the racket on our railways". As the Press Association reports, Eagle highlighted a range of proposals to stop customers feeling like the system is "always trying to rip you off" and said Labour would introduce a "new deal for passengers". Among the changes Labour would bring in is a drive to end the confusion around peak and off-peak tickets, changing the law to make sure passengers are offered the cheapest ticket no matter where or how the buy it, and ending the inflexibility on advance tickets which means passengers cannot get the following train even if it is empty. • Caroline Flint, the shadow energy secretary, has said energy companies would be forced to do their business in an open market under Labour. As the Press Association reports, she said if Labour won power in 2015, the party would prevent power companies doing their trades behind closed doors. She said there is currently no incentive for energy companies to keep prices down, telling the Labour Party conference in Brighton that power stations would no longer be run by the companies that supply gas and electricity directly to consumers. • Diana Holland, Labour's treasurer, has suggested that Miliband's reform to the union link could damage the party's finances. She delivered the warning in her speech to the conference. There are also important discussions about reforming membership and affiliation to the party. In all the discussions going forward, and about finding the best way, as your party treasurer I hope that whatever your point of view, everyone bears in mind the need to stick to the proven success of our party's finance strategy ... We cannot and must not jeopardise the hard-won financial stability of this party. Let us never forget as we develop the next steps for our party two very important truths - one, that the principles and values that unite us are far greater than the tactical and organisational differences that can divide us. • ITV have released a ComRes poll showing that 61% of people say Miliband does not come across as an election winner. Some 17% said he did, and 22% were not sure. • Gordon Brown was initially unimpressed by Barack Obama, Damian McBride has claimed in his memoirs. As Paul Owen reports, the book by the controversial former political adviser was published on Tuesday after a high-profile serialisation in the Daily Mail, with McBride's revelations about how he smeared Labour ministers gaining the most attention. But Power Trip also contains revelations about Brown's views of Obama, David Cameron, Rupert Murdoch – and the Mail's own editor, Paul Dacre. • Iain Dale, McBride's publisher, has been involved in a scuffle with a protester distracting attention from an interview that McBride was conducting. Paul Owen will be moonlighting as a bingo caller throughout Ed Miliband’s speech. He writes: The Guardian’s ruthless literary satirist John Crace has identified nine key words and phrases Ed Miliband is likely to weave deftly into his speech this afternoon. Click here for your interactive bingo card and you can tick them off when he does so. Here’s the full list: • One nation • Infighting • Recovery • Credibility • Cuts • Unemployment • Unions • Macho • Coalition I'll be following the speech and calling the words in this blog, as well as @gdnpolitics. Click here to get your bingo card. Good luck. Here's some more reaction to the plans being announced by Ed Miliband today. Cutting business rates for small firms From Helen Dickinson, director general of the British Retail Consortium The UK business rates are the highest property taxes of any EU country and lead directly to vacant shops. A consensus is emerging that the system is no longer fit for purpose and requires total reform. We therefore welcome this focus on supporting small businesses and high streets and the recognition that the cost of business rates has become unsustainable for retailers. With more than one in ten shops currently empty across the country, a complete reform of the system is required. We are keen to discuss these proposals in detail with Labour to understand how they might form part of this more fundamental root and branch review. From Alexander Jackman, head of policy at the Forum of Private Business There has been a lot of talk on the cost of living at this conference so we are pleased that Ed Miliband will use some of his speech today to highlight some of the rising costs of doing business. Taking money from a proposed business tax cut to fund a different business tax cut isn't an ideal way to fund the policy but importantly it recognises a significant rising cost facing many British small businesses. Building 200,000 new homes a year From Mike Quinton, chief executive of the National House Building Council, a body that sets standards for new homes. We welcome Ed Miliband's commitment to support the UK house building industry. Despite encouraging signs this year of a broad based recovery, there remains a critical shortage of new homes being built. Labour is right to focus on policies that will support the building of more new high quality homes and NHBC will work with all Parties to develop good housing policy. However, there is still some way to go before house building returns to pre-recession levels. As part of the Labour plan to promote more house building, developers would be penalised if they did not build homes on land granted planning permission. (See 11.49am.) At a fringe meeting at the conference, Sir Steve Bullock, London Councils' executive member of housing, backed the call for a crackdown on "landbanking". It’s frankly unacceptable that developers are hoarding land which could be used to build much needed homes for Londoners. With London’s population set to top nine million by 2021, we need to think radically about policy options to boost supply. Cracking down on landbanking could add 30,000 homes to London’s housing stock. London Councils say that in July 2013 there were 124,247 homes in London with agreed planning permission that had not been built. And the final shadow cabinet speaker this morning was Caroline Flint, the shadow energy secretary. The text of her speech is on the Labour website. Here are the main points. • Flint said Labour would introduce the "most radical, comprehensive reforms since energy privatisation" into the energy market. The big six energy firms would be broken up, she said. The days where a company generates energy, sells it to themselves … and then sells it to us … Those days will end ... Just as the banks will have to separate their investment and trading arms from the high street branches, so we will make the energy companies separate their production from the companies that supply your home. • She said Labour would make energy bills clearer. And let me say one more thing about the bills you will receive… Under Labour, on every bill you will see one standing charge and one unit price. Simple. Straightforward. Easy to compare. Easy to switch. • She said Labour would set up an energy security board "to identify our energy needs, secure investment for the future and keep the lights on". Maria Eagle, the shadow transport secretary, gave what may have been the best received speech of the morning. On policy, it was relatively meaty. The full text is on the Labour website, and here are the main points. • Eagle confirmed that Labour would impose a "proper cap" on rail fares. • She identified a string of measures that could be introduced to end what she described as "the racket on our railways". But we need a new deal for passengers too. No more talk of Super Peak fares, meaning your season ticket wouldn’t even be valid on every train. No more stretching peak time, when it’s actually about stretching profits. No more confusing tickets, but the exact time you can use it printed on the ticket. No more inflexibility when you book in advance, so you can’t get the next train – even when it’s empty. And if you do have the wrong ticket on the train, take off the price you’ve already paid from the cost of a new one. No more single and return journeys costing the same. Not just in one pilot area after 2015, as the government plans, but across the network. No more charging more at the ticket office than online, just to provide another excuse to close them. No more rip offs at ticket machines, but a new legal right to be offered the cheapest fare regardless of how or where you buy a ticket. No more inflation-busting increases in the cost of leaving your car at the station, when it’s just another way to clobber commuters. No more ripping people off with internet charges, just because you can’t afford to travel First Class. And isn’t it time that all trains had wifi in the 21st century? So let’s require it in franchises. • She said that Labour in principle supported HS2. This is what was in the text. So, as Ed Balls rightly says: we support the idea of a new north-south rail line but, if costs continue to rise – and the value for money cannot be demonstrated, we will have to ask if this is the right priority for £50billion pounds. But, as Rob Merrick points out, she added a line. • She identified a string of measures that could be introduced to boost cycling. And she said that she supported what Norman Baker, the Lib Dem transport minister, was trying to do on this. But Baker was being blocked by the Tories, she said. [Baker's] tried to get his Tory bosses to take cycling seriously. But while they’ve set out a plan to spend £28 billion on roads, he’s secured just £38million a year to support cycling. And conveniently forgotten the three wasted years that followed his decision to axe Cycling England and its £60million a year budget. Come off it, Norman: On ya bike. So, here’s what we need to do:clear goals to increase cycling. Separated routes. Redesigned junctions. Phased traffic lights. Cycling Safety Assessments for all new transport schemes. Restored targets to cut road deaths and serious injuries. Duties to support Active Travel, as Labour introduced in Wales. 20mph zones, the default in residential areas. Long term support for teaching safe cycling. Space on trains. Secure facilities at stations – required in rail contracts. Sentencing guidelines reviewed. Tough new rules on HGVs. Supporting cycling. Increasing numbers. Improving safety. • She said she wanted all InterCity trains to look the same. (Presumably this means that Virgin Trains' red carriages would get a repaint under Labour.) So, instead of all this waste, let’s reduce costs in our railway. Save money by bringing a fragmented industry together. With responsibilities currently spread across the Transport Department and multiple separate bodies, brought within a reformed and more accountable Network Rail. Save money by ending wasteful repainting and rebranding of trains and stations with every new contract. Restore a coherent InterCity identity to national train services, regardless of public or private operator. In his speech to the conference earlier Hilary Benn, the shadow communities secretary, set out more details of Labour's plans to build more homes. The text is on the Labour website. Here's an extract. First, we must admit that we can’t carry on saying on the one hand “where are the homes for the next generation?” and on the other “please don’t build them near me”. Nor will we get more homes by top-down targets. Councils and communities must take that responsibility but they need more power to be able to do so. Communities should know where land is available. That’s why we will ensure developers register the land they own or have options on. And where land is not brought forward for homes, communities should be able to do something about it. And when communities have given planning permission they should be able to say to developers: we’ve given you the go ahead so please get on and build the homes you said you would. And if you don’t then we’ll charge you and, if you still don’t, we’ll sell the land on to someone else who will. Secondly, there are areas in the country where councils and communities see the need for more homes but there just isn’t the land to build them on. So the next Labour government will give those communities a new ‘Right to Grow’, allowing them – if they want – to expand and ensuring that neighbouring areas work with them to do so. Thirdly, conference, it’s time to build new communities – new towns and new garden cities. That’s what the great Attlee Government did as they started to rebuild Britain and we need that same spirit again. So we will invite local authorities to come forward, and in return, we will make sure that they get the powers and the incentives they need to acquire land, put in the infrastructure and build. Build those new communities. You can read all today's Guardian Labour party conference coverage here. As for the rest of the papers, here are some articles I found particularly interesting. • Janan Ganesh in the Financial Times (subscription) explains the difference between Ed Balls and Ed Miliband. Mr Balls is a conventional social democrat. He believes the market is the least bad way of making economic decisions. He wants to tax and spend its proceeds for egalitarian ends – lavishly, for sure – but does not aspire to interfere with its workings. He does not fancy himself wiser than the price mechanism. He is a leftwinger who has read his Adam Smith. Mr Miliband is more corporatist. He thinks the left over-relies on redistribution. He wants to intervene earlier – to “predistribute” – by making businesses narrow pay differentials, train workers and empower unions. He is happier to second-guess the market. This intellectual difference between the two men manifests in a thousand ways. Mr Balls values the City as a financial centre and source of revenue; Mr Miliband seems to regard it as a barely necessary evil. Mr Balls would expand London’s airport capacity to meet demand; Mr Miliband prefers the environmental case against. Mr Balls tends not to sermonise about what the economy “should” look like; Mr Miliband scolds betting shops, payday lenders and other “predatory” businesses. Even in their political styles, Mr Miliband is abstract and Mr Balls remorselessly practical. In the arcane typology of the British left, Mr Miliband is “pamphlet Labour”, all discursive academia and moral certitude, whereas Mr Balls brings the campaigning grit of “leaflet Labour”. Only by grasping these differences can anyone appreciate Mr Balls’ usefulness to his party. • Rachel Sylvester in the Times (paywall) thinks Ed Miliband is still bound by loyalty to his father. It is no coincidence that Ralph Miliband’s gravestone in Highgate Cemetery, which stands just a few yards from Karl Marx’s tomb, is engraved with the words: “Writer Teacher Socialist”. Indeed, according to a family friend, one reason Ed decided to stand against his brother David for the Labour leadership was because he thought he would protect his father’s political legacy more faithfully than his more Blairite sibling. Just as Ralph emphasised the importance of the Left working outside Parliament to create a “new social order in which democracy, egalitarianism and co-operation” took hold, so Ed wants a “new politics” based in communities rather than the Commons to bring about his One Nation ideal. The influence of the older Miliband — who opposed regime change through military intervention, even the overthrow of the Pol Pot regime in Cambodia — may also be evident in his son’s decision to block action in Syria.
