Although his head knows that he must stay in the centre, Mr Miliband’s heart beats to the left. Socialism, he has said, is a “response to injustice” and “a tale that never ends”. He fears betraying his father, whom he describes as his “lodestar”, a man whose approval both he and his brother have always craved. • Benedict Brogan in the Telegraph says Labour figures are worried that the Tories are beating them at spin. Every conversation in Brighton, it seems, includes an admission that the Conservatives are winning the propaganda war hands down. If Craig Oliver and Lynton Crosby could hear what the shadow cabinet are saying in the bars, they would blush: “Their attack unit is ruthless. Their messaging is consistent and disciplined. They are beating us.” This is not something said often in Westminster – but for the past six months or so, the Labour high command has been noticing that a widespread perception of Tory incompetence lags behind the reality. The Conservatives may complain about their own machine, but the Opposition is arguably the better judge. And they are definitely hurting. There is no argument that Labour has had a terrible summer. Doubts about its leader are, if anything, strengthening. The party rages that it has the better arguments on a wealth of issues – but that the Tories are more adept at distorting events to suit their purposes. There is even talk of creating a rapid-rebuttal unit, modelled on the one that helped Bill Clinton to his first win in 1992. • Owen Jones in the Independent writes the speech he thinks Miliband should deliver. • Laura Pitel in the Times (paywall) says Unite is boycotting the Labour gala dinner. Ed Miliband’s biggest trade union backer is to boycott his gala dinner in the latest rebuff to the Labour leader. For the first time in years, Unite will not pay for a table at tonight’s event, where Mr Miliband is guest of honour. Nor is it holding its usual conference party. A source at the union said that Unite was taking a low-key approach in Brighton, adding: “We’re not sulking, just watching.” It is partly a protest at the growing corporatisation of the conference, which has become an important money-spinner for Labour. The symbolism is clear, however, with tension between Mr Miliband and Len McCluskey, the Unite General Secretary, over the Falkirk row. • Oliver Wright in the Independent explains how party conferences have become glorified trade fairs. Electoral Commission records show why, exposing just how lucrative this source of income has becomes for our political parties. In their most recent accounts the Conservatives reveal that they raised just £747,000 in membership fees last year – but £4.2m from conferences. Labour raked in £3.37m from so called “commercial activities”, including conferences, while even the Liberal Democrats raised £1.4m from events. One senior party official admitted that what was once a forum to debate and decide party policy has now become little more than a “glorified trade fair” with almost as many lobbyists as delegates. The truth is that while big donations to political parties have been cleaned up and made more transparent, the same cannot be said for the money made at party conferences. These companies and lobbyists do not pay for passes or fund events out of the goodness of their hearts: they are buying access and the possibility of influence. In the conference, in the living standards debate, a delegate has just given a speech in her dressing gown. She was talking about a protest against the "bedroom tax" involving people wearing their dressing gowns. David Sparks, leader of the Labour group on the Local Government Association and leader of Dudley council, spoke in the conference hall earlier. He said it was only a matter of time before some councils in the UK went bust. I've served 37 years [in local government] ... I can honestly say that the situation that faces local government now has never been more precarious in all that time. Under this government and under this secretary of state, if things carry on as they are, a conservative estimate, with a small c, will show that local councils have had a reduction, on average, overall, over the last four years, of 42% in their grants. The financial black hole facing local authorities is widening each year at the astronomical rate of £2.1bn. It is only a matter of time in this country before some councils cease to be viable. And if anyone thinks that's impossible, just look across the Atlantic to Detroit and see what can happen when a large council runs out of money. One section of Damian McBride’s memoir Power Trip confirms that “Gordon considered the Queen more important than [Rupert] Murdoch” – which is reassuring, sort of, writes Paul Owen. When a Treasury civil servant accidentally sent a “daft joke about the Chinese” to the entire Treasury press list, Brown was worried about “relations with the Chinese – not least Rupert Murdoch’s then-wife Wendi Deng” and felt “the Treasury should show no mercy”. But after McBride reminded him that this civil servant wrote Brown’s annual note on horse-racing for his audience with the Queen, Brown’s position shifted, “definitive proof that Gordon considered the Queen more important than Murdoch”. Murdoch gets one other mention: in the wake of the 7/7 attacks in London, “there were concerns at the highest level in the company – ie Uncle Rupert – about whether Gordon sufficiently grasped the seriousness of the security situation, and whether he could be trusted to maintain the strength of Blair’s relationship with the Bush administration”. Partly in response to this, Brown became “a whirlwind of activity” after the failed 21/7 attacks two weeks later, McBride writes. Damian McBride's publisher, Iain Dale, has been involved in a scuffle at the Labour conference. The Press Association has the full (and rather absurd) story. Verbal battles over Damian McBride's memoirs at the Labour conference escalated into actual fisticuffs today, as the ex-spin doctor's publisher stepped in to stop a protester disrupting the publicity drive for his controversial memoirs. Iain Dale, of Biteback Publishing, was involved in a scuffle with the man on the Brighton seafront in an effort to stop him appearing on screen behind McBride during a series of media interviews. The protester - a familiar face outside party conferences, where he regularly appears with banners opposing smoking or nuclear energy - managed to get himself into shot as McBride spoke to ITV1's Daybreak. But Dale decided to take action, grabbing the man's rucksack and physically hauling him out of the way of the cameras, and the pair grappled on the pavement as Mr McBride's interview continued. The barking of the protester's dog - which eagerly joined in the commotion - could be heard by TV viewers as the struggle continued. But the terrier - carrying placards reading "No Nukes" on its back - failed to live up to the loyalty expected from dogs, jumping up and biting its owner on the rear. The protester doggedly attempted to make the best of the situation, holding up his banner reading "No Nukes - Radio Active Dust Cancer Epidemic" to photographers who were busy recording the scrap. After a few moments, the pair separated and dusted themselves down, and the protester went back to trying to edge his way into view of the cameras. Writing on his blog, Dale - also a broadcaster on LBC 97.3 radio - joked: "I knew I shouldn't have had three Weetabix this morning." Dale has written more on his blog. Labour used to advocate a temporary cut in VAT as a means of stimulating the economy. But Ed Balls did not mention that in his speech yesterday, and at a fringe meeting last night he confirmed that the idea had been dropped. This wasn't a surprise. In a speech earlier this year he said that Labour proposed the idea when the economy was in recession and that, as the economy recovered, the balance of advantage would shift, and the case for other measures would grow stronger. The Lib Dems have claimed that this is "rank hypocrisy". This is from Stephen Williams, a Lib Dem Treasury spokesman. Labour have spent the last three years aggressively attacking the Coalition for raising VAT. Now they admit they won’t reduce it. This is rank hypocrisy from a party that has no answers to the big questions of the day – how to fix the economy, create jobs and help people with the cost of living. Paul Owen is reading Damian McBride’s memoir Power Trip, and he has found an anecdote about David Cameron that rather plays into his “chillaxing” image as a keen holidaymaker. McBride paints Cameron as rather callow after both Brown and Cameron had had to cancel their holidays because of the 2007 foot and mouth outbreak. During a phone call “Cameron asked Gordon almost pleadingly when he thought things would stabilise sufficiently so Gordon could go back to Dorset and Cameron could go back to Brittany. His exact words were: ‘I can’t go away until you do, and we won’t get a holiday at this rate. But you really have to go away first.’” Brown apparently responded to this call by telling McBride: “That was personal, that was private. You don’t say a word about that to anyone,” which was very saintly of him. Cameron betrayed this trust by giving a series of TV interviews criticising Brown based on information from the phone call, McBride claims. McBride says that from the moment Cameron emerged from the 2005 Tory leadership contest, he and Brown were worried. “If they [Cameron and George Osborne] were serious about modernising the Tory party; if they could succeed in neutralising the economy and public services as dividing line issues by agreeing with us on our tax and spending plans; and if – as was evident – they had the image and freshness to go with it, then they represented a real threat to Gordon’s chances of succeeding Tony, and then of winning any subsequent election.” Interestingly McBride says he rejected “the main Labour attack lines” of focusing on Cameron and Osborne’s poshness, preferring to try to paint them as “incompetent and weak”, and as “kids” who weren’t up to the job. That approach had a double value: they were “at the same time sending a not-so-subtle message to those on our own side who were giving David Miliband the glad eye”. (That approach eventually culminated in Brown’s “no time for a novice” jibe at both Cameron and Miliband.) But he praises Cameron for his response to the expenses scandal, saying the Tory leader “seemed remarkably well-prepared for the emergence of the story, and calm and decisive in his response”. Brown, by contrast, was “not only taken completely by surprise by the revelations, but also thrown into a total tailspin” by the Daily Telegraph’s decision to focus on him in its initial coverage. He attacks Cameron’s decision to block Brown as head of the IMF once he’d left Downing Street on interesting grounds, calling it not just partisan but “also tremendously short-sighted”. Why? Because “having Gordon run the IMF would have effectively neutralised the organisation’s influence on the 2015 election. Any criticism in the IMF’s reports on the UK economy could simply have been dismissed by Osborne with a ‘What do you expect?’ shrug.” The Federation of Small Businesses also likes the Labour business tax proposal. Here's the news release (pdf). And this is from John Allan, the federation's national chairman. The Federation of Small Businesses (FSB) welcomes the focus by the Labour leadership on this critical issue for small businesses. It affects thousands of our members across the UK and is an issue we've lobbied on for some time. Whilst we welcome today's announcement, the long-term solution is one that requires root and branch reform, including of the Valuation Office Agency. The system is clearly no longer fit for purpose. We know that many firms are struggling to pay business rates and FSB research suggests seven per cent of members pay more in rates than rent - and that cannot be right. In addition many small businesses are being forced by local authorities to pay the rates when it is beyond their means. But the Association of Convenience Stores likes Labour's business tax plans. This is from James Lowman, the ACS chief executive. Reducing business rates for smaller shops on high streets and in neighbourhoods would benefit tens of thousands of convenience stores. We will work with opposition and the Government to make proposals for tackling the rates burden workable. Immediate action is vital, but it must be accompanied by a commitment to a fundamental review of the business rates system. Paul Owen has got his copy of former Gordon Brown adviser Damian McBride’s memoir Power Trip, and he is busy speed-reading his way through it. Strangely the Daily Mail’s serialisation did not include McBride’s comments about that paper and its editor Paul Dacre. Indeed a Mail piece on Brown and the royal family misses out a few lines about McBride feeding a splash to the Mail about Brown wanting to fund a statue of the Queen Mother for the Mall. But disappointingly what McBride has to say about Dacre is quite tame. McBride compares Brown’s “very strong” relationship with Dacre with his relationship with Tony Blair – “bar all the shouting” – in that “because all their meetings took place behind closed doors … no one was ever quite sure how they interacted with each other in private, or how open, warm or personal they were with each other.” McBride says he never heard of any Mail journalist being instructed to “go soft” on Brown because of this relationship, and “Gordon never told me we that we were to put any particular scoops in the Mail’s direction”. He tells the story of Brown being invited into the Mail offices to tell its senior journalists about his ideas about Britishness, and McBride “couldn’t help but admire the way that Paul Dacre rocked back in his chair and let” his columnists attack Brown’s ideas, “as if thinking: ‘You want intellectual debate; here it is. You want to know what the Daily Mail thinks; we’re going to tell you.’” McBride does say Dacre “effectively scuppered one of my greatest plans”: a plan to cut the 30-year rule for releasing government papers to 15 years, and then rush out papers for all the years up to 1995 so that if Labour won the 2010 election promising to be “the only government ever to publish the secrets of our own time in office while still in power”. Brown asked Dacre to lead a review into the subject, but the eventual plan the Daily Mail chief came up with was slower and exempted the royal family, “which defeated some of the long-term news value”. And now the Institute of Directors has also criticised Labour's business tax announcement. This is from Simon Walker, the IoD's director general. This is better politics than it is economics, and it creates a false distinction between small and larger businesses when in reality, all companies benefit from a more competitive environment. Whilst the IoD welcomes any commitment to reduce the burden of business rates, Labour’s plan to finance this by abolishing the proposed cut in the main corporation tax rate is not a price worth paying. The main corporation tax rate is paid not only by multinational corporations and FTSE100 companies but by medium sized companies and smaller firms. The government has spent three years telling the world that we are open for business, and reductions in corporation tax have been a key part of that strategy. It’s a dangerous move for Labour to risk our business-friendly environment in this way, at the same time as announcing a new bank levy. Globally, corporation tax rates are falling and it is imperative that the UK has an attractive ‘headline’ corporation tax rate low enough to attract much needed foreign direct investment into the UK. This will reduce unemployment, increase economic growth and, as a consequence, increase tax revenues. Labour is going to eliminate the debt by June 2016. That's the party's debt, not the country's. Diana Holland, the party treasurer, is presenting a report to the conference now, and she has just said the party is scheduled to pay off its debt within three years. Another business leader has criticised the business tax announcement in Ed Miliband's speech. This is from John Longworth, the director general of the British Chambers of Commerce. Labour must realise that you can't rob Peter to pay Paul. Business rates are an iniquitous tax and a drag on business – hitting companies of all sizes long before they make profits. Ed Miliband is right to look for ways to ease the business rates burden, as the BCC has urged the Treasury to do for years now. Yet we question why a freeze or cut in business rates for smaller firms should be offset by a delayed reduction in corporation tax. To create an environment where companies can thrive, both business rates and corporation tax rates have to be contained, and the broken business rates system fundamentally reformed. The notion that you can offset cuts in one tax with changes to another doesn't deal with the real problem – the fact that the business rates system is bust and in need of reform. Ultimately, companies of all sizes need to be clear on taxes and rates bills, so that they can generate jobs and wealth with certainty. Sir John Armitt, who chaired the Olympic Delivery Authority and who conducted a review of long-term infrastructure planning for Labour, told the Today programme that he would be disappointed if Labour cancelled HS2. I would be very disappointed if that was happening in this case. I think we’ve had political consensus around HS2. It’s a very large project. It requires a political consensus frankly to be delivered at the end of the day and I think the value of HS2 to the country long-term is still absolutely certain and I think is understood by senior politicians in all the major parties. However, he also said that he did not think that what Ed Balls said about HS2 in his speech yesterday indicated that Labour was planning to scrap the scheme. I've taken the quote from PoliticsHome. For the record, here are today's YouGov GB polling figures. Labour: 40% (up 3 points from YouGov in the Sunday Times) Conservatives: 32% (down 1) Ukip: 12% (no change) Lib Dems: 10% (down 1) Labour lead: 8 points (up 4) Government approval: -27 (no change) According to Electoral Calculus, these figures would give Labour a majority of 100. If the Labour party thought that announcing a tax cut for small business would win the approval of the business community, it's in for a disappointment. John Cridland, the director general of the CBI, has just been on the Today programme, and he said he was not impressed. He complained that the cut in business rates for small firms was being funded by an increase in corporation tax for large firms (or, more accurately, the reversal of a planned cut). This sent out the wrong message, he said. Some of the tax changes that we hear that Mr Miliband will propose are not particularly pro-business. Reducing business rates for small companies is a good thing to do, but I would not do it by increasing taxes on investment, on research, on job creation, which is what will happen if you put up corporation tax ... I just think it's divisive to take from one part of the business community to give to another. The key point, though, is what it says about the Labour party's pro-enterprise credentials. It is giving a signal that taxes are going up for some businesses. Actually, we are fighting for our share of global markets. We are getting back to the notion that we earn our corn around the world by getting on planes to far-flung countries to export. Whether you are small, medium or large, you need to invest as a business and grow as a business, and higher taxes don't do that. Overnight the Conservative business minister, Matthew Hancock, put out a response to the stories about what Ed Miliband would be saying in his speech. Here's his statement. This is yet more economic incompetence from the same Labour politicians who got us into a mess. Ed Miliband is calling for a tax rise on business that would cost jobs. And because he would reverse our planning reforms, the only way Ed Miliband could deliver his promise to build more houses is to spend more and borrow more. Tax rises on businesses and the same old Labour policy of more borrowing and more debt would undermine the recovery. And it's hardworking people who would pay the price with their jobs, higher taxes and higher mortgage rates. Harriet Harman, Labour's deputy leader, has been giving interviews this morning. Here are the key points. I've taken the quotes from PoliticsHome and the Press Association. • Harman said Labour had been making good progress since Ed Miliband became leader. If you look at the actual polls where people are voting, since 2010, since we lost the general election, actually the momentum with Ed Miliband’s leadership has been with the Labour party. We have won 1,950 new councils seat and that’s all around the country, and if you look at actual party membership, which has been on a long trajectory of falling across all the political parties, since Ed Miliband became leader we’ve increased our membership by 14%, whereas with David Cameron his party membership has fallen by 40%. • She said Labour was announcing plans to help small firms because they were the "engine of the economy". It's a bit of a cliche but it's absolutely true that small business is the engine of the economy. And they have really struggled after the austerity and the credit crisis and they haven't been able to borrow money from banks, and people haven't had enough money in their pockets to keep small businesses going. A lot of small businesses say they are paying as much in their business rates as they pay in rent. They have suffered really more than big businesses and one of the problems with the cost of living for businesses is that business rates have gone up. So what we're doing is, instead of giving a cut for big companies on corporation tax, we're using that money instead to take that money off the cost of small businesses rates. • She said that Damian McBride could not be believed because he was a self-confessed liar. The one person who is not worth believing because he’s admitted he’s a liar is Damian McBride, so I don’t think we need to be told by him who did or didn’t know what’s going on. According to Nick Robinson on the BBC, delegates at the Labour conference are going to be given a "One Nation Economy" book at the end of Ed Miliband's speech summarising the party's growth policies. This week (as the end of Ed Balls' speech yesterday showed) Labour is very keen refute the claim that it does not have any ideas. Leaders of modern political parties need a suite of skills - being able to communicate well on TV, leadership, team management, authority at PMQs etc - and being able to deliver a powerful, set-piece speech is probably no longer an essential part of the job requirement. Very few people actually watch these speeches in full. But a powerful speech can still shape the political debate for months, or even longer, and, for all the sniping about Ed Miliband's leadership, conference speech-making has been one of his strengths. He served his political apprenticeship under Gordon Brown (a great believer in the importance of politics by speech-making) and his last two Labour conferences speeches have been unusually good. Most party conference speeches are quickly forgotten, but Miliband's 2011 speech, which included an attack on "irresponsible" capitalism, generated a real debate, and last year's speech launched "one nation Labour", a branding exercise that lifted party morale. Miliband is speaking this afternoon. Can he pull off another biggie? We've had some briefing on the speech already. Here's the start of Patrick Wintour's story in today's Guardian. Ed Miliband will propose on Tuesday to reform Britain's dysfunctional housing market by building 200,000 new houses a year by 2020, the end of the next parliament. The Labour leader's move is a response to a collapse in UK housebuilding, which has stoked house-price inflation, and will see him set up a commission designed to curtail the planning logjams preventing houses being built in sufficient numbers. Miliband will use his set-piece address to the Labour conference in Brighton to announce that Sir Michael Lyons, the respected local government finance expert and former BBC Trust chairman, will lead a review designed to ensure that an incoming government he leads will be able to swiftly draw up housing reform legislation. The Labour leader also intends to use his speech to ask voters to recognise that Britain can be better – and to ask themselves in 2015 whether they are better off than five years ago, an echo of the famous question asked by the Republican Ronald Reagan in the 1990 presidential election. I'll be covering the speech in detail, and, of course, all the reaction to it. 9.30am: Conference opens. Conference arrangements committee report. 9.40am: Diana Holland presents the party treasurer's report. 10.15am: Debate on stronger, safer communities, and living standards and sustainability, with speeches from Hilary Benn, the shadow communities secretary, Maria Eagle, the shadow transport secretary and Caroline Flint, the shadow energy secretary. 11.15am: Debate on Britain's global role. From 12.30pm: Fringe meetings, including Hilary Benn on Labour and the north, Arnie Graf, who is advising Labour on community organising, on winning votes, Maria Eagle and Ken Livingstone on transport, and Lord Glasman on Britishness. 2pm: Ed Miliband delivers his conference speech. I'll be covering all the news from the Labour conference, including looking at the papers and bringing you the best comment from the web. I'll post a summary at 1pm and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I'm on @AndrewSparrow

headline: Politics Weekly podcast: coalition Britain - a great place to live?
trail_text: Heather Stewart, Andrew Gimson and Toby Helm join Tom Clark to discuss an OECD report which ranked Britain as among the world's best places to live and work. Plus: Ed Miliband's living wage pledge; and Lynton Crosby gears up for a no-nonsense Tory election campaign
body_text: Ed Miliband this week set out his latest policy to combat what he calls a 'cost of living crisis' - tax rebates for companies that choose to pay workers the living wage. It was siezed upon by his critics as a meddlesome measure that could complicate the tax system and contribute to higher unemployment. Joining Tom Clark in the studio this week are the Observer's political editor Toby Helm, its economics editor Heather Stewart and the contributing editor of ConservativeHome Andrew Gimson. Also this week: a new report into wellbeing by the OECD found that the UK is among the best places in the world to live and work. Meanwhile, the Office for National Statistics predicted that Britain's population is set to grow by nearly 10 million by 2037. Plus: as the Conservatives hire the Australian election guru Lynton Crosby on a full-time contract worth £500,000 per year, we discuss how a no-nonsense approach to winning in 2015 will compare with 2010's "big society" campaign. Leave your thoughts below.

headline: Politics live: readers' edition - Wednesday 28 August
trail_text: Share breaking news, leave links to interesting articles online and chat about the day's political events in our open thread
body_text: I'm not writing my usual Politics Live blog today, but, as an alternative, here's Politics Live: readers' edition. It's intended to be a place where you can catch up with the latest news and find links to good politics blogs and articles on the web. Please feel free to use this as somewhere you can comment on any of the day's political stories - just as you do when I'm writing the daily blog. It would be particularly useful for readers to flag up new material in the comments - breaking news or blogposts or tweets that are worth passing on because someone is going to find them interesting. A lot of what I do on my blog is aggregation - finding the good stuff and passing it on - and you can do this, too (as I know, because it happens every day when I'm blogging). All of today's Guardian politics stories are here, and all the politics stories filed on Thursday including some in today's paper, are here.

headline: Politics as it happened - Kevin Rudd says yes to Gonski - politics live blog
trail_text: The new Prime Minister moves forward as the 43rd parliament completes its business
body_text: As the senators run for their planes and the departing ministers and staff back up their boxes downstairs, let's part ways for now. Today in politics: Kevin Rudd reconfirmed his support for the policy formerly known as the Gonski reforms and extended the deadline for the premiers to sign up by a fortnight. He promised his colleagues he would consult more, and things would be different than they were last time he was resident in The Lodge. Rudd set down some political markers on boat arrivals and reserved his right to change some of the Gillard Government's policies. He indicated his reshuffle ministry would be confirmed on Sunday or Monday. Tony Abbott gave his troops a pep talk and said he would not allow Kevin Rudd to whitewash his record. The senate adjourned - so as far as we know right at this moment, that's the 43rd parliament. Thanks for the strong support and engagement from readers for the live blog, particularly this week. You are gems, and I'm grateful. Be safe until we gather again for more conversation. If you want to look back over the day's business in the red place you can check the "red" here. There it is. The Senate stands adjourned. The 457 visa bill is now law. Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor. Most Australians would expect that employers look local first before hiring workers from overseas, and these legislative changes will ensure that occurs where it’s not already happening. On the new requirements for employers to test the market before looking to foreign workers: Market testing will be straight forward, requiring employers only to place a job advertisement and show evidence they have done so. Employers facing genuine skill shortages already take steps to ensure there are no locals available to fill these positions. Labour market testing will also be extended to those cases where there was a redundancy or retrenchment and will have to be undertaken across all skill shortage areas, with the exception of nursing and engineering, and in other areas specified by the Minister. By ensuring employers provide proof that they are all looking locally first, they will be placed on a level-playing field, ensuring that one employer is not getting an unfair advantage over another. Couple of readers, couple of contrasting thoughts. Interested in thoughts from readers on Kevin Rudd's first press conference. My quick thoughts: There was an internal message - I've changed, it won't be like last time, I will restore Cabinet government, I won't go off half-cocked. This of course relates to Rudd's previous time as prime minister when he did not consult, and went off half cocked, as many of his colleagues have said. The was a message to Tony Abbott - I will try and disrupt your conversation with the voters. The konfrontasi comment concerning the Coalition's policy to tow back the boats upped the political ante. The barometer of that was Julie Bishop's rapid reponse, and demand for a retraction. Rudd was also (in effect) trying to slow down the media cycle, there was a specific plea for time. If you do need to consult your colleagues, to think through the ramifications of your public statements, then you do need to set some parameters in your conversation with journalists - like I won't be able to give you all every little detail in five minutes. (I'd say to the Prime Minster - good luck with that one.) My colleague Lenore Taylor has been thinking about the carbon price and Kevin Rudd's options. Tweaking the clean energy package is actually much harder to do than it seems (in terms of the practical and legal implications.) Then there's the political impact of the decision. History tells us that's not straightforward. At one level it seems simple - the policy isn't popular, so jettison the policy. Except of course Rudd has been down that road before, and it didn't end well for him. The Prime Minister was making it clear in his press conference before that he would be consulting his colleagues before making any changes. Here's an excerpt from Lenore: Dumping the carbon tax – the fixed price period of Australia's carbon pricing scheme – makes for a handy headline. But it would be much harder for a Rudd government to actually do. That's why the Gillard government didn't do it some time back when cabinet examined this option in detail. To start with, it would require legislation, which can't happen before the election since the parliament has risen and which the Greens leader, Senator Christine Milne – alive for an opportunity for pre-election product differentiation – was quick to say she would not support. That would leave a Rudd government, if re-elected, seeking support from the Coalition to change the carbon pricing legislation Tony Abbott has built his entire political persona on promising to repeal. Also, the current legislation requires that, before the shift from a fixed price to a floating price can occur as planned in July 2015, the new Climate Change Authority must set an annual cap on pollution based on its assessment of what Australia's emissions reduction target should be. The authority releases a draft report in October and a final report in February 2014 before announcing the first five years of annual caps in May. That means if a Rudd government really hurried, the earliest it could feasibly start a floating price under the current arrangements would be July 2014 – one year before the price is set to float anyway. Not happy Julie. I think it's fair to say that the konfrontasi that is Australian politics has just entered a new phase. Today - Kevin Rudd and Julie Bishop. Cold. Yesterday. The warmth. We'll take this as a comment from NSW Labor secretary Sam Dastyari. Shadow foreign minister Julie Bishop has trotted up to the Sky News studios to be outraged about konfrontasi. Bishop: A massive over reach on Kevin Rudd's part. He must retract this absurd and alarmist claim. This is a major gaffe. As The Australian's James Jeffrey points out, Rudd did not say I've got to zip. He just zipped. Rudd says he will not tolerate anyone trashing Julia Gillard’s reputation. He says he's been on the wrong side of reputation trashing, and it's not pleasant. I don't think the public like it either. He's zipped. Do none of you have daggy dads? Kevin Rudd, on his tendency to say things such as cooking with gas. Rudd consults his communications director and then the room before working out whether he'll take more questions. He resolves to go on for another five minutes. Rudd warns that Tony Abbott will plunge the country into recession if he goes ahead with a major austerity drive. Laura Tingle from the Financial Review asks whether any looming policy change will be revenue neutral? That has generally been my conservative approach, but I add the caveat, I have not yet been briefed by Treasury officals. Did you leak against Julia Gillard during the 2010 election? (Nope) What will you do on gay marriage? Rudd says his first preference is to convince Tony Abbott to allow his MPs a conscience vote. If that doesn't work, then he'll have to look at other mechanisms including the recourse to plebiscite or referendum. The Australians's David Crowe asks Rudd will he offer the premiers more money to sign on to the policy formerly known as Gonski. David I'm just a reasonable guy. But the parameters for variation are very very narrow. Shadow foreign minister Julie Bishop on konfrontasi. Have you really changed? Rudd is emphasising he will consult colleagues. (Over.) I will consult my colleagues. (Unless he has to act on the spot, then he won't. But mainly he will.) Will you save Trish Crossin - the Northern Territory senator who Julia Gillard moved on? Rudd says he hasn't considered any specific preselections. What is your attitude to women and the party? I have a good record on this, Rudd says. Konfrontasi Rudd is asked whether he's foreshadowing conflict with Indonesia. Serious conflict. Over Tony Abbott's plan to tow back the boats. He invokes konfrontasi. (Indonesia's armed opposition to the creation of Malaysia.) Rudd says he hopes it doesn't come to that. He's just pointing out that the Indonesians have said Abbott's tow back policy is not something they are interested in. I think of farms, I think of animals. Kevin Rudd is responding to a question from Guardian Australia's political editor Lenore Taylor about his new approach to regionalism. A picture from Mike Bowers crying out for a caption. Do feel free. I will be speaking with my Cabinet colleagues on that. This is adjusting the clean energy package. Rudd says the government needs to consider the budget implications of any change to the carbon price. He's asked does he accept the Budget parameters and the proposed welfare cut for single Mums. I'll get back to you is his answer essentially. Then party reform? Do you support it? I'll get back to you. But: I am revolted by what happened in New South Wales. Rudd says he'll be having a deep discussion with Cabinet colleagues about future directions for NSW. Now for questions. Boats. Are you going to Indonesia? Will you stop the boats? We in this country support orderly migration, Rudd says. He says he's going to be briefed this afternoon about the Indonesia trip, and will speak to the Indonesian President after this press conference. He says he'll announce his decision about whether to go or not once he's made it. Rudd says if he goes, he will seek a broader discussion than the one envisaged by Julia Gillard. Now he'd like to talk about Gonski. He knows David Gonski, in fact he's the chairman of the board of his wife's company. (Full disclosure Rudd says - before you people all go off and write your exposes.) Rudd suggests David Gonski doesn't want the Gonski reforms to be called the Gonski reforms anymore. Rudd's announcement appears to be that he supports the Gonski policy (or perhaps the policy formerly known as Gonski). Under my prime ministership we will support the national improvement plan for Australian schools. Under my prime ministership this policy direction will continue. Rudd is extending the deadline for the states to sign on by two weeks. Then more on the new politics. I think it's time we all came together. Rudd says he wants to make policy changes and Cabinet will consider those in due course. He says his feedback from voters was people were thinking about voting for Tony Abbott because they felt they lacked a clear choice. Rudd says his offering them a choice. He's speaking now about decision-making. Rudd says one thing he's learned from his previous stint as prime minister is colleagues need to be consulted. He says he will consult colleagues, so the media needs to calm down. Policy announcements will come, but they may not come quickly. Just chill for a while, in fact chill for quite a bit. Rudd says he'd like to talk about why he sought the leadership. The government was on track for a catastrophic defeat at the upcoming elections. He says there would have been a massive majority for Tony Abbott in the House and a majority in the senate. If that happened, Rudd contends, Labor's policy legacy would have disappeared. Some of these reforms, good reforms, have been Julia's. Some of the reforms date back to the period where I was prime minister after 2007. The first shock of Kevin Rudd's press conference is he's on time. Rudd says discussions about his new ministry remain ongoing. The new ministry will be sworn in on Monday. He says he's getting briefings from officials. We've had a few things on. Nine has posted some video of an interview Tim Mathieson did this morning outside The Lodge while out walking the dog. How is the Prime Minister, Nine cameraman Mark Jessop asked the former first bloke? She's really good, a little sad. We are all sad. Mathieson noted it had been an amazing few years for me. Guardian Australia's data guru Nick Evershed has been crunching the numbers, as he does, and has produced one measure of effectiveness in government. I'll let him explain: How do we measure the effectiveness of a government? There are polls, both of opinion and at the ballot box, but these don't really offer us any measure of effectiveness. You can look at the economy and measure the health of the populace - and these are both good indicators - but are not wholly under the influence of the government of the day. One way might be to look at the ability of a government to pass legislation. Admittedly this is a quantity over quality approach, but it does offer us a quantitative measure of a government, political party or prime minister. Someone that gets a lot of legislation passed might be considered to be good at getting things done. I took all of the Commonwealth of Australia Numbered Acts and assigned them to a prime minister, political party, and parliament based on the date of assent of the act. This isn't entirely exact, as some legislation may be introduced under one PM and passed under another, though I believe it is a good proxy. From this dataset, I counted the total acts for each PM, party, and parliament. Then, I determined the number of days in office for each PM, and the number of days each parliament and party governed. Using these figures you can calculate a rate of acts per day, which accounts for different lengths of prime ministers' or governments' terms. The results? Julia Gillard had the highest rate of passing legislation with a rate of 0.495, followed by Bob Hawke at 0.491: If you want to read Nick's blog in full, have a look here. Childcare Minister Kate Ellis. Resolute in her voting intention. The ABC's Annabel Crabb has written a column today on Julia Gillard and gender - was it a factor in her undoing - and in terms of progress, what does this all mean. Here's a taste: To attribute the demise of Julia Gillard to sexism does exactly the reverse of honouring her role as the first female prime minister of Australia. To disallow the possibility that she might have lost her job because a majority of the people who work most closely with her just didn't think she was good enough at it is to consign Julia Gillard permanently to an air-locked specimen-jar in the Ponds Institute For Gender Research. It's unhelpful. To her and to us. Julia Gillard was our first female prime minister. She was also a prime minister who lost her job. In the field of former prime ministers, that doesn't make her unique. Hell, on recent form it doesn't even make her unusual. I've just asked readers on Twitter to give me some suggestions about Kevin Rudd's fantasy ministry. Karen Lyon - quick as a flash. The path of regime change - it never runs smooth. We know why Kevin Rudd wants to talk about his future, because he can't talk about his record. Abbott riffs a bit more on leadership instability. Campaign pitch for the coda - vote one Coalition. The colleagues spring to their feet with appluase. Note to readers while we continue listening to Abbott. The guidance on Rudd's 12.30pm press conference has now shifted slightly - it may be a policy announcement rather than the ministry. In any case, Kevin Rudd will address the media at 12.30pm. Now, back to Abbott. I'm not being negative, I'm being factual when I say Kevin Rudd is the best friend people smugglers have ever had. And a newsflash. Newsflash Kevin. It is not all about you. I want to see cranes over our cities. I want to see bulldozers on the ground. Tony Abbott: solidly pro-bulldozer. Abbott pays tribute to his communications spokesman Malcolm Turnbull - a man who practically invented the internet in Australia. Abbott says the election is a contest of ideas and values. It's Labor's big government versus the Coalition, which is in favour of strong citizens. Opposition leader Tony Abbott is taking his turn now at the lectern. For the second time in three years, Labor has torn down a leader. An extraodinarily grave rent in the fabric of our national life. The Australian people are seeking certainty and stability, Abbott says, and the only place they will find it is with the Coalition. Julie Bishop on Kevin Rudd. Leopards do not change their spots. Only a Coalition can deliver a grown up government. we demonstrate our capacity to work cooperatively together. Truss is throwing now to the deputy Liberal Leader Julie Bishop. There's a civil war going on inside Labor and it will not end, there is no end in sight. Will Kevin Rudd be the second Labor leader to say there will be no carbon tax under the government I lead? Warren Truss, in continuation. Hurling the zingers. Well .. another campaign. Nationals leader Warren Truss is now addressing a special Coalition party room meeting in Canberra today. Yes sir. Another campaign. That much is certain. Meanwhile .. more of the law making. The red place is just belting through today. Interesting thought isn't it? If Kevin Rudd does dismantle elements of the clean energy package - it could boost the Greens politically with progressive voters. It would give the Greens some sharpness around product differentiation between themselves and Labor. It's now official. Kevin Rudd will announce his ministry at 12.30pm. Greens leader Christine Milne is looking very chirpy this morning. Milne is listing the Greens' achievements in this minority parliament. She says she hopes voters understand how the Greens cooperation with the Gillard Labor government delivered all sorts of things - the clean energy package, Gonski, the Parliamentary Budget Office. Of course there is a but. I really warn the Labor Party against going down the track of creating uncertainty around carbon pricing. We have an exemplary package in the clean energy package. Milne is referring to Rudd's billboard sized hints that he's moving to a floating carbon price rather than the fixed price. She points out that Labor's record on raising revenue is rather poor. If you move to flexible pricing earlier there would be a huge hit on the budget. A political strategy from Rudd won't save the planet, she says, clearly derisive about Rudd's big pitch to the big polluters over the past 24 hours. Our later start today - yes I did need one more coffee, it's true - means I haven't done my normal summary of the political news. I'm assuming readers are up to speed by now, more or less, on Rudd's first day back in the big chair. But there are some terrific pieces being written about the meaning of recent events. One I'd particularly recommend is one by one of my favourite columnists, Shaun Carney. Here's an excerpt of his essay for The Conversation, the political tragedy of Julia Gillard. The truth is that the Australian Labor Party nationally has in the past three years experienced its most rancorous divisions since the split of the 1950s. Unlike the period of the split, which occurred in opposition and guaranteed many more years of it, the party has endured these divisions while holding office, and the enmities have, for the most part, grown from ego rather than ideology. If the events of the past few days are to have any meaning, they need to be seen in the context of what has happened to the Government since early 2010. First things first: Julia Gillard’s downfall as prime minister is one of the greatest personal tragedies in Australian politics. It is a tragedy because Gillard’s ambition ultimately exceeded her political talent, and to the very end she would not see it. Her speech delivered after her caucus defeat on Wednesday night attributed her removal as leader to only two causes: a loss of fortitude among past supporters who buckled under external pressure and sexism directed at her as the nation’s first female prime minister. Good morning and welcome to the final day of the 43rd parliament. The House of Representatives has already folded its tent and gone home. The Senate grinds on with the last legislative business. I think it's more than likely we'll see the new Prime Minister Kevin Rudd announce his ministry later on today, but there's no official confirmation of that just yet. There's also broad hints that Rudd will seek to adjust Julia Gillard's clean energy package over the coming days.

headline: Iranians preoccupied with politics, United Nations survey shows
trail_text: Preliminary results suggest an emphasis on 'honest government', 'political freedoms' and 'gender equality'
body_text: In 2015, the UN's Millennium Development Goals, the organisation's principal framework for eliminating poverty worldwide, will draw to a close. In the unprecedented global survey to determine what follows, MY World, citizens from all countries are being asked to vote directly – online, by SMS, and by paper ballot – for their priorities in a new development agenda. Both across the world and in the Middle East, socioeconomic concerns predominate. Iranians, however, are stressing political concerns to a greater degree than elsewhere. And moves by the new government suggest this is well understood. Respondents to the survey select six of 16 given development priorities to define a new agenda. This week at the UN general assembly, the survey hosts an event to celebrate its millionth voter – as of this writing, 1.15 million people from 194 countries have participated. Results can be viewed in real time on a dedicated website, with priorities ranked top to bottom in order of popularity. Data can be disaggregated by sex, age, education, country, region, and UN Human Development Index (HDI) cohort. The priorities range over four broad categories. Seven of the 16 options concern socioeconomic management, including issues such as access to "education", "sanitation" and "personal safety". Three priorities, "transportation", "reliable energy", and "phone/Internet access", deal with infrastructure management. The third category involves environmental management: "protecting the environment", and "dealing with climate change". Questions of political management are represented by four priorities: "honest government", "freedom from discrimination", "gender equality", and "political freedoms". As results stand right now, socioeconomic concerns occupy five out of the top six priorities around the world and in most regions and countries. In Iran, however, socioeconomic concerns account for only four of the top six priorities, with the other two both being issues of political management. Take-up in Iran as of 22 September is relatively small, at 4,201 out of 27,877 for all of western Asia. Sample selection is inevitably an issue, as respondents often come from civil society organisations already working with UN agencies. But the sample continues to grow. The survey is available in English online, and it has also been translated into Farsi for distribution in paper ballot form. Translated ballots account for over half of the Iranian total so far. The majority of these were part of a global drive by UNAIDS, the Joint United Nations Programme on HIV/Aids, to promote the survey, while encouraging participants to identify HIV/Aids as an additional suggested priority in the ballot's "write-in" field. Only 36% of Iranian respondents have more than a secondary education, so this is not simply an exercise for a liberal elite. Men dominate but not dramatically, with about three men responding for every two women. More than half the sample is under 30, but then so is more than half the country. Honest government is a top-six concern across all age groups. "Honest government" – a global favourite, ranking fourth internationally – is the second-most popular response in Iran, and a top-six concern across all age groups. As elsewhere, "healthcare", "education", and "jobs" also feature in the top six. But then so does "gender equality", which ranks only ninth internationally. And this is not a purely female-driven result: while 40% of women voted it a concern, so have 36% of men. At the beginning of September, with a little less than 2,000 votes cast in Iran, the focus on political concerns was even greater, with "honest government" and "political freedoms" as the two top responses. Now, with more than 4,000 votes cast, "political freedoms" has dropped out of the top six. The role of UNAIDS, in collecting about half of the total vote through outreach to individuals more dependent on social provision measures, may go some way to explaining the shift toward social management issues. Both in the survey's defined western Asia region and among Iran's "high" HDI cohort, four priorities continue to dominate: "education", "honest government", "jobs", and "health". Only one issue of political management features in the top six, for both groupings. In high-HDI countries overall, the only other issue of political management in even the top ten is "freedom from discrimination". What about country-level comparisons? Considering countries with broadly similar characteristics – comparable per capita wealth, population size, location, and economic make-up – Iraq, though confronted with its own particular issues, probably comes closest. Turkey is an interesting case: besides obvious political differences, it is something like an Iran without the petroleum. Finally, a broadly similar country from outside the region is Venezuela, oil exporting and of comparable wealth. In all three countries, only one political management issue features in the top six priorities, that of "honest government". In both Iraq and Turkey, the other three political issues all line up just behind the top six – in other words, political questions remain pertinent, just not predominant. In Venezuela, issues of "discrimination" and "political freedom" rank only ninth and tenth. Finally, to look at how the other half lives, we can select three considerably richer countries: the United Arab Emirates, the United Kingdom, and the United States. The UAE has the big four – "education", "jobs", "health", and "honest government" – and the regional concern with personal safety. Also paralleling Iraq and Turkey, political questions are just outside the top six: "discrimination" and "gender equality" are ranked eighth and ninth respectively. In all these comparisons so far, only the US and UK have two political management issues in the top six. A clue to understanding this comes from the other priorities chosen. All three countries list "water" and in the US and UK, "food" as well. These are not countries known for food-security problems. Rather what appears to happen is that in richer countries the survey becomes a hypothetical: "What would I most need were I to wake up in poverty?" But even hypothetically, none of these countries are as concerned with political issues as Iranian respondents are. In addition to questions about the statistical validity of such a small sample, there are questions regarding the priorities themselves. Freedom from discrimination is a broad theme that is open to interpretation. Social norms are also important. Though women in Saudi have fewer rights than elsewhere, gender equality is not a top-six concern even among women there. Another standout result is "support for people who can't work", at number three in Iran, whereas it is only 11th worldwide and 14th among high-HDI nations generally. Regionally, it is just outside the top six in Western Asia; at number six in Iraq, it is well down at 13th in Turkey. How to interpret Iranians' focus on this concern is not self-evident. The Islamic republic prides itself on the way it takes care of injured veterans: is that fuelling unusually high expectations, or does the vote in fact indicate a serious shortcoming? But overall, according to preliminary comparative results, Iranian respondents are thinking politically. Even taking into account the Arab uprisings and recent Turkish unrest, political issues do not emerge in these places in the same way as in Iran. And there is evidence that the administration of President Hassan Rouhani, now in his second month in office, recognises these concerns and is responding to them. The release last week of 11 political prisoners made headlines around the world. While this was seen by many as a signal to the international community, its domestic importance is arguably greater. Related efforts are under way to relax the conditions of house arrest imposed on Green Movement leaders Mir Hossein Mousavi and Mehdi Karroubi. And the president has publicly stated that the (typically hardline) Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps should steer clear of domestic politics. Socioeconomic factors remain significant nonetheless. Iranians are still concerned about good education, quality healthcare, and decent jobs. For all the focus on the ideological shifts represented by Rouhani and his predecessors Mohammad Khatami and Mahmoud Ahmadinejad, their electoral victories rested on responding to socioeconomic concerns as well. This is what Rouhani intends to do in New York, by opening the path to a resolution of the nuclear and, by extension, sanctions issues. While he has a lot of open fronts to deal with, he is at least demonstrating a clear awareness of them.

headline: Clegg accuses Boris Johnson of 'fairly unpleasant elitism': Politics live blog
trail_text: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of all the day's political developments as they happen, including Nick Clegg hosting his Call Clegg LBC phone-in and reaction to the U-turn on plain packaging for cigarettes
body_text: • Tim Farron, the Lib Dem president, has described Boris Johnson's speech defending elitism as representing "the worst of Thatcherism". (See 3.34pm.) Labour MPs also continued to denounce it. • The Fresh Start Project, a group of Tory MPs, has published a seven-page "Mandate for Reform" setting out the changes to the EU that it wants David Cameron to achieve. There are 22 measures on the list. They include member states having control of social and employment law, and the UK having a complete opt out from policing and criminal justice measures. There is a link to the report here. • William Hague, the foreign secretary, has said that a global summit on ending the use of sexual violence in conflict zones will be held in Britain next year. (See 2.26pm.) • Andrew Sells, a venture capitalist and major donor to the Conservative party has been chosen by ministers as the new chair of the statutory wildlife and nature watchdog, Natural England. • Stuart Agnew, a Ukip MEP, has said women often lack the ambition to achieve senior positions because their desire for a baby "gets in the way". As the Press Association reports, Agnew dismissed the impact of gender bias in education and the workplace, insisting the mothering instinct was what really held women back in their careers. If you look at the people who get degrees, more women get them and they are getting the jobs in the workplace, but for various reasons they don't have the ambition to go right to the top because something gets in the way. It's called a baby. I've never had a baby. But I understand if you do have a baby it can change your life - it changes your ambitions. So, the route is there. Those females who really want to get to the top do so. A Ukip spokeswoman said this was not the view of the party as a whole. • Lord Sugar, the Labour peer and star of the BBC's The Apprentice, has said that there are too many "jobsworths" at the corporation. As the Press Association reports, he said in a debate in the Lords that the BBC was "heavily overstaffed" and needed a more commercial approach to its work. "The BBC Trust is a complete and utter waste of time," he told the Lords. "It should be disbanded." And some management posts at the corporation should go. They are heavily overstaffed and there are too many jobsworths - and the organisation is not run in a manner that a commercial organisation should be. That's all from me for today. Thanks for the comments. I won't be blogging tomorrow - we're put up a readers' edition, as usual - but I will be writing a live blog on Sunday morning, covering George Osborne's interview on the Andrew Marr show and all the other news relating to next week's autumn statement. Here's an afternoon reading list. • Andrew Gimson at ConservativeHome says a quarter of the female Tory MPs elected in 2010 could stand down in 2015. Why are so many Tory women finding life at Westminster so unpleasant that they wish to give it up after only one term? In 2010, the number of Conservative women MPs rose from 17 to 49, yet it is possible that over a quarter of the newcomers in this group will seize the first chance to leave. This will be seen as a rebuff to David Cameron, who at the start of his leadership made the recruitment of more women in winnable seats one of his most conspicuous goals. But it will also be a rebuff to Parliament, and perhaps also to the wider public for expecting so much of MPs, including a manic level of attention to events in the constituencies ... Women MPs get a particular kind of unpleasantness directed at them via social media by bullying men who indulge with vicious relish in every kind of obscenity. One woman told me that in her previous, high-level career, she at least felt when she went home she could switch off. She now finds she can never switch off. Nor does she feel that at Westminster she is able to make a meaningful contribution to policy. This is of course a sensation that can afflict male MPs too. But some at least of the women feel that real power is still in the hands of a chumocracy, and that they are excluded from the rooms where the meaningful discussions are taking place. They arrive at Westminster with knowledge of particular fields: knowledge to which no value whatever seems to be attached ... In the intractable debate about MPs’ pay, it seems to me a great pity that so little consideration is given to the idea of having part-time MPs. These could be paid less, in return for passing fewer laws and spending less time on local events and case work which ought to be the preserve of local councillors. It would be understood that part-time MPs might have other careers in order to make ends meet. But it would also be understood that they ought not to be working so hard that they never have time to think. • Daniel Hannan profiles Syed Kamall, the new leader of Conservative MEPs. Syed Kamall MEP is the last person Labour can have wanted to lead the Conservatives into the Euro-election. He ticks pretty much every box: he’s a souverainiste, a tax-cutter and a Thatcherite, but his main interest is in the relief of poverty. He doesn’t just talk about it, either: he has spent years working with charities and entrepreneurs in developing countries and in deprived parts of the UK. That first-hand-experience has convinced him that the way to tackle penury is not through subsidy, but through trade, property rights and education. • Cathy Newman at Channel 4 News says Sajid Javid could be the next Tory leader. • Rafael Behr at the New Statesman says the Conservatives deserve better than David Cameron. The Conservatives are marooned between being the party their leader once wanted them to be and the one they suppose they might have been without him. So Downing Street has settled for unification around a goal all can agree on – trashing Labour. With the help of Lynton Crosby, Cameron’s campaign strategist, the Tories are turning every debate into a warning against the perils of electing Miliband. If there is a problem with the NHS, it is the legacy of Labour negligence. Economic malaise? Labour spent all the money. Immigration? Labour opened the floodgates. The chairman of the Co-operative Bank is alleged to have taken hard drugs? Labour took money from the Co-op so, er, well, never mind the details, there’s something a bit whiffy over there on the left. Hold your nose and turn right ... The Tories would still be unwise to change leader before the election. (Only a tiny cabal of hardline Eurosceptics even consider this an option.) They could still win or, more likely, stay in power in another coalition with the Lib Dems. The economy is growing. Miliband looks ill-prepared for the total war that is coming his way. To be back in No 10 after the next election would be victory enough for Cameron. But to the outside observer, to anyone who is interested in Conservatism as a political creed, to anyone who values debate or who recognises that there are principled MPs on the Tory benches, the party looks diminished by its leader. It is being whittled into a crude instrument for the sole purpose of bludgeoning the opposition. You don’t have to be a Tory to believe it should be more than that. • James Maxwell at Total Politics says the future of the Scottish Labour party looks bleak. Scotland’s political landscape has changed radically over the last decade but Scottish Labour has remained practically inert. Its failure to adapt has come at a high price. Regardless of the outcome of the independence referendum, the SNP is likely to win a third term in office, meaning Labour faces another eight years in total on the opposition benches. Lamont’s role, then, is essentially that of a caretaker-leader. Her job is to stabilise the party for her successor, who – it is hoped – will take charge just as Scottish voters begin to tire of the nationalists. The problem, of course, is that waiting for your opponents to run out of steam does not amount to a strategy for renewal. Scottish Labour’s crisis is deeply-rooted. The party needs to be re-built from the bottom-up. One frustrating irony for Labour is that the devolved parliament it fought so hard to establish has systematically undermined its political dominance. At this rate, it will be a long time before that dominance is recovered, if it is recovered at all. • Dan Hodges at the Telegraph says PMQs should be scrapped. Perhaps it was the sight of Michael Fabricant sitting on the back benches wearing a giant fake moustache. Or the Prime Minister’s joke about Ed Miliband “loving Engels instead”, a reference to Miliband’s Desert Island discs choice of Robbie Williams song “Angels”, which contains the line “I’m loving angels instead”. Or the fact Labour’s leader didn’t have the wit or wisdom to inflict the mercy killing the pun so richly deserved, and instead spent his own time at the Dispatch Box rambling aimlessly from one issue to another. Anyway, whatever it was, as I sat there delivering my instant Twitter verdict – “Appalling joke aside, Cameron skated through this one. Ed's PMQ's strategy seems to be to meander from one issue to another. Weird” – I suddenly thought “what is the point?” Not just “what is the point of me sending this tweet?” which would have been a legitimate thought in itself, but: “what is the point of PMQs?” • Dominic Casciani at the BBC says there is very little evidence that "benefit tourism" attracts migrants to the UK. But what’s the evidence for benefit tourism? The answer is that there is very little - and it is an extremely complex picture. That does not mean that benefit tourism doesn’t exist - but what’s clear is that the evidence points strongly in the direction that people migrate to find work or for family reasons. They are less likely to up sticks to cross borders - or even continents - just for a weekly giro. And here is some more political reaction to the Boris Johnson speech. From Tim Farron, the Lib Dem president From Labour's Jon Trickett From Labour's Kevan Jones Here are some more blogs on the Boris Johnson speech. Chris Dillow's is particularly good. • Chris Dillow on his blog says Johnson's view of inequality is too simplistic. - The urge to keep up with the Joneses doesn't just spur useful work. It might also encourage people to get into debt to spend as much as the rich, and this can (perhaps) lead to over-gearing and a financial crisis. The experience of the 00s might not be the only data-point here. Rising inequality in the 1920s also ended in depression. - Inequality can reduce trust, and distrust can reduce growth. - Inequality might be a symptom of a dysfunctional economic and political system. If we have monopoly or other market failure and/or crony capitalism, we'll see some mega-rich people, but not a thriving economy. - Inequality might itself create dysfunctional politics. If the poor push for redistributive policies which weaken investment incentives, or if the rich use their wealth to buy political favours, growth will suffer. Now, I don't say this to claim that inequality is always and everywhere bad for growth. I do so merely to suggest that Mr J was expressing a very partial point of view, and was simplifying horribly. It's a good job he is merely a minor local politician who isn't in charge of anywhere important. • James Bloodworth at the Spectator says Johnson was right to say that equality is not achievable. Most of the speech (apart from the idea that ‘greed is good’, which I don’t accept) was a straightforward assertion of the blindingly obvious. Some people are cleverer and work harder than others. As a result they do better for themselves. Ability is a result of nature as well as nurture. Many are held back by their circumstances. People who work hard should be rewarded for their hard work. Fairly banal stuff, really. Personally I tend to err a bit more on the side of circumstance than ‘talent’ when trying to explain why some get so much more out of life than others; I’m baffled, however, by the near universal pretence that ‘economic equality’ is in any way an achievable goal. It isn’t. And for all his faults Boris was right to say it isn’t. • John McDermott at the Financial Times says Johnson came "empirically adrift on a sea of his own loquacity". It is not only the claim that Thatcher took on Neil Kinnock in 1983, or some odd statistics about IQ, or the idea that social mobility is declining, or that America is a particularly socially mobile society, or that his claim that the top 0.1 per cent of people pay 14 per cent of “all taxation” refers only to income tax, or the assertion that new overseas-backed developments constitute “hundreds of thousands of new homes” when they make up at best a few tens of thousands. Rather, in a peroration imagining what Thatcher would do today, it is Mr Johnson’s chutzpah to laud grammar schools’ supposed influence on social mobility without mentioning the current renaissance of London schools. (As mayor, Mr Johnson has little control over education but if that were a criterion for his interventions we would all be poorer.) Leaving aside whether grammar schools were indeed catapults of justice, what is happening now in London is a far more relevant and interesting trend. In the Commons earlier William Hague, the foreign secretary, announced that Britain will host a global summit on ending sexual violence in conflict next year. He described it as a "moral cause for our generation", on a par with the abolition of slavery two hundred years ago. It was only when the true horror of slavery came to light in the 18th century that our nation acted against it. In our time we have come to understand the true horror of warzone sexual violence in Bosnia, Rwanda, Colombia, Somalia and many other nations, including Syria. I will never forget meeting young women in a hospital in Goma so damaged by rape that they required surgery; or the woman in a refugee camp there who said they were being “raped like animals”; or male survivors in Sarajevo, who twenty years on still live lives shattered by trauma; or meeting women in refugee camps in Darfur who were raped collecting firewood. What they all had in common was that, unjustly, they bore the stigma and shame and loneliness, while their attackers walked free and unpunished. This is rape used as a tactic or weapon of war; to terrorise, to humiliate and to ethnically cleanse. It destroys lives, fuels conflict, creates refugees, and is often a tragic link in a chain of human rights abuses – from sexual slavery, to forced marriage and human trafficking. Sexual violence affects men and boys as well as women and girls. It undermines reconciliation, and traps survivors in conflict, poverty and insecurity. Preventing it is a moral cause for our generation. David Lammy, the Labour MP and potential candidate for London mayor, told the World at One that Boris Johnson's comments in his speech yesterday were "an insult" to the poor in the city. I don’t think that’s just careless. I think it’s an insult; it’s an insult to cleaners in London, to people who are home carers in London, people who are minimum wage, giving them the suggestion that they are sort of bottom of the cornflake packet. That’s not the sort of society that I thought we wanted to live in ... There is nothing wrong with aspiration, with enterprise and indeed with competition, but not when it’s at the expense of other people and that’s what we saw from the banking crisis. It’s extraordinary for a mayor, that should be for all of London, to think it’s alright to glorify greed, a greed that’s brought a banking collapse and caused misery and hardship to many Londoners, particularly to young people who can’t get on the housing ladder. I've taken the quote from PoliticsHome. • Boris Johnson has been strongly attacked for a speech he gave last night in which he argued that inequality was essential to fostering "the spirit of envy" and hailed greed as a "valuable spur to economic activity". He also suggested that IQ was a measure of worth. (See 9.51am.) Nick Clegg accused him of "unpleasant, careless elitism". And Labour said Johnson's comments were "absolutely disgraceful". (See 10.42am.) • The Office for National Statistics has published figures showing that net migration into the UK has risen year on year for the first time in two years. • Jane Ellison, the public health minister, has said that a review into plain packaging for cigarette advertising - described as a U-turn by Labour - will take the politics out of the issue. • Ministers believe they have found a way of cutting energy bills by as much as £50 in next week's autumn statement by putting one of the green levies onto general taxation and allowing energy companies to take longer to fulfil other energy efficiency duties, it has emerged. • The Bank of England has taken the first steps to ensure a housing bubble does not emerge by scaling back the Funding for Lending Scheme (FLS) to focus on business loans instead of mortgages.There are more details on Robert Peston's blog, and here, in a letter from George Osborne to the Bank of England. • Mark Carney, the governor of the Bank of England, has ducked an opportunity to criticise the SNP's plans for Scottish independence. At a news conference he said the Bank would present its "technocratic, boring" opinion on a range of issues related to Scottish independence at an appropriate time and venue in future. He also declined to say whether he was worried about becoming the lender of last resort to Scottish banks he did not regulate. • The National Audit Office has said that the government is not doing enough to get student loans repaid and that the total value of money owed continues to rise. And here's my colleague Michael White's take on the speech. Here's an extract. What Mayor Boris was trying to tell Big Cornflakes like himself was that London needs them in a globalised economy like the one now being created, but it needs them to behave better. On current evidence – subterranean basements the size of cathedrals being hewn beneath the capital's bigger homes and tax avoided on a massive scale by some corporations and business – the mayor is uttering a forlorn hope. That is what makes his populist appeal – it included a swing at east European migrants and a plug for his doomed, anachronistic estuary airport project – so badly judged. Those lucky enough to belong to the 2% elite - as judged by imperfect IQ tests, of course – are sensible when they lead lives of quiet discretion and taste, engaging in good works too, rather than flaunting their wealth and good fortune. Many did not earn it and many are not actually so clever; they have been lucky in their actions or their birth. The sensible ones - there are many, but they are out of fashion from here to New York and on to Beijing – know this. Successful idiots think it's just because they're so smart. Saps. The rest of us are unimpressed and may resent the mayor's avowedly elitist world view because – as Warren Buffett will confirm – it's not true. Buffett once said he'd given his children enough money to "allow them to do anything, but not to do nothing" with their lives. There speaks real wisdom. Here's the Guardian video with an excerpt from Boris Johnson's speech. Here's some more Twitter comment on Boris Johnson. From George Galloway, the Respect MP (and potential candidate for London mayor) From Ann Widdecombe, the former Conservative minister From Billy Bragg, the singer and activist From the financial journalist Paul Lewis From the Guardian's George Monbiot From the Labour MP Karen Buck From Jonathan Portes, director of the National Institute for Economic and Social Research From Richard Murphy, the tax reform campaigner From Pat Kane, the Scottish musician, journalist and pro-independence campaigner From Sonia Purnell, Johnson's biographer You can read all today's Guardian politics stories here. And all the politics stories filed yesterday, including some in today's papers, are here. As for the rest of the papers, here's the PoliticsHome list of top 10 must reads, here's the ConservativeHome round-up of today's political stories and here's the New Statesman's list of top 10 comment articles. And here are three articles I found particularly interesting. • Laura Pitel in the Times (paywall) says the Labour councillor who exposed claims of vote-rigging in Falkirk has been eliminated from the race to become the town’s next MP. Linda Gow was told by party officials and members of the national executive that she had been knocked out of the race after an interview on Tuesday. Friends said that she had been barred from running purely because of her involvement in unearthing the scandal in Falkirk. “What does this say about the Labour Party’s attitude to whistleblowers?” said one. “She didn’t do anything wrong. She just made a complaint. We’ve got a local candidate who is being denied the right to go before local members and make her case.” • Andrew Grice in the Independent says Joseph Rowntree Foundation research has shown that spending cuts have hit the poorest parts of England and Scotland much harder than more affluent areas. The Joseph Rowntree Foundation (JRF), which analysed the 30 per cent real terms cut in local government spending between 2008 and 2015, said the North and Midlands are suffering more than the south, with deprived areas left about £100 per person worse off. Researchers warned that cash-strapped councils will end up serving only the neediest fraction of the population. "As a result, the willingness of those who do not rely on council services for the majority of their needs to pay for council services relied on almost exclusively by the poor could be undermined," the report added. • David Aaronovitch in the Times (paywall) says he does not like the tone of the current debate on immigration.
