headline: Theresa May defends controls on terror suspects: Politics live blog
trail_text: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of all the day's political developments as they happen, including David Cameron and Ed Balls giving speeches to the CBI's annual conference
body_text: • Theresa May, the home secretary, has told MPs that Mohammed Ahmed Mohamed, the terrorist suspect who absconded from a Tpim disguised in a burqa, does not pose a "direct threat" to the UK. (See 3.54pm.) Yvette Cooper, the shadow home secretary, said May's policies were to blame. Since the home secretary got rid of relocation orders, got rid of control orders and brought in the weaker Tpims, two terror suspects have vanished. Ibrahim Magag was previously relocated to the West Country. The Home Secretary's decision brought him back and he disappeared. Mohammed Ahmed Mohamed was previously relocated outside London and the Home Secretary's decision brought him back and he too has disappeared. Her policies brought these two terror suspects back into contact with their old networks, with people who could help them disappear and made it easier for these terror suspects to run off. • Alistair Darling, the Labour former chancellor, has called for a full inquiry into the alleged vote-rigging in Falkirk. And the conclusions should be published, he said. Something has gone very wrong in Falkirk. There needs to be a very thorough investigation. I understand the police are looking at matters now. If they proceed, then that is what'll happen but if they don't there needs to be a full inquiry and I am quite clear that the results have to be published because that is the only way in which people will be satisfied that justice is done and been seen to be done. • Sam Laidlaw, the boss of Centrica, the owner of British Gas, has waived his bonus this year after admitting that the energy industry urgently needs to rebuild trust with consumers. • Andy Coulson has hit back at claims by the prosecution in the phone-hacking trial that he had anything to do with hacking Milly Dowler's phone. As Lisa O'Carroll reports, his counsel, Timothy Langdale QC, told the Old Bailey jury on Monday that Coulson knew "mistakes" were made on the paper and that "he wishes he had made some different decisions" when he was editor of the News of the World. But, Langdale said, his defence was he did not commit the offences he has been charged with. "It is his case that he was never party to hacking phones," said Langdale. • David Cameron favours the Irish prime minister Enda Kenny as a candidate to be the next European Commission president, it has been reported. That's all from me for today. Thanks for the comments. Isabel Hardman at Coffee House thinks Theresa May sailed through her exchange with Yvette Cooper. Here's the Guardian video of Yvette Cooper challenging Theresa May over Mohammed Ahmed Mohamed. Theresa May v Yvette Cooper - Verdict: So, where does that leave us? Yvette Cooper blames Theresa May for relaxing the control order regime. (Tpims are watered-down control orders.) May blamed the law for steadily making control orders/Tpims weaker. Tory MPs blamed the burqa. But, on this at least, no one is blaming the Guardian. (See 3.53pm.) Thank God for small mercies. Since 9/11 all governments have found it increasingly hard to know what to do about people suspected of terrorist activity who cannot be prosecuted (sometimes because intercept evidence cannot be used in court, and we recently learnt more about why that is so unpopular with the security services). Labour only introduced control orders because its policy of locking suspects up in Belmarsh without trial and throwing away the key was ruled unlawful. May was moderately persuasive when she she said the courts were making executive action in this area harder, but Cooper successfully showed that Tpims were less effective than post-2007 control orders and May completely failed to answer Labour's charge that getting rid of relocation orders (the ones that subjected people on control orders to "internal exile" away from London) exacerbated the problem. Cooper had an open goal, and she whacked the ball into it very ably. What was interesting, though, was who did not get the blame. It was Lib Dem pressure (and David Davis) that forced the coalition to get rid of control orders and replace them with a more relaxed regime (plus heightened police surveillance, which clearly doesn't always work so well). But May did not highlight this at all. Was this coalition manners? Or is she more of a Davis-ite than we realised? I don't know. The other remarkable thing about the session was the level of burqa-bashing. By my count, three Conservative MPs called for legislation banning the wearing of burqas in public places. For a statement lasting barely 40 minutes, that's a lot. Even Ukip has now abandoned its call for a burqa ban. But on the Conservative backbenches support for the idea seems to be growing. May, who, to her credit, firmly rejected the idea, probably finds this a bit alarming. The statement is over. Unusually, John Bercow takes the opportunity to follow up Theresa May, who paid tribute to Jack Straw following the announcement that he is standing down at the election. Bercow says Straw has always had tremendous respect for parliament. Straw stands up: "I'm not dead yet." I'll post a summary of the May statement shortly. Philip Hollobone, a Conservative, says face coverings, such as balaclavas or burqas, should be banned. May says her views on this have not changed in the last 30 minutes. It is not for the government to tell women how to dress. Peter Bone, the Conservative, says May told the Commons some time ago she was reviewing the law on face coverings. If she had imposed a ban, Mohamed would not have escaped. May says Bone was referring to a consultation on face coverings at demonstrations. Labour's Chris Bryant says May implied that the two people who absconded were very clever. But one just jumped in a taxi, and one just slipped on a burqa. May says the review of the Magag case was undertaken by the Home Office but reviewed by the independent reviewer of terrorist legislation. She would expect the same to apply for the review of this case. Labour's Tom Blenkinsop asks when Mohamed became a British citizen. And when did May become aware of his terrorist connections? May says she learnt about the terror connections when the Tpim was imposed. She will find out when he became a British citizen and write to Blenkinsop. Labour's Nic Dakin asks how many people May will have to lose before she reviews her policy on relocation orders. May says the courts were eroding the scope of these powers. She increased funding for surveillance, she says. Labour's Angela Smith asks if Mohamed was ever subject to a relocation order keeping him out of London. May says she cannot comment on the details of this case. Chris Pincher, a Conservative, asks what will happen to anyone assisting Mohamed. May says she would expect people helping him to be prosecuted. David Nuttall, a Conservative, asks what action will be taken against Mohamed if he is caught. May says he would be in breach of his Tpim order. She would expect appropriate action to follow. Ben Wallace, a Conservative and a former soldier, says he has taken part in surveillance operations. He knows how difficult they are. Labour's Jeremy Corbyn says there is something dangerous about prerogative power being used to take away someone's nationality. May says that, where it is possible to prosecute someone, they are prosecuted. Pat McFadden, the Labour MP, says May has given more freedom to these terrorist suspects. Will May reconsider the sunset clause on the Tpim legislation? May says she increased funding available to the police for surveillance when Tpims were introduced by tens of millions. And, while 43 people were subject to control orders, all of those have now lapsed, she says. James Clappison, a Conservative, says Labour should show some common sense and realism about this. May says there is not much sign of that today. Here is the full text of Theresa May's statement. The Metropolitan Police believe that on Friday 1st November TPIM subject Mohammed Ahmed Mohamed absconded from his controls. He was last seen at 3.15PM inside a mosque in Acton. The Police Counter Terrorism Command immediately launched an intensive covert operation to trace Mohamed and inquiries continue. Ports and borders were notified with his photograph and details were circulated nationally and internationally. Acting on police advice, on Saturday I applied to the High Court for an order protecting Mohamed’s anonymity to be lifted in order to assist the police with their investigation. Last night, the police appealed for the public’s help in tracing him. The police have urged anyone who sees Mohamed or knows of his whereabouts not to approach him, but to call 999 or to contact the Anti-Terrorist Hotline. The police and Security Service have confirmed that they do not believe that this man poses a direct threat to the public in the UK. The reason he was put on a TPIM in the first place was to prevent his travel to support terrorism overseas. I have spoken several times over the weekend to the Director General of the Security Service, Andrew Parker, and to the Metropolitan Police Assistant Commissioner for Specialist Operations, Cressida Dick. I received another briefing earlier today. They have told me that they believe they have all the resources and support they need to carry out this manhunt. However, I will not hesitate to provide them with any additional assistance they require. I know the whole House will join me in thanking the police and the Security Service for their continued efforts to keep our country safe. Their focus is to locate and arrest Mr Mohamed. They are doing everything in their power to apprehend him as quickly as possible. And the Government will provide them with all the support they need. David Winnick, the Labour MP, asks May for an assurance that her spin doctors won't blame Edward Snowden or the Guardian for this. They won't, May says. Julian Huppert, the Lib Dem MP, says relocation orders are deeply unBritish. Keith Vaz, the Labour chair of the home affairs committee, asks if Mohamed is a British citizen. If so, does May have his passport? May says he is a British citizen. She does not have it, but the police do. Gerald Howarth, a Conservative, asks May to ban the burqa. It is alien to our culture, he says. May says she believes a woman has the right to choose how she dresses. But there will be circumstances where it is right to ask for a veil to be removed. Jack Straw, the Labour former home secretary, says there were no abscondees in the five years after control orders were strengthened in 2007. He accuses May of being complacent. May says the control order regime was being weakened by the courts. Angie Bray, a Conservative and MP for Ealing Central and Acton, says the mosque in Acton has a reputation for being moderate. But there are concerns about radicalisation of young people. May is responding to Cooper. She says Cooper told Sky News this morning there had been cuts to the surveillance budget. Cooper has now dropped that line, because she knows the surveillance budget has gone up. May says there was a review of the Magag case. All the recommendations will be implemented. There will also be review of this case, she says. Cooper talked about control orders (the Labour precursor to Tpims). But the courts made it clear that they would not allow people to stay on control orders for ever. If someone is determined to break a control order or a Tpim, there is nothing that can be done to stop them, she says. David Anderson, the independent review of terror legislation, says you can only stop people absconding by locking them up. She suggests that Cooper is not advocating that. There were seven abscondees under the control order regime, May says. So Cooper is wrong to suggest that these problems did not occur under control orders. She says powers to restrain people under these measures are being eroded by the courts. Cooper is wrong to suggest the police have less powers to restrain suspects. And she is wrong to suggest they have less resources, May says. Yvette Cooper, the shadow home secretary, is speaking now. She says only 10 people are subject to Tpims. Two of them have now absconded. While relocation orders were in place, no suspects absconded. But Theresa May got rid of relocation orders, she says. She asks if Mohamed was under active surveillance when he went into the mosque. What was May going to do next year when Mohamed's Tpim ran out? May has made it easier for serious terrorist suspects to disappear, Cooper says. "That is irresponsible." How many more warnings does May need? Theresa May is making her statement about Mohammed Ahmed Mohamed. He was last seen at 3.15pm inside a mosque in Acton, she says. The police launched an operation to trace him. Inquiries continue. On Saturday Theresa May applied to the high court for an order for his anonymity to be lifted. The police say anyone who sees him should dial 999. But he is not thought to be a direct threat to people in the UK. The fear is that he will go abroad to take part in terrorism. May says she has spoken several times to Andrew Parker, head of MI5. He says the security services have the resources they need. Theresa May, the home secretary, will be soon by making her Commons statement about Mohammed Ahmed Mohamed, the terror suspect who was able to go missing on Friday after changing into a burqa at a mosque. Here is some briefing material on Mohammed Ahmed Mohamed (MAM) that Labour sources have sent out. - British citizen -placed under order in March 2011 -closely linked to a network of an East Africa-based group and a UK-based group, whose functions were to assist the activities of associates in Somalia, to recruit and radicalise further individuals and to co-ordinate the transfer of money, equipment and individuals to Somalia - terrorist training in 2008 from experienced al-Shabaab operatives in Somalia, and fought on the front line in support of al-Shabaab - facilitated travel of various individuals from the UK to Somalia to allow them to engage in terrorism-related activity - aided the support of UK-based network for TRA in Somalia, including by procuring funds - procured weapons for use in TRA, including fighting and attacking plans (2008-2010) - helped plan attacks in Somalia and overseas, including an attack intended for the Juba Hotel in Mogadishu, august 2010 - part of attack planning, possibly intended for western interests in Somaliland It is understood that Mohamed is the terror suspect referred to as CC in this court case reported in the Sunday Telegraph and in this ruling reported by the BBC. And here is the court judgment explaining why Mohamed (or "CC") lost an appeal against the order imposing a Tpim on him (covered by the BBC story). Here's an extract. The Security Service assesses that CC is linked to a group of six British nationals who received terrorist training from Al Qaida operatives, Saleh Nabhan and Harun Fazul in Somalia in 2006. It assesses that the group returned to the United Kingdom in late 2006 and early 2007 and has evolved into a well-established and prolific extremist facilitation network, actively supporting extremist associates in East Africa. The network consists of a UK-based group and an East Africa-based group. The UK-based group supports the activities of the associates in Somalia and also seeks to recruit and radicalise further individuals. The UK-based group maintains contact with the East Africa-based group to co-ordinate the transfer of money, equipment and individuals to Somalia. For the reasons set out in my closed judgment in this matter, I have come to the clear conclusion that the Secretary of State had and has reasonable grounds for suspecting or believing (respectively) that CC has been involved in terrorism-related activity. In particular, the evidence before the Secretary of State supports that conclusion in the following respects: (1) CC is closely linked to the network described above. (2) CC travelled to Somalia where he received terrorist training in 2008 from experienced Al-Shabaab operatives. CC attended a terrorist training camp in Kamsuma, Southern Somalia, from 2008 onwards in preparation for fighting along side Al-Shabaab. (3) CC fought on the front line in Somalia in support of Al-Shabaab. (4) CC facilitated the travel of several individuals from the United Kingdom to Somalia to enable them to take part in terrorism-related activity. He was involved in facilitating the attempted travel of Mohammed Aden (September 2008), Mustafa Ferole (June 2009) and the travel of Mohammed Sharif Nur to a training camp (August 2009). (5) CC facilitated the support of the UK-based network for terrorism-related activity in Somalia. The network, which includes Ibrahim Magag and Jama Hersi, has had access to money, false passports and documentation, as well as equipment. CC was involved in procuring funds for terrorism-related activity. (6) Between 2008 and late 2010 CC was engaged in procuring weapons for use in furthering his terrorism-related activity, including fighting and attack plans. (7) CC played a role in planning attacks in Somalia and overseas. CC was involved in an attack plan with Saeed Mohamed and Mohammed Ali Sharif Nur intended for the Juba Hotel in Mogadishu, Somalia in August 2010 and he was willing to participate in a terrorist attack. (8) Shortly before his arrest CC was involved in attack-planning with Hersi. These plans were potentially intended for western interests in Somaliland. And here is a story about Ibrahim Magag, who absconded on Boxing Day 2012 when he was subject to a Tpim. Here's the Guardian video of David Cameron defending HS2 at the CBI conference. At 3.30pm we are going to get a Commons statement from Theresa May, the home secretary, about Mohammed Ahmed Mohamed, the terror suspect who was able to go missing on Friday after changing into a burqa at a mosque. Yvette Cooper, the shadow home secretary, has put out this statement about the case. This is extremely serious. This is the second terror suspect to abscond from a Tpim [terrorism prevention and investigation measures order] in London in 10 months. We must support the police and security agencies in doing everything possible to locate this terror suspect. Tpims are only granted in exceptional cases where the courts agree there is a significant risk of terrorist activity to protect public safety. But the home secretary also needs to urgently explain what has happened in this case. Theresa May was warned repeatedly against downgrading control orders to Tpims and she denied there would be any additional security risk. Yet to lose two serious terror suspects in just ten months is extremely worrying and we need to know urgently why this happened. The home secretary needs to explain urgently what controls were in place for Mohammed Ahmed Mohamed, what decisions were taken and why the controls were not sufficient. And we need to know what the scale of the risk is too. The independent reviewer needs to investigate urgently what has happened here, and we also need to know whether his recommendations after the last disappearance were implemented. Dealing with terror suspects is too important to allow them to so easily flout the controls that the courts have imposed. • David Cameron and Ed Balls have both said that party politics should not derail the HS2 high-speed rail project. In rival speeches to the CBI's annual conference, Cameron said that he wanted Labour to "put the national interest first" while considering the project, while Balls said that businesses needed "stability and predictability". With Cameron saying he would like the costs of the project to be cut, Balls welcomed Cameron's "belated recognition that he needs to get a grip". Balls also stressed that Labour was in principle committed to the project, and overall the two speeches gave the impression that cross-party support for HS2 is now marginally stronger than it was. Sir Mike Rake, the CBI president, used his speech to urge both sides to reach agreement and stick to it. This is what he said. We cannot have every major infrastructure decision continuously re-debated at every turn - as we're seeing with HS2. Undoubtedly, a better effort should be made to communicate the benefits of high speed rail, and this must be positioned within an overall, long-term strategy of what the country needs across all modes of transport. Decisions have to be made, and once they have been made then we should stick to them. • The CBI has renewed its attack on Labour's plan to freeze energy bills. After Balls' speech John Cridland, the CBI director general, said: "The CBI also welcomes the shadow chancellor’s recognition of the vital need to build UK infrastructure but we still do not see how a price freeze on energy costs would encourage investment.” And, in his speech, Sir Mike Rake, the CBI president, said "the public deserves better than politicians playing the blame game [on energy prices]". • Cameron has welcomed a CBI report strongly advocating Britain's remaining in the EU. (See 12.58pm.) • The Home Office has said that Theresa May, the home secretary, will make a Commons statement at 3.30pm about how Mohammed Ahmed Mohamed, a terror suspect, went missing after changing into a burka at a mosque. I will be covering it in detail at 3.30pm. • Johann Lamont, the leader of the Scottish Labour party, has said that the party will "certainly have to look" at reopening its inquiry into allegations of vote-rigging by Unite in Falkirk. • Boris Johnson has described the delay over a decision about expanding airport capacity in the south east of England as "unforgivable". He made the comment in an extract released in advance from the speech he will give to the CBI this afternoon. Johnson wants Sir Howard Davies, who is reviewing airport capacity, to rule out a new runway at Heathrow. When Howard Davies publishes his interim report, he must put an end to this unforgivable, baffling and ludicrous uncertainty and explicitly rule out any expansion at Heathrow. If Heathrow expansion is politically and environmentally impossible, which it is, we need to stop dithering, decide now on a hub airport solution to the east, and get on with it. I hope a man as wise as Howard Davies gets that, and rules out Heathrow now before it is too late. • Johnson has announced that the London living wage will go up 25p to £8.80 an hour. • Rebekah Brooks was involved in a deliberate effort to hide material from police during the "panic-stricken" days around the closure of the News of the World, the jury in the phone-hacking trial has been told. • The Serious Fraud Office has launched a formal criminal investigation into two of the government's biggest suppliers, G4S and Serco, following claims by the justice secretary, Chris Grayling, of tens of millions of pounds of overcharging on electronic tagging contracts for offenders. • The Ministry of Defence has refused to deny a report saying the price tag for the navy's new aircraft carriers will rise to more than £6bn. Philip Hammond, the defence secretary, is expected to be asked about this during defence questions in the Commons this afternoon. Here's the full text of Ed Balls' speech. There is no text of David Cameron's speech. He was speaking from notes. Earlier I said that the feud between David Cameron and Ed Balls was one of the most interesting in British politics. (See 8.59am) But that will surprise anyone in the CBI audience this morning who does not follow Westminster closely and who heard both men speak because what was striking was how, on various issues, they sounded remarkably similar. They were both quite eloquent about wanting more school pupils to be inspired by the prospect of a career in business, although, curiously, Balls was more passionate about this (see 11.52am and 12.03pm) than Cameron (see 10.04am). More importantly, on Europe, they were both firmly in favour of Britain staying in a reformed EU, but also alert to the need not to take public support for the EU for granted. And on HS2 they were both in favour - provided the costs don't rise. So, is this the start of a harmonious new relationship? Of course not. It's more a case of politicians telling an audience what it wants to hear (or, to be more accurate, politicians doing their best to find common ground with the people they are addressing). But the nuances were interesting. Here are the main points. • David Cameron has admitted that the government put too much emphasis on speed when it originally tried to win public support for the HS2 high-speed rail project. As the Telegraph's Christopher Hope reports, this is what Cameron said about this in the Q&A. One of the problems we have with HS2 is that the early argument - a lot of it was about speed. I make no apology for that – it is important to get from A to B quickly. Is there a businessman here who likes to take the slow train who likes to go more slowly – of course not. Everyone wants to get places quickly. But the real argument about HS2 isn’t speed it is capacity. The line is full – we need a new line and building HS2 will not just make it faster and more capacity to get from London to Birmingham, or London to Leeds or London to Manchester, it will have enormous knock-on benefits for other destinations. This is an argument that Lord Adonis, the Labour former transport secretary (and a strong supporter of HS2) made in an article in the summer. Cameron also said that he wanted HS2 to come in "under budget" and, as trailed in advance, he said that Sir David Higgins, who is taking charge of the project, was being asked to cut costs. (See 9.38am.) • Ed Balls has interpreted Cameron's comments as a vindication for Labour. "I welcome the prime ministers' belated recognition that he needs to get a grip [of HS2 costs]", Balls said, as he repeated his insistence that Labour will not give the project "a blank cheque". • David Cameron has backed a CBI report saying that Britain should stay in a reformed EU (pdf). This was brave because, in Eurosceptic demonology, the CBI ranks alongside Peter Mandelson near the very top. (See, for example, this tweet.) Cameron also wants Britain to stay in a reformed EU, but in the past he has not ruled out backing withdrawal from the EU if it refuses to cede back powers to the UK by 2017. However in today's report the CBI said that would be foolish. No alternative option to full EU membership can combine all the benefits of EU membership with none of the costs; such solutions are simply unrealistic. While the UK could certainly survive outside the EU, none of the alternatives suggested offers a clear path to an improved balance of advantages and disadvantages or greater influence over the terms of UK interaction with its nearest neighbours. Today, in response from a questioner who wanted Cameron to make the case for EU membership, Cameron said: "I think the CBI have hugely helped this morning with a very positive report." • But Cameron also said support of the EU was currently "wafer thin". That was why he wanted reform, and then a referendum, he said. But be in no doubt; in the end you cannot stay in these organisations that give up quite a bit of your national sovereignty unless you take the British people with you. The British people were told about a common market. They were told about an economic area. So much has changed about this organisation, and so little consent has been granted, that it's time to make those arguments, seek that consent, and as prime minister of this country that's exactly what I will do. He also said he was convinced that the EU would reform. I sit round that European Union table at these summits and I can see this organisation is going to change. It is changing in front of our eyes because of the euro. • Balls has hinted that he favours a referendum on Britain's membership of the EU. At least, that is how I read this passage. Europe needs reform, but to walk away from our EU membership would be reckless, foolish and deeply damaging. On Britain’s future in Europe, the national interest must come first. But at a time when most people in our country are seeing their living standards falling year on year, we cannot take public support for this open, global vision of a dynamic market economy for granted. UPDATE at 5.40pm: An aide to Balls has been in touch to say that he thinks I am wrong to interpret this as a hint that Balls favours an EU referendum. It was actually one of the most strongly pro-Europe speeches Balls has given, the aide said. • Cameron has said that he wants 1,000 business leaders to sign up to the Speakers for Schools initiative launched by the BBC's Robert Peston. The Balls Q&A is over. I will post a summary soon. Q: How will increasing the top rate of personal tax encourage entrepreneurs? Balls says Labour cut capital gains tax for entrepreneurs to 10%. At the time the Treasury said that was a bad idea. But he was strongly in favour. As an economist, he would rather tax rates were lower rather than higher. But when family budgets are under pressure, the government has to make choices. Cutting taxes for the rich when living standards are falling was "quite wrong". It sent out the wrong message. Q: What can people in this room do to promote entrepreneurship? Balls says he is in favour of enterprise education. But it is not about teaching a course once a week. It is about inspiring 13-year-olds, and making them think that a career in business is an option. If you go and talk to pupils this age, the first 10 or 15 minutes will be difficult, but you will find that there are pupils who, for example, want to start a fashion business, he says. Q: Is it credible to separate large business from small business? Balls says setting big business against small business would be the wrong thing to do. But the tax system does differentiate between the two. Labour's plan to cut business rates for small firms is not about setting the two sectors against each other. Q: Most of the money coming into energy is private investment. Will you make sure you do not undermine this? Balls says competition in the energy sector is not working at the moment. But he acknowledges that the private sector and the public sector need to work together. Q: You say you want to make local enterprise partnerships fit for purpose. What does that mean? Balls says getting rid of local development agencies was a mistake. LEPs are here. But they do not have the "firepower" they need to do the job. Balls is taking questions now. Q: Help to Buy is having an effect on jobs. Please recognise that. And what is your view on landbanking? Balls says he supports Help to Buy. He does not want to get rid of it. But he wants to consider the details. And it is not sensible to stimulate demand unless you also increase supply. Currently there are lots of sites with planning permission that are not being developed. Councils need to be given more powers to make firms with planning permission use it. But this is not about confiscation, he says. Balls ends with an anecdote about pupils winning an enterprise competition designing a website to market Wakefield as the rhubarb capital of Britain. They found that they could get paid for doing things on computers they enjoyed, he says. Turning to skills, Balls says Labour would give employers more control of the government's £1.5bn spending on workplace skills and apprenticeships, on a sector by sector basis. We will ask you to tackle free-riding and deliver an increase in the high end apprenticeships in these sectors and supply chains, including in all companies seeking to bring in more skilled migrants. You will decide what qualifications work best for you and your employees and who delivers the training. Just a few weeks ago I met the Chief Executives of a number of big IT companies at E-Skills. They were singing the praises of the IT Diploma, which they designed. I remember the head of Vodafone telling me he knew this qualification would work when the head of a school’s computer science department complained he could only teach it in partnership with the business department. “Exactly,” cried the Vodafone executive, “teach them how to use IT to solve problems. That's what we want.” Balls turns to energy. In your report, the Colour of Growth, the CBI made a powerful case for long-term certainty to drive investment and innovation and new technology. You called for a 2030 decarbonisation target and we agree. But fully consistent with the need for long-term stability in vision, price and regulation, we also need an energy market that works in a transparent and fair way for household and business customers alike. And it patently isn't at the moment. That is why we have said that we will introduce a new, tougher and more transparent regulatory regime for what remains a highly concentrated utility industry. Balls says as chancellor he would be a strong advocate for infrastructure investment. But the chancellor should not favour particular projects, he says. As chancellor, I would be a strong advocate for infrastructure investment. And I believe the government should be acting now to bring forward that long-term investment, as the IMF has also argued. But the chancellor should never simply become a cheerleader for any particular project. Building a consensus about long- term infrastructure does not mean turning a blind eye to value for money. Balls says Labour would set up an infrastructure commission, as recommended by Sir John Armitt, the former Olympic Delivery Authority chair, in his review for the party. Of course, the test for any such reform is how they would affect big decisions currently on the table. So let me address two. First, on airports, we welcome the Howard Davies review and it is Labour’s view that it should report before the General Election and not after. Under the Armitt plan, it would not have been possible to set up the Davies review without proper cross-party consultation, or kick the report into the next parliament. And if any future government then were to sit on the recommendations, the Armitt plan would give a clear remit to the independent commission to chivvy and chase. Second, on High Speed 2, the whole purpose of the Armitt plan is to ensure that in future the case for and the costs of any large project is properly and thoroughly and independently investigated. Labour supports HS2 and the idea of a new North-South rail link because of capacity constraints on the existing rail network. But our support for it is not at any cost. The Labour Party cannot – and will not - give the government a blank cheque. That is what you would expect from any credible official opposition seeing a Government desperately mismanaging a project. And that is what is happening here with the costs having shot up to £50 billion. Indeed, the costs have gone up by a staggering £10 billion in the last year, so I of course welcome the Prime Minister's belated recognition that he needs to get a grip. Balls says there is no point businesses trying to undercut emerging economies. That is why Labour has asked Mike Wright, executive director at Jaguar Land Rover, to lead a review on strengthening manufacturing supply chains. Some of you will say government should just get out of way. And let me say, if government action is undermining confidence and investment then that is what government should do. But on innovation, skills, planning, infrastructure - for government to walk away would be for government to abandon the long-term partnership we need to succeed. As Labour’s last Business Secretary, Lord Mandelson concluded: “ministers and markets can and should mix - selectively and strategically.” Balls says he and Osborne both agree that they cannot reduce the deficit without growth. But he is concerned about Osborne's reliance on housing demand, he says. We share your concerns about the chancellor’s current reliance on boosting housing demand through taxpayer-guaranteed mortgages to secure recovery through the Help to Buy scheme, while failing to take action to boost housing supply. The danger is that by boosting demand while failing to build more homes, the Chancellor will deliver an unbalanced recovery and make home ownership even further out of reach for the aspiring first time buyers his scheme should be helping. Balls says Labour will make "tough and difficult" choices. So we will have a cap on structural social security spending and go ahead with increases in the retirement age. And I have told my party that we cannot continue to pay the winter fuel allowance to the richest pensioners. And because we must make tough choices about priorities when resources are tight, we have set out difficult choices on tax too. After the financial crisis, I believe it is right to repeat Alistair Darling’s tax on bank bonuses to fund a youth jobs programme and raise the bank levy to help make work pay by expanding free childcare for working parents. And while we have supported - and indeed began - successive cuts in the main rate of corporation tax to make our tax system more competitive, down from 33 per cent in 1997 to 28 per cent by 2010 and 23 per cent today, we do not think we can justify another tax cut for large businesses in 2015, when so many small and medium sized business are under such pressure. That is why we have said the next Labour government will instead cut and then freeze business rates for 1.5 million business properties, a tough choice we need to make to help more businesses when resources are tight. Balls says he and George Osborne agree on the need for deficit reduction. But they disagreed on the pace of deficit reduction. But with the deficit and debt now set to be much higher over the rest of this decade than anyone envisaged back in 2010, there will be no complacency from me and the next Labour government on deficit reduction and spending control. That is why I was very clear with my party in Brighton in September: that the next Labour government will have to be very different from our predecessors; that we will be cutting spending in 2015-16 and not raising it, with no more borrowing to cover day-to-day spending; that we will have tough fiscal rules to balance the current budget and get the national debt falling; and that we want every tax and spending commitment in our manifesto to be independently audited by the Office for Budget Responsibility. On banking reform Balls says it is important to follow a balanced approach. In my view, government should always remember the US response to the World Com and Enron accounting scandals a decade ago. The US Congress reacted with a heavy-handed piece of rules-based legislation – Sarbanes-Oxley. But it didn’t work. It did not stop the subsequent financial crisis - which after all started in the US. And its complexity drove jobs and tax revenues out of the US year by year. This audience doesn’t need telling that rigid rules-based regulation is often not the answer, and that small and medium-sized businesses, desperate for much needed risk capital and fair terms, are likely to be the losers if regulation is too inflexible, and heavy-handed. Balls turns to the subject of consensus. I know it is not fashionable to talk in Britain about the importance of consensus. And let us not be naïve - consensus is not always a good thing, as we learned to our cost when Britain joined the ERM. But I know how much business needs stability and predictability – that it is the clear view of business that, where possible, we politicians should seek to build a consensus in the national interest. I know you will be expecting me to highlight my differences today with coalition politicians. And I will. But there is much we can agree on too. So I want to set out where, on each of the three big economic questions of our time – banking and regulatory reform; deficit reduction; and the long-term prospects for jobs and growth in the UK – the chancellor George Osborne and I agree on the strategic goals - even if we disagree on how to get there. Balls says Labour "failed to see the dangers that were emerging in our banking system in the middle of the last decade, and the inadequacy of bank regulation". He goes on: "I have certainly learned from that experience. " Balls reminds the audience that he has long experience of working with CBI leaders. This may be my first annual conference speech, but over the years I’ve worked closely with five CBI Director-Generals, from Sir Howard Davies onwards. Most recently, of course, John Cridland – who’s been attending CBI conferences for a lot longer than I have. And Sir Richard Lambert, who gave me my first job as an economics leader writer at the Financial Times back in 1989. And Lord Adair Turner - who I worked with closely on the New Deal youth jobs programme and the Climate Change Levy, recommended by the then CBI President Sir Colin Marshall in his 1998 report, which also made the case for extra help for heavy energy users. And of course, the unforgettable Lord Digby Jones. And he tells an anecdote about Digby Jones telling an explicit joke to an audience of schoolchildren when they appeared at an event together. But politicians cannot ignore public concern about Europe, he says. But at a time when most people in our country are seeing their living standards falling year on year, we cannot take public support for this open, global vision of a dynamic market economy for granted. I know, as an MP with, until recently, the largest BNP membership of any constituency in the country, how some on the extremes of left and right see the solution to be isolationism, turning inwards, setting their face against the rest of the world and the global economy. They are wrong. Business is key to the solution, not the problem - as is Britain properly engaged in a reformed Europe. Balls says he and Ed Miliband share the CBI's vision of Britain prospering "in a reformed global Europe". You are right to challenge us politicians to maintain British influence and lead the debate about the reforms that Europe needs to deliver value for money, open up markets and secure rising prosperity. But Britain is better placed to shape Europe’s future if we are fully engaged rather than having one foot out the door. We are clear that there is no future for Britain in walking away from our biggest market – or threatening to do so for reasons of internal party management. Europe needs reform, but to walk away from our EU membership would be reckless, foolish and deeply damaging. On Britain’s future in Europe, the national interest must come first. Balls says this is no time for complacency. We meet here with economic recovery finally getting going again after a long and protracted period of stagnation, and that return to growth is something to celebrate and nurture. But with business investment still on hold, bank lending to SMEs still contracting, youth unemployment still very high, and living standards still falling for millions - meaning that for most people there is so far no recovery at all - this is no time for complacency. The Cameron summary will have to wait. Ed Balls is speaking now. Balls says he first attended a CBI conference in 1995. But this is the first time he has addressed one, he says. I'm back at my desk. It was a test. I'll post a summary of David Cameron's speech in a moment. A fire alarm has gone off in the Commons press gallery. I can't smell any smoke, or see any flames, but the officious voice on the PA system is telling us we must leave the building. Hopefully I will be back in time for Ed Balls. I'll post again when they let us back in. It seems that the part of the Q&A that I missed might have contained some of the best material. This is from the Telegraph's Christopher Hope. David Cameron's Q&A is now over. From what I heard (I missed a bit while trying to get the CBI live feed working), it was better than the speech itself, which was rather pedestrian. But I'm not sure his strong endorsement of the CBI's Europe report will go down particularly well with Conservative backbenchers. I will post a summary shortly. The Q&A is still going on, but BBC News and Sky have done the usual nonsense of turning away just as it gets interesting. But the CBI has a live feed. The BBC's Nick Robinson asks Cameron if he will heed the CBI's warning to stop playing games with the EU membership. Cameron says he is not playing games with EU membership. His view is that consent for Britain's EU membership is "wafer-thin". He says his approach has the overwhelming support of the British people. And it has strong business support too. You cannot stay in these organisations unless you take the British people with you. Cameron says those against HS2 are putting the future of the north of England at risk. Q: How will you ensure that the EU debate is a balanced debate that recognises the advantages of Europe, and takes on the "headlines in the Daily Mail"? Cameron says he welcomes the CBI report on the benefits of EU membership today. Before he delivered his speech, the debate was slipping away, he says. He has made the case for a reformed EU. And winning the argument to stay in that will be much easier than winning the argument to stay in the EU as it is now, he says. Sitting around the table at EU summits, he can see that the EU is going to change. As a result the debate will be much more balanced. Staying in a reformed EU "is an argument we can win", he says. Q: [From someone from Birmingham City University] How do you rate the performance of local enterprise partnerships? Cameron says they have worked well. They are better than regional development agencies. They are business-led. Some are good. But some are less good. It is the right model. The last thing we need is to "fiddle" with the model, he says. The city deals are an important part of the regional growth agenda, he says. The speech is over. Now Cameron is taking questions. Q: [From the director of the Open University] We have launched Future Learn. Aren't new ideas part of Britain's competitive advantage. That's absolutely right, Cameron says. Jobs will come from insurgent firms. That is why the government has introduced visas for the brightest and the best. But the government needs to get better at linking universities to businesses. Insurgency, new ideas, innovation - it will be those countries that get those things right that really win. Cameron says his agenda is "deeply progressive". If the government accumulates too much debt, it is the poor that suffer. If the education system does not work, the poor suffer. He wants everyone to be able to play their part in society. He is turning the country around, he says. But it is a long-term job. Cameron says his fifth point is more "esoteric". There needs to be a culture change in favour of business, he says. The government can do some things. For example, it has introduced start-up loans. But there is only a certain amount government can do. He says he has an appeal for the CBI. He would like to get more businesses into school to inspire pupils. He cites Speakers for Schools, the organisation set up by Robert Peston. The government has agreed to get 1,000 business figures into schools through the programme. Third, the government needs to reform welfare to make work pay, Cameron says. And, fourth, the government, has to invest in infrastructure. He says the government's infrastructure plans are "really ambitious". Spending on roads will treble. Investment in rail is bigger than at any time since the Victorian era. And he turns to HS2. (See 9.32am.) Cameron says he has a message for people who think HS2 will take up too much money. Between 2015 and 2020 the government will be spending £73bn on road and rail, he says. Only £16bn will go on HS2. Second, the government needs to improve education, Cameron says. He cites a depressing statistic: 64% of pupils on free school meals do not get five good GCSEs. The government is being very strict on rigour, he says. This "radicalism" is essential. Cameron says he sometimes challenges his children to name any job that does not require English and maths. What about a footballer, his son said. (Smart lad.) But even footballers need to be able to count their money, Cameron says he told him. Cameron says the country still faces the challenge of needing a "fundamentally different" economic model. The economy needs to be rebalanced, he says. He is going to set out five aspects of this. First, the government needs to continue with deficit reduction. It has already done much. But more needs to be done. For example, the long-term costs of public sector pensions have been cut by 50%. Cameron says the government faced criticism from bodies that wanted it to abandon Plan A. But he is glad that the CBI was a staunch ally in urging the government to stick to its deficit reduction plans. David Cameron is speaking now. He says he is pleased to be able to report that the economy is growing. We are on the right track. Stewart Wood, the Labour peer and adviser to Ed Miliband, thinks it is a bit rich for Cameron to accuse Labour of betraying the north. Overnight Downing Street released extracts from David Cameron's speech dealing with HS2. This is what David Cameron will say when he speaks in about 10 minutes. (For once, he and Boris Johnson seem to be saying the same thing - see 9.32am.) Britain is in a global race for jobs and wealth. Our infrastructure is decades out of date and we urgently need to invest and build. Those who want to delay or obstruct HS2 show a lack of vision. They are playing politics with Britain’s prosperity. They are betraying everyone North of Watford. And they want to condemn Britain to the slow lane. We can either tell our grandchildren we made big, long-term decisions to build a better country... Or we can tell them we dithered for decades while the world raced ahead. That kind of no-can-do spirit will get us nowhere. Fortune favours the bold– not the weak and indecisive. I ask everyone across politics to put their own interests aside– and put the national interest first ... Britain has shown it can build great infrastructure like HS1 or the Olympics on time and on budget. And with Sir David Higgins in charge - the man who built the Olympics - we will do that for the north-south line too. He has agreed that the first vital step will be to bring his penetrating eye and expertise to a specific task. To report on the costs. And to maximise the benefits for all parts of the country as quickly as possible. He has already said the line could come in “substantially” under the current budget. And he has also made it clear he needs cross-party support to do it. You can read all today's Guardian politics stories here. And all the politics stories filed yesterday, including some in today's papers, are here. As for the rest of the papers, here's the PoliticsHome list of top 10 must reads, here's the ConservativeHome round-up of today's politics stories in the papers and here's the New Statesman's list of top 10 comment articles. And here are five articles I found particularly interesting. • Paul Bentley and Daniel Martin in the Daily Mail say a key witness at the centre of allegations that Unite was engaged in vote-rigging in Falkirk has rejected the union's claims that she has withdrawn her story. Lorraine Kane triggered a major probe into alleged electoral corruption when she revealed her family had been signed up to the Labour Party without their consent. It happened as Unite activists were waging a bitter battle to seize control of the safe Labour seat of Falkirk, where she lives. This led to claims that she and others had been signed up as Labour members without their agreement in a bid by the union to manipulate the candidates’ selection. They allegedly hoped to flood the seat with members who they thought could be persuaded to back Unite’s choice to be the party’s prospective MP. But, speaking for the first time, Mrs Kane, 61, told the Mail: ‘I did not change the testimony. I did not change anything. I did not withdraw anything.’ She is now demanding to know whether the evidence she gave was tampered with by Unite. ‘I want all the emails to see what’s what,’ she said. ‘I haven’t seen them. This has been going on for months. I don’t know what the emails are saying. I want to see everything so I know what was said and if anything was changed from what I said.’ Her statement is entirely at odds with public claims made by the union. Only today [Sunday] Unite leader Len McCluskey said: ‘The evidence from the families has been withdrawn’, adding that Unite had ‘no case to answer.’ • James Blitz in the Financial Times (subscription) says the cost of building Britain's two new aircraft carriers has risen by £800m. Britain will this week reveal another substantial increase in the cost of building its two new aircraft carriers, declaring that total funding for the programme will rise by another £800m to £6.2bn. Philip Hammond, defence secretary, is expected to say that a new set of demands – including the need to build a sophisticated aircraft landing system on the ships – have added to the financial burden of the Royal Navy’s flagship project. The revised price tag for the 65,000 ton carriers – which will not operate until the end of this decade – will alarm some opposition MPs. When the last Labour government gave the green light to build the ships in 2007, it set the projected cost at £3.5bn. That figure is now close to being doubled. • Deborah Haynes in the Times (paywall) says General Sir Nicholas Houghton, the chief of the defence staff has said that British troops will step up operations in the world’s terrorist heartlands after their withdrawal from Afghanistan. Despite not having a war to fight for the first time this century, General Houghton does not want his forces sitting around waiting for the next crisis, particularly when the budgets of all Whitehall departments are threatened with further contraction. “There are a whole range of things which the UK’s Armed Forces do for the country both at home and abroad, which I think makes us hugely good value for money,” he said. One focus is to help to combat the spread of al-Qaeda-linked militancy across Africa, with groups of British troops already training indigenous forces in Somalia and Mali. They also have a larger training presence in Kenya, the target of the most recent large-scale terrorist attack. The carnage at the Westgate shopping centre in Nairobi in September was carried out by al-Shabaab militants based in neighbouring Somalia. General Houghton indicated that this sort of pre-emptive intervention would increase. “We have West and East African training missions. There is more that we can yet do in Somalia,” he said. “We have a similar mission in South Africa . . . and we are embarking on a range of other relationships with Gulf States, in the Far East.” • Emily Ashton in the Sun (paywall) says Jeremy Hunt, the health secretary, is blocking a proposal to allow women to stockpile the morning-after pill. • Boris Johnson in his Daily Telegraph column criticises Labour for playing politics with HS2. One of the most interesting feuds in politics today is the one between David Cameron and Ed Balls. To be fair, Balls does not seem to have any particular animus towards Cameron. But there is something about Balls that really riles the prime minister. Cameron once described him as "the most annoying person in modern politics". And today they are virtually going head to head at the CBI conference. According to the advance briefing, Cameron will say he wants to cut the costs of HS2. Balls may well claim this as a personal victory, because he has been more critical of the rising costs of HS2 than any other senior Labour figure. But the speeches will see both men make much wider arguments about the economy. They will both be taking questions, and those could be lively too. With the CBI today making the case strongly for staying in the EU, the exchanges on Europe could be interesting. I will be covering both speeches and Q&A sessions in detail. Here's the agenda for today. 9.30am: Boris Johnson, the mayor of London, announces the new value of the London living wage. 9.45am: David Cameron gives a speech to the CBI conference. As Rowena Mason reports, he will say that he is ordering the the new head of the HS2 high-speed train line to cut the cost of the £42bn project. 11.20am: Ed Balls, the shadow chancellor, gives a speech to the CBI conference. 2.30pm: Philip Hammond, the defence secretary, takes questions in the Commons. As usual, I’ll also be covering all the breaking political news as well as looking at the papers and bringing you the best politics from the web. I’ll post a lunchtime summary at around 1pm and another after the home affairs committee hearing. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I’m on @AndrewSparrow.

headline: Vince Cable accuses Tories of cynical, ugly politics
trail_text: Business secretary tells Lib Dem conference that Conservatives have reverted to type as nasty party
body_text: The business secretary, Vince Cable, has launched a series of audacious broadsides against the Tories, saying he never agreed to join a coalition with Ukip, and dismissing them as cynical purveyors of fear and champions of an ugly politics. He told the Liberal Democrat party conference in Glasgow: "Theresa May once described the Tories, a decade ago, as the nasty party. After a few years trying to be nice and inclusive it has reverted to type: dog-whistle politics, orchestrated by an Australian rottweiler [strategist Lynton Crosby]. "Hostility towards organised labour, people on benefits and immigrant minorities. The list of people the Tories disapprove of is even longer: public sector workers, especially teachers; the unmarried; people who don't own property. Their core demographic excludes pretty much anybody who wouldn't have qualified for the vote before the 1867 Reform Act." Cable accused the Conservatives of "a cynical calculation in difficult times that fear trumps hope; that competence requires callousness. That is not our kind of politics. It is ugly. And we will not be dragged down by it." He urged his party never to cede the mantle of progressive politics to the left. Cable spoke in the hall after he had agreed to vote for a motion on the economy backed by the leadership and so rejected a call from the party's left to implement a more flexible fiscal mandate. Asked why he had earlier suggested he might not back Clegg in the economy debate, he said: "Why would I duck out of an issue like that? "I wanted to support the motion," he explained, but added: "There was some very good stuff in the opposition amendments," including on housing. He explained there were other aspects on the Bank of England that he could not accept. Told that one of his colleagues had described his behaviour as "endless vanity and gross insubordination", he said he did not think his friends regarded him like that. In a quietly delivered speech, Cable reissued a warning that the return to growth was bringing dangers of a property-price-fuelled boom. "We mustn't now settle for a short-term spurt of growth, fuelled by old-fashioned property boom and bankers rediscovering their mojo. We have seen it all before and there are already amber lights flashing to warn us of history repeating itself. "The prime minister says I am a Jeremiah. But you will recall from your reading of the Old Testament that Jeremiah was right. He warned that Jerusalem would be overrun by the armies of Nebuchadnezzar. In my own Book of Lamentations I described how Gordon Brown's New Jerusalem was overrun by an army of estate agents, property speculators and bankers. "The problem we have now is that the invaders are coming back. They have a bridgehead in London and the south-east of England. They must be stopped. Instead we need sustainable growth." He also hinted at his differences with Nick Clegg, saying: "Fairness takes us so far – but in my view not far enough. We are not just a nicer version of the Tories. There are fundamental differences about how to create a stronger economy and more jobs. "In essence, the Tories have a simple world view: private good, public bad. Labour offers the polar opposite. As Liberal Democrats we value both public and private sectors. I support private business, big and small. I also support mutuals and employee ownership. And even Tony Benn couldn't claim to have launched two state-owned banks; the Green Investment Bank – based in Edinburgh, which we promised three years ago, has already committed £685m to green projects – and the Business Bank." He said: "By contrast, the Conservatives' spiritual home is in the United States. They have become the Tea Party Tories. They want to throw overboard any tax or regulation which gets in the way of their blinkered small-state ideology. Deep down they believe that there is no alternative to unhindered individual self-interest; that attempts to tackle big disparities of income and wealth takes us down the road to socialist serfdom." Cable also expressed his despair that the Tories were going back on a previous pledge that the country was open for business. "Brazilian and other students who would bring economic and wider benefits to British universities are being told they are burdensome immigrants – so they go to the United States instead. Many Chinese tourists and businessmen are so fed up with the hassle and humiliation involved in trying to visit Britain to invest here that they are taking their money to Germany and France. "What they hear is that we are closed for business. That must change." He continued: "Let's remember that we voted to join the present coalition. We did not vote to join a coalition with Ukip. "Of course, the Tories are frightened by the public reaction to overseas workers. But there is something deeply opportunistic about people who lecture our workers, and the rest of Europe, about the need for free and flexible labour markets, but then squawk with panic when those free and flexible labour markets bring in foreign workers." He called for action on zero-hours contracts, the level of the minimum wage and the need to tackle reckless directors. He also attacked aspects of the Labour culture, saying of his time as a Labour member in Glasgow: "There was also an unhealthy tribalism and a Tammany Hall political machine in which union bosses had excessive influence in picking candidates and deciding policy. Judging by Falkirk, and other Labour fiefdoms, nothing very much has changed." He ended by calling for the Lib Dems to remain distinctive. "In our tribally divided politics, the country badly needs the one party that can bridge these dangerous divides. This isn't just a matter of splitting the difference between other parties' policies but setting out a clear and distinctive vision."

headline: The Unknown Soldier, Paul Keating and the politics of remembrance
trail_text: Paul Daley: Tony Abbott has insisted that the phrase 'Known unto God' remains on the tomb at the Australian War Memorial – and though this is a secular shrine, he's right
body_text: As an orator, in parliament and on the stump, Paul Keating was excellent when fuelled with the fierce partisanship that drove his politics. Labor diehards still pore over the words of that infamous 1994 “fireworks” speech in which he evoked John Hewson as the fizzer skyrocket; John Howard as the flower pot that “promised a dazzling performance” but falls away to nothing, and Bronwyn Bishop as “the Catherine wheel ... they’d take off, spreadeagle the kids, burn the dog, run up a tree and then fizzle out going round in circles”. And many Liberals still hate him for it. Paradoxically, Keating was best when he pitched beyond the ears of the True Believers and spoke to the nation. And so he delivered what are widely regarded to be the two finest speeches of his prime ministership: at Redfern in 1992, to acknowledge the grotesque injustice to Aborigines at the heart of Australian sovereignty, and on Remembrance Day 1993, to dedicate the tomb of the Australian Unknown Soldier at the Australian War Memorial in Canberra. Both speeches tapped sleeping national sentiments (of pride, of sorrow, of profound loss, of anger, guilt and burning injustice) about two very different stories critical to the definition of Australian nationhood. That both speeches are equally reprised two decades later by Keating’s legion of ideological foes – not least Tony Abbott – and his allies alike, speaks profoundly of the emotional insights he drew upon. It seems somewhat ironic, then, that a recent plan to replace the traditional Rudyard Kipling epithet “Known unto God” on the tomb of the unknown soldier with words from Keating’s 1993 Remembrance Day oration, has earned the speech the controversy for which it was strikingly devoid at delivery. So much that, according to a report in the Australian, Tony Abbott personally intervened to stop “Known unto God” being replaced with the first line from Keating’s 1993 speech – “We do not know this Australian’s name and we never will.” Another inscription near the tomb – “He symbolises all Australians who’ve died in war” – may yet be replaced with Keating’s 1993 line – “He is all of them. And he is one of us." This, it seems, is a compromise, after the memorial council’s reversal, at Abbott’s behest to memorial director Brendan Nelson, of the decision to remove and replace “Known unto God”. Since it opened in 1941, the Australian War Memorial has become the country’s foremost secular shrine. So it may seem unusual that Abbott – mirroring the views of many others – should be exercised to intervene in the name of God. But “Known unto God” is the epithet that marks the graves of more than 200,000 world war one Commonwealth soldiers – including many thousands of Australians – who were so disfigured at death that they could not be identified. When you walk amid the gravestones that punctuate the European western front, for example, where some 20,000 Australians were never found or could not be identified, the words of Kipling – whose 18-year-old son was killed in the Battle of Loos – carry a profound, deeply moving resonance. Such resonance translates with historic and geographic symmetry, linking the lost soldier in Canberra to the hordes of our unidentifiable dead on the old battlefields. To the non-religious, the words might well say “Known unto him” – meaning, only to the soldier himself. But if they did, there would be just as little justification to remove them as there is “Known unto God”. Nelson reportedly says it was his idea to have Keating’s words displayed in the Hall of Memory, where the soldier is entombed. He said the memorial council then decided to place the words on the actual tomb. Regardless, he will now be more mindful of the cultural/political intensity of remembrance and the arbitrary taboos therein. It is hard to think of a genuinely compelling reason for the council’s initial actions, especially when Nelson confirms that a plaque of the full text of the Keating speech will be fixed outside the hall (it was originally intended for inside – why not in the first place?). Politics and commemoration are old but uneasy bedfellows. Politicians of all persuasions like to harness public sentiments surrounding loss, battlefield “sacrifice” and their purported reflection on national character. It is left to the historians, artists, journalists and writers to dissent and challenge – which will happen, with increasing intensity, as the centenary of world war one nears. Plenty of questions will, for example, be asked about how the commemoration will take shape at the memorial (which is spending more than $30m to upgrade its first war galleries). But revisiting the words on the tomb of the unknown soldier seems like an unnecessary digression that won’t service the remembrance of our dead under headstones “Known unto God”.

headline: Politics live: readers' edition - Wednesday 14 August
trail_text: Share breaking news, leave links to interesting articles online and chat about the week's political events in our open thread
body_text: I'm not writing my usual Politics Live blog today, but, as an alternative, here's Politics Live: readers' edition. It's intended to be a place where you can catch up with the latest news and find links to good politics blogs and articles on the web. Please feel free to use this as somewhere you can comment on any of the day's political stories - just as you do when I'm writing the daily blog. It would be particularly useful for readers to flag up new material in the comments - breaking news or blogposts or tweets that are worth passing on because someone is going to find them interesting. A lot of what I do on my blog is aggregation - finding the good stuff and passing it on - and you can do this, too (as I know, because it happens every day when I'm blogging). All of today's Guardian politics stories are here, and all the politics stories filed on Tuesday including some in today's paper, are here.

headline: Women in African politics: a vote of confidence
trail_text: Women are increasingly challenging the traditional male monopoly of African politics. In Cameroon, campaigners have worked tirelessly to boost the chances of women standing in the country's pending elections
body_text: On a sunny day in early May, in Ndu, northwest Cameroon, a group of women, many of whom have taken the day off from work on their farms, are evaluating the performance of an aspiring politician. One suggests smiling more, another that the candidate project her voice more and not look at her feet while talking. The women are here in the Bishop Shanahan Centre to improve their public-speaking skills and learn more about electoral procedures. There are no party divisions in the room – women from the Cameroon People's Democratic Movement (CPDM) sit cheek by jowl with the women of the Social Democratic Front (SDF) – because the issue of women's participation in politics transcends petty party politics. In Cameroon, finally, the women are coming. The look of African politics is decidedly male. Leaders fall into the "grand statesman" mould, Nelson Mandela; the tyrant, Charles Taylor and Sani Abacha; misunderstood man of the people, Robert Mugabe; and the curiously long-lasting, Equatorial Guinea's Teodoro Obiang and Angola's José Eduardo dos Santos. In recent years, however, things have begun to look a little less XY. In 2006, Ellen Johnson Sirleaf was elected president of Liberia, and five years later, won a second term. In April last year, Malawi elected its first female president, Joyce Banda; Forbes placed her as the 47th most powerful woman in the world. Only last week, Aminata Touré was elected as prime minister of Senegal (although her cabinet consists of four women and 28 men). Last year, the executive director of UN Women, Michelle Bachelet, called for "stronger commitment by leaders to increase women's participation in politics", and went on to argue that quotas were the way to change the gender inequality at the heart of so many national parliaments. The often-lauded Scandinavian countries of Norway, Sweden and Denmark have high rates of female representation at parliamentary level: 40%, 45% and 39% respectively, according to the World Bank. But they pale in comparison with the small central African state of Rwanda, which stands at a whopping 56% – the highest proportion of female parliamentarians in the world and well above its 30% quota. By contrast, the UK has a middling 23%. A report by UN Women and the Inter-Parliamentary Union (IPU) found that 17 of the 59 countries that held elections in 2011 had introduced quotas and, in those 17, women gained 27% of parliamentary seats, compared with 16% in those without quotas. Quotas are very much on the minds of the would-be candidates gathered at the training session in Cameroon, as local and parliamentary elections will take place on 30 September. This will be the first election in which the parties have been pressured by campaigners into meeting them. Quotas – and other routes to equal representation – are also being closely monitored by Voluntary Service Overseas (VSO), which last summer launched its Women In Power campaign, working to increase the involvement of women in decision-making at all levels. On Tuesday 10 September, VSO volunteers will be holding a mass lobby of parliament – timed to coincide with the attendance of the international development secretary Justine Greening at the UN General Assembly – on the issue of women's political participation around the world. The lobby will be followed by a 40-page report looking at the causes and effects of gender imbalance among decision-makers, and how to reduce the barriers to political participation. The fact that quotas alone are not the answer looks evident in Cameroon, where the main parties instituted a 30% female quota target over recent years, but few councils adhere to it. In the city of Bamenda, a local NGO, Community Initiative for Sustainable Development (Cominsud), is working with VSO to mobilise women and minority candidates to become councillors. The feedback reported a lack of confidence and ignorance of the electoral system. "We built up a training programme to get to these women – how to build up their confidence, how to campaign and so on," says Bih Pascaline, lead project staff on the Democracy and Empowerment of Women programme at Cominsud. So far, they have trained more than 450 women. In Rwanda, the 30% quota has been exceeded ever since it was introduced, and neighbouring countries in the region are not too far behind: Burundi and Uganda have female parliamentary representation at over 30% as well. What makes Rwanda so successful? Dr Agnes Matilda Kalibata has been minister for agriculture since 2008. She puts the higher-than-average proportion of women in office down to a restructuring of Rwandan society post-genocide. "Bringing women out of the home and fields has been essential to our rebuilding," she told the Washington post in 2008. "We are becoming a nation that understands that there are huge financial benefits to equality." Elections are due on 16-18 September, and there are 204 women competing for 80 seats in the lower house. With a quota for young people and a reserved seat for a candidate with disabilities, women are expected to win more than the 24 seats allocated to them again. >In Cameroon, John Fru Ndi, the leader of the opposition party, SDF, is aware that things could be a lot better, and not just in terms of gender. "Our councils must be more representative – we must meet youth, ethnic and gender quotas, to represent the interests of all people," he says. He introduced a 25% quota in the party years earlier, he says, but it has been difficult to hit the target. "Democracy here is still young, and the men are reluctant to give to women," he says. "Some of the women are shy. Twenty-three years [in opposition] and we've managed to bring more women in, and more training programmes can empower them even more." Chris Mbunwe, a journalist at The Post, says this is mostly down to ignorance. "A lot of the men still believe that when you empower a woman, you are empowering her to hurt them," he says. But he acknowledges a steady change in the male mindset. "They discovered that there was something lacking – and the women's performance has been key," he says. "When a woman says: "I am going to build a bridge here," she builds it. On councils and in parliament, they are generally more transparent, more accountable." With the Cameroonian elections weeks away, political parties have submitted their candidate lists and the signs are encouraging. In Ndu, which has the lowest number of female councillors in the Northwest Region (just one in 41), things have improved only slightly: the SDF has put forward a list with five women (12%) and CPD one with 11 (27%). Michelle Hain, a VSO volunteer working with Cominsud, reports a determination among the women. "The training about public speaking and the electoral code has one objective and that is to build the confidence of women to act in political arenas," Hain says. There were a few reports of 'missing' files belonging to women candidates, and at least one woman had to go to court in the capital, Yaounde, to defend her place on the list. "I think we are seeing [this confidence] in their determination to stand firm and united when faced with challenges."

headline: George Osborne's interviews and payday lending cap announcement: Politics live blog
trail_text: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of all the day's political developments as they happen, including George Osborne's morning interviews
body_text: • William Hague, the foreign secretary, has told MPs that the deal with Iran on its nuclear programme represents "a significant step towards enhancing the security of the Middle East". • Alistair Darling, head of the Better Together campaign, has said that the white paper on independence being published by the Scottish government tomorrow must be credible. The nationalists have to give a credible and costed plan. If they ignore the cost of independence or claim that a vote by Scotland can force other countries into doing what Alex Salmond wants, it will be a work of fiction. There is only one guarantee which the White Paper can really make. If we vote to leave the United Kingdom, we will leave the United Kingdom, whatever the cost. That is why it is so important that the White Paper offers a realistic assessment rather than an uncosted wish list. • The government has sold £890m of student loans to a debt management consortium for £160m. • David Cameron has welcomed the release of the last British member of the Arctic 30, his constituent Philip Ball. That's all from me for today. Tomorrow I will be in Glasgow covering the launch of the Scottish government's white paper on independence. Thanks for the comments. Why did George Osborne announce his surprise payday lending cap U-turn today? My colleague Patrick Wintour has been looking into it. One factor seems to have been the prospect of losing a vote on the subject this week. There is also fragmentary evidence that neither the FCA or Treasury official had been primed for an announcement on Monday morning, or that any longstanding fresh policy research had been conducted beforehand. It felt more as if there was a political decision to deal with a festering sore. The latest manifestation, apart from the daily tweeting by Labour's consumer affairs spokeswoman, Stella Creasy, was a freelance Liberal Democrat operation in the Lords. Lord Sharkey had tabled an amendment on a cap on loans set at £300, with no rollovers. The amendment for inclusion in the banking bill was due to be debated on Tuesday. Last week Sharkey met the Liberal Democrat consumer business minister, Jo Swinson, and separately spoke on the phone to Alexander. They were listening rather than making a commitment, Sharkey says of the conversations. There was a decent chance that Sharkey could have won the vote, as crossbenchers in the Lords are angry that previous commitments to cap payday loans did not seem to have been honoured by the coalition. Boris Johnson, the London mayor, has a long history of offending people living in places outside the capital.
