When I had finished my very serious presentation I asked the press if there were any questions. I little hand went up. ‘Yes sir’ I asked. He said ‘what are you vital statistics?’ That was 20 years ago, and on that day I was the first woman to break into a very male dominated industry. But I had the last laugh. I made a trading profit in my first year in charge for the first time in the clubs modern history; I floated my business on the stock market in 1998, making me the youngest CEO of a PLC in the country and when I sold the business in 2009, 75% of the senior management team running the business were women! Karren Brady, the businesswoman and the Apprentice star, is speaking at the conference now. She is introducing George Osborne. Patrick McLoughlin, the transport secretary, spoke in the hall earlier. My colleague Gwyn Topham has sent me this assessment. Patrick McLoughlin's speech was a fairly robust and unequivocal defence of HS2, with fewer concessions to the fears of opponents than he has made in previous outings. But he certainly didn't get much love from the hall for doing so - even a fairly bland commitment to faster wifi for most rail passengers by the end of the decade got a better reaction than the faltering HS2 oratory. He played the northern and regional card by attacking the hypocrisy of “moaning London commentators” who oppose HS2 while welcoming infrastructure schemes that benefit the capital. “We need a new north-south line to make the country stronger." McLoughlin twice employed that phrase that Labour has been trying to popularise – the “north-south line” - that emphasises HS2 simply as an additional and crucial part of the railway rather than talking about time savings or speed. He stressed that the investment would not affect funding for other transport schemes. But he told the Conservative conference that the paucity of fast rail links was “bad for our nation and it has got to change.” The reaction however suggests grassroots Tory opposition to HS2 is strong - at fringe meetings Conservative members have raised the spectre of Labour stealing an electoral march by ramping up opposition to the scheme, after Ed Balls' recent comments. Labour remains officially supportive of a scheme that promises to benefit its heartlands. But there is of course recent precedent for a party deciding to oppose major infrastructure plans ahead of an election: the Conservatives eventually scrapping Heathrow's third runway in 2010. In the conference hall Michael Fallon, the small business minister, has just finished hosting a discussion with some young entrepreneurs. Like some of the other Conservatives who have already spoken, he had a go at the business secretary. We've had enough red tape and enough yellow Cable, he said. On Twitter Andrew Fisher has sent me this about the TaxPayers' Alliance comment about the "work for benefits" plan. (See 11.11am.) Liam Byrne, the shadow work and pensions secretary, told BBC News that George Osborne's "work for benefits" plan was "unravelling already" because it would only affect 2% of claimants. This scheme that we've heard today will only actually affect 2% of people on the dole. So our perspective on this is very, very simple, we don't think it's big enough or bold enough. I've taken the quote from PoliticsHome. Byrne has also written a blog for the New Statesman setting out his case in more detail. David Willetts, the science minister, spoke to the conference earlier. He said the government wanted to double the proportion of women studying engineering. We are not going to win in the global race if we waste the talents of half the British people. The proportion of engineers who are women is one of the lowest in Europe and we’ve got to raise our game. That is why we support the ambition to double the proportion of engineering degrees taken by women. Today I can announce two initiatives to help us achieve that. We will extend fee loans to part time students of engineering, technology, and computer science who already have a degree in a different discipline. And we will invest £200 million in new teaching facilities for science and engineering in our universities. Universities will have to match it with private money. So that makes £400 million of investment so that students can be taught on the latest equipment ready for the world of work. That is our commitment to working with universities and business to help win the global race. As you would expect there was also a huge amount in his speech about the achievements of British science. My favourite line was this one. Of course we must always leave room for our scientists to pursue their own ideas, like the scientist in Newcastle University wondering how locusts manage to fly in such dense swarms without colliding. So she analysed locust brains to see how they worked. Now she is going to sell her anti-collision software to the car industry. On the "work for benefits" plan, it is also worth reading what the centre-right thinktank Policy Exchange said about workfare in a report published at the end of last week. Here's the news release and here's the report (pdf). And here's an extract from the report. Rolling out workfare for large numbers of benefit claimants would be prohibitively costly (with recent suggestions costing upwards of £1 billion in the first year alone) and would ignore the very deep barriers to employment facing many jobseekers. It is vital for these to be addressed with personalised and intensive support and coaching. In short, workfare is not suitable for all, or even a large proportion of, benefit claimants. However, for some groups of benefit claimants, the experience of work that workfare can provide and the diversionary impact of increasing conditions could both be effective in helping them into work. Workfare schemes are also popular with the public. Previous polling commissioned by Policy Exchange showed that 80% of the public supported community work in return for benefits for those who had been unemployed for over 12 months. New YouGov polling commissioned for this report found that, by a margin of over three to one, the public would rather reform labour market policy to introduce a workfare scheme than one that guaranteed long-term benefit claimants a job at the National Minimum Wage using taxpayers’ money. However, importantly, it also found that general support for the principle of workfare does not apply to all groups of claimants; while just 8% of the public say that young people under 25 without children should not be subject to workfare, that figure rises to over two thirds (67%) for mothers with pre-school children and more than a half (52%) for people with medical conditions affecting their ability to work. Here's some reaction to George Osborne's "work for benefits" plan. • Rachel Reeves, the shadow chief secretary to the Treasury, said Labour's proposed jobs guarantee would be more effective. It's taken three wasted years of rising long-term unemployment and a failed Work Programme to come up with this new scheme. But this policy is not as ambitious as Labour's compulsory jobs guarantee, which would ensure there is a paid job for every young person out of work for over 12 months and every adult unemployed for more than 2 years. With Labour's plans we would work with employers to ensure there are jobs for young people and the long term unemployed - which they would have to take up or lose benefits. Under the Tory scheme people would still be allowed to languish on the dole for years on end without having a proper job. • Graeme Cooke, research director at the IPPR thinktank, says relatively few unemployed people will be affected. George Osborne’s welfare announcement today is not a revolution. It will probably end up affecting less than one in twenty JSA claims, which accounts for 2.5 per cent of all benefit spend. People have ‘had to do something in return for their benefits’ since 1911. There is nothing wrong in principle with expecting the long term unemployed to undertake work experience or a more intensive job search. The key issue is how such schemes are designed. If they give people real experience of work and the practical employability habits that go with it, they can help people be more attractive to prospective employers. But if it is pitched as a punishment where people do menial tasks, it risks acting as a signal to employers that these are people not to employ. The government’s mandatory work activity pilot, which mirrors today’s announcement, was found to have no impact on future employment, only a short term impact on JSA claims and it actually increased claims for disability benefit. By contrast, job guarantee schemes like the Future Jobs Fund – which created real work placements, paid at the minimum wage, and compulsory for the long-term unemployed – have much better results. We should be guaranteeing everyone a job paid at the minimum wage after they have been out of work for more than a year, but also insisting that they work or risk losing their benefits – limiting the amount of time society is prepared for anyone to be unemployed. • Boycott Wokfare says the proposal as the "criminalisation of unemployed people". Here's an extract from their news release. Unemployed people and campaigners have condemned George Osborne’s announcement that long-term unemployed people will be forced to work unpaid or face losing their social security as a criminalisation of unemployed people. The maximum community sentence that a judge can hand out is for 300 hours, but claimants on six-month workfare schemes are already being forced to work without pay for 780 hours. The four-week Mandatory Work Activity scheme is already the equivalent of a medium level community service order that a person might receive if they were found guilty of drink driving or assault. When a similar scheme was introduced in the US, thousands of jobs in the Parks Department were lost in New York alone - to be replaced with forced unpaid workers. Similar case studies have emerged in the UK, where workfare placements are already taking place in hospitals, council offices, charities and businesses. • The TaxPayers' Alliance has welcome Osborne's proposal. The TaxPayers' Alliance has given a warm welcome to George Osborne's announcement that the long-term unemployed will be required to do unpaid full-time work or job seeking or lose their benefits. It comes just three weeks after the TaxPayers' Alliance published Work for the Dole: A proposal to fix welfare dependency, which called for mandatory participation in community work and training in return for the continued payment of benefits payments. The TaxPayers' Alliance report, written by award-winning entrepreneur Chris Philp and published on 4 September, provides the most comprehensive analysis of how a Work for the Dole scheme along the lines of those adopted in Australia and the US could be successfully introduced here in the UK. • Fiona Weir, chief executive of Gingerbread, the single parents' charity, says the plan will stigmatise the unemployed. This announcement seems to be a smokescreen for a failing system: acknowledging that the Work Programme isn’t delivering the ‘revolutionary approach’ promised to support people into work, and ignoring the weak labour market. Single parents are highly motivated to work but face practical barriers such as childcare and a shortage of family-friendly jobs – none of which are addressed by Help to Work. Workfare has been shown to be ineffective in helping those with multiple barriers to work, and ineffective in a weak labour market (2). This announcement will only deepen the stigma that unemployed people face, and does nothing to offer new, constructive solutions to help them back into work. Jonathan Portes has responded to George Osborne's comment about him. (See 10.31am.) You can read all the Guardian's Conservative party conference coverage here. As for the rest of the papers, I've already mentioned the Nigel Farage article in the Times. (See 8.27am.) Here are some other articles I found interesting. • James Slack and James Chapman in the Daily Mail say Theresa May will announce that she will change the rules so that people who want to appeal against deportation have to do so from abroad. Foreign criminals, terrorists and illegal immigrants will be kicked out of Britain before they get the chance to claim their human rights are being breached. In a massive shake-up of immigration law, Theresa May today tells the Daily Mail the Government plans to ‘deport first, and hear the appeal later’ – after they have been put on a plane home. The Home Secretary will also slash the number of grounds on which migrants can lodge an appeal from the current 17 to just four after the fiasco of the deportation of Abu Qatada, who finally returned home to Jordan earlier this year after a 12-year legal battle. • Boris Johnson in his Daily Telegraph column that Ed Miliband's promise to freeze energy prices is a con. [Miliband] says he will imitate the catastrophic policies of the emperor Diocletian, by imposing a price freeze on energy bills for the 20 months succeeding the election. And, er, then what? Well, then the energy companies will of course recoup their losses by whacking the prices jaggedly upwards again. In the meantime, the Labour government would have achieved all sorts of undesirable outcomes. By their meddling jiggery-pokery, they will send out the worst possible message to anyone thinking of investing in this country, or buying shares in British businesses. Worse still, perhaps, he will trigger all sorts of perverse behaviour by the companies – none of which is likely to be in the interests of the consumer. The energy companies will sullenly cut costs by laying off staff – so that you spend even longer waiting for a human being to answer the phone, and have to wait in all day for a repair man to come. • David Cameron tells the Sun in an interview (paywall) that his wife Samantha wants him to serve a full second-term if he wins the next election. (There have been rumours that she wants him to stand down before 2020.) It sounds as if Conservative MPs have all been reading Jesse Norman's biography of Edmund Burke over the summer. William Hague cited Burke in his speech yesterday. And Owen Paterson, the environment secretary, did the same in his speech to the conference a few minutes ago. Here's what Paterson said. It was after all Edmund Burke who wrote that we are the “temporary possessors and life-renters” of the earth who must live in a way which doesn’t “leave to those who come after… a ruin instead of a habitation.” If we do, history will quite rightly judge us harshly. Here's the full text of the Paterson speech. And here are some other key points. • Paterson said that the broadband rollout would have more impact on the rural economy than anything else. I believe that nothing will have a more spectacular impact on the rural economy than the roll out of superfast broadband. For the first time, we have a technology that can truly bridge the gap between urban and rural. • He said that biodegradable bags could be exempt from the new 5p tax on plastic bags. (I suppose you could see this as amounting to a tax cut announcement.) In tandem with Nick Clegg’s announcement of a 5 pence charge on plastic carrier bags, I want to work with industry on setting a standard for biodegradable bags. Any bags complying with this new standard could be given out by retailers for free or at a lower cost, reducing their environmental impact and incentivising a whole new industry. • He said the government was exploring biodiversity offsetting. Biodiversity offsetting resolves the long-running conundrum of how to grow the economy at the same time as improving the environment. It could, for example, see the extension of a dairy, which will result in the loss of a pond, get the go ahead. This would, however, only happen after every other avenue of mitigation had been explored and would be on the basis of a long-term agreement to enhance an existing pond or create a new one elsewhere. Any such offset would need to deliver measurable and sustainable biodiversity gain. The concept has been around for some time now and on a small island such as ours, with some of the most delicate ecosystems on earth, we must give it serious consideration. That’s why I launched a consultation at the beginning of September. I’ve seen it working well in Australia. And we can learn from other countries such as Germany, India and the United States. We already have six pilots underway in England but the time has come for us to broaden the discussion, to unleash our ambition. • He criticised Labour for politicising the badger cull. Labour's approach to bovine TB amounted to "doing nothing", he said. George Osborne's Today programme interview was just one of several that he gave this morning. Overall, he covered a broad range of topics. Here are the main points. I've taken some of the quotes from PoliticsHome and the Press Association. • Osborne said the "work for benefits" plan being announced today was designed to help the long-term unemployed. Previous governments of all colours have abandoned those people and just allowed them to recycle in and out of jobcentres, collecting the dole, and people didn’t really do anything about them. I think that’s wrong. I think it’s something we’ve got to learn from what went wrong in the past. We’ve got to help these people, but in return expect them to put something back into their community. On the basis of this comment, and other similar ones, you might conclude that Osborne is investing in better welfare provision. But this message does not seem to have got through to the papers who are presenting it as a punitive move, such as a "crackdown" (in the Daily Mail's headline) or an attack on "workshy Brits" (in the Sun's story). It is hard to believe they can both be right. • He rejected suggestions that his proposal was harsh. I’ll tell you what I think is heartless, it is leaving the long-term unemployed to just recycle through jobcentres and never get a proper chance of getting a job … I think it is a very compassionate approach to people who previous governments just ignored. • He rejected claims from a leading economist that the plan would not work. On the Today programme Sarah Montague asked him about comments from Jonathan Portes, head of the National Institute for Economic and Social Research and a former government economist, who has been speaking out about the plan, on his Twitter feed and elsewhere. Yesterday Portes used Twitter to highlight a blog post about government research showing that a similar programme has not worked. Here's how it starts. The Department for Work and Pensions today published an impact assessment of the Mandatory Work Activity (MWA) programme. The analysis compares participants on the programme with "comparable" (as determined by sophisticated statistical techniques) non-participants. Briefly, what the analysis shows is that the programme as currently structured is not working. It has no impact on employment; it leads to a small and transitory reduction in benefit receipt; and worst of all, it may even lead to those on the programme moving from Jobseekers' Allowance to Employment and Support Allowance. Sadly, the DWP's press release doesn't mention any of these key points. Osborne said he did not agree with Portes' analysis. Portes was "comparing apples with pears", he said. • Osborne said he did not accept that today's announcement meant the work programme (the government's current scheme to get the unemployed into work) was failing. "The work programme is working," he said. • He said his "work for benefits" plan would save money in the long run. I think it will make savings to the welfare bill, because of course if you get people off welfare, that’s a huge saving to the taxpayer and they themselves become contributors through the taxes they pay in work so there’s a huge benefit in that. • He rejected claims that he regarded Iain Duncan Smith, the work and pensions secretary, as "thick". Montague was paraphrasing a comment in the Sunday Telegraph yesterday, which was serialising Matthew d'Ancona's new book about the coalition, In It Together. Here's the Telegraph extract. Nor was [Osborne] confident that IDS had the IQ. “You see Iain giving presentations,” he confided in allies, “and realise he’s just not clever enough.” Duncan Smith believed that Osborne had been captured by the Treasury and its institutional arrogance. “I can see his whole sense of government changing,” he told colleagues. “He’s not a gambler at all.” Asked if he had described Osborne as "thick, or not very clever", Osborne said he had not. No, I did not. And I, by the way, work incredibly closely with Iain Duncan Smith. I think he is a remarkable individual who has transformed, almost single-handedly, the entire debate about welfare in this country and is deeply compassionate ... and clever .... Just because it’s in a book doesn’t make it true. I have huge, huge respect and time for Iain Duncan Smith. Osborne also said he and Duncan Smith worked well together. And, by the way, here’s a staggering truth about this government: the fact that the chancellor of the exchequer and the welfare secretary can work together and deliver programmes like this and talk to each other is itself a sign that we have a functional government that is working for hardworking people. • He said the Conservatives would not allow their candidates to do stand as joint Conservative/Ukip candidates at the election. In an article in the Times today, Nigel Farage, the Ukip leader, said he would let local Ukip parties strike deals of this kind. But Osborne said it would not be allowed. There aren’t going to be any deals with UKIP and there are not going to be Conservative-Ukip candidates locally. The only candidates who will stand for the Conservative party at the election are Conservative candidates ... There’s a very clear rule in our country, which is that candidates have to be approved by the party and the Conservative party will be fielding Conservative-only candidates. • He rejected a claim from Chris Huhne, the Lib Dem former energy secretary, that the Treasury was blocking government moves to get people to insulate their homes. This is what Huhne told the Today programme earlier. I think the government has made a lot of progress on green issues. It does depress me that there are still some very easy hits, for example on energy saving we could be saving a lot of energy and reducing our bills if there were greater incentives for the green deal. Our homes are incredibly inefficient. They leak energy, and that costs an incredibly amount. It has been the Treasury that has really been the obstacle to that. Osborne said the Treasury was funding these schemes. Chris would know, because I worked with him on designing this, we do have a programme funded by the Treasury or funded by taxpayers more to the point, or indeed energy bill payers, that does help insulate the homes of those who are the poorest or in fuel poverty. It’s not just the Green Deal. We also have something called the ECO, which is a scheme that energy companies use to go into some of the poorest homes or the homes with the highest energy bills relative to their income and the size of their home to make sure they’ve got all the energy efficiency that they can possibly have. • Osborne said the Conservative party was not moving to the right. The Conservative party is appealing to all parts of our country – we’re not moving to the left or the right; we are on the centre ground of British politics. And I think policies on immigration, welfare and Europe which are sometimes called ‘right-wing policies’ or policies that appeal to people who might be tempted to Ukip, I think they have a very broad appeal. I find people of all political colours worried about immigration, worried about welfare. And which party is tackling those problems? The Conservative party. • He said the global debt crisis was not over. And anyone who thinks the debt crisis is over, they only have to watch your news bulletins and they’ll see problems in Italy, problems this week in Washington DC. • He said governments could not tackle the living standards crisis without fixing the economy. We have to go on delivering an economic plan because that is the only credible plan for living standards. If you don’t fix the economy, people don’t get jobs, mortgages go up in cost because borrowing gets out of control, you’re not able to take people out of tax – all those things would be a total disaster for this country’s economy and a disaster for living standards. • He said raising the tax allowance was worth as much to low-earners as a 10% increase in the national minimum wage. • He said Labour's' plan to freeze energy bills was "just a gimmick". He went on: "It’s not a serious economic policy and I think the country understands that. • He said he accepted that man-made climate change was happening. I do accept that climate change is happening and I accept the overwhelming evidence that it is man-made and I want us to be part of the international solution to that. My argument has always been that saving the planet should not cost the earth – what good does it do Britain to have a set of policies that closes, let’s say, an aluminium smelter in the north of England and then that same company opens an aluminium smelter in China? • He welcomed the announcement that the delivery firm TNT is creating 1,000 new jobs in Manchester. • He said he might have a pint of Our Maggie beer, which is on sale at the conference, after his speech. Now we get the Nick Robinson post-match analysis. Robinson says Osborne "put to bed" the idea of a Tory/Ukip pact. In the Times today Nigel Farage is saying Ukip and the Conservatives could do local deals at the election. Here's an extract from the Times story (paywall). Writing in The Times, the UKIP leader says that he is despised by David Cameron, and a formal electoral pact with the Conservatives would be nonsensical. However, Mr Farage indicates that he would not stop agreements in individual constituencies, an intervention that threatens to split the Conservatives during the party’s annual conference in Manchester. Although he claims that UKIP is diametrically opposed to Mr Cameron, he says that there are many MPs who take a different view from the Tory leadership, naming Peter Bone and Jacob Rees-Mogg. “If either they, or others like them, even Labour MPs, with their local associations, chose to propose running on a joint ticket then I would leave the local UKIP association to have those negotiations,” he writes. “If after discussions they feel that it would be a better way to serve their constituents, then I and the National Executive Committee would be happy to hear reasoning. After all we are a party that believes in real localism and doesn’t think that the centre is the repository of all wisdom.” I'll post a summary of Osborne's interview on Today, and the others he has been doing this morning, shortly. Q: Would you mind Conservative candidates doing deals with Ukip? Osborne says that will not happen. The only candidates standing for the Conservatives will be Conservative ones. Q: So if a Conservative MP puts something on a leaflet saying they are supported by Ukip, you will stop that? Osborne says Conservative candidates have to be endorsed by the party. The Conservatives are moving to the centre ground. Policies on immigration and welfare have a broad appeal, he says. Q: Chris Huhne this morning said the Treasury was blocking tax breaks for energy savings. These measures would cut bills, he says. Osborne says he worked with Chris on the government's scheme. The government is doing various things. But the government needs the right mix. Q: People think you do not believe in these measures. Do you accept the IPPC conclusions on climate change? Osborne says he does. Climate change is happening. But he thinks there is no point closing an aluminium smelting plant in the UK only for it to open in China. That does not help the environment. The argument he has been making has now come to the forefront of debate: how do you have an energy policy that also cuts bills. Osborne says, without an economic plan, you cannot have a cost of living plan. Growth is needed to improve living standards. Q: But for all? Labour says a rising tide now just lifts the yachts, and that you are just for the rich? Osborne says he does not accept this Labour criticism. Under Labour, the rich payed lower tax than their cleaners. Q: Are you satisfied everyone will benefit from the government's policies? Osborne says he wants everyone to benefit from government policies. The minimum wage is going up tomorrow. And the increase in the tax allowance is worth a 10% rise in the minimum wage. Increasing the personal allowance was easy. The hard thing was paying for it, he says. Q: Why are you announcing this, not Iain Duncan Smith? The Matthew d'Ancona book says you think he's "thick" Osborne says that's not true. He never said that. Just because it's in a book, that does not make it true. He says he has huge respect for Duncan Smith because he has transformed the welfare debate in this country. Q: Are you serious about taking people's benefits away? Osborne says he is serious about helping people into work. Q: But there will be sanctions. Osborne says the sanctions are important. They will help people into work. There needs to be a bit of tough love. Sarah Montague is interviewing George Osborne. Q: This will affect 200,000 people. It suggests the work programme has not worked. Osborne says he does not accept that. The work programme has got 350,000 into work. More than 150,000 of them have got jobs lasting more than six months. The plan announced today will help people into work. Q: It will cost £350m. Jonathan Portes, who used to be a Cabinet Office economist, says it won't work. Osborne says Portes is comparing apples and pears. There is a broader point. In the past cities like Manchester accepted some people would be workless. But he does not think we should accept that. The government should fix the problem of "endemic worklessness". It will help those on welfare. But it will also be fair to people who pay for it. George Osborne, the chancellor, is speaking at the Conservative conference today and he is already dominating the headline. Overnight he released details of his plan for tougher conditions for the long-term unemployed receiving benefits. Here's the Guardian story about it, and here's how it starts. George Osborne will continue the government's push to reduce welfare spending by announcing a nationwide scheme on Monday to force 200,000 long-term unemployed benefit claimants to either undertake community work, attend a jobcentre every day or go on a full-time intensive programme to tackle the underlying reasons for their failure to find work. In his set-piece speech to the Conservative party conference in Manchester, the chancellor will also repeatedly warn that the battle to secure Britain's recovery is not yet "even close to being over" – a message designed to remind voters of the risk of returning to Labour. The £300m jobs programme, appealing to the electorate's demand for stronger welfare measures, will start in April and will be aimed at 200,000 jobseekers allowance claimants. Polling suggests there is continued public support for ever tougher welfare crackdowns and with the Tories trailing in the polls and in need of a strong response to Ed Miliband's populist conference speech last week, strategists have returned to one of their strongest and most familiar policy areas to push the message that they are on the side of hard-working people. Osborne will be talking about this on the Today programme in a moment. I will be covering the interview in detail, as well as the speech and all the reaction to it. Here's the agenda for the day. 8.10am: George Osborne is interviewed on the Today programme. 9.15am: Session on the environment and climate change, with Owen Paterson, the environment secretary. 10.30am: Session on the economy, with speeches from Patrick McLoughlin, the transport secretary, and George Osborne, the chancellor. 12.30pm: Fringe meetings, including Nigel Farage on Europe, Liam Fox on conservatism and Jeremy Hunt on the general election. 2.30pm: Session on home affairs and justice, with speeches from Chris Grayling, the justice secretary, and Theresa May, the home secretary. I'll be covering all the breaking news from the conference, as well as looking at the papers and bringing you the best conference-related comment from the web. I'll post a summary at 1pm and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I'm on @AndrewSparrow

headline: Politics live: readers' edition - Thursday 15 August
trail_text: Share breaking news, leave links to interesting articles online and chat about the day's political events in our open thread
body_text: I'm not writing my usual Politics Live blog today, but, as an alternative, here's Politics Live: readers' edition. It's intended to be a place where you can catch up with the latest news and find links to good politics blogs and articles on the web. Please feel free to use this as somewhere you can comment on any of the day's political stories - just as you do when I'm writing the daily blog. It would be particularly useful for readers to flag up new material in the comments - breaking news or blogposts or tweets that are worth passing on because someone is going to find them interesting. A lot of what I do on my blog is aggregation - finding the good stuff and passing it on - and you can do this, too (as I know, because it happens every day when I'm blogging). All of today's Guardian politics stories are here, and all the politics stories filed on Wednesday including some in today's paper, are here.
