It's a heavyweight, serious book, but it's a pleasure to read and it's full of shrewd insights. I'd recommend it highly. Ian Paisley, the DUP MP, asks if anyone in government has thanked the Americans for dumping on their oldest allies, the French, and taking the Syrian issue to Congress. Philip Hammond says the government does not tell other countries how to conduct their affairs. Labour's Barry Sheerman asks what the government's strategy over Syria. From last week's debate it became clear that no one in government had spoken to the new leadership in Iran or in China about this. Philip Hammond says Britain is pushing for a diplomatic solution. Sheerman should raise this topic at Foreign Office questions tomorrow, he says. Labour's Ben Bradshaw asks why the intelligence document published by the Americans was so much more informative than the document published by the government on Thursday? Why did the government force MPs to vote too early? Philip Hammond says the government wanted to avoid the mistakes made by Labour over Iraq. It published an assessment from the joint intelligence committee, based on information that could be scrutinised by MPs. In the Commons Labour's Tom Watson says a commentator in Australia recently characterised the Syrian conflict as not "goodies versus baddies, but baddies versus baddies". Does Philip Hammond agree? Hammond says simple descriptions are often unsatisfactory. But in this case it is true to say that some of those on the rebel side are deeply unpleasant, he says. In the Commons the SNP's Angus Robertson has just asked Andrew Murrison, the defence minister, about revelations that Britain has helped to train Syrian army officers. Murrison said that he did not know the details, but that the army did train foreign officers and that it kept these arrangements under review. That may be all we get on Syria from defence questions. I suppose Philip Hammond, the defence secretary, has clarified what Number 10 meant when it said that there were "absolutely no plans" for a second vote on Syria. (See 12.27pm.) This is what Hammond said. Circumstances would have to change very significantly before parliament would want to look again at this issue. Jim Murphy, the shadow defence secretary, says Cameron ruled out UK action after last week's vote. In what circumstances would the government bring this issue back to parliament? Hammond says it is "a bit rich" for Murphy, who voted against the government last week, to ask when the government will revisit this issue. He says the government thinks parliament has spoken and that it is unlikely to want to revisit it unless the circumstances change "very significantly". Sir Gerald Howarth, a Conservative, asks if Hammond agrees that the strength of the relationship between the US and the UK is crucial. Hammond says this relationship is key to Britain's defence. The relationship is a resilient one. David Cameron spoke to President Obama after last week's vote. Hammond says that, following that conversation, he is confident the relationship will remain close. Labour's Gisela Stuart asks why the government has ruled out a second vote on Syria. Hammond says the government cannot keep asking parliament the same question. The situation would have to change quite considerably for another vote to be justified, he says. In the Commons Anne McIntosh, a Conservative, asks what discussions Philip Hammond has had with Nato and the Arab League about Syria. Hammond says Britain maintains the closest possible dialogue with regional partners. The Nato secretary general issued a statement today. He says Britain is the second largest donor of humanitarian aid. Labour's David Winnick asks about the proposal to export chemicals to Syria. Is there any regime in the world Britain won't trade with? Hammond says Winnick makes his case with passion, but without facts. Those export licences were revoked, he says. He also says that many industrial chemicals can be misused. ICM have just sent me the full results of their Syria poll for the BBC. I've already mentioned the headline findings (see 1.49pm), but the one that most surprises me is the one about the special relationship. ICM asked: "Britain is often thought to have a ‘Special Relationship’ with the United States of America. Given the decision of British MPs to stop the British government participating in an American-led military intervention in Syria, do you agree or disagree that the Special Relationship is not relevant in the modern age and we should not be concerned about hurting American feelings?" And the results were: Agree: 67% Disagree: 27% So, 67% of the public apparently think the "special relationship" is "not relevant in the modern age". And here are two other interesting findings. • Some 49% said the Commons vote against military intervention in Syria would damage Britain's reputation and standing in the world, while 44% disagreed. • By a narrow margin voters seem to think that David Cameron has handled the Syria situation better than Ed Miliband. When respondents were asked if they approved or disapproved of their handling of the current situation regarding Syria, Cameron scored 40% approve/42% disapprove (net disapproval of -2) while Miliband scored 33% approve/39% disapprove (net disapproval of -6). Given that voters thought MPs were right to reject Cameron's motion, this is slightly odd, and, given that Miliband normally performs well below Cameron in overall approval ratings, the fact that he is only four points behind Cameron on this question might be deemed a good result. Philip Hammond will be taking defence questions in the Commons shortly. Syria is bound to come up, but not immediately. The tenth question on the order paper is about the crisis there. And it may well come up in topical questions at 3.15pm. • Downing Street has reaffirmed its opposition to the idea of giving MPs a second chance to approve military intervention in Syria. Although the Foreign Office minister Alistair Burt said the government should "never say never" to a second vote, Number 10 said David Cameron had "absolutely no plans" for such a vote and Nick Clegg went even further. I cannot foresee any circumstances in which we would go back to parliament again on the same question and the same issue. We can't go back asking the same question over and over. So no, I can't foresee such circumstances. Curiously, while the coalition is backing away from military action, some on Labour's front bench are stressing that they are not opposed to the idea. In a blog Jim Murphy, the shadow defence secretary, said that "a policy of indefinite inaction regardless of what happens in Syria or at the UN would be unwise" (see 12.46pm) and this morning Chuka Umunna, the shadow business secretary, said that "if in light of changing circumstances, the Prime Minister chooses to come back to parliament, then as a responsible opposition we must consider that." We have not quite got to the point where the two sides have switched, but, with the coalition positon shifting abruptly since Thursday, and Labour adopting a slightly different tone, we have now got to the point where Labour are sounding more interventionist. Perhaps that's a sign that neither Labour nor the coaliton are entirely comfortable with where they ended up last week? • Cameron has been urged by Conservatives to reconsider his decision to rule out a second vote on Syria. In his Telegraph column Boris Johnson suggested that the government should stage a second vote at some point in the future. (See 9.30am.) Bob Stewart, the Conservative MP, said he was "saddened" that Cameron was ruling out military intervention. Clearly the government’s view at the moment is it's game, set and match with regard to a military option and I’m rather saddened by that. I think we should always leave all our options on the table and frankly we just don’t know what will happen in Syria. After all what will happen to public opinion if lots more people are killed either by chemical weapons or, indeed, by other forms of killings such as artillery. And Andrew Mitchell, the former chief whip and international development secretary, told the World at One that a second vote should not be ruled out. • A BBC poll has found that 71% of the public believe MPs were right to vote against military intervention on Syria last week. As the BBC reports, the poll also suggests 72% do not think the move will damage the UK-US relationship - although two thirds would not care if it did. • Clegg has said the number of two-year-olds entitled to free childcare will double next year. • A Commons committee has published plans that would make it easier for MPs to get backbench legislation onto the statute book. Under the proposals from the procedure committee, timetabling would be introduced to stop private members' bills being blocked by a handful of MPs filibustering and bills popular with MPs would be given precedence. The government would also be forced to say whether backbench bills were actually "handout" government bills. Charles Walker, the committee chair, said reform was needed. The initiation, scrutiny and passage of private Members’ bills goes to the heart of the function of the House of Commons as a legislative assembly. The ability of any Member to bring forward a legislative proposition, and to have it debated, is the clearest indication that so far as legislation is concerned the House is not a mere sausage machine, churning out endless bills introduced, timetabled, amended and whipped through by the executive. Yet over a period of many years the House and its members have allowed this important aspect of its procedures to be devalued and degraded. The weight of evidence which we have received demonstrates a clear desire across the House for change. • The TUC has complained to the European Commission about the way Britain implements the agency workers directive. It says that a loophole means that agency workers are still not always getting paid the same as the people they work alongside. • Margaret Hodge, the chair of the public accounts committee, has called on HMRC officials and Treasury ministers to urgently examine an £84bn deal being brokered by Vodafone that is expected to avoid UK taxes. • An army colonel has said that claims that British soldiers mutilated the bodies of Iraqis killed in battle were "baseless rumours". As the Press Association reports, Colonel Adam Griffiths told the Al-Sweady Inquiry he had not seen any evidence to suggest that bodies taken back to a British camp after the Battle of Danny Boy were mistreated. • Nick Clegg has rejected calls for the introduction of legislation banning the advertising of unlawful unpaid internships. • NHS England is to publish quarterly lists of all the "never events" recorded in the NHS, broken down by each trust, the Health Service Journal has reported. As the Press Association reports, such events are serious, largely preventable patient safety incidents which are never supposed to happen in the health service. • Sir Malcolm Bruce has said he is standing down as MP for Gordon at the next election. Jim Murphy, the shadow defence secretary, says he is unhappy that David Cameron has now ruled out military action in Syria. He's explained his thinking in a blog. Here's an excerpt. There is some unease about the outcome off the vote and I share it. It's not what I wanted. I supported the amendment tabled by the Labour Party that explicitly didn't rule out military action if certain stringent conditions were met. That was the case made by my Party Leader, Ed Miliband. I agreed with every word of what Ed Miliband said in his speech. That's what I wanted to be passed. That's what I wanted to happen. I believe in a conditions-based approach to any military intervention in Syria. All three Party leaders in the Commons on Thursday night each made a variant of that argument. Mr Cameron made a bad argument from a reasonable case and couldn't persuade Parliament. I share the unease that we have gone from a stringent conditions-based approach to any UK military action to an unconditional policy of UK military inaction. The unusual thing about Thursday's vote is that most MPs voted for an in principle policy of not ruling out military action in the future. The Labour policy attracted 220 votes and the Government motion won the support of 272, meaning that of the 550-odd MPs who voted 492 supported a version of conditions-based potential use of UK military force if very tight criteria had been met. Just a minority of MPs in all parties opposed any military action in all circumstances for reasons of conscience or concern. Yet that is where our country ended up. The government are right to say that Parliament has spoken and I agree. But a policy of indefinite inaction regardless of what happens in Syria or at the UN would be unwise. An attack on an ally by Assad or further chemical atrocities would give the PM a right to bring this back to Parliament. But he cannot just re-run last weeks vote and hope for a different result. Labour's Chuka Umunna told BBC News that it was "insulting" to suggest, as Nick Clegg did (see 9.36am) that Labour voted against the government on Syria last week for party political reasons. What was at the forefront of our minds and what is still at the forefront of our minds is 'would any proposed action put the Syrian people in a better position and would it make matters worse?' Issues of politics as I have seen discussed in the media have simply not been at the forefront of our minds. Frankly I find it quite insulting when people make these allegations because I know certainly, personally, as a member of Parliament, when you vote on and make decisions on these types of issues, you look at what is in the national interest and you look at the lives of the people you will affect if you sanction any action. I've taken the quote from PoliticsHome. Here are the main points from the Number 10 lobby briefing. • David Cameron is opposed to calls for parliament to reconsider the case for military intervention in Syria at some point in the future in the light of new evidence emerging and next week's vote in Congress. "The prime minister's view is that parliament has spoken," the prime minister's spokesman said. "That's why the government has absolutely no plans to go back to parliament". The spokesman did not say that the government would never allow a second vote, but he strongly played down the idea that this might happen. He used the phrase "absolutely no plans" several times. That's a new piece of spin lingo to me. It's stronger than "no plans" (which normally means there's a distinct possibility of said eventuality happening, but they're just not announcing it yet), but weaker than a "ruling out". • Downing Street rejected claims that Cameron had over-interpreted last week's Commons vote. Although Cameron says the Commons voted against military intervention, around 500 MPs voted for at least one of the two motions (the government one and the Labour one) accepting the case for military intervention in principle. One colleague said that MPs had just rejected Cameron's timetable, not the case for military intervention per se, and that, by ruling out military action, Cameron was misreading the will of parliament. The spokesman did not accept this. • The spokesman would not rule out Britain assisting an American attack on Syria by allowing the Americans access to a British base or through the supply of intelligence. On bases, he said that there had been no request for the use of British bases, but he refused an opportunity to say Britain would not provide assistance in this way. And, on intelligence, he said: We have a close intelligence relationship with a number of allies, particularly with the US. One feature of this is that we do not comment on the details. • Downing Street dismissed concerns about the government approving the export of chemicals used to make sarin gas to Syria. (See 10.50am.) The fact that the chemicals were never actually sent to Syria shows the EU sanctions regime, of which Britain is "a very active part", is working, the spokesman said. The facts are that the licences were revoked and the exports did not take place. The prime minster's view is that that demonstrates that the system is working. • Number 10 dismissed concerns that the lobbying bill would stop charities from campaigning. In the 2010 election only "very, very few charities" had to register as third-party participants, the spokesman said. Provided charities are not promoting the electoral success or otherwise of particular parties or candidates, then they will not be affected [by this bill]. That dragged on, to little purpose. The key point is that Number 10 is not interested in another vote on Syria. I'll post a summary in a moment. More on Boris. (See 9.56am.) I'm just off to the lobby briefing. I'll post again after 11.30am. You can read all today's Guardian politics stories here. And all the politics stories published yesterday, including some in today's paper, are here. As for the rest of the papers, here's the PoliticsHome list of top 10 must-reads, here's the ConservativeHome roundup of today's political stories, and here's the New Statesman list of top 10 comment articles. I've already mentioned the Boris Johnson's column. Here are two other articles I found particularly interesting. • The Independent says officials authorised the export to Syria of two chemicals used to make sarin gas a year ago. The Government was accused of “breathtaking laxity” in its arms controls last night after it emerged that officials authorised the export to Syria of two chemicals capable of being used to make a nerve agent such as sarin a year ago. The Business Secretary, Vince Cable, will today be asked by MPs to explain why a British company was granted export licences for the dual-use substances for six months in 2012 while Syria’s civil war was raging and concern was rife that the regime could use chemical weapons on its own people. The disclosure of the licences for potassium fluoride and sodium fluoride, which can both be used as precursor chemicals in the manufacture of nerve gas, came as the US Secretary of State John Kerry said the United States had evidence that sarin gas was used in last month’s atrocity in Damascus. • Geeri Peev in the Daily Mail says Jack Straw's son Will has been selected to fight Rossendale and Darwen, a target seat, for Labour. It was a Labour seat before the 2010 general election when the Tories’ Jake Berry won it with a majority of 4,493. Mr Straw, a former Fulbright scholar and Oxford graduate - who dabbled as a drum and bass music promoter - beat five other local candidates to win the post. The Today programme had Peter Hain, Rory Stewart and Nigel Farage talking about Syria before 9am. Here are the key quotes. Peter Hain, the former Labour cabinet minister, condemned "instant" interventionism. I was in the Cabinet and supported Tony Blair in the decision to invade Iraq on the basis of WMD evidence which we largely believed, which was proved to be false, and that really did destroy the credibility of the instant interventionists as it were. On the other hand, the aftermath of the invasion was disastrously handled by the Americans in the main, and the way in which Afghanistan has dragged on and on and on without any clear political strategy has made anyone question whether these interventions are the right thing. Last week’s Syria vote stands on it’s own. The prime minister tried to bounce Parliament prematurely, and I think he created circumstances where we said – hang on, we don’t want anything to do with this. Rory Stewart, the Conservative MP and a former diplomate in Iraq, said Britain needed a middle ground approach. I think that the real choice is that we make sure we don’t lurch from over extension from occupying countries like Iraq to complete isolation and the problem that both the prime Mimister and President Obama is how on earth to hit that middle ground; how do you avoid this obsession - which dominates US foreign policy - of thinking either we go in hard or don't do it at all. That's the most dangerous way of looking at the world and we need to find another approach. Nigel Farage, the Ukip leader, said the Commons was right to vote against intervention last week. I think when you look at the war aims when we started off in Afghanistan and in Iraq, and as the years have gone by those war aims appear to change and here we are now years down the road and the general feeling that we’ve got a lot of our people being wounded, we’re expending a lot of money and we’re not really achieving anything. So I personally took the view that firing hundreds of cruise missiles into Syria would not make things any better ... Harold Wilson was really pressurised very strongly by Lyndon Johnson to send British troops into Vietnam, he was told ‘we have a special relationship, your duty-bound’ and Wilson said ‘no’. That didn’t damage our relationship with America or our standing in the world. We should be able to make independent decisions. I've taken the quotes from PoliticsHome. Earlier I described Boris Johnson's Telegraph column as "unequivocally supportive" of David Cameron. (See 9.30am.) On reflection, I'm not sure that's quite right, not least because the Johnson/Cameron relationship is one of the most complex and intriguing in British politics. I've read the line about President Obama's decision to delay an attack on Syria being a "huge tribute" to Cameron several times to see if there is any hidden irony in there and I suspect there is. Ostensibly Johnson is being supportive, but Johnson's a subtle writer and that "huge tribute" carries a reminder that Obama's tribute consists of not making the same cataclysmic cock-up as Cameron. It's also worth noting that, while the government is ruling out a second vote on Syria, Johnson is craftily ramping up the pressure for one. This is typical. Whatever Cameron proposes in policy terms, Johnson seems temperamentally inclined to advocate the opposite. But Nick Clegg has said that he cannot see the case for a second vote on Syria. He was speaking to journalists after delivering his childcare speech (see 9.14am) and he was echoing what William Hague and George Osborne were saying yesterday. We're not going to keep asking the same question of parliament again and again. We live in a democracy, the executive cannot act in a way which clearly is not welcome to Parliament or the British people, so we're not proposing to do so. I can't foresee any circumstances that we would go back to Parliament on the same question, on the same issue. He also accused Labour of "point-scoring" over the issue. My own view is that the Labour Party seemed to take this as an opportunity to score party political points as much as rise to the challenge and the gravity of the issue. From time to time there are just issues - and this is clearly one of them - where I actually don't think the British people are particularly interested in political point-scoring at Westminster. They really just want their representatives to look at it in parliament in a sober and thoughtful way, which is by and large what happened last Thursday in the debate. I've already mentioned Boris Johnson's column on Syria in the Telegraph, but it's worth quoting at length (not least because it's a rare example of a Johnson column that it is unequivocally supportive of Cameron). Johnson says that President Obama's decision to delay military action is "a huge tribute" to Cameron. It is a recognition of the role he has played in leading the response to the atrocities of Assad of Syria. It is also, frankly, a reflection of the quandary we all face. It seems overwhelmingly likely that the forces of the Syrian regime have indeed used chemical weapons, and killed hundreds of civilians in an act of utter savagery. I wrote in this space a few weeks ago of my deep anxieties about our getting embroiled in Syria — and I still have them. He predicts that Congress will eventually back a limited strike against Syria. And he suggests that parliament should have a second vote so that Britain can participate too. There is one British figure that excites the unanimous scorn of all American politicians, and that is Neville Chamberlain. The Americans have a horror of appeasement and the notion of failing to stand up to dictators. After due consideration, I bet Washington will endorse a limited and punitive strike against Assad — provided it can be shown that the intervention will not escalate and provided there is no hint that it will lead to boots on the ground. By then, too, the evidence against Assad may have solidified. The UN will have had longer to report. If there is new and better evidence that inculpates Assad, I see no reason why the Government should not lay a new motion before Parliament, inviting British participation – and then it is Ed Miliband, not David Cameron, who will face embarrassment. The Labour leader has been capering around pretending to have stopped an attack on Syria – when his real position has been more weaselly. If you add the Tories and Blairites together, there is a natural majority for a calibrated and limited response to a grotesque war crime. I predict that by the end of this episode it will be Labour that looks divided, and David Cameron who looks the statesman. In the meantime, the West has longer to weigh up the two evils – doing nothing and doing something. That is a delay for which we can thank the British Parliament, and proof (if you really needed it) that Britain matters a great deal. At the end of last week it looked relatively simple; the House of Commons voted against military intervention in Syria, and Washington seemed poised to go ahead with missile strikes on its own. But, following President Obama’s surprise announcement at the weekend, the attack has now been delayed, Obama is following the British government in trying to get the approval of his legislature before he acts (although he will be hoping to do rather a better job of persuading his lawmakers than Cameron did) and in Britain some figures are now arguing that parliament should think again. William Hague and George Osborne rejected this idea yesterday, but the proposal is unlikely to go away quickly. In his Telegraph column Boris Johnson, the mayor of London, suggests that there should in due course be a second Commons vote on Syria. Today this argument may surface in the Commons when Philip Hammond takes defence questions. His Labour shadow, Jim Murphy, was involved in an f-word embellished row with Michael Gove over Syria after the vote last week. For all the global developments on Syria, do follow our Middle East Live blog. Here’s the full agenda for the day. 8.30am: Nick Clegg delivers a speech on childcare. As Nicholas Watt reports, he will say that nearly half of all two-year-olds in England who will benefit from 15 hours of free childcare from next September. 9.30am: The Al-Sweady Inquiry into claims that British soldiers murdered 20 or more Iraqis and tortured detainees after the “Battle of Danny Boy” in Iraq in 2004 starts is expected to start taking evidence from military witnesses. 11am: Number 10 lobby briefing. 11am: The Commons procedure committee publishes a report on private members’ bill. 2.30pm: Philip Hammond, the defence secretary, takes questions in the Commons. As usual, I’ll also be covering all the breaking political news as well as looking at the papers and bringing you the best politics from the web. I’ll post a lunchtime summary at around 1pm and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I’m on @AndrewSparrow.

headline: SNP conference - Alex Salmond's speech: Politics live blog
trail_text: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of the SNP conference in Perth, including a keynote speech from Alex Salmond, the SNP leader and Scotland's first minister
body_text: • Alex Salmond, Scotland's first minister, has used his speech to the SNP's conference to urge Scots to complete their 100-year journey towards home rule. He made the call in a speech that also announced 26 November as the date of the publication of the Scottish government's white paper on independence, declared that an SNP government in an independent Scotland would never let the value of the minimum wage rise by less than inflation, and unveiled a £60m investment programme. (See 4.12pm.) It is our privilege in this generation to determine the next chapter of Scotland’s story. And when the pages of books yet unwritten speak to generations yet unborn of this time and this place, of our Scotland today, what is the story they will tell? They can say that we who lived at this special time recognised a priceless moment for what it was. That those who saw this chance did not baulk at it. That those who were given this moment did not let it pass by. And that we, Scotland’s independence generation, reached out and grasped the opportunity of a lifetime when it came our way. • Labour has accused Salmond of just offering Scots "empty promises". This is the statement put out in response to the speech from Anas Sarwar, Labour's deputy leader in Scotland. Alex Salmond spoke for nearly three quarters of an hour but didn't have a single thing to say about people's lives today - just empty promises about an imagined world after independence. When will our first minister stop acting like he is in opposition and realise he is in Government? This speech was all about the powers he wants, not about what he will do with the powers he's got. Scotland remains on pause while the nationalists dream up goodies for the referendum campaign that they know they won't have to deliver on. At the start of his conference, the first minister asked the people of Scotland who they trust. After days of uncosted promises and baseless assertions, the answer won't be Alex Salmond and the SNP. • Alistair Carmichael, the Scottish secretary, has said the SNP has still not provided answers about independence. He issued this statement in response to Salmond's speech. The SNP has had eighty years to work up its independence position so it is surprising that it has taken them this long to produce their White Paper. Let's hope the wait will have been worth it. Scots deserve answers based on fact, not assertion. In areas like pensions, financial services, defence sector jobs where they would have the power to act, the Scottish government should answer the detailed questions about what independence would mean. In those areas where it could not act alone, but would need to negotiate with others - including on the terms of EU membership and where it wants to share arrangements with the rest of the UK on the currency that Scotland would use and welfare systems - the Scottish government should acknowledge that fact too. So far the SNP's track record has been to say anything and do anything to win the referendum. That's all from me for today. Thanks for the comments. Alex Salmond's speech - Analysis: The first sign that this might be a rather news-lite speech came when I heard Radio Scotland say this morning that Salmond might use it to announce the date of the white paper on independence. Admittedly, the date of a long-awaited announcement is a story. But it's a very dull one, of interest only to news desk planners (I know - I used to be one eons ago), and it does suggest that Salmond's media people were a bit short of meat to feed the media. To be fair, there were better stories in the speech. The £60m investment sounds like a proper, solid announcement. And the fair work commission (the one that would ensure the minimum wage always rises at least in line with inflation) was a real story too, even if the suggestion that this amounted to a £675 increase was a tad disingenuous. (Because this is not a sum he's planning to hand out. This sum just represents the amount the low paid might have got if this policy had been in force for the last five years. Salmond explained this - see 3.37pm - but a casual listener might have got the wrong impression.) What was interesting about this announcement is that, like Nicola Sturgeon on energy, it's another example of the main political parties converging on the same policy agenda. Ed Miliband went into his party conference promising to raise the minimum wage, and Vince Cable is looking at this area too. Still, even if the speech did not have much news firepower, it had other virtues. The challenge to Ineos was bold, although it could backfire if the firm ignore him. Salmond's declaration that, if David Cameron is not prepared to debate independence with him, he should stay out of the argument altogether has a certain logic (although it won't persuade Cameron). And, even if the phrase "independence generation" is one that Salmond has used before, in his peroration (see 3.48pm) Salmond finally achieved a gear-change from workmanlike to inspiring. What was best, though, was the argument at the heart of it, and the attempt to bind the case for independence with a defence of Scottish collectivism. "In a properly-run democracy governments exist to reconcile collective action and individual aspiration," Salmond said, and he provided a host of examples - Scottish Water, Royal Mail, youth unemployment, the Commonwealth Games, personal care, tuition fees - to show that Scotland is more collectivist than Westminster. Even if the point about youth unemployment was a bit naughty ( I have never heard any Tory proposing turning down jobs money on the grounds it comes from Europe - see 3.27pm), the list overall was persuasive. "Today's nationalist focus is all about defending the sense – and to some extent the reality – that Scotland is the last bastion of the 1945 welfare state nation," my colleague Martin Kettle says in the Guardian today. Salmond's speech was an eloquent demonstration that he's right. There were five news lines in the Salmond speech. • Salmond said that an SNP government in an independent Scotland would guarantee to increase the minimum wage every year at least in line with inflation. • He announced a £60m investment in 43 economic development projects. These would create over 3,000 jobs, he said. • He said the white paper on independence would be published on 26 November. • He told Ineos, the firm that owns the Grangemouth oil refinery, that it should "fire up the plant ... now" as a step towards resolving the industrial dispute there. • He challenged David Cameron to either hold a debate with him - or stay out of the argument about Scottish independence. I will post a more detailed analysis shortly. And here's the peroration. We should remember that Scotland has been on a Home Rule journey for well over a century Twice in the more recent chapters of that story have the people been asked the question: “Yes or No?” And twice already they have said “Yes”, once narrowly and then overwhelmingly. So it is our privilege in this generation to determine the next chapter of Scotland’s story. And when the pages of books yet unwritten speak to generations yet unborn of this time and this place, of our Scotland today, what is the story they will tell? They can say that we who lived at this special time recognised a priceless moment for what it was. That those who saw this chance did not baulk at it. That those who were given this moment did not let it pass by. And that we, Scotland’s independence generation, reached out and grasped the opportunity of a lifetime when it came our way. We will NOT wake up on the morning of 19 September next year and think to ourselves what might have been. We WILL wake up on that morning filled with hope and expectation – ready to build a new nation both prosperous and just. After almost a century of Scotland moving forward to this very moment – let us ask ourselves these simple questions: If not us - then who? If not now - then when? Friends - we ARE Scotland’s independence generation. And our time is now. People are starting to imagine a better life, better communities and a better country. We are truly privileged. Because in less than one year’s time we can stop imagining. And we can start building. Building the Scotland we know is possible. Salmond quotes the late Iain Banks, the writer. The late, great Iain Banks, said independence will boost the morale of the nation. He wrote of what he believed would be the “sheer energisation of a whole people.” That energy can already be felt across Scotland, Salmond says. The independence white paper will do two things, Salmond says. First, it will spell out the platform that we will establish for Scotland between the referendum next year and the first elections for an independent Scottish Parliament in the spring of 2016. It will therefore be clear that independence is not at its heart about this party or this administration or this First Minister but about fundamental democratic choice for Scotland - the peoples’ right to choose a Government of our own. And secondly the White Paper will set out the why of independence, our vision of Scotland – the Scotland that we seek. Salmond says the independence white paper will be published on 26 November. Salmond turns to wages. Delegates – right now a Westminster Government that people in Scotland overwhelmingly rejected is giving tax cuts to millionaires at the same time as cutting the income of the low paid. In contrast - almost exactly a year ago the Scottish Government announced it was bringing in a new living wage of £7.45 per hour. This covers the 160,000 people in Scotland working for central government, its agencies and the NHS. This is part of what we call the “social wage” - the contract between the people of Scotland and their Government ... With independence we should have the aspiration to achieve a living wage for all workers – not just those under the responsibility of government. Yesterday Nicola explained how we intend to encourage the private sector to move towards the living wage. And today I can announce further steps toward achieving that ambition. Around 70,000 people currently receive the minimum wage in Scotland. In real terms the minimum wage has failed to increase in almost a decade. Indeed in every single year since the recession of 2008 that minimum wage has failed to keep up with the cost of living. If elected, on independence - this Scottish Government will establish a Fair Work Commission. THE CENTRAL PILLAR OF THAT COMMISSION WILL BE TO SET A MINIMUM WAGE GUARANTEE. I CAN ANNOUNCE THAT THIS GUARANTEE WILL ENSURE A MINIMUM WAGE THAT RISES - AT THE VERY LEAST - IN LINE WITH INFLATION. LET US PLEDGE THAT NEVER AGAIN WILL WAGES OF THE LOWEST PAID IN SCOTLAND FAIL TO KEEP UP WITH THE COST OF LIVING. If this had been in force in the last five years – the lowest paid Scots would today be a total of almost £675 better off. Friends, no-one in this party claims that an independent Scotland will be able to wish away global competition. We will still be affected by it, influenced by it and often challenged by it. No-one in this world owes Scotland a living. But there is a difference that will make the difference. An independent Scotland will have a Parliament with the full range of powers and the people will have a Government which is on their side. Salmond turns to the Grangemouth dispute. I grew up in Linlithgow and I have been conscious of the fires of Grangemouth all of my life but right now that plant is idle and cold. And as the standoff continues the threat to Grangemouth grows. So let us inject some commonsense into this position. To the union drop any strike threat To the management fire up the plant and then negotiate against the background of a working facility not one which is in mortal danger. Find common ground. So let me be quite clear. SCOTLAND WANTS TO SEE GRANGEMOUTH OPERATING AND THE PEOPLE OF GRANGEMOUTH WORKING. FIRE UP THE PLANT AND DO IT NOW. The economic system is not working for Scotland, Salmond says. The difference in economic performance between areas is greater in the UK than any other European Union nation. It has created one of the biggest gaps between rich and poor in the developed world – the fourth most unequal society in the OECD. That means people from ordinary backgrounds find it harder to get on and to get good jobs. And it means something else. This level of inequality offends the very basis of a good society. IT FOSTERS DIVISION. IT STIFLES AMBITION. AND NO INDEPENDENT SCOTTISH GOVERNMENT WOULD EVER ACCEPT SUCH AN APPALLING WASTE OF TALENT AND POTENTIAL. In a properly run democracy governments exist to reconcile collective action and individual aspiration. An effective water service and a socially committed postal service are essential platforms which allow business to grow and communities to prosper. Salmond says Scottish Water shows the benefits of public ownership. Despite privatisation elsewhere in the UK, Scottish Water remains in public hands and now Scotland has the lowest average household water bills in these Islands. Conference - If we can make a success of Scottish Water we can make a success of the Royal Mail. If elected in an independent Scotland I give this pledge: An SNP government will bring our Royal Mail back into public hands. The Edinburgh playwright David Greig says the independence debate allows us to explore every aspect of our national life and ask ourselves the question – ‘does it have to be like this?’ I don’t believe it does. The Royal Mail is an example, Salmond says. Four years ago the previous Labour Westminster government attempted to sell-off the Royal Mail. The plans were fiercely resisted in Scotland and rightly so. But this month we were reminded of a painful lesson. Regardless of what people in Scotland think or believe if Westminster is determined to sell off Scotland’s public assets then it will find a way. In the face of massive public opposition and ignoring the wishes of almost all Scottish MPs our postal service was privatised by a government we didn’t elect. They hailed the sale of a profitable business at a knock-down price as a good thing. It is the equivalent of selling off £10 notes for a fiver and calling it a success! The third subject at the London meeting was the bedroom tax, Salmond says. Delegates, in the 1990s, the Poll Tax became a symbol of why devolution was necessary. The Bedroom Tax is becoming a symbol of why independence is necessary. Remember - the Bedroom Tax was opposed by 90% of Scottish MPs in the House of Commons - yet it still passed and now penalises 80,000 households in Scotland – 80% include a disabled person. In August it was condemned by a special rapporteur for the United Nations. The Chairman of the Conservative Party said it was “disgraceful” that she was commenting. THAT IS NOT THE DISGRACE. THE DISGRACE IS THAT A UNITED NATIONS REPRESENTATIVE WAS FORCED TO COMMENT ON AN INJUSTICE IN 21ST CENTURY SCOTLAND. DELEGATES – LET ME BE CLEAR - ONE OF THE FIRST ACTS OF THE SNP IN A GOVERNMENT OF AN INDEPENDENT SCOTLAND WILL BE TO SCRAP THE BEDROOM TAX. Then we had a discussion on the great sporting and cultural events being held in these islands. The Olympic Games was a great event, the Derry/Londonderry cultural city is a great event. The Commonwealth Games will be a great event. But one reason we all feel positive about Glasgow 2014 is that we are not financing a great Games out money which should be going to good causes. More than a year after the Olympics Scottish good causes are still waiting for the promised return of £114 million of lottery funding which was diverted to fund the London Games. IN CONTRAST EVERY PENNY PIECE OF FUNDING FOR THE COMMONWEALTH GAMES AND ITS LEGACY IS BEING DELIVERED BY THE SCOTTISH GOVERNMENT, THE CITY OF GLASGOW AND COMMERCIAL SPONSORSHIP – AND IT WILL BE THE GREATEST SPORTING EVENT THAT SCOTLAND HAS EVER SEEN. Salmond says he met Cameron on Wednesday for the joint ministerial committee meeting in London. Let me give you a taste of three aspects of that meeting to see the difference of perspective between the Governments – not just Governments but political cultures- of these islands. Youth unemployment – a fundamental issue facing society as we move out of recession. Scotland is the only country in Europe with a dedicated Youth Employment Minister in Angela Constance. We have a youth guarantee in Scotland that any youngster between 16-19 without a job, education or training gets the offer of help within 4 months. It is quick intervention and it works. There is a European proposal to extend this. Westminster opposes it not because it is not a good idea but basically because it is European. We need the freedom to respond to European initiatives in the best way for Scotland, not to have a proposal on training for youngsters distorted through a Eurosceptic lens. I’m delighted to announce one such further initiative today. THE ALLOCATION OF A 60 MILLION POUND PACKAGE SUPPORTING 43 PROJECTS ACROSS THE COUNTRY CREATING MORE THAN 3,000 JOBS. FRIENDS – FROM THE DEVELOPMENT OF A NEW GATEWAY TO WESTER ROSS TO AN INVESTMENT FUND IN FIFE, ALL OF THESE PROJECTS WILL SUPPORT VITAL OPPORTUNITIES FOR OUR YOUNG PEOPLE. And you know what? The fact that it comes from European initiative is something which will not concern the young people who are benefiting - not one jot. WE WILL NOT ALLOW ACTION ON YOUTH UNEMPLOYMENT TO BE RESTRICTED BY THE PAROCHIAL INSULARITY OF WESTMINSTER. These policies show that Scotland has chosen "a different path", Salmond says. A path that reflects Scotland’s social democratic consensus, our shared progressive values - our priorities as a society. Now Labour and Tory dismiss these gains as the luxuries of a something for nothing country – really? Personal care for older people, free tuition for young people, to be cast aside as something for nothing? THIS IS NOT A SOMETHING FOR NOTHING COUNTRY BUT A SOMETHING FOR SOMETHING SOCIETY AND THIS PARTY SHALL DEFEND THAT SOCIAL PROGRESS MADE BY OUR PARLIAMENT. The central question of the coming referendum campaign is who should be taking decisions about Scotland – those who live and work here or Westminster politicians. IT IS A COMMON-SENSE ARGUMENT BASED ON OUR EXPERIENCE. And Salmond sets out the SNP government's record. The record of Scotland’s Parliament has demonstrated that is undeniably the case. Free personal care supported unanimously by every party in the Parliament. The ground-breaking ban on smoking in public places. Protecting the NHS from privatisation. Access to education based on the ability to learn not the ability to pay. A 39 year low in crime and a record number of police officers on our streets. And the Council Tax Freeze – saving hard-pressed families in Scotland £1,200 when just about every other household bill is rising. Mr Burnham’s suggestion is a worthy addition to all the rest – the mobile phone charges, the annexation of Faslane, embassies refusing to hold whisky receptions! Project Fear? More like Project Farce. Salmond says the No campaign describe themselves as Project Fear. Labour’s UK Health spokesman, Andy Burnham, captured the mood of that project. Last month he actually said he is opposed to independence because he doesn’t want to drive up the M6, get out his passport and start driving on the right when he comes to Scotland. Mind you, I thought Labour has been driving on the right for some time. Salmond says one subject he would like to debate is oil. For every one of the last 30 years Scotland has generated more tax per head than the UK as a whole. And without a single penny from the North Sea our national income, per head, is virtually the same as the UK’s. With oil, our economy is almost one-fifth bigger. And we produce six times as much oil as we need. North Sea oil and gas is a huge bonus. Not, however, according to the No campaign. They depict the continuing asset with a wholesale value of 1 and a half TRILLION POUNDS as something else. As a national catastrophe. Perhaps it’s because they are embarrassed. Well so they should be. There are only two countries in the world with the great fortune of having huge oil resources who have failed to establish a savings fund to benefit future generations. The UK and the Republic of Iraq. Salmond renews his call for a debate with David Cameron. [Cameron] promised a respectful debate but then turns the full guns of the Whitehall machine on Scotland. He wants to dictate the terms of the debate without of course debating himself. He wants the power without the democratic responsibility and that is simply not on. SO HERE’S THE DEAL PRIME MINISTER. WE’LL PUBLISH THE WHITE PAPER THEN YOU AND I MUST DEBATE. FIRST MINISTER TO PRIME MINISTER. THE CHOICE IS YOURS. STEP UP TO THE PLATE – OR STEP OUT OF THIS DEBATE! And let us look and laugh at a’ that day tripping of UK Cabinet Ministers coming up to tell us that the ten plagues of Egypt would descend on an independent Scotland. Mind you - you don’t need to be a London Minister to make a fool of yourself on Scottish geography. I’m grateful to the Herald newspaper for pointing out that Danny Alexander yesterday announced a fuel discount scheme for two Highland villages. The problem is these villages no longer have filling stations! Only the Liberal Democrats... Salmond turns to a point made forcefully by John Swinney yesterday; even the opponents of independence accept an independent Scotland would be perfectly viable. The independence debate will always be hard-fought on both sides but on this central issue of whether Scotland could become a successful independent country there is, in fact, no longer any real debate. Just ask the most senior figures in the No Campaign: The Prime Minister says: “It would be wrong to suggest that Scotland could not be another successful, independent country.” Alistair Darling says - the question is not whether Scotland can survive as an independent country. “Of course it could,” he says. I don’t make a habit of agreeing with the Prime Minister and Mr Darling - these days I leave them to agree with each other - but on this issue they are correct. And here's why Scotland could be independent. Scotland is a country rich and fortunate in both human talent and natural resources. · We have more top universities per head than any other country in the world. · Our food and drink industry is entering a golden era. · Scotland has been declared the world’s top travel destination · Foreign investment is at a 15 year high. · Wind, wave and tidal energy can make Scotland the green powerhouse of Europe for decades to come. · We are a hotbed of life science innovation. Recently the SNP's Stewart Hosie debated independence with Labour's George Robertson. At the start of that debate 59 per cent were No voters and 21 per cent Yes. After hearing Stewart and Lord George: 51 per cent Yes and 38 per cent No. A swing by my calculations of 25 per cent! It must have been quite a shock for Lord George. After all didn’t he once predict that devolution would kill the SNP “stone dead.” According to George, we should all be past tense by now. WELL, I HAVE A MESSAGE FOR LORD GEORGE AND THE NO CAMPAIGN: THE SNP ARE THE MAJORITY GOVERNMENT OF SCOTLAND. THE MOVEMENT FOR NATIONAL SELF DETERMINATION IS ALIVE AND WELL AND WE INTEND TO WIN THIS REFERENDUM. Salmond says those who have not made up their minds are likely to vote Yes when they have heard the arguments. I know people at home watching right now and across the country want to know more about independence. They want to hear more about the benefits. They are hungry for information. And we have undertaken to provide that information Because delegates – we know that the more people know about independence the more likely they are to vote YES. Last month broadcast debates were held to mark a year to go to the referendum. The audience was balanced between those in favour of independence, those against and those undecided. The significant thing is this - at the end of a couple of the debates, having heard the arguments, people were then asked how they would vote. The result on both occasions – a majority for independence. WHY? BECAUSE WHEN PEOPLE HEAR THE CAN-DO OPTIMISM OF THE YES CAMPAIGN UP AGAINST THE CAN’T-DO DIRGE OF THE NO CAMPAIGN THEN THEY CHOOSE YES. Salmond says independence will be "an act of national self-confidence and self-belief" We - the people of Scotland - have - by far - the greatest stake in its success and we are - by far – best placed to take decisions about our future. Salmond pays tribute to the late Brian Adams, and says the internship scheme at Holyrood will be named in his memory. We are standing on the shoulders of giants who kept Scotland’s flame alive. And how they would have relished the next eleven months. So let’s keep our colleagues in our hearts as we remember how lucky, how lucky we are to live in this moment. And let’s use their inspiration to secure Scotland’s place as a full and independent member of the family of nations. Salmond is on stage. He starts with a joke. His diet is like the independent campaign; they have come far, but have some way further to go. He turns to next year's vote. In less than one year’s time the people of Scotland will have the opportunity of a generation. That opportunity on September the 18th 2014 is this: TO BUILD A PROSPEROUS COUNTRY TO CREATE A JUST SOCIETY. TO BECOME AN INDEPENDENT COUNTRY. (I apologise for the angry capitals. I'm cutting and pasting from the text I've just been sent, and do not have time to clean up the copy.) Nicola Sturgeon, Alex Salmond's deputy, is introducing Salmond. Salmond says many attributes with Beyonce, she says. It is not his singing voice, she says. It is his new slim-line physique. Some people think he has been taking diet tips from Mervyn King. But in fact he is on the same diet as Beyonce, she says. He is fighting fit, she says. In the conference hall they are now showing a video before Alex Salmond's speech. It starts with a picture of a scan showing a foetus in a womb, narrated by the baby (Kirsty) who says she is going to be born on 18 September next year, the day of the referendum. What type of country will she be born into, she asks. A Scotland where the wealth is in Scotland's hands? Or more of the same old thing? Alex Salmond will be speaking in about 15 minutes. You can follow it live here. Delegates have passed a resolution on the bedroom tax saying an SNP government would scrap it within a year of independence. Some 80,000 households in Scotland are affected by it, the resolution says. Of those, 80% are homes with a disabled adult. NSPCC Scotland and other charities hosted a fringe meeting at lunchtime about children. The speakers included Aileen Campbell, Scotland's minister for children, and the journalist Lesley Riddoch. Here are some tweets from the event. Another clue as to what Alex Salmond will say in his speech. • The SNP has renewed its call for Scotland to be included in the HS2 high-speed rail project following the publication of research showing that Aberdeen and Dundee are among 50 places in the UK expected to be left worse off as a result of HS2. Keith Brown, the Scottish transport minister, said: This information reinforces the case that the Scottish government, business and civic Scotland have been making, which is that HS2 must include Scotland to realise economic benefits for our communities. It also amplifies the point that the business case for the project is strengthened if Scotland is included. That is why we are keeping the pressure on Westminster, and finalising with the UK government, the remit of a study for planning high-speed rail routes linking Scotland and England. • Richard Lochhead, Scotland's environment minister, has claimed that Scottish farmers would lose £850m between 2014 and 2020 because of the stance the UK government has taken in common agricultural policy (CAP) negotiations. (See 11.09am.) • Andrew Wilson, a former MSP, has said that if the SNP wins the independence referendum, it will need to reach out to other parties as it creates a new Scotland. Senior Scottish Labour, Tory and Lib Dem politicians should be included in the team negotiating Scotland's exit from the UK, he suggested. (See 1.20pm.) Here are the the main points from Andrew Wilson's Donaldson lecture. The lecture is given in memory of Arthur Donaldson, an SNP leader from the 1960s. • Wilson said that, if the SNP wins the independence referendum, it will need to reach out to other parties as it creates a new Scotland. Senior Scottish Labour, Tory and Lib Dem politicians should be included in the team negotiating Scotland's exit from the UK, he suggested. During World War II, when Tom Johnston accepted the post of Scottish Secretary he did so on the basis that he could set up a Council of State for Scotland, composed of all the living ex-Secretaries of State for Scotland, of all parties, to advise on Scottish policy. This is an interesting example. Because when we win all of the current opponents of independence will cease to be opponents of independence and should be given a clear stake in shaping the outcome to embed its legitimacy. Maybe our two former First Ministers and one former Labour, Tory and Lib Dem Secretary of State for Scotland could be included in the team negotiating the terms of independence and Scotland's relationship with the rest of the UK? They are hardly going to argue against their country’s interest – although you could be forgiven for mistaking that in some of the more intemperate rhetoric of the moment. • He suggested that there should be more cooperation between the SNP and Labour. And whisper it, maybe the SNP and the Labour Party will be able to recognise that there is so much more that we agree about than disagree and find a way to work together more to create the progressive, unified and successful Scotland we all want? • He said the SNP should transform itself from a nationalist party to a national party. That the next transformation for the SNP is from nationalist to National Party as the unifying leaders of the country as we go about creating the society we seek. There is no existential threat to Scotland and Scottish identity any more and we must define ourselves by much more than that. No party is better placed to lead the country on our next step in the journey and we must bring others with us as we seek to deliver on our exciting vision of a vibrant economy underpinning a much fairer society. • He said the SNP should prepare for defeat as well as for victory in the referendum. Especially as we move through this referendum we need to think carefully about how we retain our unity, discipline and sense of purpose whatever the result. It is crystal clear to me that the only threat to the SNP winning the subsequent election is the SNP itself. Especially in victory, but also in the event of a result we do not seek, it is critical we prepare for our next transformation. • He said the need to tackle inequality was the best argument for independence. The centralised economic and political model is producing a lousy outcome that is unsustainable and must reform whatever happens next September. The theme of inequality is one that will remain in our politics and grate at the established position until it reforms. We must do something about it! • He said the SNP should do more to persuade people that leaving the British state would not lead to Scots having to abandon their British identity. • He said political leaders should not get "stuck in managerialism". If politics gets stuck in managerialism it will never lead the way it is required to. Leaders should lead and administrators and managers should administrate and manage. I often feel that some of the oddest questions faced by our arguments now would be like listening to Nye Bevan outline the case for the NHS, healthcare for all free at the point of need regardless of means, would have been challenged by the politics of now with questions like: “That’s all very well Mr Bevan but how many bedpans will you need in Wishaw and who is going to pay for them”? Now more than ever we need leaders to lead and to offer a vision, direction and goal for our collective ambitions as a society whatever we choose in a year’s time. There was also one other quote that I will include because it's quite fun. As a wise person I cannot source once said, “the most important thing is to make the most important thing, the most important thing”. The SNP is discovering its inner Zen. Here's the full text of Andrew Wilson's Donaldson lecture. I'll post a summary shortly. Andrew Wilson has just finished his Donaldson lecture. Here are some tweets from it. I'll post a summary when I've seen the full text. There is an anti wind farm protest at the conference today. It has been organised by Scotland against Spin (nothing to do with Alastair Campbell). Here's an extract from their news release. Alex Salmond is now defying the rule of law in his obsession with rolling out ever more wind farms. Borders campaigner Graeme Steel said: "Anyone else would be called a criminal for defying a judge but Alex Salmond and his Big Wind cronies think they can keep get away with it. He is gambling with the rural vote for independence and he loses more supporters every day another wind farm is proposed. With seven new wind applications a day across Scotland, that is a helluva lot of votes he is waving goodbye to." There are also protests taking place against fracking and against the bedroom tax. Here's a clue as to what we can expect in Alex Salmond's speech. Delegates have now voted overwhelmingly in favour of a resolution backing the Common Weal project led by the Jimmy Reid foundation, describing Reid's 1972 rectoral address on alientation (described as one of the great speeches of the 20th century) as "as pertinent now as it was when he made it" and committing the SNP to look at the Common Weal proposals. Here's a paper on the Common Weal project (pdf). And here's an extract from it. There has been much talk about how to achieve a ‘more Nordic’ Scotland. It is important to note that not only Nordic countries have things to teach Scotland and also that the Nordic countries themselves are very different. It is also important to note that they developed in a particular context so there is no ‘standard’ set of practices and policies which will make a country more ‘Nordic’. However, drawing conclusions about what they and others do well provides the starting- point for an alternative model of how Scotland might move forward. It is here referred to as the ‘Common Weal’, a distinctively Scottish version of the type of society that has been achieved in the Nordic area. This short note suggests a ‘spine’ of the key economic and social transformations that are needed to move towards a Common Weal vision. It is not prescriptive and is not ‘owned’ by anyone. It is a model for mutual development, onto which many other transformative ideas can be built. So for example, a Common Weal Scotland would place a strong emphasis on issues such as a diverse and high quality media, a strong arts and cultural identity, a transformed approach to education, new attitudes to transport and urban planning, careful management of natural resources and the environment and so on. Developing all of these and more represent work ahead for those who support a Common Weal vision for Scotland. Here's Reid's 1972 alienation speech. Here's an extract. Alienation is the precise and correctly applied word for describing the major social problem in Britain today. People feel alienated by society. In some intellectual circles it is treated almost as a new phenomenon. It has, however, been with us for years. What I believe is true is that today it is more widespread, more pervasive than ever before. Let me right at the outset define what I mean by alienation. It is the cry of men who feel themselves the victims of blind economic forces beyond their control. It's the frustration of ordinary people excluded from the processes of decision making. The feeling of despair and hopelessness that pervades people who feel with justification that they have no real say in shaping or determining their own destinies ... To the students I address this appeal ... A rat race is for rats. We're not rats. We're human beings. Reject the insidious pressures in society that would blunt your critical faculties to all that is happening around you, that would caution silence in the face of injustice lest you jeopardise your chances of promotion and self-advancement. This is how it starts and before you know where you are, you're a fully paid-up member of the rat-pack. The price is too high. It entails the loss of your dignity and human spirit. Or as Christ put it, "What doth it profit a man if he gain the whole world and suffer the loss of his soul?" Profit is the sole criterion used by the establishment to evaluate economic activity. From the rat race to lame ducks. The vocabulary in vogue is a giveaway. It's more reminiscent of a human menagerie than human society. The power structures that have inevitably emerged from this approach threaten and undermine our hard-won democratic rights. The whole process is towards the centralisation and concentration of power in fewer and fewer hands. The facts are there for all who want to see. Giant monopoly companies and consortia dominate almost every branch of our economy. The men who wield effective control within these giants exercise a power over their fellow men which is frightening and is a negation of democracy. And here's what Kirsty MacAlpine, an SNP activist, said in the debate. I think [Reid's speech] is one of the greatest speeches ever made and one which still resonates today. [He was clear that] in order to bring about radical change you have to combat alienation, so that people believe that change can happen. He perfectly defined alienation as "the feeling of despair and hopelessness that pervades people who feel with justification that they have no real say in shaping or determining their own destinies". We are in the privileged position of having the opportunity to forge a new path for Scotland but people still feel alienated. James Dornan, the MSP, told the conference that the party did not agree with every idea in the Common Weal document. But the SNP and the Jimmy Reid foundation were "travelling in the same direction", he said. Richard Lochhead, Scotland's environment and rural affairs minister, has just finished his speech to the conference. Here are the main points. • Lochhead claimed that Scottish farmers would lose £850m between 2014 and 2020 because of the stance the UK government has taken in common agricultural policy (CAP) negotiations. Scotland went into the negotiations with a pitiful 3rd lowest level of farm support in Europe – Europe then agreed a formula to close the gap between the countries with lowest payments with those of the highest. Scotland has now been leapfrogged by Latvia and Lithuania whose Ministers sit at the top table and make their farmers a priority. Now Conference we are stuck at the bottom of the league. We didn’t benefit from the new funding formula because it only applies to member states. And the UK Government refused to seek a fairer share of the budget for Scotland. As a result, our farmers have lost out big time - indeed, to the tune of a massive £850m. This Scottish government paper (pdf) has more details. • He criticised Owen Paterson, the UK environment secretary, for claiming that an independent Scotland would not be able to promote its food industry effectively. I had to laugh when UK Secretary of State Owen Paterson said last week that an independent Scotland would never be able to promote our produce overseas with the same conviction as the UK Government. Well I’ve got a message for Owen Paterson – when I am overseas I am constantly told that we have the best beef, salmon and whisky in the world. What promotes our food and drink industry abroad is the reputation and quality built up over generations by hard working and talented people and companies. Not politicians in London that make fleeting visits to Scotland. • Lochhead said that he was now dealing with the fifth UK fisheries minister to be appointed since he became Scotland's environment minister. But the inexperienced UK minister still took precedence, he said. On Thursday, I had my first meeting with the new UK fisheries minister [George Eustice] – the 5th UK fisheries minister to come through DEFRA’s revolving door I’ve had to deal with. Once again, a new fisheries minister to get to know, messages on key Scottish issues to get across. And once again, I made the request to speak at the Fisheries Council on our interests like the mackerel dispute – where we hold 75% of the UK quota and are the EU’s largest shareholder. Once again, the UK said no. So, Scotland’s fisheries minister in his 7th year in the job, stopped from speaking by a UK fisheries minister barely 7 working days in the job. • He said some of the most successful countries in the world were small countries. Some of our close neighbours are the most successful. This year, Norway was declared the country with the best quality of life. Sweden was named the world's most sustainable nation And Denmark was crowned the world's happiest country! All small northern European nations, just like us. Here are four of the most interesting articles on the SNP conference from today's papers. • Martin Kettle in the Guardian says the SNP are coming face to face with political reality. The SNP is a fascinating political phenomenon. The only political party in Britain with a really popular leader. The only governing party in Britain with a clear poll lead. One of the few governing parties in Europe whose support has grown through the financial crisis. A party with an enviably energised and effective propaganda machine. And the SNP conference in Perth is the only one this autumn in which the leader's speech seems to have nothing to prove. And yet there's a descant in Perth to the major-key optimism that has marked every SNP conference in the last few years. That descant is political reality. Try as the SNP machine might to spin a more favourable story, the opinion polls continue to point solidly towards a no vote next year. Increasingly, you can discern a more minor-key debate among some Nats. How well can we do to keep the independence issue alive after 2014? What would be an honourable defeat? Or even, given the consistency of the polls, a moral victory? • Alan Cochrane in the Daily Telegraph accuses Nicola Sturgeon of scaremongering in her speech yesterday. [Sturgeon] countered what she says is the fear-agenda of the "antis" with some scare stories of her own, a horror list of what it will be like to remain British. A No vote next September should set alarm bells ringing all over Scotland, she said, as the United Kingdom government dismantles the Scottish health service. Unless we all vote Yes, she said, in another addition to her blood-curdling list, "Westminster will turn the screw". In that event Scotland's social security – presumably including old age pensions and welfare benefits – would be dismantled. It was all so much hokum, and every bit as daft as some of the loonier frights that the Better Together ultras have tried on the Scottish public and which this column has criticised in the past. Ms Sturgeon did have a couple of goodies in her speech – a £70 per year pruning of energy bills and a repeat of the Nat promise to scrap the "bedroom tax". But, of course, her effort was rapturously received by the SNP faithful and, the ludicrous hyperbole aside, it was a first-class conference speech aimed directly at the constituency she's known best: Scotland's Labour vote. • Gerry Hassan in the Scotsman says he is not impressed by the idea that Andrew Wilson will propose in his speech to the conference this morning for a new Scottish constitution. [Andrew Wilson] addresses how an independent Scotland puts together a constitution. Really what Wilson is talking about here is how we do politics and social change. His proposal is for the Scottish Government, in association with a new Council of State (a body originally set up by Tom Johnston in the Second World War) comprising former Secretaries of State for Scotland, to preside over this. There are now a staggering ten living former Secretaries of State for Scotland: nine men and one woman (Helen Liddell), the highest number there ever has been, thanks to New Labour’s constant reshuffles. Is this really the best we can do? It is similar terrain to the post-Leveson discussions ending up with a Royal Charter and the Privy Council, the latter one of the most secretive parts of British government. A post-independent Scotland needs expert advice and authority but what Wilson is doing here is two fold. First, creating mood music about after a Yes vote, and second, placing the SNP as the party of respectability and the new establishment. This might be understandable for party leadership aspirations, but we do need more imagination and boldness in creating a different kind of democracy. • Magnus Gardham in the Herald says the SNP need to win over those Scots who are apathetic about politics. Turn-out at the last Holyrood election was a fraction over 50%. In other words two million people did not cast their ballot. It's a good bet that at least half of them will be tempted to the polls by the historic nature of the referendum but they pose something of a problem for the campaigns. How do they suddenly appeal to people who are sceptical about politics and probably not following every twist and turn in the independence debate? It's worth considering that if you've failed to vote in the last three successive elections you've fallen off the parties' radar completely. You've been marked down as a lost cause, not even worth a knock on the door. The SNP's answer is to keep the message simple and wear out some shoe leather. Strategists speak bafflingly of "geographical and sectoral" campaigning, of taking a "granular approach," of "rearranging the mental furniture," but what it boils down to for hard-to-reach voters is knocking on doors. It's Alex Salmond's day at the SNP conference in Perth and I'm going to start with this pen portrait of Scotland's first minister, from Iain Macwhirter's excellent book, Road to Referendum. It's probably not the kindest thing ever written about Salmond, but it's a nice piece of writing and, if you had to explain to a Martian what's he's like and why he matters, this is as good a summary as any. "The most dangerous man in Britain" does not have a lean and hungry look. Alex Salmond is fat, some would say obese, and is said to bear a disturbing resemblance to a bleached version of the cartoon ogre Shrek. But Salmond is widely regarded as one of the most gifted politicians of his age, with an extraordinary record of achievement, and remains immensely popular - in Scotland at least - even after six years as first minister of Scotland. Salmond is the nearest Scotland has had to a truly national leader in the past 300 years. Indeed, his biographer, David Torrance, invited comparison with Robert the Bruce - though detractors say they would never find a helmet big enough. With less than a year to go until the referendum on Scottish independence, Scotland's pre-eminent "truly national leader" is approaching his moment of destiny. Today he will make his case in his speech to the conference. According to the extracts from the speech released overnight, Salmond will say that voting for independence would be an act of "national self-belief". My colleague Severin Carrell has filed a preview. Here's an extract. Alex Salmond will argue on Saturday that supporting independence in next year's referendum is a natural, commonsense act of "national self-belief" rather than a dangerous leap into the unknown. The first minister, in his main address to the last full Scottish National party conference before next year's vote, will try to strike a reassuring tone, saying Scotland's voters have already had "a taste of independence" thanks to 14 years of devolution without fully realising it. He will say that independence would be a natural evolution of those powers, adding control over welfare, pensions, taxation and defence to the Scottish parliament's already significant responsibilities for schools, hospitals, transport and policing. In a deliberate attempt to play down middle-class voters' anxieties about the economic and political risks of leaving the UK, Salmond will insist that his government and the Scottish parliament have already proved their credentials. "This central question of who should be taking decisions is not an academic argument," Salmond will say. "It is a commonsense argument based on our experience." A yes vote would be "above all, an act of national self-confidence and self-belief". I will be covering the speech in detail, and the reaction to it. Here's the agenda for the day. 10.30am: Richard Lochhead, Scotland's environment minister, gives a speech. 10.45am: Debate on the Common Weal report. 11.30am: Fraternal address from Jonathan Edwards, the Plaid Cymru MP 11.40am: Andrew Wilson gives the Donaldson lecture. Wilson is a former MSP and one of the party's leading thinkers. One official described him to me as “our Douglas Alexander”. 12.30pm: Fringe meetings 2pm: Debate on repeal of the bedroom tax. 3pm: Alex Salmond delivers his speech. 3.40pm: Further debates. 4.30pm: Alex Neil, Scotland's health minister, gives a speech. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I'm on @AndrewSparrow.

headline: Politics live: readers' edition - Friday 4 October
trail_text: Share breaking news, leave links to interesting articles online and chat about the day's political events in our open thread
body_text: I'm not writing my usual Politics Live blog today, but, as an alternative, here's Politics Live: readers' edition. It's intended to be a place where you can catch up with the latest news and find links to good politics blogs and articles on the web. Please feel free to use this as somewhere you can comment on any of the day's political stories - just as you do when I'm writing the daily blog. It would be particularly useful for readers to flag up new material in the comments - breaking news or blogposts or tweets that are worth passing on because someone is going to find them interesting. A lot of what I do on my blog is aggregation - finding the good stuff and passing it on - and you can do this, too (as I know, because it happens every day when I'm blogging). All of today's Guardian politics stories are here, and all the politics stories filed on Thursday including some in today's paper, are here.

headline: Jeremy Hunt's statement on hospital staffing: Politics live blog
trail_text: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of all the day's political developments as they happen, including Jeremy Hunt's Commons statement on hospital staffing levels and the government's final response to the Francis report on the Mid Staffs scandal
body_text: • Robert Francis QC, who led the inquiry into the Mid Staffs hospital scandal, has welcomed the government's response to his report. Jeremy Hunt' proposals would "contribute to the development of the openness, transparency and candour that will maintain the public's confidence and promote a true partnership with patients", he said. (See 3.57pm.)
