It was James Wharton who moved the closure motion. (See 12.30pm.) Labour's Kevan Jones seems to think that that was unreasonable. And, just after I posted my roundup of Twitter tributes to John Cole, this one arrived. Turning away from the debate, here are 25 of the most interesting Twitter tributes to John Cole from journalists, politicians, celebrities - and a football club. A Tory MP has moved a motion calling for a vote (a closure motion).They are now having a vote on whether to have a vote on the amendment. And the Lib Dem MP Martin Horwood was on the Daily Politics with James Wharton. He said the Lib Dems did not want Britain to leave the EU, and he cited the warning today from Nissan as a reason why that would be disastrous. Andrew Neil said Nissan said much the same thing about what would happen if Britain did not join the euro. (Perhaps he has been reading Daniel Hannan's Twitter feed?) Horwood replied: "Well, do you want to take the gamble?" James Wharton told the BBC's Daily Politics that his bill would not complete its report stage today. But it would "live to fight another day", he said. There will be further time to debate it. James Wharton, the Conservative MP who tabled the bill, has just told the BBC's Daily Politics that the bill is making "reasonable progress" but that "it's going to be slow going". Opponents were trying to hold up the bill, he said. But he said that he was impressed that Conservative MPs were resisting the urge to speak. The party was being "disciplined and focused in trying to deliver this bill", he said. Gareth Thomas, the shadow Europe minister, is speaking now. He says he is surprised that the government forgot Gibraltar when it drafted the bill. (Remember, although this is a backbench bill, it was drafted by Tory HQ.) Dawn Primarolo, the deputy speaker, reprimands Meg Millier for "drifting away" from the subject of the amendments. Willie Bain has finished. Labour's Meg Hillier is speaking now. Conservative backbenchers are keeping quiet because they don't want to delay proceedings (see 9.31am), but the relatively small number of Labour MPs are on loquacious form. Hillier is talking about whether 16 and 17-year-olds should be allowed to vote. This would encourage schools to improve civic education for teenagers, she says. Back to John Cole. Here's the BBC's obituary. Labour's Barry Sheerman raises a point of order. He says that MPs have been debating the bill for two hours but that James Wharton, the bill's sponsor, has not yet spoken. Is that in order? Dawn Primarolo, the deputy speaker, says it is up to Wharton to decide if he wants to contribute. She says she has a long list of MPs who want to take part. The Labour MP Willie Bain is speaking now. He has tabled an amendment saying citizens of British overseas territories should be allowed to vote in the EU referendum. Back in the Commons Mike Gapes has finally finished. David Lidington, the Foreign Office minister, is speaking now. He is supporting new clause 1, the one that would allow Gibraltarians to vote in the referendum. Turning away from the debate for a moment, here's some sad news for those who care about political journalism. Mike Gapes gets to amendment 48. It's one he "wants to spend some time on", he says (ominously). It would allow prisoners to vote in the referendum. Mike Gapes is showing no signs of coming towards the end of his speech. He has just made the case for British citizens living abroad in other EU countries to be allowed to vote in the referendum. Conservative MPs are complaining about his delaying tactics on Twitter. Here are three blogs relating to today's vote which are worth a look. • Daniel Hannan, the Conservative MEP, says in a Telegraph blog that the arguments against the EU referendum bill do not stand up. • Isabel Hardman at Coffee House says the various Tory Eurosceptic factions are now generally working together. • George Easton at the New Statesman says today's warning from Nissan illustrates how the Tories' stance on Europe is undermining their economic message. Mike Gapes is still on his feet. He is engaged in what sounds like a learned exposition of the law relating to voting rights and nationality, but I'll spare you the details. Outside the chamber the Ukip leader Nigel Farage is, unsurprisingly, urging MPs to back the Adam Afriyie amendment. Labour's Kevan Jones raises a point of order. Is it in order for James Wharton, the backbench sponsor of the bill, to take advice from the Foreign Office officials in the officials' box in the chamber? (There is a row of seats in the chamber where officials sit, enabling them to pass notes to ministers at the despatch box.) Jones says this is supposed to be a backbench bill, not a government bill. Dawn Primarolo, the deputy speaker, says the officials are only supposed to help ministers. Martin Horwood has now finished. The Labour MP Mike Gapes, who has tabled more amendments to the bill than anyone else, is speaking now. The new clause 1 on Gibraltar does not go far enough, he says. A vote on Britain's membership of the EU would have an impact on other overseas territories, such as the Falklands. He also promises to go through all his amendments in detail. (Oh dear. That sounds ominous.) Martin Horwood is still banging on. He is now saying he supports an amendment tabled by Mike Gapes saying 16 and 17-year-olds should be allowed to vote in the referendum. Labour's Steve McCabe intervenes to say that it is unfair for 16 and 17-year-olds to be allowed to vote in the Scottish independence referendum, but not the EU referendum. The Lib Dem MP Martin Horwood is speaking now. He doesn't sound as if he is in a hurry either. What would happen if Gibraltar wanted to stay in the EU, but the UK as a whole didn't, he asks. And he points out that, under James Wharton's plan, the Scots could get a vote on Britain's membership of the EU even if they have already voted to leave the UK. Here is the text of James Wharton's bill (pdf). And here is the list of amendments being debated today (pdf). The Lib Dem MP John Hemming and the Labour MP Thomas Docherty have now spoken on the Gibraltar issue. Docherty and Gareth Thomas, the shadow Europe minister, did not seem minded to rush things. At one point they started discussing why the amendment included a reference to the Colonial Laws Validity Act 1865. Barry Sheerman, the Labour MP, is speaking now. He backed Rosindell's amendment. Gareth Thomas, the shadow Europe minister, says Labour backed an amendment at committee stage to allow Gibraltar to be included. But at the time the government was not in favour, he says. Here is the Commons library note on the EU referendum bill (pdf). It covers what happened at the bill's committee stage. Andrew Rosindell, a Conservative, is speaking now. He is moving new clause 1, which he and James Wharton have tabled. It ensures the bill covers Gibraltar. MPs voted by 290 votes to 0 not to sit in private. John Bercow is now taking points of order before the debate gets underway. Adam Afriyie, the Conservative MP trying to amend the bill so that the referendum would take place next year, has been speaking to BBC News. Here are the main points. • Afriyie said that he wanted the referendum to be held next year to ensure that it actually does take place. What I’m trying to do, I’m trying to give a voice to the British people. This can has been kicked down the road time and time again. We’re in danger of doing it again today. • He rejected claims that he was unpopular with colleagues. The truth is I get on very well with my parliamentary colleagues. But, according to the Mail on Sunday last month, Afriyie is certainly unpopular with Nicholas Soames. This is what Soames is reported to have said to him. You are a chateau bottled nuclear powered ****. You are totally f***ing disloyal, a f***ing disgrace to your party, your fellow MPs, your Prime Minister and your country. • Afriyie said the comments from the head of Nissan underlined the need to end uncertainty about Britain's relationship with Europe. He’s absolutely right. This is not about being in our out of the EU, it’s about letting you and the whole British public have their say about our future relationship, because nobody has ever had a say about whether or not we’re a member of the EU. So I’m kind of with any business director, any company, anybody, who says ‘gosh let’s end this uncertainty.’ That is what I’m trying to do today – to get a referendum within this parliament so we know whether or not we’re in the EU or we’re outside the EU. I've taken the quotes from PoliticsHome. Here's the scene in the House of Commons at the start of proceedings. As you can see, the Conservative benches (on the left) are reasonably full. The Labour ones are pretty empty. They started with David Nuttall, a Conservative, moving that the House sit in private. This means there will have to be a vote, last 15 minutes. MPs won't vote to sit in private, and this is just a procedural device. Sometimes votes like this are used as a delaying tactic, or as a means of finding out how many MPs there are in the Commons available to vote. But Nuttall is a supporter of the Wharton bill, and the BBC's Mark d'Arcy says Nuttall is calling a vote now so that opponents cannot use the "sit in private" device later. James Wharton has been speaking to BBC News about today's debate. Here are the main points. • Wharton said that he expected opponents of the bill to try to delay proceedings today. Supporters would keep their speeches short, he said. But opponents would try to delay proceedings so that the bill runs out of time. What I expect to see happen is those who want to frustrate the progress of this bill, who don’t want to give the British people a say on our membership of the European Union, will try to talk at great length in the hope that it runs out of time. Those of us who want to deliver it, who believe that the people should have a say on our membership will hopefully remain relatively quiet and brief, with the intention of getting to votes, getting through these various clauses and progressing the bill to its next stage. It looks as if the Labour MP Mike Gapes may be the chief filibusterer today. He has tabled dozens of amendments to the bill. • Wharton claimed that it was now Labour, not the Conservatives, who were divided on Europe. It’s funny now that you look at the debate over the last six months, a lot has changed. It’s now the Labour party that’s divided over Europe – that’s why Ed Miliband won’t be here today, he’s too weak to lead his party in any particular direction on this issue which is of such importance and yet he cannot express a strong view on it. • He said there was "not a lot of support" in the Conservative party for holding the referendum before 2015. • He played down the comments from the head of Nissan, saying that he was not advocating British withdrawal from the EU. I won’t be in Parliament today making the argument that we should come out of the EU or that we should stay in. The argument that I’m making is that we should give the British people a choice, and I think that for all of the debate that will go on around it, it’s pretty hard in a democracy to deny people a choice on such a fundamental and important issue. I've taken the quotes from PoliticsHome. Some people aren't particularly bothered whether Britain holds a referendum on EU membership in the next few years, but for others it is the most important issue in national politics. Since this group includes a chunk of Conservative MPs, a large number of people who will select the next Conservative leader (party members) and Ukip supports (who may have a big influence on the outcome of the general election), the subject is worth following closely. The Conservative MP James Wharton has introduced a backbench bill that would oblige government to hold a referendum by the end of 2017 and today MPs will debate its report stage in the Commons. Another Conservative backbencher, Adam Afriyie, wants to amend it so that the referendum is held next October and today that could be put to a vote. We are not expecting many MPs to support Afiyie (assuming his amendment gets put to a vote), but we are likely to see what would amount to a small Tory rebellion. (It will be a rebellion because Number 10 is backing Wharton.) More generally, the debate may reveal a bit more about how MPs from all parties feel about the referendum issue. It will start at 9.30am and run until 2.30pm. I will be covering it in detail. Before we get going, here's some background reading. • Nicholas Watt in the Guardian today explains the background to today's debate. • Paul Goodman at ConservativeHome urges Afriyie to drop his amendment. Adam Afriyie has championed holding the poll [next year]. I don’t agree with him, but whether one does or not is beside the point – which is that the Wharton Bill might pass through Parliament, but the Afriyie amendment will not. There is no majority in the Commons for a pre-2017 referendum. All he will achieve if he moves it this morning is to risk delaying the Wharton Bill, and he thus runs the risk of helping to kill it off. Once the amendment is on the floor of the House it will no longer be his property. I hope he withdraws it before proceedings begin today. • The Telegraph's Benedict Brogan in his morning briefing email says that Afiyie is deeply unpopular with his colleagues, but that Tory MPs have reservations about David Cameron too. Views of Mr Afriyie among backbenchers seem to be universally uncomplimentary and in some cases downright violent. The anger voiced by Nicholas Soames was felt by many, even if some felt the attack was unwise. There is a suspicion that some of Mr Afriyie's supporters are using him to cause maximum damage to David Cameron. In Downing Street he is loathed as an untrustworthy upstart of questionable motives who is showboating, no more. But Mr Cameron will be aware that his popularity among his colleagues is built on success or the absence of it, not natural loyalty. And there is a sense from the backbenches that the operation has poorly handled the Bill. It is felt that the Conservatives have inadvertently ramped up its significance by making it a three-line whip; a "free vote" with a nudge here and a wink there may have been less destructive. There is also unhappiness about the lack of clear movement from Mr Cameron on exactly what powers he would like to reclaim from the EU in renegotiations. • A survey from ConservativeHome suggests a third of Conservative members want an EU referendum in 2014. • A Guardian story about the head of Nissan suggesting the firm would leave Britain if it voted to leave the EU. • A story in today's Financial Times (subscription) saying that Cameron review of EU powers is proving disappointing to Conservative Eurosceptics. Theresa May and the Foreign Office are locked in a battle over the benefits of European immigration to Britain, as the home secretary struggles to find evidence to support her case for imposing tighter restrictions on migrants. Ms May has infuriated the Foreign & Commonwealth Office by “dragging her feet” in drafting a report on the impact of the EU’s free movement rules, amid claims that the evidence does not support her political narrative of the system being abused. The stand-off reflects growing tensions inside David Cameron’s Conservative party as it appears that a vaunted review of the balance of powers between Westminster and Brussels is failing to provide ammunition for the eurosceptics. In July, Mr Cameron published the first batch of review papers, covering areas including the single market, which found the balance of “competences” was “broadly appropriate”. Some Tory MPs claimed the work reflected the bias of a “europhile Whitehall elite” and are hopeful that a second round of reports – due by the end of the year – will provide some grounds for Britain’s EU membership to be renegotiated. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I'm on @AndrewSparrow.

headline: Edinburgh festival: is art above politics?
trail_text: Lyn Gardner: International festival director Jonathan Mills makes waves by saying that he will avoid the upcoming Scottish referendum
body_text: A politics-free zone Jonathan Mill's announcement that he would be excluding independence-themed productions from the 2014 festival has inevitably attracted attention, and not just in Scotland. The recently knighted outgoing international festival director says his plans for the 2014 festival programme haven't been influenced by the upcoming referendum, and instead he will be concentrating on the 100th anniversary of the outbreak of the first world war. In truth, probably many of his plans were well advanced even before the announcement of the referendum, but I still find his reasoning bizarre. Using the royal "we" he declared: "We would not wish our festival to be anything other than it has always been, which is a politically neutral space for artists. It is important that it remains that." If Mills really believes that there is such a thing as a "politically neutral space for artists" and EIF provides it, he is a far less intelligent man than I'd previously thought. Where theatre happens, the context in which it takes place, how and why it is programmed, the conditions under which it is made and shown are all inherently political acts – and they don't happen in a vacuum. There is an agenda embedded in every choice and commission that Mills makes when curating the EIF, whether he is conscious of it or not. If he doesn't realise that, then he shouldn't ever have been running a major arts festival. As the theatremaker and co-director of Forest Fringe, Andy Field, observed on twitter: The toughest job in British theatre? There's the well-worn saying in theatre that you are only as good as your last show. Fortunately a great show can banish all memories of the poor show that came before. But most theatres don't have to lay out an entire stall in one go, which is what happens at the Traverse during the festival. I reckon that Orla O'Loughlin, whose job it is to programme, has one of the hardest jobs in British theatre. Of course as former artistic director, Dominic Hill, always pointed out, the Traverse has an impressive year-round programme of new work and it is not, as most of us English critics view it, simply just there for the festival. But the Traverse is such a festival within the festival and it gets such close critical attention that inevitably the work it shows during these three weeks in August is highly scrutinised and exposed. I've heard lots of people say that it's not a great year at the Traverse. Sure, there are some duds and some brave failures. But not every show in any theatre is for everyone. I can't see that any programme which has thrown up The Events, Grounded, Long Live the Little Knife, Quietly, Have I No Mouth and the upcoming Brand New Ancients from Kate Tempest can be regarded as anything other than a success. Coughing it up Amidst all the gaudy razzmatazz of the fringe, something remarkable is taking place this evening at the brilliant Hunt and Darton Café in St Mary's Street. For five hours Martin O'Brien, who has cystic fibrosis, will be creating a five-hour durational performance http://liveartliveblog.net/?p=961 called Mucus Factory, during which he performs the physiotherapy and exercises necessary to clear his airways and keep him alive. This is a shorter version of a piece I've seen previously, and it is a quite extraordinary experience – at times quite horrifying as he beats his chest raw, and certainly not easy to watch, but it's well worth dropping by for a few minutes or an hour or two. The performance is free, and it really makes you question the way that illness and disability are portrayed on stage.

headline: Infrastructure is built with rigorous cost-benefit analysis, not politics
trail_text: Tony Abbott has decided to fund the Melbourne east-west link before a business case has been made
body_text: Throughout the election campaign, one of Tony Abbott’s most common refrains was his desire to be known as an “infrastructure prime minister”. This was often linked with the claim that his government would build the roads of the 21st century. Just what a 21st century road is that a 20th century one wasn’t was never made very clear, but perhaps any UK readers might know, because, coincidentally, the UK conservative government is also building “roads for the 21st century”. While I might be somewhat glib about the sloganeering, pledging to be an infrastructure prime minister is a worthy goal. The importance of infrastructure was outlined last week in a speech by the deputy governor of the Reserve Bank, Philip Lowe. Dr Lowe’s speech, titled Productivity and Infrastructure, began by showing what probably is Australian economists’ favourite graph: Click here for the graph on mobile From 1985 to around 2001 Australia’s gross domestic income per hour of work grew at the same pace as gross domestic product per hour of work (the standard measure of productivity). But from 2001 onwards, while productivity growth slowed, domestic income continued to zoom. The reason was the increase in our terms of trade. Without doing anything differently, suddenly the rest of the world was giving us more money. None of this had much to do with any great skill on Australia’s part, other than for us to have been lucky enough to settle in a country sitting on top of a massive deposit of iron ore. But if, as expected, the growth in demand for iron ore declines, and the growth in prices declines with it, a repeat of the good fortune of the 2000s will need more than luck. And this is where infrastructure comes in. Philip Lowe noted that in the recent past we’ve done well at building infrastructure for mining, but “we need to do more if we are to achieve that same success elsewhere”. Such infrastructure necessarily involves transport because time spent waiting in traffic is a cost for both people and goods – time that could be used more productively. But good transport infrastructure also has other benefits. As Lowe noted, it brings people from various parts of a city together and thus brings more people into contact with more businesses. This has the effect of improving competition – because customers can travel further and businesses can more efficiently deliver goods. So infrastructure is important, but the solution is not necessarily more of it. For all Tony Abbott’s palaver about building roads of the 21st century, the reality is in the past four years the ALP government has already been doing a lot of it – in fact more roads than ever have been constructed since the ABS began calculating such activity: Click here for the graph on mobile Part of this is because 2000 to 2008 was a near decade-long valley of infrastructure spending, as both federal and state governments valued achieving a surplus above spending money to improve infrastructure. But not all infrastructure spending will improve productivity. The Grattan Institute’s recent report, Balancing Budgets: tough choices we need to make, comments that infrastructure only improves economic growth “if it is the right infrastructure in the right place, at the right time, for the right price”. Lowe noted that “strong governance is critical to make sure money is spent wisely in this area”. He also stated that Infrastructure Australia, which was created by the Rudd government in 2008, had assisted progress, but that “there is no substitute for rigorous and transparent cost-benefit analysis”. It is pleasing then that last month the government introduced legislation to amend the governance of Infrastructure Australia. Among changes will be a requirement for Infrastructure Australia to “recommend infrastructure projects on the basis of a transparent and rigorous cost-benefit assessment of their viability” and to “review all projects seeking Commonwealth funding worth more than $100m and publish the reasons for its decisions”. This is a good idea, but there is a touch of cynicism in the move. Much of the speech introducing the legislation by the minister for infrastructure and regional development, Warren Truss, seemed an attempt to justify the government’s decision to fund the east-west link in Melbourne despite the Victorian government failing to make a complete business case for it in time for a full recommendation. Subsequently, Infrastructure Australia’s June report to Coag ranked the east-west link as merely having “real potential”. Crucially, it ranked the project a stage below the ALP’s favoured Melbourne metro project. During the election campaign Tony Abbott was unambiguous in his support for the east-west link over the metro and for roads over rail generally – based on the belief that commuter rail is a state matter, but that the east-west link is a national project. His responses, when confronted over the lack of cost-benefit analysis for the east-west link, however, were rather at odds with his government’s new desire for transparency and evidence-based decision-making. In August, when pressed on whether he had actually seen the business case for the east-west link, Abbott replied, “Look, there is a business case, there is a business case … I mean, there are lots of things that I haven’t seen but I know, and I know that there is a business case for the east-west link. It says there is $1.40 worth of benefit for every $1 of spending.” The Age newspaper in October revealed that the Victorian state government’s business case, which included the claim of $1.40 worth of benefit, relied on assumptions that had “not been used in any of [the transport department’s] other public transport projects or program modeling to date”. The view has been challenged by other commentators, but everyone is operating somewhat in the dark because the Victorian government has not published the full business case. Given the Commonwealth government is on the hook for $1.5bn, you’d think the public have a right to see why our money is being spent on it rather than on another more productive project. The decision is even more absurd given the underlying presumption of the proposed changes to Infrastructure Australia is that its decisions should be fully transparent. The changes to Infrastructure Australia to provide a transparent and rigorous cost-benefit assessment are sensible, and given what has happened with the east-west decision they appear necessary. Unfortunately, it would seem the rules governing such cost-benefit analyses are being changed more to ensure projects like the east-west link are favoured than to drive investment in the most productive infrastructure – regardless of whether it is rail or road. If Abbott actually wants to become an "infrastructure prime minister”, he’ll need to apply a bit more rigour and a lot more transparency to future transport projects than he has thus far.

headline: Nick Clegg's press conference: Politics live blog
trail_text:
• John Kerry's press conference with William Hague on Syria - Summary
• George Osborne's economy speech - Summary
• Lunchtime summary
• Nick Clegg's press conference - Summary
• Afternoon summary
body_text: • David Cameron has said that he ruled out Britain taking party in military action in Syria after the Commons vote, even though MPs were not voting to reject the idea permanently, because Labour were playing politics with the issue. Speaking in the Commons, he implied that he could not trust to support the government in a future vote. This is what he said to Labour's Ben Bradshaw when Bradshaw said 492 MPs had voted not to rule out military action (by backing one of the two motions debated) The point I would make is that I put into the government motion the fact that we should listen to the weapons inspectors, we should have a process at the United Nations, we should have a second vote before action. I included everything that your frontbench wanted - every single thing. But the fact that [Labour] didn't vote for it shows me that they are not serious about this issue. They are serious about political positioning and as prime minister it is very difficult to deal with that, so that is why I believe the House spoke quite clearly. • Nick Clegg has said that he does not agree with Chris Huhne about Huhne's stance on phone-hacking helping to explain why Rupert Murdoch's News International sought to destroy his career. Asked if he agreed with Huhne, this is what Clegg told his press conference. Chris has to speak for himself. All I can tell you is that is his opinion. Is it exactly my opinion? No it isn't because, guess what, we're different people. I'm not Chris's keeper. Clegg also said he supported "muscular" journalism. Newspapers play an incredibly important role of keeping people in positions of authority and power on their toes, uncovering wrongdoing where that takes place, uncovering the truth where it is not revealed. That is the great tradition of British journalism. I think the kind of rigorous, muscular tone in British journalism is actually a model for many other parts elsewhere in the world. • Clegg has said Labour is "perilously close" to the point where people no longer know what it stands for. The Labour party constantly likes to criticise the difficult things we have had to do in government. I am perfectly entitled to point out that they don't have very many clear policies any more, what they'd actually do in the face of the dilemmas that we face whether it is on Syria, whether it is on long-term investment and infrastructure, whether it is the long-term economic management of this country after the disastrous mismanagement that they presided over. You ask people difficult questions and you expect compelling answers. Maybe we will hear them at the forthcoming party conference but at the moment I think Labour is perilously close to that point where people don't know they stand for any more. • Mark Thompson, the former BBC director general, has told the Commons public accounts committee that a £1m pay-off to his deputy Mark Byford in 2010 would not have been approved today. The hearing is still going on, and you can follow it on our live blog. • Chris Grayling, the justice secetary, has said a specialist planning court should be established to speed up development projects and drive out "meritless" objections. • The TUC has passed a motion saying it is opposed to replacing Trident. Kate Hudson, General Secretary of CND, said: “We welcome the support of the trade union movement for the scrapping of Trident replacement. Today’s vote reinforces the shared values of the TUC and CND – public spending to meet people’s needs, not to build weapons of mass destruction." That's all from me for today. Thanks for the comments. A Labour spokesman has been in touch about that last post. He says that the party has not changed its position and that Labour still thinks "it is wrong to promise a referendum now in several years time" because that would create uncertainty. (But the point about Labour's line is that it is a holding position. The party hasn't ruled out backing a referendum. One proposal within the party is that Ed Miliband should try to trump David Cameron by calling for one to be held six months after the general election. There's more in this Financial Times story [subscription]) Tony Blair has written a piece for the Evening Standard about Britain and the EU. He thinks that it would be madness for Britain to leave. No surprise there. But I was struck by his introduction. There is now, almost certainly, going to be a vote on whether Britain leaves the European Union. This is not about whether we join the euro, agree to any new Constitution or about whether we negotiate different terms of membership; but about whether, after almost 40 years of membership of the world’s largest economic market and biggest political union, we leave for good. That will be an option on the ballot paper. That is therefore a possible outcome. We would become the only nation in the EU, having joined, then voluntarily to depart. Read that first sentence again. I suppose it could be a sign that Blair expects the Conservatives to win the next election. But the only interpretation that really makes sense is that Blair now thinks Labour will go into the next election promising a referendum on Europe. Cameron says it is no longer fair to describe crown dependencies and overseas territories as "tax havens" because they have taken steps to introduce tax transparency. Back in the Commons, Labour's Ben Bradshaw asks Cameron why he has ruled out military action in Syria when MPs were just voting against military action at this point. Cameron says he listened to Labour's concerns on Syria and put everything that they wanted into the government motion. But their decision to table a rival motion showed they were "not serious" about the issue. They were only serious about political positioning, he says. Turning back to George Osborne for a moment, my Guardian colleague Phillip Inman has identified seven ways in which he thinks Osborne was rewriting economic history in his speech. Dennis Skinner, the Labour MP, says talking about a real recovery is insulting to the 4m people without a full-time job. Cameron says he is sorry that Skinner cannot welcome the 1m-plus private sector jobs. And if Skinner is so against zero-hours contracts, he should talk to the Labour councils that use them. John Redwood, the Conservative, asks about reports that Angela Merkel, the German chancellor, has said she will back some of Cameron's plans for EU reform. Cameron says his conversations with Merkel at the G20 were about Syria. But he has had good discussions with Merkel about EU reform as well, he says. Cameron is replying to Miliband. If Britain needs to sponsor a UN security council resolution to improve humanitarian access to Syria, it will do so, he says. On the economy, he says the G20 is now taking tax transparency seriously. Britain will soon consult on whether to make a register of beneficial ownership public. As for Britain, many economic indicators are on the up. What is "down and out" is Miliband's economic reputation, he says. On Syria, Miliband asks when the UN inspectors will report? Will there be a new resolution at the UN security council? What more can be done to help those countries taking refugees from Syria? What is being done to "progress the timetable" for the Geneva 2 Syria peace talks? Miliband says Cameron failed to carry the House and the country on arming the Syrian rebels, and on joining military action. But he will carry the House and the country on strengthening humanitarian aid. Ed Miliband is responding. He says Cameron's statement was characteristically modest. We may be a small island, but we are a great nation. It's just a shame about the government, he says. Miliband quotes from the summit communique. It says unemployment is too high. Instead of George Osborne claiming to have saved the economy, he should stop being so "complacent". At the moment this is just a recovery for those at the top. Britain made a leading contribution to the summit. We may be a small island, but we are a great nation, he says. Turning to the economy, Cameron says that when he went to his first G20 summit three years ago Britain had the most indebted economy, the highest level of household debt and the worst banking sector. In 2014 Britain will have the strongest growth of any European country. Cameron says Britain is leading the world in terms of the humanitarian response in Syria. At the G20 he held a meeting with other countries and agreed to push for unfettered access to Syria for humanitarian aid workers. He says he is also pushing for a political settlement. Here's an extract: Maybe things would have been better, the past few years, with a different chancellor at the helm. We will never know. Given this uncertainty, some economists would say it was complacent for Mr Osborne to leap on the recent momentum in the economy as proof that there could never have been better way out of the financial crisis than the one he has followed. That has not been proved. It never will be, either in favour of Ed Balls or George Osborne. But that economic momentum is real enough, and you have to bet it will translate into momentum of the political variety for the government as well. The argument about Plan B is over, give or take. The argument about the best way to rebuild UK living standards is just getting going. George Osborne is so fond of visiting building sites that the Guardian was able to produce a picture gallery last week showing him in an assortment of high-vis jackets. Today (without the high-vis outfit) he was at another construction site in the City of London where a development started in 2007, ground to a halt for three years, but has now resumed. It was a metaphor for the economy, he explained. As I said earlier, this was a heavy-duty economics speech. But while the content may have been all economics, the motive was pure politics – "Trust me, not Labour, because I've been vindicated." Somehow Osborne managed to deliver it without sounding too hubristic, but it was still a bold exercise in I-was-rightism. Here are the main points. • Osborne said it was now clear that austerity was not to blame for Britain's recovery taking so long. He said there were two explanations for the long period of flat growth. See 11.27am. Ed Balls has blamed austerity. But Osborne said that instead the Office of Budget Responsibility had been right to say that external inflation and the eurozone crisis were responsible for growth being so sluggish last year. Those in favour of a Plan B have lost the argument. The reason is simple: proponents of the “fiscalist” story cannot explain why the UK recovery has strengthened rapidly over the last six months. The pace of fiscal consolidation has not changed, government spending cuts have continued as planned, and yet growth has accelerated and many of the leading economic indicators show activity rising faster than at any time since the 1990s. If the fiscal multipliers were much higher than the OBR estimates, as the fiscalist story requires, this should not be possible. • He rejected claims that Treasury policies such as Help to Buy were fuelling a house price boom. House prices are down a quarter from their peak in real terms, and relative to earnings they are back at 2003 levels. Mortgage approvals are running at only a little more than half, and transactions a little more than two-thirds, of pre-crisis levels. That is why the Government’s Help to Buy scheme is a sensible, time-limited and necessary financial intervention to fix a specific financial problem: the dramatic reduction in the availability of high loan-to-value mortgages. The median LTV [loan to value] for first time buyers has fallen from a long term average of 90% to just 80% now. This change is not something we should welcome, it is both a market failure and a social problem – imagine if you’d had to find twice as big a deposit for your first home. 90% and 95% LTV mortgages are not exotic weapons of financial mass destruction – they are a regular part of a healthy mortgage market and an aspirational society. • He said the cost of living problem highlighted by Labour started when Tony Blair was in power. There is a focus today on the issue of household income growth. What is less recognized is that household income growth had started to slow sharply from the early 2000s. Annual growth in household disposable income more than halved from 3.6% on average over the five years before 2003, to an average of 1.7% in the five years after that. The proceeds of unprecedented global prosperity and rising trade were not finding their ways into the pockets of British families. Osborne said this contributed to rising household debt in the years before the crash. • He said Labour was to blame for the fact that Britain suffered so badly during the recession. GDP fell by 7.2% from peak to trough, almost twice as much as in the US, he said. In the past Conservatives have focused their attack on the last Labour government's spending record - allowing Labour to respond by saying this had nothing to do with the sub-prime crisis in the US that triggered the crash. Today Osborne said Britain fared badly because the entire economy was "fundamentally unbalanced" under Labour. What happened in Britain was the product of a pattern of economic development that had been fundamentally unbalanced and unsustainable for many years. The bailout of RBS was the biggest in the world because British banks had been allowed to become among the most leveraged and unstable in the world. The banks reflected an economy that had become, on some estimates, the most indebted in the world – with total private sector debt reaching 470% of GDP by the beginning of 2010. And the soaring budget deficit had its roots in an unsustainable increase in public spending in the eight years before the crisis – the second most rapid increase in spending as a share of GDP of all OECD countries. As a result the government ran rising deficits even at the peak of the boom and entered the crisis with a structural deficit that the IMF estimates was more than 5% of GDP, the highest in the G7. At the time most economists took a narrow view of sustainable growth: if inflation was low, and unemployment was close to its so-called ‘natural’ rate, then that was deemed to be enough. Clearly that was a huge mistake. We now know that truly sustainable growth also depends on sound public finances, well capitalized banks, healthy balance sheets, and a system of financial regulation that is alert to broader risks to the economy like asset bubbles and excessive debt. • He said government policies were having a "huge" impact in terms of helping people with the cost of living. The falls in mortgage rates that our plan has delivered are worth £2,000 a year to a family taking out a standard fixed-rate mortgage of £100,000. Our record increases in the personal allowance have already saved a basic rate taxpayer £600 a year, rising to £700 a year by next year. We’ve used billions of pounds to help with fuel costs by freezing fuel duty for over two years. Low mortgage rates. A £10,000 Personal Allowance. Fuel duty frozen. And soon tax free childcare. These make huge, positive impacts on the cost of living. • He said Labour plans to increase spending would have "disastrous" impact because interest rates would rise. • He defended Mark Carney's decision to issue "forward guidance" promising to keep interest rates low while unemployment remains above 7%. Some have interpreted more recent increases in gilt yields as a sign that forward guidance has somehow failed, but that is, I believe, a misunderstanding. I’d argue that market movements since the August Inflation Report vindicate the need for forward guidance: the counterfactual would have been even bigger increases in yields in response to positive economic data. And the evidence suggests that forward guidance is succeeding in changing expectations in the real economy: the Bank of England’s Inflation Attitudes Survey shows a marked fall in the proportion of people expecting interest rates to rise over the next year. • Osborne said he was "passionately" in favour of HS2. Last night he had dinner with Mahmoud Abbas, the Palestinian president, he says. He says he is encouraged by the way the participants in the Middle East peace talks are sticking with the process. On Syria Hague says the position of the UK is well known. The government fully respects the decision made by parliament. But the UK still has four priorities. 1. Pushing for talks at Geneva. 2. Offering humanitarian aid. 3. Trying to strengthen the moderate opposition. 4. Taking a resolute stand on the use of chemical weapons (CW). Many countries have called for a strong international response to the use of CW. It is to the credit of the US that they are taking the lead. The Americans have the "full diplomatic support" of the UK. The UK has offered a total of £400m in humanitarian support. And it will be pushing at the UN for aid agencies to have unfettered access to Syria. There can be no political solution in Syria if the Assad regime is allowed to eradicate the moderate opposition. So he and Kerry discussed ways of continuing their aid to them, he says. The US/UK special alliance is, at heart, an alliance of values, Hague says. They continue to work together on a range of issues. William Hague and John Kerry are here. Hague starts. He says he and Kerry spent most of their time talking about Syria. But he starts by saying he wants to pay tribute to what Kerry achieved in getting Middle East peace talks going again. The press conference will focus on Syria, but it would be a shame if it goes by without anyone asking about today's Sun splash (paywall) saying that a World Trade Center girder, given to Britain as a 9/11 memorial, is now rusting in a farmyard. On the Today programme Lord West, the former Labour security minister, said he was appalled that the girder did not have a proper home. In the US, memorials featuring girders from 9/11 almost have sacred status, he said. They will be here soon, apparently. William Hague and John Kerry are about to hold their news conference after their talks at the Foreign Office. I'll be covering it. Today the Commons public accounts committee has published a withering report about the HS2 high-speed rail project. Shiv Malik has written it up for today's Guardian here. This morning Patrick McLoughlin, the transport secretary, was responding. He said that the PAC report was based on work done in 2011 and that HS2 was essential for Britain's long-term future. The simple fact is we have not built a new railway line north of London for 120 years, there has been massive growth on our railways in the last 15 years; 750m journeys to 1.5bn journeys, inter-city journeys, have doubled, and the truth is we can't not do anything. This is planning for the long-term future of our railways. This railway will be up and running in 2026. It is essential that we have good connections between our main cities. I've taken the quote from PoliticsHome. How do you give a speech welcoming economic recovery without sounding like Norman Lamont? By attacking Ed Balls and not saying anything about "green shoots", for starters. At least, these seem to be two features of the speech we will get from George Osborne later this morning. It's an important speech because economic recovery changes the political terms of trade quite significantly. Calls for a "Plan B" will fall away, or at least mutate into something different, and coalition ministers will assume that they will gain some kind of electoral payback (although the historical evidence on this is mixed). Osborne's speech should be a thoughtful one; given the time it's taken for the economy to turn, he's certainly had long enough to prepare it. Here's an extract from Patrick Wintour's preview story. Chancellor George Osborne will claim on Monday that critics of his economic policy have now decisively lost the argument and will say the economy has "turned a corner", and that a sustainable recovery is under way. But he will insist that it is premature to consider any slowdown in his deficit reduction programme in the light of better economic forecasts, saying it is vital to stick to the course he has set. "Even if the improving economic news eventually leads to an improvement in the fiscal outlook, the job will not be done," he will say in a major speech aimed at setting the parameters of the 2015 election. "More tough choices will be required after the next election to find many billions of further savings." A bullish chancellor will claim that critics of his deficit reduction plans are at a loss to explain why the recovery is now taking place. He will point out that the spending cuts are at their deepest now owing to the backloading of the cuts programme. I will cover the speech in detail. Later I will also be focusing on Nick Clegg's press conference and David Cameron's statement in the Commons on last week's G20 summit. Here's the full agenda for the day. 9am: William Hague, the foreign secretary, hosts a press conference with the US secretary of state, John Kerry. 9.50am: Frances O’Grady, the TUC general secretary, addresses the TUC conference. 11am: George Osborne, the chancellor, gives a speech on the economy. 11am: Ed Davey, the energy secretary, gives a speech on shale gas. According to the Sunday Times (paywall), he will say: “We can’t bank on shale gas to solve our energy challenges today or this decade.” 2pm: Nick Clegg holds his monthly press conference. 3.15pm: Mark Thomspon, the former BBC director general, and Lord Patten, the BBC Trust chairman, give evidence to the Commons public accounts committee. As Jason Deans reports, amid reports that ministers are planning to axe the BBC Trust and hand responsibility for governance of the corporation to media regulator Ofcom, MPs on the PAC will attempt to get to the bottom of who knew what and when about controversial settlements, including the former deputy director general Mark Byford’s £1m payoff. 3.30pm: David Cameron makes a statement in the Commons on last week’s G20 summit. As usual, I’ll also be covering all the breaking political news as well as looking at the papers and bringing you the best politics from the web. I’ll post a lunchtime summary at around 1pm and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I’m on @AndrewSparrow.

headline: MPs debate lobbying bill: Politics live blog
trail_text: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of all the day's political developments as they happen, including Andrew Lansley giving evidence to a committee on the lobbying bill and MPs debating the bill, and its impact on charities, at second reading
body_text: • Andrew Lansley, the leader of the Commons, has rejected claims that the government's lobbying bill will stop charities campaigning. On a day which saw the bill criticised by the Electoral Commission in the morning, and by MPs from all sides of the House during the second reading debate in the afternoon, Lansley made a distinction between charities and other campaigning organisations. Most charities would not be affected because charities are not allowed to engage in party politics, he said. The bill doesn't constrain the ability of charities to campaign. Look back at 2010 - only two charities registered for the purposes of expenditure on electoral purposes and spent very little ... So the idea that charities are in any way constrained I think is completely wrong. But the bill would constrain other third-party groups that campaigned in elections, he said. That was the point; it was meant to. (An analysis shows that Unison is the organisation most likely to be affected by the news rules - see 1.04pm.) In electoral law already there is a very clear provision that if third parties wish to engage in expenditure the intent or effect of which is to procure electoral success, that they are beyond a certain point required to register with the Electoral Commission in relation to that expenditure and there are limits on that expenditure and that expenditure is controlled. All the bill does, which is the right thing to do, is to make sure that the limit is a more appropriate limit for the future and doesn't allow those third parties to engage in distorting activity in relation to elections. It extends the definition of controlled expenditure so that it includes advertising, rallies and suchlike as well as election material and it dis-aggregates that into parliamentary constituencies so that third parties cannot disproportionately concentrate their spending in individual constituencies - all of which I think are perfectly rational ... The point is if you are undertaking expenditure for electoral purposes you should expect to be regulated and there should be proper account made of it. But Labour's Angela Eagle said the bill was "one of the worst pieces of legislation I've seen any government produce in a very long time". The bill is bound to get a second reading when MPs vote on it at 7pm tonight. There are bound to be rebellions and concessions as the bill continues its passage through parliament, but at this stage it is hard to be sure quite how much damage the bill's opponents will be able to inflict. • William Hague, has rejected Labour calls for Iran to be included in Syrian peace talks, saying the Iranian regime was actively engaged in assisting widespread murder by the Assad regime, and had not yet expressed support for a transitional government in Syria. • Sir Hugh Orde, the president of the Association of Chief Police Officers, has said that the European arrest warrant is an "essential weapon" for police. • Justine Greening, the international development secretary, has said she was "absolutely gutted" to miss last week's vote on Syria. She voted against the Labour amendment, but missed this main vote. This is how she explained it on the World at One. We were in Parliament and indeed I has having a meeting with Mark Simmonds, Foreign Office minister, at the time, between votes, I voted in the first vote. Unfortunately for us we were in a room that it proved, apparently, that you cannot hear the division bell from. We were unaware the vote had been called because we were unable to hear the division bell. This is the key bell that MPs have relied on literally for 150 years to tell them what's going on in Parliament. It was deeply unfortunate and I was absolutely gutted to have missed the vote. • The Commons authorities have released figures showing their have been thousands of attempts to access pornography through computers on the parliamentary network. I've already quoted what the Public Relations Consultants Association (see 11.13am) and the Chartered Institute of Public Relations have said about this bill. This is from Iain Anderson, deputy chairman of the Association of Professional Political Consultants (APPC) This is a muddled mistake. As currently drafted it will do precisely nothing to increase lobbying transparency. Indeed, the only thing the government will achieve by pushing forward with the bill in its current draft is to unite all common sense and reason against it.” In two key respects it falls woefully short of achieving its aims. In registering only third-party consultants - and amongst them only those who conduct a "substantial" proportion of their work as lobbying business - it will not even succeed in capturing all agency lobbyists - the very lowest benchmark of success that the Government has set itself with this unambitious bill. Secondly, it will not even cover backbench MPs, who by all accounts are powerful actors in the policy-making process. In other words all lobbyists will be free to lobby the majority of MPs without ever having to register: the idea that you can meet with, for example, the Leader of the Opposition or a select committee chairperson and for this not to require registration renders this bill a laughing stock." We want to see a properly drafted bill that captures all lobbying but which doesn’t get in the way of legitimate democratic activity. This is not a difficult thing to achieve aided greatly by the fact that is a wide cross-section of opponents to this bill who are ready to work with the Government to make this a reality." The APPC wants to see all professional lobbying governed by a statutory lobbying register. The APPC has been operating our own register for nearly 20 years and yet we had absolutely no engagement with the government until this bill was produced. This is despite the fact that the industry had worked hard to produce a definition and a framework that would be workable and a registration process to provide proof of concept as to how a system could operate effectively. In the light of Shaheen's article in the New Statesman about a new party to the left of Labour (see 3.54pm), it is worth flagging up, for anyone who missed it, what the Unite general secretary Len McCluskey was saying at the end of his interview with John Harris in the Guardian on Saturday. [McCluskey] mentions the four senior Labour figures who quit Labour in 1981 and founded the Social Democratic Party. "There were things happening in the Labour party that they profoundly disagreed with, and they left and created their own party. Now, that's politics for you…" And does he think that might happen to the people he calls Blairites? "Or it could be the outcome for those individuals on the left of the party who believe, 'Well, we're getting nowhere.'" Including him? "I'm the general secretary of Unite. In our rule book, we're affiliated to the Labour party. And for that to change, our rules would have to change. We're not there yet. But the Labour party has no God-given right to exist. Ed Miliband's challenge is to demonstrate to ordinary working people – including trade unionists – that it's their party." This is a big moment, in other words. "It's absolutely huge." And he doesn't rule out Unite eventually taking its ball elsewhere? "I wouldn't rule anything out," he says. "In extraordinary times, extraordinary things happen. In my view, students of politics in 50 years' time may well look back on this period and realise just how incredibly important it was." Though he is working hard for a Labour victory in 2015, he thinks this will be possible only with "a radical alternative to austerity". If the Tories win, he tells me, "I fear for the existence of the Labour party. None of us know what would happen after a defeat of that nature. And it won't necessarily be the normal process of the leader stepping down, and a new leader taking over." As he says, extraordinary times. McCluskey has dropped vague hints before about Unite leaving Labour, but this was his clearest hint yet that he's considering the idea. Here's a short afternoon reading list. • Salman Shaheen at the New Statesman says Left Unity could become Labour's Ukip. In response to an appeal by [Ken] Loach, almost 10,000 people have signed up to the Left Unity campaign to form a new party of the left, with around 100 local groups springing up organically across the country. While for many this is not their first shot at uniting a fractured left and the painful experiences of the Socialist Alliance and George Galloway’s Respect are still fresh in their mind, there is a sense that there is something different about Left Unity. Undominated by a central charismatic figure, and uncontrolled by a single Trot group such as the SWP, Left Unity is a movement that is genuinely being built from the bottom up by local activists sick of austerity and fearful of the future of the NHS. As Left Unity moves towards its founding conference at London’s Royal National Hotel on 30 November, the task of harmonising such a large choir of angry voices will not be easy. But the space is certainly there for a left-wing party to fill ... The space is there to the left, the votes are there, and if Labour will not fill it, then Left Unity will. • Paul Goodman at ConservativeHome says Michael Gove is only one point behind Boris Johnson in a poll of ConHome readers on who should be the next Tory leader. Boris maintains his narrow one point lead over Gove, and May's recent advance inches further forward. The Mayor has risen slightly after falling back nine points last month: being a bit out of the news is the best explanation for the recent drop. The explanation of Gove's rise must be that he is seen by respondents as one of the Government's most effective Ministers. And David Cameron has also been talking about Syria. He told broadcasters that his decision not to hold another vote on military intervention did not mean Britain was not offering humanitarian help to the country. I think parliament spoke very clearly and it’s important to respect the view of parliament and so I’m not planning to return to parliament to ask again about British military action. That doesn’t mean we do nothing on Syria – we are already the second largest aid donor ... Britain has spent over £300m, our aid has saved tens of thousands of lives. I’ve been to Jordan and seen one of the refugee camps myself, and I know how much money and how much effort is going in. Obviously it’s difficult to get some of the aid into Syria itself and we must continue to try and crack these problems, but Britain, as ever, is a world leader on helping those who need help, and the people of Syria are right at the front of that now. I've taken the quotes from PoliticsHome. Turning away from the lobbying bill, I see that David Miliband has written an article for the Financial Times about Syria (subscription). It is mostly about the humanitarian crisis there, and Miliband does not express a view on whether or not MPs were right to vote against military intervention last week. But he does warn against disengagement. The humanitarian priorities should be clear: reach those in Syria caught in the crossfire or its aftermath, and shore up the livelihoods of those in neighbouring countries. For those in the country, non-governmental organisations cannot stop the killing, but they can staunch the dying. That requires safe access for food and medical help, across the country and across combat lines. For doctors to fear reprisals for treating the wounded – and I have heard such concerns myself from Syrian surgeons – takes us back to the dark ages. In neighbouring countries, there is desperate human need and growing political pressure. The host governments at the local and national levels, especially in Jordan, Lebanon and Iraq, cannot be expected to manage population flows on this scale alone. Their demands for help should be heard. It is clear that, while international engagement is decreasingly popular in the advanced democracies, a multipolar world makes it increasingly necessary. Humanitarian intervention is about human need, not political sides – but it has political consequences. There is capacity to save more lives, but this needs resources and political will. The drums of war are reason to redouble humanitarian efforts, not forget them. So, where does that leave us? Angela Eagle described the lobbying bill as one of the worst pieces of legislation that she had seen for ages and, on the basis of what we have seen today and over the last few weeks, she clearly has a point. It has received an uncharacteristically withering verdict from the Electoral Commission, the regulator that is supposed to enforce it. The charity sector and others affected by part 2 are strongly and, it seems, universally opposed (as Eagle said, if Guido Fawkes and Owen Jones are agreed that that it is flawed, there must be something wrong) and organisations representing lobbyists are also distinctly unimpressed. Even Tory MPs were struggling to show much enthusiasm. And yet? Listening to the debate, I did not get the impression (rather to my surprise) that the government is going to be hit by a massive revolt. Tonight, at 7pm, MPs will vote on the second reading. David Davis hinted that he will vote against the programme motion (the one saying how many days should be set aside for debate), but I don't recall other coalition MPs declaring that they would defy the whip tonight. On bills like this the rebellions come later on and, with all MPs entitled to take part in the three committee stage debates next week, there are bound to be some government revolts. But over what? The opponents do not seem to be coalescing around a key demand that could form the basis of a government-defeating amendment. Some of those most unhappy this afternoon were raising concerns (like the potential impact on the work of MPs, and the Bill of Rights) of little interest to others. And the government does not seem to be in a mood to give in. What was most revealing about Andrew Lansley's speech was the bit when he explained the thinking behind the bill. The curbs on third-party campaigning have not been introduced by accident. They are there because the government believes in them. There are bound to be some sort of concessions but, based on what we've heard today, there is no reason to believe they will be significant. Many Labour MPs have been expressing their opposition to the lobbying bill on Twitter. Here are two Tories who have been sounding off too. Eagle says the government has given no reason why unions, but not other membership organisations, should have to endure these cumbersome regulations. Free trade unions are part of a vibrant society. The attempt to interfer with their work is another sinister aspect of the bill, she says. And that's it. The two opening speeches have finished. Eagle says that officials have been unable to explain the reasons for part 3 of the bill (the section dealing with unions). It has been said that it came out of a meeting between David Cameron and Nick Clegg, she says. Gavin Shuker, the Labour MP, says the government wants better information about union memberships. But we know how many members unions have. And we know how many members Labour and the Lib Dems have. The one organisation that will not release information about its members is the Conservative party, he says. Eagle says that Lansley's speech made it clear that the government deliberately wants to gag charities. The flaws in the bill are not just the product of poor drafting, she says. She says third parties accounted for less than 10% of election spending in 2010. Eagle says the bill is a threat to the Big Society. Tories may not like 38 Degrees, she says. But we live in a democracy and they are entitled to campaign. The TaxPayers' Alliance is among the groups protesting about it, he says. Even Guido Fawkes and the leftwing commentator Owen Jones are united in opposing it, she says. Angie Bray, a Conservative, says 38 Degrees is a lobbying organisation. Lobbying is what it does. Eagle says that Bray's government think it is a third party organisation. Eagle says the bill is so bad it has united transparency campaigners and lobbyists in condemning it. Labour will table amendments calling for a universal register, backed by effective sanctions. And it will close the loophole than enables Lynton Crosby to operate at the heart of Number 10 without declaring his clients. Eagle turns to part 1, the section dealing with lobbying. Only this government could introduce a bill on lobbying that would not affect Lynton Crosby, she says. And only the government could produce a bill that would not affect a lobbyist lobbying an MP on the lobbying bill. She says the bill will cover fewer than one fifth of people working in the £2bn lobbying industry. It would not cover any of the lobby scandals that have hit this parliament, she says. And she says the new register will be less extensive than the current voluntary register that operates. Labour's Gavin Shuker says that under the bill Hope Not Hate, which produces information about far-right candidates standing at elections, would be allowed to spend just a fraction of the BNP. Eagle says Lansley has provided an abject lesson in how not to produce legislation. Lansley should be ashamed of the bill, she says. Labour's Seema Malhotra says this bill could lead to elections being reduced to just political parties talking to each other. Eagle says that is a powerful point. Graham Allen says standing orders should be changed to say that pre-legislative scrutiny should be standard practice, save for bills introduced in an emergency. If that had happened in this case, there might be consensus on the bill, he says. Eagle says the political and constitutional reform committee is so concerned about this bill that it has had to hold emergency hearing over the recess. Graham Allen, the committee chairman, described the bill as a "dog's breakfast", she says. That prompted a response from the Tory MP Douglas Carswell that Allen was wrong because "far more thought has gone into pet nutrition", she says. Labour's Dennis Skinner says the Lib Dems are supporting this bill because they do not want the National Union of Students campaigning against them over tuition fees in 2015. "What a scandal," he says. Eagle says Skinner has made a good point. Labour's Stephen Doughty says he was involved in the Make Poverty History campaign in 2005. He says that, under the terms of the bill, campaigns like that could be stopped. Eagle agrees. That is what the Electoral Commission has said, she says. David Davis, the Conservative MP, says Eagle has criticised the "haste" with which the bill has been introduced. In relation to part 2, he tends to agree, he says. He asks if Labour will be voting against the programme motion. By convention, constitutional bills like this one are not subject to the guillotine, he says. (That seems to be a hint that Davis will vote against the programme motion.) Angela Eagle, the shadow leader of the Commons, is speaking now. This is one of the worst pieces of legislation I've seen any government produce in a very long time. Lansley has been landed with this "risible" bill because other ministers have been reluctant to touch this bill "with a barge pole". She says the bill is hurried, badly drafted, and a combination of the sinister and the partisan. Labour's Paul Flynn says the government should study the virtues of humility. Eagle says that's good advice. She says the bill could be summed up as "furious displacement activity" from a government that hopes the public will not notice its other problems. Lansley has now finished. The Labour MP John Denham says he thinks Lansley had an even harder time than he did over the health bill. Lansley is now turning to part 3 of the bill. Unions have to keep an accurate register of their members. The bill will build on this, he says. The extra burden on unions will be very modest, he says. Lansley says the bill will force third parties spending significant sums campaigning for one party to need approval from that party. This will increase transparency, he says. Graham Allen, the Labour chair of the political and constiutional reform committee, says the new rules cover staffing costs. If these costs are included in the defintion of election spending, they will not be able to use staff for other campaigning. Lansley says charities generally do not engage in party political campaigning. If they do engage in election campaigning, they should expect to be regulated. Lansley says the bill will not stop charities campaigning during election periods. It will just limit how much they can spend on election-related spending. Andrew George, a Lib Dem, says he used to work for a charity before becoming an MP. Will Lansley agree to delay the bill so the concerns of the Electoral Commission and charities can be addressed? Lansley says there are existing uncertainties in electoral law about what constitutes spending for electoral purposes. He suggests the new rules will make little difference. (But the Electoral Commission disagrees. I posted a link to its briefing for MPs earlier. See 10.32am. This is what it says about how the bill defines election spending. The current PPERA rules on non-party campaigning are relatively narrow in scope (only covering ‘election material’) and the definition of what is covered is relatively clear, so we are able to produce guidance that builds on the legislation. However, it can still be hard for campaigners to understand what activity is regulated. For instance, activity by charities (which are not allowed to be party political under charity law) can be covered. A common example is where an organisation invites prospective candidates at an election to say whether they support its views, and then issues a leaflet setting out the names of candidates who have expressed support. This will be controlled as election material if it is distributed to the public, even though those producing the material may argue that their aim is only to comment on public policy, or to influence politicians’ agendas, rather than to persuade the public to vote in a certain way. The Bill makes major changes to the current rules. The new rules are closely modelled on those that currently apply to political parties, but will have to be read and applied in a quite different context because third parties are so different from political parties ... This will be particularly challenging for campaigners because of the need to apply the definition of ‘election purposes’, which is new and untested in the context of non-party campaigning.) Lansley says charities will not be affected because they do not engage in party political activity. But some charities set up political wings that are not charities. And some campaigning is done by groups that are thought of as charities but which are not charities, he says. He says Labour acknowledges this in its amendment to the motion. Here's the text of the Labour amendment. That this House affirms its belief in the need for greater transparency in the lobbying industry and in British politics, and considers that there should be a universal register of all professional lobbyists backed by a code of conduct and sanctions, clear rules on third party campaigning, and real reform to get the big money out of politics; but declines to give a Second Reading to the Transparency of Lobbying, Third Party Campaigning and Trade Union Administration Bill because the proposals on lobbying cover only a tiny minority of the industry and will make lobbying less transparent, and the proposals on third party campaigning amount to a gag on charities and campaigners who have a democratic right to participate in important debates in the run up to elections; and strongly believes that the publication of such a Bill should have been preceded by a full process of pre-legislative scrutiny and consultation with affected parties. Labour's Chris Bryant says that, under the bill, every lawyer and in-house PR official at BSkyB would be able to meet ministers without having to register as a lobbyist. Only someone working for an outside firm would have to register. How can that be right? Lansley says that if people learn that BSkyB staff have had a meeting with ministers, they can guess that those staff were lobbying for BSkyB. Kevin Barron, the Labour chair of the Commons standards committee, says his committee met this morning to agree a short report saying the bill should be amended to ensure that MPs are not covered by it. Lansley says that the government will take notice of the committee's concerns. Labour's John Denham says the bill was introduced after the controversy about Lynton Crosby working for the cigarette industry at the same time as advising ministers to drop plans to ban branded tobacco packaging. Lansley says he was health secretary when the government consulted on cigarette packaging. He is in a position to say that Denham is talking "tosh". Sir Gerald Howarth, a Conservative, says the first part of the bill underlines the Bill of Rights, saying what is said in the Commons cannot be questioned in the courts. But the bill also legislates for lobbying. What would happen if Howarth were to raise an issue relating to defence that was not related to his constituency? Would he be covered? Lansley says the government does not want the bill to cover the normal activities of an MP. If there is any doubt, the government will amend the bill to make this clear. (Kevin Barron, the chair of the Commons standards committee, has also raised this concern.) John Hemming, a Lib Dem MP, asks if MEPs or councillors would be covered by the bill. Would they have to register as lobbyists? No, says Lansley. The bill covers consultant lobbying. Cheryl Gillan, the Conservative former Welsh secretary, says she is concerned that the bill could silence groups campaigning against the HS2 high speed rail project. Lansley says groups will still be able to campaign. But if an anti-HS2 group were trying to ensure that particular candidates lose, it would be covered by existing legislation. James Duddridge, a Conservative, says at the last election the third-party organisation that spent the most was Unison. That is why Labour are so angry, he says. Lansley says Duddridge has a point. Angela Eagle, the shadow leader of the Commons, quotes from the Electoral Commission's briefing. (See 10.32am.) She says this shows that Lansley's "complacency" is unjustified. Lansley says he had a meeting with the National Council for Voluntary Organisations yesterday. They have their own legal advice on the impact of the bill But that shows that the uncertainties supposedly in the new bill are actually in current legislation, he says. Here's a chart from the House of Commons library note (see 12.48pm) showing what impact the new rules would have on third-party groups that got involved in election campaigning in 2010. Tracey Crouch, a Conservative, says she used to work as a lobbyist, in the same firm as John Bercow. Can Lansley explain how the bill will not gag charities? Lansley says that the government has no intention of changing the boundaries governing what charities can do. Labour's Glenda Jackson interrupts. She says the bill has created a "firestorm" in her constituencies. People are angry about charities being silenced. Lansley says the things charities are worried about will not even happen. At the last election only two charities engaged in election-relating campaigning. Charities will still be free to campaign. The bill only affects election-related activities, he says. Andrew Lansley, the leader of the Commons, is opening the debate. Labour's Tom Clarke asks if the government will publish the results of its consultation on the bill. Lansley says the government consulted on the lobbying aspects of the bill. Labour did not even respond, he says. The second reading debate on the lobbying bill is starting. For reference, here is the Commons library note explaining in detail what the bill will do (pdf). • The Election Commission has strongly criticised the government's plans to tighten the rules on what charities can spend on campaigning in an election period. Jenny Watson made her comments in evidence to the political and constitutional reform committee ahead of this afternoon's second reading debate on the lobbying bill. Our view is that the bill as drafted creates a high level of uncertainty which will make it difficult for campaigners to be confident about how their activity from May onwards will be affected, while simultaneously imposing significant new burdens on such organisations. The wide definition of 'election purposes' gives us a great deal of discretion to interpret what activity will be regulated as political campaigning. This is discretion which we did not seek. It is likely that some of our readings of the law will be contentious and challenged, creating more uncertainty for those affected. This is made more likely by the change in our remit which the bill also makes and which again we did not seek. Some of the new controls in the bill may be impossible to enforce and Parliament will want carefully to consider what the changes might achieve in reality and balance this against the new burdens which the bill imposes. Andrew Lansley, the leader of the Commons, told the committee that the government did not believe the bill would have a significant impact on charities and that the government was just legislating to ensure that in future third-party spending could not distort the results of an election.
