But Graham Allen, the Labour chair of the committee, said the bill should be shelved for six months to allow it to be redrafted. Charity organisations have condemned the proposals in strong terms. And some lobbying groups are unhappy with the proposals on lobbying. Today the Public Relations Consultants Association said the bill should be dropped or rewritten. (See 11.13am.) And yesterday Jane Wilson, chief executive of the Chartered Institute of Public Relations, also criticised the plans. We are completely in favour of well researched and thought out ways of increasing transparency in political processes in order to build a greater level of trust in our democracy, but this bill is very poor and will not achieve that outcome ... The government should go back to the drawing board rather than try to force this legislation through in the face of good advice from organisations including the NCVO and the Electoral Commission. • Michael Gove, the education secretary, has criticised Labour MPs for cheering when the government lost the vote on Syria last week at the same time as news came through of an attack on children. (See 9.57am.) The Labour MP Michael Dugher said Gove's remarks were "grossly offensive". • Boris Johnson, the Conservative mayor of London, has renewed his call for David Cameron not to rule out a second Commons vote on attacking Syria. I have been very hostile and sceptical about British involvement in Syria, we all remember what happened in Iraq. But if you see someone gassing innocent civilians, that is a chilling, chilling memory for people in Europe of what happened in the 1930s and 1940s and of the cost of doing nothing. What I have said is if it can be proved that it really was the Assad regime that was responsible, and there's more proof, there's more evidence coming in, and if the Americans - who after all are the only real power that has the men and the material to deliver any retribution - can come up with a plan that is coherent, that is limited, that is punitive in some way but does not actually engage the West in some long-term commitment in a Syrian conflict, then I think there is a case for Britain looking again at whether or not we should participate. • George Osborne, the chancellor, has said there would be "very significant" tax rises or cuts to public spending if a Scottish oil fund was set up post-independence. He spoke as the Treasury published a fresh report on the economic impact of independence (pdf). • The Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development has sharply increased its growth forecast for the UK. As the Press Association reports, the OECD is predicting 1.5% growth in the UK in 2013, up from a forecast of 0.8% issued in May. It said: The pace of recovery in the major advanced economies improved in the second quarter and growth is expected to be maintained at a similar rate in the second half of the year. Activity is expanding at encouraging rates in North America, Japan and the United Kingdom, while the euro area as a whole is no longer in recession. • David Cameron has said that 93 new free schools are opening this month. • The Local Government Association has said that almost half of England's school districts will have more primary pupils than places within two years. Graham Allen goes next. Q: What is the purpose of part 3 of the bill? (Part 1 deals with lobbying, part 2 with third-party election spending, and part 3 tightens the rules about unions having to keep a register of their members.) Lansley says the government wants to make sure that parties have reliable information about their members. Q: Why not do that for political parties too? Lansley says union membership figures are relevant to decisions relating to votes in strike ballots and the operation of political funds. Those arguments do not apply to political parties, he says. And that's it. The session is over. Lansley will be up again in the Commons in about half an hour. I'll post a summary in a moment. Stephen Williams takes over again. Q: Have you been surprised by the depth of opposition to part 2 of the bill? Lansley says he is "disappointed", but not entirely surprised. People often over-characterise what government legislation will do, because that creates a row and gets them into the paper. But this bill will not stop groups campaign. It won't even stop 38 Degrees. Perhaps it might be nice to constrain 38 Degrees, he says. But the bill won't stop what they do. Q: I met 38 Degrees yesterday. They think the bill will constrain what they do. Why does the bill cut allowable spending by third-party groups so much? Lansley says the new limits will not affect many groups, but it will stop third-party groups spending more on election campaigning in future. Lansley says in the last session of parliament 13 measures were subject to pre-legislative scrutiny. But not everything can be subject to pre-legislative scrutiny. And, as the election gets closer, that gets harder for the government. Graham Allen steps in. Q: The lobbying bill was going to be about lobbying. The committee published a report. The government delayed responding for a year. Then suddenly the government publishes a bill with new sections that were added by surprise. Why was that? Lansley says that because the government had not announced anything, that does not mean work was not going on behind the scenes. The government never said it would introduce a bill dealing just with lobbying. Bills often end up including more than one part, he says. The three parts of this bill are connected, he says. The government consulted on its plans for lobbyists. But that does not mean the government should not be allowed to introduce other measures in the bill. Q: You are not winning many friends. This seems intended to alienate the groups affected by this. Lansley says he shares "a sense of regret" for the government's failure to consult with the committee. But there is substantial time available in the Commons to debate the bill. (The committee stage will take three days next week, with the debate taking place on the floor of the Commons because it is a constitutional bill.) Stephen Williams, the Lib Dem MP, is asking questions now. Q: Why was the bill not included in the Queen's Speech? Lansley says the proposals were not ready at that point. But it is normal to introduce bills in a session not included in the Queen's Speech. Q: Whose idea was part 2 (the section dealing with charities)? The government's, says Lansley, He will not elaborate. He says the government decided to legislate because it was concerned that third-party spending during elections could over time increase relative to spending by political parties, possibly distorting the result in some constituencies. Q: So you planned to stir up a gale of protests from charities? Lansley says he is an old hand. Groups complain about government. Q: This is an atrocity on a par with the Dangerous Dogs Act. Will you apologise for time it took responding to his committee? Lansley says his colleagues have already apologised to the committee about that. But the government has engaged in more pre-legislative scrutiny than any previous government. Paul Flynn, the Labour MP, goes next. Q: Did David Cameron deliver a major speech denouncing campaigning by groups like Oxfam? No, says Lansley. Q: So why are these groups a major feature of the bill? They are not, says Lansley. He has regular meetings as an MP with someone local from Oxfam. But that does not relate to election campaigning, so that kind of activity would not be covered. Q: Not a soul has offered a word in support of this bill. That's not true, Lansley says. He concedes that the bill will not stop people behaving badly. The government believes in transparency. It has taken substantial steps to make this "the most transparent, open and accountable" ever seen. A gap has been identified. This bill will address this. Q: Campaigning groups say this bill is worthless. They are wrong, says Lansley. Lansley says that for about 18 months before he became an MP he worked for a firm that was engaged in lobbying. Q: Why do the lobbying rules in the bill apply to people lobbying UK ministers and officials, but not ministers or officials from the devolved parliaments? That's because the government does not want to trespass on the rights of the devolved parliaments, Lansley. Andrew Turner, a Conservative, is asking the questions now. Q: Why is Nick Clegg not in charge of this? Lansley says Clegg recused himself from decisions relating to lobbying for family reasons. (That's because Clegg's wife, Miriam, works for a law firm involved in lobbying.) But that's not why he got involved, Lansley says. He got involved because it needed someone at cabinet level to take charge. Allen is still asking the questions. Q: Can you understand why parliament, charities and others are angry aboutyour failure to consult us properly? Lansley says he only became involved in this issue three months ago. He can understand why the committee is unhappy that it took the government so long to respond to its report on this. The government has been taking its time because it has been thinking hard about it. Q: The Electoral Commission and others are not "threatening" the government. They want the legislation to work. So why did you publish the bill one day before the recess, with the second reading only three working days later. Why don't you get people on your side to get a better bill? Lansley says part 1, the section dealing with lobbyists, has been under discussion for some time. On part 2 (the section dealing with charities, that was introduced by surprise), Lansley says he could understand Allen's concerns if the government was trying to use part 2 for the purposes charities claim. But it is not doing that. Sometimes it takes a while to get things right, he says. Allen says Lansley is suggesting there is not a problem with charities. But people do think there is a problem with lobbying. Yet the bill will have little impact on lobbying, but a big impact on charities. Lansley says the government is getting more transparent about the meetings ministers have with lobbyists. Once you have a culture of transparency, it is important to know who lobbyists XYZ are representing when it is revealed they have had a meeting with a minister. Lansley said that at the last election just two charities registered as third-party participants in the election. And there were 30 registered third parties overall, he says. He says the government expects that number to double under the bill. Andrew Lansley, the leader of the Commons, is giving evidence now. Graham Allen, the committee chairman, invites him to make an opening statement. Lansley says that, in the midst of discussion about the bill, people have forgotten what it is trying to do. The government is not setting out to control the lobbying industry, or regulate the whole of lobbying. He says the coalition programme talked about a register of "lobbyists", not a register for lobbying. It was always intended to limit the scope of the bill. And on part 2 (the section dealing with charities), third-party election campaigning is already regulated. The government does not want to substantially change that. But it does want to limit the amount of money spent, and tighten the definition of controlled expenditure so that it mirrors what is controlled expenditure by political parties. He says this should not have a "chilling effect" on charities. By definiton, charities should not be engaging in party politics anyway, he says. Andrew Lansley, the leader of the Commons, will be giving evidence to the committee in a moment. First, though, here's a statement I've just received from Francis Ingham, director general of the Public Relations Consultants Association. The PRCA wants the lobbying bill to be radically rewritten or dropped. This is what Ingham says: This bill is pointless because it will add literally zero to our understanding of who lobbies whom. Every single organisation that might be on the government’s register is already on an existing voluntary register. The scope of the register is so narrow that less than a quarter of our existing register members think that they might be caught by it. And that is because the bill limits itself to ‘third party advocates’; and - bizarrely and without reason - defines lobbying as direct contact with ministers or permanent secretaries, despite the fact that the amount of lobbying that third party advocates do with ministers and permanent secretaries is minuscule. It will also be costly. Half a million in year one, according to the government. £200,000 a year thereafter. The government believes a thousand organisations will be on the register. At most, it will probably be about a dozen. So the costs for those who are indeed captured by it will be correspondingly enormous. So what should the government do? Simple. Reform or drop. Reform this Bill radically right now and include everyone who lobbies, including agencies, lawyers and in house teams. The PRCA is one of several organisations representing firms involved in lobbying. It says it represents the majority of the top 150 UK PR consultancies. Graham Allen asks about fox hunting. Different groups campaign on different sides, he says. During an election this could become a key issue. As an MP, he enjoys having to justify his views. But isn't there a chance that the rival groups could end up challenging spending by their opponents? Jenny Watson says that question highlights a problem for the Electoral Commission. It would need extra resources to enforce compliance. But it wants to hear what parliament wants before asking for extra money. Allen says he would rather see the commission promoting public debate. Shouldn't the commission have been consulted about these plans? Yes, says Watson. She was "surprised" the commission was not consulted. Graham Allen says at the moment there is great respect for the Electoral Commission. But if the commission had the powers set out in this bill, that could lead to people being put in charge of it for partisan reasons. Is Jenny Watson worried about that? Watson says this is a matter for parliament to debate. Graham Allen asked Jenny Watson a moment ago if the bill could lead to "the corrosion of the Big Society, particularly in terms of its interaction with democracy". Watson said she would prefer to let charities answer that question directly (which makes it sound like she thinks the answer is yes). She would prefer to comment on the workability of the legislation, she said. In the committee Graham Allen, the chairman, says his committee will be suggesting amendments to the bill. How will the Electoral Commission try to get the bill amended? Jenny Watson says she hopes that there will be an ongoing discussion with government. That would have happened if more time had been available for consultation. In the past the commission has sought support from individuals to put amendments down, she says. In their evidence Jenny Watson and her colleagues have been making some of the points set out in the Electoral Commission's briefing for MPs. (See 10.32am.) Watson said that it would be possible to address some of the commission's concerns by rewriting the bill as it goes through parliament. But that would bring its own problems, she said. It might be possible to recast the bill's definition of what's covered in a way which provides more clarity about parliament's expectation about where different types of regulated activity might be caught. I think we say that in our written evidence. The difficulty with that is that we are effectively re-engineering the vehicle as it is being driven along the road, which isn't necessarily ideal. The Electoral Commission has set out its concerns about the lobbying bill in a 13-page briefing for MPs (pdf). The commission would have to enforce the new rules on campaigning by charities (see 9.11am) and normally, when asked to comment on government electoral proposals, it adopts a constructive tone. As you'll see, the language in this briefing is diplomatic. But, in Electoral Commission terms, it's as strong as it gets, even quite caustic. Clearly, the commission is deeply unhappy about these plans. Here are some extracts. • The Electoral Commission says the bill raises some "significant issues of workability". The Bill both widens the scope of the current rules on non-party campaigning that affects parties and groups of candidates, and imposes some additional controls on such campaigning. In our view, as drafted, the Bill raises some significant issues of workability that you may wish to explore at Second Reading. Areas that you may wish to focus on in particular include that: • the Bill creates significant regulatory uncertainty for large and small organisations that campaign on, or even discuss, public policy issues in the year before the next general election, and imposes significant new burdens on such organisations • the Bill effectively gives the Electoral Commission a wide discretion to interpret what activity will be regulated as political campaigning. It is likely that some of our readings of the law will be contentious and challenged, creating more uncertainty for those affected. While we as the independent regulator should be free to decide when the rules have been broken, and how to deal with breaches of the rules, we do not think it is appropriate for us to have a wide discretion over what activity is covered by the rules • some of the new controls in the Bill may in practice be impossible to enforce, and it is important that Parliament considers what the changes will achieve in reality, and balances this against the new burdens imposed by the Bill on campaigners. • It says the government has not explained properly why many of the proposals in the bill are needed. In our view the Government has not yet set out clearly the rationale for many of the changes in the Bill, and it is therefore hard to assess whether the Bill delivers the Government’s policy objectives. You may wish to explore the exact policy aims of the Bill at Second Reading. • It says campaigners will not know what activity will be covered by the bill. It confirms that blogs could be covered by it. (This is ConservativeHome's concern - see 9.23am.) This will be particularly challenging for campaigners because of the need to apply the definition of ‘election purposes’, which is new and untested in the context of non-party campaigning. In the limited time available we will aim to produce guidance to assist with this, and will offer advice on particular queries where possible, but our experience strongly suggests that it will not be straightforward to apply the new rules to many specific types of activities. Campaigners will face additional uncertainty if there is a legal challenge to our interpretation of the law. These factors will create a lot of complexity and uncertainty for those who may be covered by the rules. Some brief examples of the sort of issues that may emerge under the Bill as drafted include ... • a website includes a blog which comments on the activities and views of politicians from various parties in the run-up to the election. Some politicians believe the blog is biased against them or their party. Will the cost of producing the blog, and a proportion of the cost of maintaining and promoting the website and associated social media activity, count as ‘for election purposes’? Our current view is that it is doubtful that the blog’s website is covered by the PPERA exemption from the non-party rules for “the publication of any matter relating to an election, other than an advertisement, in a newspaper or periodical”. • It says the government has probably severely under-estimated the financial impact of the bill on charities. The Impact Assessment also estimates that the changes to registration thresholds will lead to between zero and 30 additional campaigners needing to register in 2015 compared to 2010. It is difficult to estimate the likely level of additional registration given the uncertainty around the scope of activity covered by the Bill, but again this appears likely to be a severe under-estimate on the basis of our recent discussions with campaigners. • It says the bill would give the commission too much power. By defining something as election-related, the commission could end up effectively banning events like marches or rallies. We do not think it is appropriate for us to have the sort of wide discretion over the meaning and scope of the regulatory regime that the Bill as drafted appears to provide. Electoral law avoids giving a wide discretion to unelected officials, whether those who run elections or those who regulate campaigning, precisely because of the high risk of being drawn into political controversy and challenge. • It says the rules saying third parties would have to report their spending weekly during the election period are impractical. The new requirement for weekly reporting after Parliament has been dissolved is likely to be particularly onerous and potentially impracticable for large organisations with branches or other complex structures. Ed Miliband is also calling for a rethink on the bill. He has just posted this on Twitter. Graham Allen, the chair of the Commons political and constitutional reform committee, has just said that he wants the government to delay the lobbying bill for six months so that it can be send to a special committee for redrafting. He was speaking as his committee was taking evidence. It is just about to start hearing now from three witnesses: Jenny Watson, chair of the Electoral Commission, Tony Stafford, its head of guidance and policy, and Peter Horne, the its director of party and election finance. Back to Syria for a moment. On BBC News this morning Michael Gove, the education secretary (and a leading cabinet hawk - he once wrote a book saying the West needed to take a very robust approach to Islamist extremism), was asked about last week's vote. Gove reportedly shouted "You're a disgrace" at MPs who had voted against the government. He was asked whether that was true, and whether he wanted parliament to vote on the matter again. He replied: I did become heated last week, that is absolutely right. At the moment that the government lost the vote on the motion, there were Labour MPs cheering as though it were a sort of football match and they had just won. At the same time on the news, we were hearing about an attack on a school in Syria and the death toll there rising - and the incongruity of Labour MPs celebrating as children had been killed by a ruthless dictator, I am afraid got to me and I did feel incredibly emotional. I do feel emotional about this subject. The prime minister explained about the vote and that is all I want to say. Asked again if he wanted the Commons to revisit the subject, he said: "That is all I want to say." It seemed fairly clear that he believed the answer should be yes. And the Today programme broadcast an interesting interview with Jack Kean, the former vice chief of staff of the US army. He said the Commons defeat on Syria last week introduced "palpable fear" in Washington. We operate side by side with the UK and we know who our closest ally is. We certainly would much rather do this with the UK side by side, that's how the military feels, I really think the leaders of the country feel. I think, if I may use some rich language here, the humiliating defeat the prime minister suffered in Parliament, I can only surmise was stunning to the resident and I think it impacted on him. I think that's one of the motivations that introduced what I call palpable fear and one of the reasons why he is seeking political cover himself. Andrew Lansley, the leader of the Commons, was interviewed about the lobbying bill on the Today programme this morning. Here are the key points. • Lansley said the bill would not stop charities campaigning on political issues. I’ve been talking to the charities, we had a meeting yesterday and what I explained to them, and I hope the public will understand, is that we’re not setting out in any sense to constrain any charity or organisation who wants to campaign on policy issues. The simple issue is that, as already exists, at election time, in election periods, if an organisation, a third party that isn’t a political party wants to spend a significant amount of money trying to influence that election directly, that is promoting a candidate or a party, that should be registered and there should be limits on that just as political parties have limits on their expenditure. • He said the lobbying provisions in the bill were designed to close a loophole in the current transparency arrangements. Under the current system, ministers have to publish the meetings they hold with firms that lobby them. But if they have a meeting with professional lobbyists, the public do not know who the lobbyists are representing. The bill will make lobbyists declare the names of their clients. • He said ministers would be publish more information about the meetings they hold with people lobbying them. • He said the lobbying parts of the bill were deliberately limited because the government did not want to create "a bureaucratic monster". • He said the coalition was "the most transparent government we’ve ever seen". We’re publishing much more information, not only about ministers’ diaries but about procurement and procurement is absolutely instrumental, remember. Publishing data about procurement is a vital part of the process of transparency and avoiding any undue influence. • He said he wanted Labour to agree to publish shadow ministerial diaries showing who lobbyies the opposition Labour "because they are part of the political system too". I've taken the quotes from PoliticsHome. Charities and other groups worried about the lobbying bill are going to parliament today to raise their concerns with MPs. They include ConservativeHome, the leading Conservative website. ConservativeHome's Mark Wallace explains his website is taking a stand. Alongside representatives of a wide range of organisations, including the British Legion, Guide Dogs, the Wildlife Trust, Oxfam, the Children’s Society, the British Youth Council, the Quakers in Britain and LabourList, I will be going to Portcullis House to raise our concerns about it with MPs . It's now two weeks since Paul warned of the threat the Bill poses to the independence of blogs like ConservativeHome. The more we examine it, the more damaging it looks. As David Allen Green wrote for us last Friday: "...unless the government re-thinks its proposals, then a number of bodies – such as Conservative Home – are going to be caught up in misconceived and illiberal legal regime." This isn't just about blogs - though for obvious reasons we believe the blogosphere should not be strangled by regulatory red tape. The Bill is so loose in its language and so vague in its drafting that anyone who spends over £5,000 on anything that can be in any way said to potentially affect an election will be caught up in the rules it lays out. MPs are debating the lobbying bill today or, to give it its full name, the transparency of lobbying, non-party campaigning and trade union administration bill. It was supposed to be about creating a statutory register of lobbyists, but when it was published just before the summer recess attention focused on the surprise inclusion of measures that could drastically curb campaigning by charities on political issues in the 12 months before a general election. Under the current rules third-party groups such as charities can spend up to £988,800 during this period on material, such as leaflets, that could affect the outcome of the election. The bill not only slashes this limit to £388,080, but also broadens the definition of what counts as spending – to include overheads and staff costs – and widens the definition of what counts as election-related activity to include work that could affect the outcome, even if that was not its purpose. It also limits spending per constituency to £9,750, and lowers to £5,000 the amount charities can spend before they have to register with the Electoral Commission. In an editorial today the Guardian describes it as “shameless, shameful and shaming.” And Polly Toynbee has more in her column. Why the rush? To get the law in place by next May, so the clock starts ticking to gag campaigning for the year’s runup to the 2015 election. Nick Clegg is a prime mover on this bill, as minister in charge of constitutional reform. Why are the Lib Dems so gung ho for something so illiberal? They are said to fear targeted campaigns, such as the National Union of Students reminding voters of Lib Dem MPs who signed up “in blood” to a pledge never to raise tuition fees. The government hotly denies this bill will do anything other than prevent US-style abuse by “Super-Pacs” spending unlimited sums on campaigns and supporting a party without mentioning its name. But that’s already banned. The government is in a hurry to get this bill through the Commons because it wants the Electoral Commission to be ready to enforce the new rules from May next year, when the 12-month countdown to the next election begins. That’s why Andrew Lansley, the leader of the Commons who is in charge of the bill, is giving evidence to a select committee about it this morning before opening the debate an hour or so later. I’ll be covering Lansley’s evidence and the opening of the debate in the Commons in detail. There is quite a lot of other politics on the go too. Here’s the agenda for the day. 9am: Boris Johnson, the mayor of London, hosts his Ask Boris LBC phone-in. 9.30am: George Osborne gives a speech to an oil and gas conference in Aberdeen. The Treasury is also publishing a paper on the economic impact of Scottish independence. As Severin Carrell reported yesterday, Osborne will say that Scottish households will be about £2,000 better off and freer to work and trade with the rest of the UK if voters reject independence. 10am: Jenny Watson, chair of the Electoral Commission, gives evidence to the political and constitutional reform committee about the lobbying bill. At 11.15am Andrew Lansley, the leader of the Commons, gives evidence. 11.30am: William Hague, the foreign secretary, takes questions in the Commons. 12.40pm: MPs start debating the second reading of the lobbying bill. 3.30pm: Sir Hugh Orde, president of the Association of Chief Police Officers, gives evidence to the Commons home affairs committee about EU justice and home affairs opt-outs. As usual, I’ll also be covering all the breaking political news as well as looking at the papers and bringing you the best politics from the web. I’ll post a lunchtime summary before the debate starts and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I’m on @AndrewSparrow.

headline: Politics as it happened
trail_text: Inspector-General to examine complex asylum cases - Coalition declares national security failure
body_text: Labor's most famous backbencher is coming up very shortly. I've seen a preview of the interview, and having passed the embargo at 7.30pm, can now share. The key points of Rudd’s interview with host Leigh Sales are as follows. Rudd argues that Tony Abbott’s asylum policy is based on an absolute lie. The Opposition leader can’t stop the boats and tow them back to Indonesia. It's just not going to happen - and he knows it. Indonesia has made its position clear. It’s a slogan, not a substantive position. The Coalition’s campaign on debt deficits - the crisis in government finances - is another absolute lie, Rudd contends. Australia has a Triple A credit rating. Rudd mocks Abbott’s transformation in the Liberal leadership from conservative hardliner to policy moderate. Just because you put on a blue tie doesn’t mean you’ve changed your spots. He rejects suggestions he and his supporters are destabilising the Prime Minister. Rudd declares he’ll be campaigning his heart out for Labor. I’m going to take the argument up to the Australian people wherever I can. Leigh Sales asks Rudd whether voters have stopped listening to Julia Gillard? He reponds with an argument for collective responsibility. 100 days, he says, is an eternity in politics. Everyone has the opportunity to argue the case for Labor. He's asked will he stay the full term in parliament? The lips purse characteristically. Of course I will. I don’t believe in bringing on unnecessary by-elections. Sales presses on. Are there any circumstances in which you’d take on the Labor leadership? Rudd says the caucus voted and I accepted their response. The caucus affirmation was a convincing and strong win for Julia Gillard, he says. My verdict on this performance? A full trowel-load of helping. Let no-one say Kevin is not helping. Let noone say there's no 'K' in team. Good night to you all. See you again soon on Politics Live. If you'd prefer just the twenty questions to Christine Milne, and her twenty answers - without the journalistic filter - you can see the full transcript here. I see we've just posted the next instalment of one of my special projects for Guardian Australia - find a way of getting some oxygen and change-of-pace into a political interview. When we interviewed Prime Minister Julia Gillard last week we put 20 questions to her from a panel of Australians who were interested in the opportunity of an open-sourced interview. Our same group posed a question each to the Greens leader Christine Milne. I've written a piece of analysis weaving the questions and answers together. You can read that here if so inclined. Here's an excerpt to give you a flavour: Christine Milne remembers The Miracle of Our Lady of Fatima, the feature film made in 1952, because it was her first visit to the cinema. In response to a question put to her by film director Glendyn Ivin, Milne recalls: “It was a huge outing for us but I’ve never forgotten it because the children in the movie were threatened with being put in a big pot of boiling oil and it traumatised me for years.” Things improved after the advent of the Carry On films, which came with bonus carbohydrates. “After that, my father loved taking us to the Devonport drive-in in his old ute to watch the Carry On films and we would have hot chips at half time, which was always lots of fun.” From a man who ought to know. We'll take this as a comment on events of the evening and the week by Kevin Rudd's former press secretary, Lachlan Harris. Possibly a little eccentric, but I'll do the summary before we cover Kevin Rudd's interview at 7.30. We just need to stand still and work out where things stand with 100 days to polling day. It's very hard to believe there are only two more sitting weeks in this 43rd parliament. So, today: The Coalition continued to pressure the Gillard Government over the case of an Egyptian man detained in a low security immigration detention facility in the Adelaide Hills, even though he'd been flagged by Interpol. The government dug in, resisting calls to widen the inquiry it announced yesterday would be undertaken by the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security. The government argued that the IGIS (not the parliament) was the right body to examine the issues surrounding this case. It has not released any detailed terms of reference for the probe. The Prime Minister told parliament the IGIS could not be directed by the parliament - it was fully independent, and the government would give full cooperation. Julia Gillard accused Tony Abbott of substituting slogans for substance, and running a campaign of fear in relation to boat arrivals and national security. She continued to attack the Coalition's policy in this space as unworkable. That issue dominated the political day. The government also introduced legislation giving effect to its much telegraphed crackdown on the use of 457 visas. Rudd addresses the two subjects he's asked most often about in his 7.30 Report outing: China and the Labor leadership. The ABC has released a teaser of Kevin Rudd's interview on the 7.30 Report this evening. Labor should not be constructing alibis for defeat. I'll hang on this evening and cover that interview live. The questions relate to a story the newspaper is obviously pursuing in relation to asbestos and the NBN roll-out - an issue that we've covered extensively on the blog this week. Bit of background might help here. The Gillard Government has had it's up and downs with News Limited. I think that's fair to say. Senior ministers believe the Murdoch media has it in for the Labor government. There have been cycles of confrontation between Labor ministers and News running over this term in parliament. Labor went through a phase a while ago of releasing questions put to it by News Limited publications in order to highlight that answers had been supplied to journalists queries. It was an effort to highlight any unfairness in coverage. (Of course releasing answers to questions also had the effect of knocking off a "scoop". News Limited was furious about this escalation.) Then there was the whole cycle of confrontation around the Gillard Government's media reform package - tougher regulation for newspapers following the Finkelstein and Convergence Reviews. That package hit the fence after a huge push back from media companies - all of them really - but News was the loudest opponent. Interesting to see that Senator Conroy is back in the business of releasing questions and answers prior to publication. Bit of a theme going this afternoon it would seem. The Communications Minister Stephen Conroy has just issued a statement letting rip against The Daily Telegraph. It's headlined: Daily Telegraph: not interested in the facts Today I was contacted by the Daily Telegraph. I was asked seven questions (responses below). It is clear that the Daily Telegraph is not interested in the facts in this case. When dealing with something as serious as the safety of workers and the community, it is important that the facts are reported without misrepresentation. We must be absolutely vigilant each and every time we deal with asbestos. There can be no short cuts. This is the Tweet to which John Murphy refers. Murphy refers to a recent Tweet from Murdoch. Labor is not frightened of Rupert, he declares. How dare Rupert Murdoch tweet “Oz polls show nothing can save this miserable government. Election cannot come soon enough. People decided and tuned out months ago”. The reason people tune out Mr Murdoch is because of the tripe served up in your tabloids and your cross-promotion of that tripe. Everyone knows you twist the political agenda to suit your business agenda and in so doing you crush and corrupt our democracy. We are not frightened of you. And we will not surrender to your massive media power encapsulated in your tweet. Labor backbencher John Murphy is making a blistering contribution in parliament's adjournment debate on one of his favourtite subjects - Rupert Murdoch and his news organisation. His publications print nothing but "tripe", Murphy declares. Murphy: It fits Mr Murdoch’s political and business agenda to knock this Labor government. Make no mistake, Mr Murdoch’s political and business agenda are one and the same. If it’s good for Mr Murdoch’s business, he will support it. If it’s bad knock it down and destroy it. If citizenship gets in the way of business expediency, trade it in for another. If a newspaper and its staff get caught up in a phone-hacking scandal, don’t expect Mr Murdoch to accept personal responsibility. Blame the journalists, blame the editors, blame everyone else. Then shut the newspaper down. Australian employees of Mr Murdoch should not expect a better response when their turn to face the axe comes. And it will come soon. Why? Because it is expedient for business. The financial bottom line comes first and foremost ahead of all else. Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor has just added to answer he made in Question Time today. There are no other people in immigration detention that are subject to Interpol notices he says - but there are 55 people in high security detention subject to adverse security assessments. Call me maybe. Kevin Rudd on his way out of the chamber. Picture worth a thousand words. Some stellar eye contact there between the Prime Minister and Kevin Rudd. Not a sight you see every day, Tony Abbott kneeling before Malcolm Turnbull. I've been chasing the terms of reference for the inquiry by the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security since the Prime Minister announced the initiative yesterday. The Prime Minister's office has responded just now. I'm told there are no terms of reference beyond what the Prime Minister said in the chamber yesterday. I'll repeat that for the benefit of Politics Live readers. The Prime Minister: Today I have directed the Inspector General of Intelligence and Security to examine the management of Australian government agencies of persons seeking asylum who present complex security issues, particularly this case. That's it. No further criteria, or dot points. For the record, that's your terms of reference. The general unwinding over the past few days extends to the House being unable to maintain a quorum after Question Time. Perhaps there is a jolly afternoon tea somewhere. Stink eye. Exhibit A. Liberal frontbencher Bronwyn Bishop, a lady who puts great store in the House of Representatives practice, would like Speaker Burke to reconsider. But the lady is not for turning. But some biffo nonetheless. The MP bringing forward the afternoon's Matter of Public Importance debate - Labor's Stephen Jones - is not in the chamber. The debate can't proceed as usual. Mr Abbott objects in strenuous terms. He wants Speaker Burke to bring forward another debate. Speaker Burke is annoyed at his reflection on the chair. Abbott explains that wasn't his intention, to reflect on the chair. He says he's trying to defend her position. I am embarrassed. I ashamed. Speaker Burke insists that is not within the standing orders to dial up another MPI debate. Liberal MPs continue to argue the toss. The Speaker is not amused. She pushes on. The Prime Minister has placed further questions on the notice paper. No suspension of the standing orders this afternoon. Tactics dear fellow, tactics. Tony Abbott and Christopher Pyne. Manager of Opposition business Christopher Pyne is raising other media reports of asylum seekers behaving badly. He wants to know whether the new inquiry will look at the full range of matters. The Prime Minister parodies Pyne's reliance on newspaper clippings as the source of his questions, and shouts about kooks and cranks on the internet being his next source of material. The Opposition benches are cracking up laughing. They are roundly unconvinced by the Prime Minister's line of attack. The body language suggests the Coalition knows it's thrashing Labor on the politics of this issue. Julia Gillard: It's just a game to you. It's been a game all week. The Health Minister wanted to know if her Queensland shadow, Peter Dutton, would stand up for the interests of his home state, or sit there silent, as he has done for years. Is this a pool fence I see before me? The Prime Minister declares in conclusion that Tony Abbott can be trusted with a slogan but not with national security, which is a complicated business. Health Minister Tanya Plibersek gets a Dorothy Dixer on Queensland health and has an uncharacteristic shout at those opposite. Tony Abbott would like the Prime Minister to guarantee that the Inspector-General's inquiry will be completed and made public before the election. Julia Gillard: It is impossible for me to give a direction to the Inspector-General. She's an independent office holder, Gillard says. It's the law. It passed through the parliament. We won't go around thugging people, the Prime Minister says. Gillard is asked to withdraw. She withdraws. Transport Minister Anthony Albanese lets fly with a morale builder for the backbench about a lack of spending by the Queensland Government on infrastructure. The Tokyo solution. The Indian solution. Albanese is making a point about Quensland transport officials cramming commuters onto trains and buses. Much chortling. Deputy Liberal leader Julie Bishop asks a question of the Attorney-General Mark Dreyfus. Isn't the issue of whether the Egyptian man has committed offences in Australia rather irrelevant if he's committed serious offences elsewhere? Mark Dreyfus says the man in question remains in detention, has been in detention at all times, is now in detention, and in addition there is no evidence he has committed any offence while in Australia. Meanwhile, back at BobKat ranch. Croaky Scott Morrison is at the dispatch box. That voice will go very soon I suspect. Why, he asks, is the Prime Minister continuing to give the benefit of the doubt to a convicted jihadist terrorist? The Prime Minister says she will always put Australia's national security first. Morrison is back again. Will your inquiry also examine the conduct of your ministers? The Prime Minister says the Inspector-General is not subject to direction by her. The Inspector General has serious, wide-ranging and intrusive powers. The government will cooperate in every way with this inquiry. While Question Time is underway in the House, Bob Katter has his own personal Question Time, via social media, in his office. It's his practice on a Thursday. Mike Bowers went down to have a look. Here's Bob and his peeps, with the Prime Minister thundering away in the House on the TV in the background. Nationals leader Warren Truss would like the Prime Minister to agree that the mishandling of the Egyptian case has undermined public confidence in the management of border security. The Prime Minister contends that the Coalition has been the shambles this week, not the government. Australia is well served by our security agencies. This person was always in detention. She adds it was appropriate for the Inspector-General to look into the case, so she's been asked to do so. A Dorothy Dixer now on education. The Prime Minister is advocating the Gonski package and congratulating New South Wales for supporting the reforms. She notes it is possible to be a conservative and support changes beneficial for children. He (the NSW Education Minister) cares about Australia's children and Australia's future. Why can't those opposite do the same? Leader of the Opposition Tony Abbott asks the Prime Minister why she initially decried the Egyptian case as a beat up and has now launched an inquiry into the matter. The Prime Minister retorts that she's launched the inquiry becuase she is a supporter of facts. Julia Gillard: You have no solutions on border security. No answers, just a campaign of fear, where the facts have never mattered. A Dorothy Dixer now to allow Brendan O'Connor to argue the case for the changes he's introduced this morning to 457 visas. The Prime Minister subsequently asks the Manager of Opposition Business Christopher Pyne to withdraw an unparliamentary interjection. It would appear to be xenophobia. Pyne withdraws, but objects to having to withdraw. It being 2pm, Speaker Anna Burke is in the chair. Shadow Minister for Border Security Michael Keenan opens the batting. It's a question to Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor. Any update from your officials? O'Connor: These are of course very serious matters. The advice I've received is there are no other IMAs (irregular maritime arrivals) in the network that are the subject of an Interpol red notice. Keenan is back with a picture of a pool fence. (The Coalition's riff is the Egyptian was held behind a "pool fence" at Inverbrackie). Can the minister guarantee there are no more terrorists being held in detention? Brendan O'Connor essentially repeats his previous answer. Gallows humour from the Prime Minister's communications director, John McTernan? news.com has jumped in, boots and all. Everyone, and I mean everyone, should pull their heads in, Kevin Rudd said yesterday. Tonight, he'll stick his head out. Where has politics touched down this lunchtime? The Coalition is continuing to press for a wide-ranging parliamentary inquiry into the case of an Egyptian held in a low security immigration detention facility despite being flagged by Interpol. The government argues it is pursuing the appropriate course of action by handing this issue to the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security. The Greens have marked 100 days until the election campaign by launching their first political advertisement, which focuses on gay marriage. Queenslander Bob Katter has marked the day by announcing Peter Mailler as the Katter's Australia Party senate candidate for NSW. The government has introduced legislation giving effect to its proposed crackdown on 457 visas - documents that allow temporary skilled migration. The government claims some employers are rorting the scheme. Reverse ferret. ABC managing director Mark Scott issues statement declaring fruit to live another day. ABC TV confirms that it will continue to broadcast Bananas in Pyjamas, one of the globe’s most popular and iconic children’s programs. The latest series has 156 new episodes, with the current block of episodes premiering in May this year. In order to make the most of our significant investment in the hugely popular program, we will continue airing these episodes to a new generation of Australian pre-schoolers, as has been our standard practice in children’s television for more than two decades. Another series of Bananas in Pyjamas will go through the usual commissioning process to determine future production of the program. This process does not relate to current government funding. McKibben is speaking about growing resistance by activists to the expansion of fossil fuel industry in the United States, and around the world. We can't outspend them, but we can out organise them. Bill McKibben - global warming activist, scientist and Methodist Sunday school teacher. We are riding a carbon bubble, and either the bubble pops or the planet pops. Queensland MP Bob Katter is in the senate courtyard announcing the Katter Australia Party's NSW senate candidate for September. From one end of the spectrum to another, over at the National Press Club, scientist Bill McKibben is making the case for reducing carbon emissions. A new column from Guardian Australia political editor Lenore Taylor. After a week where some MPs seem intent on reducing Labor to a sideshow, here's the case for being a credible alternative. You can read Lenore's full post here. Here is an excerpt. Only 30% of the electorate say they will give their primary vote to Labor, according to Newspoll. Obviously, that represents electoral disaster for the ALP. But it also represents more than 4.6 million people who are backing Labor despite its leadership sideshow and its backflips on promises and policies. Those 4.6 million people want a choice. And political parties owe it to the electorate to offer a credible choice, even when it’s pretty damn clear an overwhelming majority of voters are preparing to vote for their opponents. Some within Labor still seem to get that. But over the past few days a number of them look like the bloodied remnants of an army where the soldiers have downed weapons, stopped fighting and started heading for the hills to save their own hides. Are you thinking what I'm thinking B1? What are you thinking B2? Labor men Simon Crean and Ed Husic have a pow pow in the chamber. Rudd men. Lot's of interesting groups around the place this week. Like ripples on a river. Are you thinking what I'm thinking B1? Absolutely no idea, B2. Bananas. The weeping. Does this make (the ABC managing director) Mark Scott the ultimate Fruit Ninja? Greens communications spokesman Scott Ludlam gets this question from a reporter in the Mural Hall. His response? I wish I'd thought of that line Jeremy. I'm un-Australian enough to be unmoved by the reported demise of the bananas. Senator Mark Bishop on the other hand is looking much cheerier today. The Labor Senator featured on this blog, and all the television news programs last night, banging the table in a committee hearing in total frustration. Bishop's outburst was an emblem of a crazy day. Better this morning, evidently. Politics will shortly express its grief that the ABC has axed Bananas in Pyjamas. (Yes, it will.) Get down B2. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison is seriously in danger of losing his voice. He's on ABC24 with political editor Lyndal Curtis. Morrison is being quizzed about how long it will take the Coalition to make a significant impact in stemming irregular maritime arrivals. Morrison: We will make a difference from day one. Curtis persists. You won't stop the boats on day one will you? Morrison: No-one has made that suggestion. Morrison says the Coalition will be accountable for its statements on this issue after the election. Lots of committee hearings going on around parliament this morning. The head of Treasury, Martin Parkinson, is giving testimony in the Senate Economics Legislation Committee. Dr Parkinson says we are in for a decade of macro-economic volatility on a scale not seen since the 1970s. Parkinson: It's going to be an interesting ride for us globally, and in Australia in particular. With reports this morning that some Labor MPs are already packing up their offices in preparation for the great wipe out of September 14 (according to the collective wisdom of opinion polls) - Mike Bowers paid a visit to Victorian Labor man Alan Griffin. Griffin was one of the MPs mentioned in disptaches this morning as packing his bat and ball and taking it home. Bowers recieved an unequivocal no to his polite request to document the dismantling. Something about legs was mentioned. Packing didn't interfere with parliamentary duty evidently. Here's Griffin with Robert McClelland. Another Rudd man. If you've never heard of the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security - the statutory office holder who will undertake this inquiry at the behest of the Gillard Government, here's a link to their website. The IGIS, according to their blurb: Monitors what the Australian intelligence and security agencies do. Conducts inquiries, either self-initiated or at the request of government. Investigates complaints about the agencies. Makes recommendations to government. Provides annual reports to the parliament. The IGIS has extensive powers to obtain information and can: require any person to answer questions and produce relevant documents take sworn evidence, and enter agency premises The IGIS' inquiries are conducted in private, but many of them are reported in annual reports. The current Inspector-General is Dr Vivienne Thom. I've been seeking terms of reference for this inquiry since it was announced in Question Time yesterday. So far, I haven't seen any. 100 days, and all is world-weary with readers of politics live. PWS. Here was the Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor on the ABC's AM program this morning with reporter, Samatha Hawley. Who is responsible? SAMANTHA HAWLEY: Does the Government take ultimate responsibility for this situation? BRENDAN O'CONNOR: Well, we've made clear at all times that the person in question has been in detention, there have been dealings, there has been issues between the, and a good working cooperation amongst the agencies. SAMANTHA HAWLEY: There hasn't been great work, has there, among the agencies and between the agencies and the Government? Chris Bowen for instance was sent a brief on this, as you have pointed out or corrected on the record, in September last year. Are you surprised at all that he never laid eyes on that, his staff did nothing about it and now it's lost? BRENDAN O'CONNOR: The department advised me that the submission held by the former minister's office was not returned to the department. But let's be very clear here … SAMANTHA HAWLEY: So who is responsible for that? BRENDAN O'CONNOR: Well, let' be very clear here. The department and the Australian Federal Police and ASIO have been dealing with this matter. It is agencies who deal with operational matters and indeed make decisions around the detention and security of detainees. SAMANTHA HAWLEY: And a senior minister in the Government was told that this man posed a risk and he never laid eyes on that letter, his staff did nothing with it and now evidently no-one can find it. BRENDAN O'CONNOR: What I've made clear to you is we've obviously announced a review. That's the right thing to do. There's been a round of duelling media interviews this morning about the Egyptian man, and the inquiry the Prime Minister set up yesterday by the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security. On the Coalition side, we've had Tony Abbott, Scott Morrison and Michael Keenan. For the government, we've had Brendan O'Connor and the Attorney-General Mark Dreyfus. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison, by close of business, may set a new record in how many times he can utter the phrase convicted jihadist terrorist in front of a TV camera. Perhaps someone could keep count? Tony Abbott told reporters this morning he wanted a full independent inquiry into this case. (Which means a parliamentary probe, which would also examine the role of ministers in this case.) The Coalition would like the option of probing why the former Immigration Minister Chris Bowen got an officials briefing on the case of the Egyptian man late last year, and didn't tell Brendan O'Connor seemingly until this week. Partisan politcs aside, given the history in this portfolio of less than stellar administration, the gaps in this case study look worrying. The Attorney-General has said the inquiry the Prime Minister proposed yesterday will look at the national security implications of complex asylum assessments. Mark Dreyfus was not engaging on the question of why Bowen didn't bring O'Connor into the loop. Dreyfus: I don't know what happened there and I'm not going to speculate. Readers will be aware from our top story this morning that Guardian Australia's political editor Lenore Taylor has interviewed the Greens leader, Christine Milne. With senate balance of power very much in play in September, Milne talks about a possible shift in upper house king making. Have a watch. Legislation on the recognition of local government in the Constitution - a procedural step required before the question is put at a referendum later this year - passed the House of Representatives last night. Two Liberal MPs, Alex Hawke and Dennis Jensen, voted against this legislation. There is a growing conservative campaign against the proposed change. People like former Howard Government minister Peter Reith believe recognising local government in the Constitution doesn't help people in the regions, it merely centralises power in Canberra. One reader this week was concerned that there hasn't been enough coverage of one significant issue - the proposal to recognise local government in the Constitution - to enable him to make up his mind about which way to vote. With that in mind, I can point you to a Comment is Free piece that Local Government Minister Anthony Albanese has contributed to Guardian Australia this morning. You can read the full piece here. Here's an excerpt: The change being put to the Australian people is modest and practical. It simply adds into the Constitution the right of the Commonwealth to grant financial assistance to any state or to any local government body formed by a law of a state, under any terms or conditions as the Parliament thinks fit. It in no way changes the role of State Governments which will stay responsible for local councils. Under the Bill before the parliament, the Commonwealth couldn’t provide funding with terms and conditions that wouldn’t be valid under state law. It also does not interfere with the state’s rights to change local councils through, for example, amalgamations or by sacking them if the need arose. There's lots of ways to chime in to the daily discussion around politics live. You can comment here on the blog, or engage on Twitter, using the hashtag #politicslive You can get in touch with me there @murpharoo I've opened the batting on Twitter this morning by asking for thoughts on 100 days to go. This response from Andrew McCormack is fairly representative. In the House of Representatives, the Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor is speaking about the changes the government wants to the 457 visa program. In our wrap of the news, I've pointed you to an interesting story in The Age this morning which uncovered 200 cases of rorts in this scheme. O'Connor is telling the House the package of reforms aims to rebalance the system. The government has concerns that some employers are turning to overseas workers first without investing in training. The government believes Australian residents deserve the chance to get jobs on Australian projects. Labor has copped a lot of flack for this crackdown both externally and internally. The government stands accused of caving in to its trade union base (which has long opposed temporary skilled migration programs because of the risk such programs will drive down local wages and conditions); and playing to the politics of xenophobia. But this move is popular with Labor's base. There is a strong under current of economic nationalism in the electorate right now. Here was the Opposition leader, Tony Abbott, out with reporters just a little while ago with his talking point on 100 days to go. Have a listen. With 100 days to go until we all cast our votes, the Greens have this morning launched their first election ad. The pitch is gay marriage. We, the Greens, are into it. If you are into it, Vote 1 Green. Good morning and welcome to our live coverage of politics from the national capital. Given the mad day yesterday - the outbreaks of self indulgence and panic by various Labor figures - Mike Bowers went early this morning to visit two legends of Labor history. The wartime leaders: John Curtin and Ben Chifley, immortalised in bronze in the parliamentary triangle. As the walkers walked by, Curtin and Chif were keeping their own counsel about the circus on the hill. To our day. We are still firmly in the national security frame after the Prime Minister announced yesterday an inquiry by the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security into complex asylum cases requiring security assessments. We’ve not yet seen terms of reference for this probe. The Coalition is displeased. It wants a parliamentary inquiry. The Opposition leader Tony Abbott has been out to the doors of parliament to declare the case of the Egyptian man a major breach of national security. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison - voice hoarse from the flu - has batted off questions from reporters about whether he is prejudicing this man’s claim for asylum by constantly declaring him “a convicted jihadist terrorist.” The Immigration Minister Brendan O’Connor has been on radio. Where does the buck stop with this case Minister? Clearly these are important matters. Let’s get into our parliamentary sitting day with a review of the main political stories. 100 days 'til poll day .. but who is counting? For more than two years, Rod Cameron's pessimism about Labor's prospects has been a strictly private affair. Although he dubbed Tony Abbott unelectable, the former ALP pollster remained circumspect on the challenge facing the party he served in more than 50, mostly winning, state and federal campaigns. ''I didn't want to throw any curve balls in while there was a prospect that the party would do what I thought it would do - and that's just act out of self-interest,'' Mr Cameron explains. Now, ''more in sorrow than anything'', he is predicting an epic Labor disaster. He is not alone. While the mood of the Labor caucus has been despondent for months, it seems that only now, 100 days from polling day, the gravity of what is in prospect is really sinking in. (Fairfax Media) Labor faces an unprecedented wipeout in five western Sydney and Central Coast seats, with more than twice as many voters claiming they are backing Tony Abbott over Julia Gillard. With 100 days to go before the election, western Sydney has spoken. And the message was clear. While there was little love for Mr Abbott, they have had enough of Ms Gillard and want to teach her government a painful lesson. A straw poll by The Daily Telegraph of more than 100 voters yesterday revealed that asylum seekers and Ms Gillard's leadership were the hot-button issues. (The Daily Telegraph) At least two veteran Labor MPs have packed up their Parliament House offices in anticipation they will not be returning to Canberra after the September 14 federal election. With widespread despondency pervading the government at all levels, Victorian MP Alan Griffin and south-western Sydney MP Daryl Melham packed up their Canberra offices. (The Australian Financial Review, paywalled) Boats and national security Julia Gillard has ordered an investigation into how an al-Qa'ida terrorist was housed for months in low-security immigration detention, as Labor seeks to fend off opposition attacks suggesting the flood of boat arrivals is jeopardising national security. The Prime Minister caved in yesterday after days of having resisted opposition demands for an inquiry into the case of convicted Egyptian jihadist Maksoud Abdel Latif, who Tony Abbott said was housed behind a "pool fence" in the Adelaide Hills. (The Australian, paywalled) A Sri Lankan asylum-seeker who committed suicide in Sydney's Villawood detention centre by consuming a fatal quantity of cyanide had been diagnosed just months earlier with leprosy. The death in 2011 of the Sri Lankan asylum-seeker known as Victor triggered a system-wide search for cyanide within the detention network. (The Australian, paywalled) 457 visas - rorts Hundreds of foreign workers and students are being forced into debt bondage after paying up to $40,000 for an Australian skilled worker visa, with some signing contracts stating they will be sacked if they engage in ''trade union activities''. A Fairfax Media investigation can reveal the most extensive rorting of the 457 and 187 visa schemes ever to be exposed, with up to 200 cases across Australia. (The Age) Peter Slipper - unpaid legal fees The law firm that defended Peter Slipper against a sexual harassment claim in the Federal Court is suing the former speaker for $237,000 over alleged unpaid legal fees. Maurice Blackburn has filed a claim in the Supreme Court in Brisbane, alleging Mr Slipper still owes the firm $231,762 in unpaid invoices, plus more than $5300 interest. Greens leader - interview The Greens leader, Christine Milne, has suggested Tony Abbott is not fit for leadership because of his stance on climate change, but she is preparing a Democrats-style “keep the bastards honest” election campaign to stop Abbott’s “excesses” in the Senate, on the assumption he will be prime minister. (Guardian Australia) I’ll also point you in the direction of a couple of interesting columns published this morning. Mark Latham - Labor’s micro-economic reform record After two terms in office, Labor … has developed a substantial environmental and social policy agenda, (but) micro-reform has been a black hole. History will record an extraordinary paradox: how the previous Labor government (1983-96) drove a hard-edged reform program, while the Rudd and Gillard cabinets were missing in action. The party, in effect, has never recovered from its first term in opposition after the 1996 election. A pair of ex-ACTU presidents, Simon Crean and Martin Ferguson, abandoned Paul Keating’s legacy by reviving an interventionist platform of corporate welfare and protectionism. No wonder conservative commentators now universally praise the two union leaders – they wrecked Labor’s economic credentials for a generation. (The Australian Financial Review, paywalled) Jonathan Green - the politics of xenophobia Politics is nothing if not a mirror of the society it serves … that it, in every sense, represents. We provide the clay they work with. If there wasn't a vote in hate, fear and prejudice then there would be no gain in pandering to any of them. The great Australian shame is that not only are there votes to be had here, but that this is the heartland in which our political game is lost and won. The likes of Eddie McGuire aren't even a pimple on its backside … and in many ways the star chambers that assemble around these public transgressions just blind us to the greater reality of a public whose blind-peeping anxieties breed an agenda that turns that suburban fear to populist political profit. (ABC - The Drum)

headline: Politics, pop, scoops and Sloanes: how magazines helped shape  society
trail_text: They may be known for gossip and fashion, but the UK's weeklies and glossies have also broken major news stories – and as the publishers' association celebrates its 100th anniversary, they seem to be in ruder health than ever
body_text: From home-baking recipes to luxury car reviews, from diet advice to celebrity interviews, British magazines continue to be the informed friends the public turns to in a quiet moment. This month sees a retrospective look at a century of titles: the good, the bad and the stylish. If the bleakest predictions had been proved right, the 100th anniversary of the industry's trade body, the Professional Publishers Association, would have been a mournful, nostalgic look back at a bygone business model. Instead, its celebration of key moments from magazine history shows how new trends have taken off while reliable old publications have frequently found a fresh audience. A brochure, put together by Gill Hudson of Reader's Digest – a former editor of the Radio Times, Company and Maxim – for the association's celebratory event on 21 November, kicks off its survey of 100 pivotal magazine moments with Margaret Thatcher's resonant interview from 1987, when the prime minister told Woman's Own: "There is no such thing as society. There is a living tapestry of men and women and people, and the beauty of that tapestry and the quality of our lives will depend upon how much each of us is prepared to take responsibility for ourselves." The scoop demonstrated that the more relaxed atmosphere of a feature interview can sometimes make more waves than the front pages of a newspaper. As Thatcher rang the bell for the start of the age of the individual, so Gordon Roddick, the co-founder of the Body Shop, and John Bird were preparing to help those individuals not likely to make it on their own with the launch of the Big Issue in 1991. Produced in response to the growing number of rough sleepers on the streets of the capital, Bird and Roddick's magazine offered an induction course and a fixed pitch on the pavement for an army of homeless sellers. It now works with around 2,000 vulnerable people. Women's magazines have undergone a design and content revolution in the last century, from the proliferation of catwalk coverage to the huge demand for celebrity-based photo-journalism. While the influential arrival in 1972 of both the feminist title Spare Rib and the straight-talking Cosmopolitan helped give women a stronger voice in society, it is pure fashion and gossip that now drive trade on an unimagined scale. An attempt to supply the everyday woman with a portion of advice and entertainment that would last no longer than a cup of tea came in 1990 with the launch of Take a Break. Still the bestselling women's magazine in the country, it created a new publishing genre: true-life weeklies. John Dale, who edited the title for 20 years, said: "I held certain beliefs – first, that working-class women, especially in the north, were the victims of institutional discrimination; second, that such women were ill-served by magazines produced by home counties types; third, that these women needed stories about themselves rather than the Queen Mum and Joan Collins; and fourth, that the journalist should get out of the way so women could speak directly to other women in their own voice." One of the best judged pieces of social class commentary since Nancy Mitford wrote about "U" and "non-U" manners for Encounter magazine in 1955 was hidden between the covers of the October 1975 edition of Harpers & Queen. This is where Peter York first identified the phenomenon of the Sloane Ranger. The label lived on long after Lady Diana Spencer and her tribe had stopped wearing strings of pearls and upturned collars. Style punditry quickly became a major engine of the industry as the 1980s came to an end. Alongside the Face and Esquire, GQ, a magazine that celebrated its 25th birthday last week, set out to target a sophisticated, young audience. The US parent magazine was bought by Condé Nast in 1983, and the company launched several international editions. In 1998, the UK edition began its enduring, high-profile quest to find the men "whose own peers expect to make a major impact in the year ahead" and its Men of the Year awards enjoyed their 16th outing two months ago. Past winners make an eclectic line-up: Michael Douglas, Russell Brand, Sir Bradley Wiggins, athlete Steve Backley, writer Kazuo Ishiguro and Patrick McLoughlin, now secretary of state for transport. In 1998, the event was marked with a drinks reception; this year there was a black-tie dinner at the Royal Opera House. The capacity for smaller publications to take the most important risks has been proved repeatedly over the last century, not least last year when the Oldie was the first to repeat in print the rumour that Jimmy Savile had committed child abuse, alluded to in Miles Goslett's feature about the BBC's aborted Newsnight item. Similarly, in 1992, the subversive Private Eye printed the allegation that the heart surgery unit at Bristol Royal Infirmary was known as the Killing Fields because of high death rates: "Despite a long crisis of morale among intensive-care staff, the surgeons persistently refused to publish their mortality rates in a manner comparable to other units." Editor Ian Hislop said: "It followed that pattern of Eye stories where you have to keep on telling it and eventually it comes good." In a Britain where many of the young men who once hunched over a copy of NME have now graduated to comparing dishwasher reviews in the consumer magazine Which?, Nicholas Coleridge of publisher Condé Nast has spoken of a potential new golden age for magazine journalism. Sales of printed copies may have dropped, but he believes the public are still devoted readers of their favourite titles. "Circulation figures don't show that people enjoy reading them on the new devices," he told BBC Radio 4's The Media Show last week, adding that his leading title, Vogue, has had its "most profitable year" yet, and that a veteran such as House and Garden sells in the same numbers it did 50 years ago. Talking to the Professional Publishers Association in the runup to its centenary, Coleridge recalled his early, independent days working for editor Tina Brown on Tatler. The publication was, he said, "so broke that, in order to pay the restaurant critic Julian Barnes, one of my jobs was to ring up publishers and pretend we were doing a feature on illustrated books. They would duly bike over all these fabulous volumes, which I'd then take in a taxi to a secondhand book dealer called D Levin of Grape Street, unload them, flog 'em, come back with the cash, and hand (most of it) to Tina. She'd then hand it to JB and he would go off somewhere like the Connaught for lunch and pontificate about it." Hudson, who compiled the PPA's 100 moments in consultation with contacts across the industry, believes the magazine business still relies on bringing content to people in a way they can enjoy, whether online or in print. While new electronic platforms allow for much more involvement with readers, she warned editors of the future to keep a clear idea what they want to create. "There is so much more customer feedback now, but then there is also a danger that you only give customers what they want and not what they don't realise they want yet," she said. Barry McIlheney, the PPA's chief executive and a former editor of music title Smash Hits and movie magazine Empire, is also heartened to see that reports of the death of print magazines have been exaggerated. "I kept being told that print was over at conferences, but it doesn't seem to be true," he said. "Things look very different now to even five years ago. Print magazines go very deep into the affections of their readers and many are also working really well on tablets: a high percentage of the top 50 rated apps are magazines." While McIlheney regrets that Smash Hits is no longer on the newsstands and selling a million copies an issue, he celebrates the survival of Empire, which he launched. "It is still going stronger than ever." • This article was amended on 20 November 2013 to make it clear that it was a Newsnight item about Jimmy Savile that was dropped, not a Panorama programme.

headline: Egg wars: how politics of influence block free-range labelling reform
trail_text: John Kaye: In their quest for even greater profits, Australian industrial egg producers are appropriating the free-range label while depriving their animals of room to move. Reform is needed
body_text: The chief consumer affairs bureaucrat of Australia's largest state has just done something quite unusual for a public official. NSW fair trading commissioner Rod Stowe has departed from script on free-range labelling – usually along the lines of "there’s nothing to see here" – to respond to a super complaint from national consumer advocacy giant CHOICE. In doing so, he has admitted to the major failure of the free-range egg market. Cases of false labelling, inconsistencies across standards and a proliferation of high-cost, low-welfare eggs led him to call for nation-wide legally enforceable standards. By doing so, he has now made it impossible for governments, the egg industry and large industrial producers to continue hiding behind their stone wall of denial. Despite the consumer tide rapidly turning against factory farmed foods and the grotesque levels of animal cruelty they involve, Australian governments have blocked, reversed or subverted almost every move to stop free-range being little more than a marketing tool. In August of this year, the NSW Coalition government used its numbers in the lower house to axe a Greens bill to impose real meaning on the free-range label. In Queensland, the Newman Liberal National party government quietly dumped that state's requirement that free-range egg farms stock no more than the industry standard of 1,500 birds per hectare. In both cases, Coalition governments danced to the tune of the big industrial producers. They allowed the ethical motivations of consumers to continue to be exploited and cashed in for massive profits. Yet the figures show just how out of touch they are. Increasing numbers of consumers are looking for a better animal welfare alternative to factory farmed meat and eggs. In 2011, 34%of all eggs sold in Australia were labelled free-range, representing 44% of the entire value of the market. If recent trends continue, the appetite for ethically produced eggs will overtake the caged products early next decade and it will spill over into other foods including poultry and pig products.
