The interim report of the detainee inquiry has identified 27 issues for further examination, underscoring the need for an effective, independent, human rights-compliant inquiry. Combating torture requires coming clean about when and where it occurred, who was involved and holding those responsible to account. The intelligence and security committee is the wrong body for this vital job and today's announcement is yet another effort by the UK to shirk its responsibilities. Handing the investigation over to the ISC raises the prospect that much of the truth may remain buried, and that no full judge-led inquiry will ever materialise. • David Cameron has pledged to redouble efforts to combat extremism. He was speaking following the conviction of Michael Adebolajo and Michael Adebowale for the murder of the soldier Lee Rigby near a military barracks in London, in the first al-Qaida-inspired attack to claim a life on British soil since 7 July 2005. Cameron said: The whole country was completely shocked by the murder and the whole country united in condemnation at what happened. We have to redouble our efforts to confront the poisonous narrative of extremism and violence and make sure we do everything to beat it. • The Conservartive MP Jacob Rees-Mogg has used an article for the Telegraph to say that the Tories have no chance of winning the general election unless they reach an accord of some kind with Ukip. Here's an extract. Ukip’s success is making it extremely difficult for the Conservatives to win in marginal seats. This risks a strongly pro-European Labour Party having a majority against the combined interests and superiority in terms of votes of the Right. The Survation polls indicate that about a third of Ukip voters self-identify as former Tories and a similar percentage as being willing to leave Ukip to stop Mr Miliband. This is bound to understate the true number as people rarely want to appear indecisive in their voting choice. Conservatives and Ukip agree on a wide range of policy areas and there is a growing realisation that the European Union and the European Court of Human Rights are key to a domestic political agenda. The electorate wants decision-making returned to Westminster and a combination between the two parties of the Right could be sufficient to make it happen. With a division it cannot. That's all from me for today. I've got a meeting to go to. Once again, I hope you all have a Happy Christmas. Back to the Gibson report. Libyan politician Abdelhakim Belhadj, who has accused Britain of complicity in his rendition to the regime of ousted dictator Muammar Gaddafi, said the World at One that an independent inquiry into the rendition allegations remained important. It is really central to our efforts to uphold human rights in this country and abroad. We don't want what happened to us to happen to others. It's one of the most important issues concerning the future of Libya and its human rights. I'm not finishing for the day yet, but I'd like to take a moment to wish all readers a Happy Christmas, and to thank those you who have been passing on season's greetings BTL. I deeply appreciate, well, not quite all the comments posted on my blog, but certainly the vast majority of them, and the comments, links and information posted here, as well as the quality of the conversation (generally) adds immeasurably to the quality of the blog. I hope you all have a very good break. Except you don't need to have a break. I will try to ensure that we have a readers' edition running on most weekdays during the holiday period. I will be back blogging again regularly from Monday 6 January. • An official inquiry has found that British agents may have been "inappropriately involved" in the rendition and ill-treatment of terrorist suspects. In a statement to the Commons, Kenneth Clarke, the minister without portfolio, said that the 27 questions raised by the inquiry by Sir Peter Gibson, which never got the chance to finish its work because criminal proceedings are underway, would be investigated by parliament's intelligence and security committee. Gibson said: It does appear from the documents that the United Kingdom may have been inappropriately involved in some renditions. That is a very serious matter. And no doubt any future inquiry would want to look at that. • Nick Clegg has indicated that he may be prepared to drop his party's opposition to the expansion of airport capacity in the south-east of England. • Vince Cable has said that London is London "is becoming a giant suction machine draining the life out of the rest of the country". He made the point as he was explaining why he thought regional airports should be expanded, not Heathrow. • Ed Balls has asked all shadow cabinet members to set out initial ideas for money-saving public service reforms by the spring in what is being billed as a "zero-based spending review" due to be completed in government. • General Sir Nicholas Houghton, head of the armed forces has said that Britain is in danger of being left with hollowed-out armed forces. In a speech, he said that although the armed forces had "exquisite" equipment, training levels were being squeezed and manpower was increasingly seen as "an overhead". • Chris Grayling, the justice secretary, has revealed that the private outsourcing firm Serco has agreed to repay £68.5m plus VAT in overcharging on its contract to provide electronic tagging of offenders. • Sir Bernard Hogan-Howe, the Metropolitan police commissioner, has admitted there is cause for concern over the accuracy of crime statistics. In an interview on the Today programme, he insisted allegations that figures were being fiddled to make them look better were being investigated. • David Cameron has used a visit to Flanders to announce that the government will provide £5m to help fund war memorials. Here's a statement from the intelligence and security committee about the Gibson report. The Detainee Inquiry, headed by Sir Peter Gibson, has today published its report summarising the preparatory work of the Inquiry and highlighting the themes and issues which the Inquiry believes should be subject to further examination. The Intelligence and Security Committee of Parliament welcomes the publication of this interim report. The report raises important questions as to the UK Agencies’ and Government’s policies, procedures and conduct in relation to detainees in the decade following the terrorist attacks of 11 September 2001. The Prime Minister has asked the Committee to consider those issues identified by Sir Peter Gibson. The Committee has agreed to undertake this responsibility. In previous Parliaments, the ISC has published reports on detainees and rendition, and submitted reports to the then Prime Minister on allegations of complicity in mistreatment of detainees, and on the Government’s draft consolidated guidance on detainees. Those previous Reports were incomplete and, in some important respects, unsatisfactory since, after they had been published, new information which had not been made available to the Committee became known. At that time the intelligence Agencies were not under any statutory obligation to provide the Committee with all relevant information in their possession. They were entitled to reach their own decision as to what to provide. Since those reports were produced, the ISC has been given important new powers under the Justice and Security Act. The Agencies are now under a statutory obligation to provide the Committee with all relevant material. The Committee now has a statutory right to consider all aspects of work across the intelligence community, which is backed by the power for its staff to go into the Agencies themselves and inspect their files. These powers are already being used and will be available for all the Committee’s future work. The Committee is, therefore, satisfied that the problems that occurred in the past will not, and cannot, recur. The Committee is currently focussed on its detailed investigation into the intelligence and security Agencies’ actions in relation to those charged with the murder of Drummer Lee Rigby in Woolwich. The Committee will also be undertaking a major inquiry into the balance between privacy and security. In order to ensure that the work arising from the Gibson Inquiry receives the level of scrutiny required, the Committee has therefore requested additional resources commensurate with the task and received assurances that those will be provided. The Committee will also be given access to all of the information (including to primary material) that Government departments and Agencies had provided to the Gibson Inquiry. Once the Committee receives the Agencies’ written responses to the 27 questions identified by Sir Peter and his panel, it will commence its investigation. And here is some more Twitter comment on the Gibson report, and the Commons statement about it. From the Daily Mail's Tim Shipman From Reprieve UK From the Guardian's Tom Clark From Lord Strasburger, a Lib Dem peer From Labour's Sadiq Khan From ITV's Lucy Manning Peter Riddell, the former Times journalist who was a member of the Gibson inquiry until he left to take up a post as director of the Institute for Government, says questions remain unanswered. The Commons statement is now over. Here's the Guardian's story about the Gibson report. And here's how it starts. Dozens of questions about the UK's involvement in the rendition andtorture of terrorism suspects after 9/11 remain unanswered, an official inquiry has concluded. In his interim report, the inquiry head Sir Peter Gibson raised 27 questions to be asked of the involvement of government ministers and officers from MI5 and MI6 in the mistreatment of detainees in the so-called war on terror. Gibson concluded that the questions concern four broad themes: interrogation; rendition; the training of intelligence officers and the guidance they received; and communications between the intelligence agencies and government. He also highlighted the rendition of two Libyan nationals who were taken to Tripoli against their will, along with their families, in 2004. "There are serious allegations of UK involvement in rendition in relation to the two Libya nationals. These plainly required investigation," he said. Other questions that Gibson said demanded answers included "whether there was an apparent willingness, at least at some levels within the agencies, to condone, encourage or take advantage of a rendition operation". He also questioned "whether government and agencies became inappropriately involved in such renditions". Here are some extracts from Kenneth Clarke's statement On the broad findings of the Gibson report It does identify 27 issues which do require further examination. These are grouped under four broad themes, relating to interrogation and treatment, renditions, guidance and training, and matters relating to policy and communications. The period of time was one in which we and our international partners were suddenly adapting to a completely new scale and type of threat from fundamentalist, religious extremists. Many UK intelligence officers had to operate in extraordinarily challenging environments subject to real personal danger. But everyone in the government and everyone in the agencies accept this bravery has to be combined with clear rules of proportionality, accountability, to ensure we uphold the values we are working hard to defend. While we accept intelligence operations must be conducted in the strictest secrecy, we also expect there to be strict oversight of those operations to ensure at all times they respect the human rights that are a cornerstone of this country's values. On British agents not being "directly responsible" for the mistreatment of detainees [The report] finds no evidence in the documents to support any allegation UK intelligence officers were directly responsible for the mistreatment of detainees held by other countries overseas. No material has been referred by the panel to the police for further consideration. On the specific failings identified in the report It is now clear that our agencies and their staff were in some respects not prepared for the extreme demands suddenly placed upon them. The guidance regulating how intelligence officers should act was inadequate. The practices of some of our international partners should have been understood much sooner. Oversight was not robust enough and there was no mechanism in the civil courts for allegations against the security and intelligence services to be examined properly. Most of these problems related to a relatively short period of time in the early 2000s but there is some damage to our reputation, which prides itself as a beacon of justice, human rights and the rule of law. On the government's "regret" I believe I speak for the whole House, on all sides, when I say if failures and mistakes were made in this period that is a matter of sincere regret. On the new ISC inquiry It would be wrong to leave these issues, many of which relate to matters of policy, unexamined for the unknown amount of time it will take for the police to complete their related investigations ... The prime minister has discussed and agreed with the intelligence and security committee (ISC) of parliament they will inquire into the themes and issues which Sir Peter has raised. They will take further evidence and they will report to the government and to parliament on the outcome of their inquiry. Andrew Mitchell, the Conservative former chief whip, says Jack Straw was dealing with quite extraordinary circumstances after 9/11. Straw acted with "complete responsibility" and "total integrity". Clarke endorses that. He says there is nothing in the report that casts doubt on Straw's integrity. Here's an extract from the statement Sadiq Khan, the shadow justice secretary, gave when he was responding to Kenneth Clarke earlier. Notwithstanding the crucial work these agencies do in keeping us safe, any allegations of involvement by members of our security and intelligence services in serious breaches of the law need proper and full investigation. Any acts that might contravene the law in the ways alleged would run counter to everything our nation stands for and believes in. It’s for this reason it’s important there is a full and proper investigation with any wrongdoing being exposed and those responsible brought to account. We also need to ensure the appropriate lessons are learnt and that there is no repeat in the future. And we need to do this in as open and transparent a manner as possible, in a way that maintains the confidence of the public. Back in the Commons Richard Ottoway, the Conservative chair of the Commons foreign affairs committee, asks if Clarke will have discussions with the human rights groups. Clarke says he has the greatest sympathy with human rights groups. They are "on the side of the angels". They are backing ideas he supports. But they are being "wholly unrealistic" if they think it is right for the workings of the intelligence agencies to be discussed in open court, he says. Here is some reaction to the report from theKeith Best, chief executive of the Freedom from Torture campaign. Today’s announcement hints at a spectacular u-turn on this Government’s repeated commitment to hold an independent, judge-led inquiry to get to the bottom of unresolved allegations that the UK was complicit in torture in the aftermath of 9/11. The government has sat on the findings of Sir Peter Gibson’s aborted inquiry for 18 months. This time could have been used to design a new human rights-compliant inquiry that would be credible both in the eyes of those who were subjected to torture and the British public. However, it appears that the Government has been busy trying to concoct an even less transparent and independent process. Unless Mr Cameron delivers the judge-led inquiry he promised, he will lose the moral high ground he sought to assert when he first came to power and announced his intention to draw a line under this appalling episode. Instead he will face accusations that he has slowly but surely become part of the whitewash. Those who suffer torture have a right to redress. And we know from our work with thousands of torture survivors that seeing justice done enables them to move on with their lives. We joined the boycott of Sir Peter’s Gibson inquiry precisely because this group of survivors was thrust to the sidelines; they have been in limbo ever since. Mr Clarke’s ambiguous announcement today suggests they will stay there. My colleague Richard Norton-Taylor has sent another note about the Clarke statement. Clarke insists the ISC was "fully independent of government". But it still relies on the cooperation of the heads of MI5, MI6, and GCHQ and still meets in private, with its members approved by the PM. And its resources are still inadequate - as its members have admitted. Andrew Tyrie, a Conservative MP and a campaigner on rendition issues, says the intelligence services do a good job. But this statement will not help them. There should be a full independent inquiry, he says. How can people trust the ISC when it said in an earlier report - wrongly - that British officers were not involved in rendition. Clarke says he shares Tyrie's concerns about the idea of British officials being involved in rendition. But an inquiry could not take evidence alongside an ongoing police investigation, he says. So do we do nothing? Or do we get the ISC to investigate? Clarke says he is sorry Tyrie is dismissive of the ISC's powers. But the ISC now has "far more powers than it previously had". Last time the ISC was not given information assembled by the agencies for their own use. It is highly unlikely that would happen again. Jack Straw,the Labour foreign secretary at the time of 9/11, is speaking now. He says he welcomes the fact that there will be an ISC inquiry. Straw says he acted at all times as foreign secretary in accordance with his legal duties and international law. At no time was he complicit in rendition by the US or any other state. But he did agree that the UK would not stop British nationals being transferred to Guantanamo Bay. He did so after legal advice, and knowing that the alternative was their continued detention in Afghanistan. But he did not agree to them being mistreated, he says. And the British government made repeated complaints to the Americans about the way they were being treated, he says. He reminds MPs that this happened in extraordinary circumstances - after the 9/11 attacks. The bravery of the agencies helped to keep people safe, he says. Clarke says he has considerable respect for Straw. And he has sympathy for the position he finds himself in, where it has been difficult for him to protect his reputation. Back in the Commons, Sir Malcolm Rifkind, chair of the ISC, is speaking now. The ISC has already published reports on the treatment of detainees. But they were "incomplete" and "unsatisfactory" because the agencies did not provide all the information need. Now the committee has the power to go into the agencies and demand to see files. Because of that, and because it is getting extra resources, the ISC is happy to carry out the inquiry that David Cameron wants, Rifkind says. And this is what it says about training. The adequacy of training and guidance for UK personnel is an issue relevant to many of the matters considered by the Inquiry. The Inquiry has not identified significant grounds to doubt that the overall tenor of the instruction to personnel was that detainees must be treated humanely and consistently with the UK’s international legal obligations. But officers deployed on the ground, especially to an area of military operations, need clear guidance on when and with whom to raise concerns, mindful of the sensitivities involved in multi- national military and intelligence operations. The question of sharing with liaison partners intelligence relating to individuals who are or may be detained raises questions of moral and legal complexity that are also sensitive and difficult. There is an issue whether, from 2001 – 2004, guidance may have been slow to develop, and may have been too limited, being reactive to individual events and lacking in detail. The combined effect of the ISC’s investigations and detainee issues becoming high profile in the spring of 2004 led to formal written guidance in the summer of 2004. This borrowed from military guidance and identified specific forms of unacceptable treatment. From 2004 onwards, the guidance developed and in stages improved in its clarity, detail and the range of issues it covered. The question whether these improvements may have been slower to develop than might legitimately have been expected warrants investigation. It was not until 2010 that guidance addressing both liaison relationships and detainee interviewing was consolidated. Also there are questions as to whether support for rendition operations was adequately addressed in the guidance and, prior to 2010, whether the guidance dealt sufficiently with all the factors relevant to the legality of detention abroad. And this is what it says about collusion in rendition. Rendition may be regarded as a form of mistreatment, as the Inquiry’s terms of reference recognise. The US’s rendition programme expanded rapidly and changed in nature in the aftermath of 9/11. The Inquiry would have wished to examine when the Government came to understand the scope of the US policy and whether the Government and its Agencies responded adequately once they became aware of renditions or proposed renditions of British nationals and UK residents. There is an issue as to whether the Government and the Agencies may have become inappropriately involved in some cases of rendition, and whether an adequate policy was formulated and guidance issued to personnel, addressing the extent to which it was proper for the UK to support or assist renditions carried out by other countries. This is what the report says in its summary about collusion in the torture of detainees. The documents received by the Inquiry indicate that in some instances UK intelligence officers were aware of inappropriate interrogation techniques and mistreatment or allegations of mistreatment of some detainees by liaison partners from other countries. Many of these instances were reported to Agency Head Offices. The Inquiry would have wished to examine whether that reporting was adequate and, in particular, whether the Agency Head Offices then responded adequately or, in some cases, at all. There is an issue as to whether the Agencies raised allegations of mistreatment of detainees with liaison partners with sufficient vigour, and as to the adequacy of assurances sought by the Agencies. In some cases, documents indicate that the Agencies continued to engage with liaison partners in relation to individual detainees where treatment issues may have justified withdrawal or the seeking of appropriate assurances. The Inquiry would have wished to investigate whether the legality of the detainees’ detention abroad and the Agencies’ own methods of questioning were subject to sufficient scrutiny and consideration. Here's the Gibson report (pdf). Some journalists have had a chance to read the report under embargo. Here are some of their tweets. My colleague Richard Norton-Taylor has sent me a quick note about Kenneth Clarke's statement. Ken Clarke choses key words carefully: there is no evidence to support allegations that the UK was "directly responsible" for the treatment of detained terror suspects and reforms needed, because of damage to Britain's "reputation" - though he admits some "failures and mistakes" . Ken Clarke's statement is finished. I can now post the text of a news release sent out by the Gibson inquiry earlier under embargo. Here it is. Today the Government has published a report submitted to the Prime Minister by Sir Peter Gibson and Dame Janet Paraskeva, the Panel of the Detainee Inquiry, on the Inquiry’s work. The Inquiry’s Report speaks for itself. It is a rigorous, thorough and independent piece of work. It reveals more information than ever before about the workings of Government and the Agencies, on the issues highlighted in the report. Sir Peter said: “There are matters which deserve further investigation. That is what the documents have disclosed and we explain why in our report.” Dame Janet said: “We have worked hard to put as much as possible into the public domain. I do hope the Government will decide to build on our work in a future Inquiry and give the detainees a chance to have their say.” The library of documents, the analysis of information and preliminary identification of potential witnesses the Inquiry carried out, will save any subsequent Inquiry a huge amount of time and resource. The report does not find facts or reach conclusions. It is based on the scrutiny of documents, no witness has yet had the opportunity to explain or add to this information. But the Inquiry has shone a bright light onto issues which might be investigated further by a future Inquiry or on which the Government can take action now. The Inquiry covered four separate themes: interrogation and treatment issues, rendition, training and guidance as well as policy and communications. Its work revealed 27 separate issues the Inquiry would like to have investigated further and which might be followed up by a future Inquiry. In summary the report says: Interrogation and Treatment issues: Documents indicate that in some instances UK intelligence officers were aware of inappropriate interrogation techniques and mistreatment or allegations of mistreatment of some detainees by liaison partners from other countries. Rendition Documents indicate that Government or its Agencies may have become inappropriately involved in some cases of rendition. Training and Guidance No reason to doubt that instruction to personnel was that detainees must be treated humanely and consistently with UK’s international legal obligations. But officers on the ground needed clear guidance on when and with whom to raise concerns. Policy and Communications Documents raise the question whether the Agencies could have identified possible patterns of detainee mistreatment more quickly and whether or not sufficient information was given to the ISC to enable it to perform its duties. Clarke says, as a result of these changes, a line has been drawn under the past. David Cameron has agreed that the intelligence and security committee (ISC) will look at the themes and issues that the Gibson report raises. The committee will get extra resources, so that it can undertake this work without undermining its inquiries into the Woolwich killing and privacy and security. The agencies are being asked to respond to the claims made about them in the Gibson report in a submission to the ISC. Once the ISC work is over, the government will consider whether a full judicial inquiry into these matters is needed. Clarke says the security agencies now operate under much clearer guidance. This covers how detainees should be monitored. It is scrutinised by the intelligence services commissioner. Ministers must be told about any risk to detainees, he says. The intelligence agencies are accountable to the intelligence and security committee. It has been given stronger powers, Clarke says. Clarke says his "secret courts" legislation (my phrase, not his) now means some of these issues can be addressed in a court of law. Clarke says most of these problems relate to a relatively short period of time. But there has been reputational damage to the UK, he says. This is a matter of regret. Clarke says British officials were working in difficult circumstances at the time. They worked in dangerous conditions. But they should also be expected to operate under a clear framework of values. Clarke says the report presents a picture of agencies struggling to cope with a new environment. In some respects, their preparation was not adequate. Clarke says the police launched a criminal investigation into rendition to Libya. This raised the prospect of indefinite delay before the Gibson inquiry could start taking evidence. As a result, it was decided that the Gibson inquiry would present an interim report, based on the findings of their preliminary research. It is a substantial report, Clarke says. The panel looked at 20,000 documents. They found no evidence of torture by British officials. But there were 27 cases meriting further investigation. Ken Clarke is making his statement now. He says Sir Peter Gibson's inquiry was set up in 2010. After 9/11 there were allegations that British officers were aware of detainees being treated improperly. When he set up the inquiry, David Cameron said there was no evidence of British officials actually being involved in torture themselves. Cameron said he wanted to settle the civil compensation claims submitted by British detainees sent to Guantanamo Bay. A significant settlement was agreed. The Gibson inquiry was set up. John Bercow, the Speaker, says some MPs will be named personally in the statement. Jack Straw is one. He will be given extra time to ask a question, Bercow says. Today's Daily Mail says the Gibson report will say that British spies were aware of up to 40 detainees being tortured. We should get the full details any minute now. Kenneth Clarke, the minister without portfolio, is due to give his statement to the Commons about the Gibson report shortly. Here's the Guardian's preview story. Call Clegg has finished. The most interesting thing Nick Clegg had to say was about Heathrow. In their 9.30 news bulletin, LBC interpreted Clegg's remarks as his coming out against a third runway at Heathrow. But LBC got it wrong. What is interesting is that Clegg, and the Lib Dems, are now softening their opposition to a third runway at Heathrow. He said that he has always opposed Heathrow expansion "in the past" on environmental grounds. And he said he still believed that airport expansion in the south east should only take place provided that it does not make noise pollution worse, and that Britain keeps its carbon emission targets. This sounded like opposition to Heathrow. But, actually, the Airports Commission report says that the number of people affected by noise around Heathrow is expected to fall by 2030 (because aircraft are getting quieter) and that the effect of a third runway would be "roughly neutral". It also says that all the runway options it proposes are compatible with Britain meeting its emissions targets. In the past the Lib Dems used to oppose all airport expansion in the south east. Clegg's comment suggest the party could just about accept a new runway at Heathrow, although it would probably much prefer one at Gatwick. Clegg said the party would not take a view until the Airports Commission published its final report in 2015. In a separate interview on the Radio 4 Today programme Vince Cable also indicated that the Lib Dems' opposition to Heathrow was softening. When asked if he still believed that a third runway was not going to happen, as he once said categorically in an interview, he said that he now thought it was "very unlikely". However, Cable also stressed that he was personally very strongly opposed to Heathrow expansion. He is MP for Twickenham and, for him, this is a huge local issue. I have got to go to a meeting. I will post again at about 11.30, when the Gibson statement starts. Q: Is it offensive for two white people to have a conversation and to use the word golliwog? Nick Ferrari says he's raising it because of this legal ruling. Clegg says this is not a word that he would use, but he does not know the case and does not want to comment on the ruling. Q: Do you have any memories of Peter Wright, the headmaster at Caldicott school who has been convicted of child abuse? Yes, says Clegg.Wright was headteacher when he was there. He was a "slightly remote" figure. It is shocking to learn that this abuse was going on, when the school seemed normal to pupils. Q: Are you opposed to grammar schools? Clegg says he is not against grammar schools. Schools funded by the taxpayer should not be able to select children on the basis of ability. If they can do that, they can "cream skim" the brightest children. Q: What should be done about Lords reform? Clegg says the Lords should be elected. But the main parties blocked Lords reform, he says. People should speak to "David Miliband", Clegg says before correcting himself and saying Ed Miliband, and David Cameron about this. Q: Is it true that former MEPs lose their pensions if they criticise the EU? So how can we trust what you say about the EU? Clegg says he has heard plenty of "loopy" theories, but that this one takes the biscuit. He laughs. He can guarantee the caller that his pension is not affected by what he says about the EU. Q: What do you think about the plan for an England/Afghanistan football match at Wembley? Clegg says the Afghans are incredibly proud people. Their football team should be acknowledged. Q: But isn't it an odd idea? Cameron visiting the troops, and saying here's Michael Owen, let's have a game of football. It's nuts? Clegg asks what's wrong with having a football match with Afghanistan. Q: What do you think about David Cameron saying the mission has been accomplished in Afghanistan? Clegg says Cameron did not say mission accomplished. He said that, when the British troops left, they would be able to leave with their heads held high. No one is pretending Afghanistan will be live Switzerland. But Cameron was making the point that British soldiers performed the job they were asked to to. Q: So it is mission accomplished? Of course, for the military mission. But Cameron was not claiming Afghanistan is problem free. That is not what he said, and not what he thinks. Q: Boris Johnson thinks you are a "lapdog of David Cameron" and a "yellow albatross" around the neck of the coalition. Clegg says Johnson is upset that Clegg disagrees with his "patronising" remarks about IQ. Q: Why do you not appreciated the economic case for expanding Heathrow? I live under the flightpath and favour expansion, because it would be good for local jobs? Clegg says he has always opposed Heathrow expansion in the past on environmental grounds. He would not back anything that would increase noise levels, or breach Britain's climate change obligations. (Actually, the Davies report says Heathrow can be expanded without noise levels increasing, and without Britain's climate change obligations being met.) Q: So what do you want? Clegg says he does not want to see aviation expansion take place in the south east of England in a way that would make noise or environmental pollution worse, or that would breach climate change rules. The Davies report says the increase in flights in the future will come from point-to-point flights. It questions the need for a hub airport. He says Boris Island would not remotely meet these tests. Q: What do you think about the plan that the Home Office is reportedly looking at, for a cap of 75,000 on EU immigration? Clegg says that would be really bad for the economy. Q: Why? Clegg says it's about the principle of free movement. Q: But what about people who come to camp in Marble Arch and defecate in the bushes? Come on, says Clegg. We're talking about Germany lawyers, and Swedish engineers. More than 3m people have jobs that depend on the UK being in the EU. They would not benefit if Britain were to pull out. If Britain just pulled up the drawbridge, other countries would too. Q: How many immigrants would you allow into the country? The Commons does not seem able to do anything. Clegg says the government can act. It already imposes immigration controls. And, as Clegg has said before, he would like better exit checks. Migration figures also depend on how many Britons decide to leave the UK, he says. Q: You have no control over who comes to this country. Clegg says that's not true. And he says it is important to point out that the right to come to the UK is not the same as the right to claim benefits. Today the government is publishing the report from Sir Peter Gibson, who led an inquiry set up to investigate allegations that MI5 and MI6 colluded in the torture and rendition of terrorist suspects. Rather, it is publishing Gibson's interim report, because the inquiry was shelved before it had finished its work when a criminal investigation was launched. Ian Cobain, the Guardian's expert on this subject, said last week that the report was "reported to have concluded that British intelligence officers were aware that detainees were being mistreated in prisons across the globe." And today he and Richard Norton-Taylor report that Gibson will call for the inquiry to be continued by the intelligence and security committee. Here's the start of their story. The stalled official inquiry into the UK's involvement in rendition and torture in the years after 9/11 is to be handed to the controversial intelligence and security committee (ISC), the government will announce on Thursday. The decision follows years of assurances by ministers that the inquiry would be headed by a senior judge, with the prime minister having told MPs that no other arrangement would command public confidence. It triggered warnings from human rights groups that the government risked complaints of a cover-up. The ISC is the oversight body that has been condemned for failing to report on the bulk surveillance operations being conducted by the UK's signals intelligence agency, GCHQ, until after they became public. Its inquiry into rendition concluded only that MI5 and MI6 had been slow to spot what the CIA had been doing. Some details about the involvement of the UK's intelligence agencies in the abuse of terrorism suspects may appear in the interim report of the stalled inquiry when it is published on Thursday. The report is being redacted before publication. The report will be published at about 11.30am and Kenneth Clarke, the minister without portfolio, will be making a statement on it to parliament. I'll be covering it live. Before that we've got Nick Clegg's LBC phone-in at 9am. I will be covering that, but I have to go to a meeting this morning and so I'm afraid the blog will be on automatic pilot (ie, it won't get updated) between about 10am and the start of the Clarke statement. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I’m on @AndrewSparrow.

headline: Politics live: readers' edition - Friday 25 October
trail_text: Share breaking news, leave links to interesting articles online and chat about the day's political events in our open thread
body_text: I'm not writing my usual Politics Live blog today, but, as an alternative, here's Politics Live: readers' edition. It's intended to be a place where you can catch up with the latest news and find links to good politics blogs and articles on the web. Please feel free to use this as somewhere you can comment on any of the day's political stories - just as you do when I'm writing the daily blog. It would be particularly useful for readers to flag up new material in the comments - breaking news or blogposts or tweets that are worth passing on because someone is going to find them interesting. A lot of what I do on my blog is aggregation - finding the good stuff and passing it on - and you can do this, too (as I know, because it happens every day when I'm blogging). All of today's Guardian politics stories are here, and all the politics stories filed on Thursday including some in today's paper, are here.

headline: Let's kick uglism out of politics
trail_text: David Schneider: Shallow voters reject Ed Miliband simply because he looks like a platypus. Was Labour's struggle to make themselves electable just about ditching unattractive leaders for one who was reasonably hot?
body_text: I was appalled to see in a recent opinion poll that 74% of voters would be more likely to vote Labour if Ed Miliband changed his face. Actually, I wasn't appalled because I made that up. But it wouldn't surprise me if Labour's lead hit double figures on condition that Ed swapped the Miliface for someone else's. Possibly his brother David's. I'm sure most politicians would change their face if it would win them an election (most of them have got two anyway), but are voters really that shallow? François Hollande clearly thinks so, putting pressure on French news agencies not to show a picture of him gurning like a hamster who'd overreached himself cheekwise. Similarly, David Cameron can't be happy about the picture of him getting 40 winks before his sister-in-law's wedding – ironic, seeing as his government is planning to cut the number of winks to 32 (except for those earning over £150K. We don't want to drive big winkers out of the country). Everyone from seven-year-olds who look crap on school photo day to Neil Kinnock arse-over-tipping on the beach knows that images can be devastating. Or redeeming. Remember that footage of Gordon Brown leaving Downing Street for the last time, hand-in-hand with his two little kids? I watched that next to a Labour supporter who referred to the boys as "10 seats" and "20 seats", because that's how many marginals he felt could have been won had Brown allowed them to be photographed with him during the campaign. We may try to pretend that it's all about policy, that the next election will be fought over welfare or the NHS, but the truth is it's more likely to be won and lost on whether someone can get a picture of Cameron or Miliband taking a dump. And the Tories might not even need dumpgate. Judging from my highly scientific poll of a group of people I had a row with in Garfunkel's, Labour may be deprived of a majority at the next election simply because some people could never vote for Miliband as he "looks like a human platypus" (at least it's more original than Wallace and Gromit). What do they want? A manifesto that includes guarantees of above-inflation rises in cheekbones and the complete elimination of monobrows by 2020? Is that how things are now? Was Labour's struggle in the 80s and 90s to make themselves electable just about finding a leader who was reasonably hot (if you'll forgive me for making you think of Blair in those terms)? Not so much clause IV as clause phwoar. It's the dental work, stupid, not the economy. Of course, Cameron is no Ryan Gosling when it comes to looks. He can't be much more than a six, even to his wife – even to a Tory shires lady who really hates badgers. Clegg was maybe a seven just before the last election and a three after it. At best. Which all sounds a bit shallow, and yet that's how people seem to be judging Miliband, certainly in the Heathrow Garfunkel's. I suspect that one reason the accusations of weakness and indecision stick to him, even though he has often showed considerable boldness (Murdoch, Syria, the unions, fratricide), is because his body language seems so apologetic (has no one in the Labour party heard of Pilates?). It seems that people who would never dream of not voting for someone because they were black or gay feel it is all right to not vote for someone because they "don't look right". Of course, it's not just politicians whom we judge by their looks. Looks are what count on TV, in the office, even in the bedroom (OK, that's probably allowable). As an actor, I've profited from being "funny-looking". A review described me as "incredibly grotesque, with a face you'd only expect to see on The X-Files". It was meant (and taken) as a compliment. I'm fine with my looks (do I protest too much?), but they have made me aware of how much our faces define us. Look at TV presenters. The Law of Brucie states that men stay attractive until roughly 30 years after their demise (those worms coming out of our skull just make us look "distinguished"), but if Holly Willoughby ever sprouts so much as a frown-line, she'll be bagged up and carted off before you can say "hot flush". This is always framed in terms of sexism and ageism, but seeing as men with a deformity or a birthmark find it as difficult to get presenting gigs as women over 40, perhaps the opposition isn't men/women or young women/old women but attractive/unattractive. Perhaps it's uglism, plain and simple – like the faces of those who are excluded (as you'd say if you were uglist). I'm sure the quite hot Richard Dawkins (a solid seven, I reckon) would say the response to a person's looks is instinctual, part of our biological makeup. But that shouldn't stop us checking our prejudices and rejecting them, especially when it comes to who we want to run the country. So let's kick uglism out of politics. We need to protect ourselves from ignoring the best candidate because he or she looks a bit "other". Maybe we need positive discrimination and all-ugly shortlists, or we could make the veil compulsory for all politicians. Maybe the electoral reform we need isn't AV or PR, but changing our voting system so it's like The Voice, with voters unable to see candidates till they've cast their votes by hitting a red button on a swivel chair. And yes, installing 40m swivel chairs nationwide could be costly, but who are we to put a price on democracy? It's the only way. Otherwise we'll just end up choosing our leader according to who looks best in a swimsuit, and I for one don't fancy Miliband's chances in Speedos.

headline: MPs debate the bedroom tax: Politics live blog
trail_text: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of the day's political developments
body_text: • Several Lib Dem MPs have indicated that they will rebel when the Commons votes tonight on a Labour motion calling for the abolition of the bedroom tax. Tim Farron, the Lib Dem president, is planning to vote against his government and, in the chamber, Charles Kennedy, the former Lib Dem leader, and Andrew George have both said they are opposed to the bedroom tax. Another four Lib Dems have signed a Commons motion strongly criticising the bedroom tax. Party insiders believe that up to 15 Lib Dems could rebel when the votes comes at 7pm. • Jeremy Hunt, the health secretary, has been accused of "running scared" after health department officials briefed newspapers and not parliament on a report due to be published on Wednesday on plans for a total overhaul of the NHS accident and emergency departments. • Ed Davey, the energy secretary, has accused the big six energy companies of using customers as "cash cows" by raising bills unfairly. As Terry Macalister reports, he also renewed his attack on Ed Miliband, describing the Labour leader's proposal to freeze energy prices as "intellectually bankrupt". • Michael Gove, the education secretary, has said that more struggling children's services departments in England could be taken over by independent providers. You can read the full text of his speech here. • Kathryn Hudson, the parliamentary commissioner for standards, has decided that there is insufficient evidence to justify an inquiry into Mark Pritchard, the Conservative MP accused by the Daily Telegraph of offering to exploit foreign contacts to set up business deals. • Theresa May, the home secretary, plans to strip British terror suspects of their citizenship and take away their UK passports even if it leaves them stateless, Whitehall sources have confirmed. • The government has unveiled plans to stop legal firms bombarding people with unsolicited phone calls and texts in order to find customers for compensation claim cases. That's all from me for today. Thanks for the comments. Here's a politics afternoon reading list. • John McDermott at Financial Times says Sir John Major is right about the influence of private schools, but that it is not clear what he wants to change. Whether the “upper echelons of power” are slightly more or slightly less closed than a generation ago, Sir John’s comments hint at an important truth: Britain is a deeply unfair meritocracy. This sounds contradictory but as the great historian David Kynaston has written, private schools are “those highly resourced, highly skilled and ferociously dedicated blockers of downward mobility”. They perpetuate networks of privilege but they are presumably also fantastic schools. This combination of positional and actual good helps explain why their fees have gone up so quickly in the two decades – and why many families scrimp and save to send their kids there ... It is less than clear what Sir John is recommending be done. Perhaps he just wants the Tory leadership to come across as less posh than they are. He dutifully mentions education, which is of course important, especially in the early years. But if what he genuinely wants is an end to private schools’ dominance of British life he would surely need to engage with what Prof Kynaston says: that unless something is done to stop them being so exclusive and so good at what they do, then “the current political consensus is … almost meaningless.” • Lord Ashcroft at ConservativeHome says he can understand why Ed Miliband wants leaders' debate before the election, but that they could backfire. The risk for Mr Miliband is that far from giving him a chance to explain why he thinks price controls are workable, TV debates would further expose the idea’s superficiality. As President Bartlet observed in the West Wing, the ten-word answer can be deadly in politics, but where are the next ten words, and the ten after that? And as Henrique Capriles, the Venezuelan opposition leader, said of President Maduro, “every time he opens his mouth, he scares away the investments that create employment and worsens the crisis”. Rather than transforming perceptions of Miliband after five years as Labour leader, TV debates could confirm them. Meanwhile, they would allow Cameron and Clegg to argue at greater length that higher living standards are achieved by proper economic management, not least controlling the deficit, and by encouraging businesses rather than threatening them. These are risks, not certainties. A gamble on TV debates may yet help Labour, if the Tories can be persuaded to take part despite having more to lose. But Miliband’s eagerness to hold them is not a signal of confidence. It’s the opposite. • Grant Shapps has used a Buzzfeed list to attack Labour. He has produced a Buzzfeed posted entitled: 12 Facts - Why Energy Bills Are Sky High And What We Could Do About It. • Philip Cowley and Mark Stuart on their Revolts blog list the 10 most interesting votes in the Commons since 2010. The Hansard for the opening of the bedroom tax debate is now on the Hansard website. In the Commons the Tory MP David TC Davies defended the government's bedroom tax. He even suggested that the welfare cuts did not go far enough. He made the point when he told an anecdote about doing some filming with the BBC with people affected by the policy. I met a young couple, a teenage couple, 17 years old, neither of whom had ever worked in their lives, they were in a two or a one-bedroom flat and were being expected to suffer some inconvenience, perhaps move into a studio flat. And again I say to the ministers on my front-bench, you are being far too generous in many instances. Why should the state be paying for two people to set up in, frankly, a teenage love nest? When I was 17 years old, if I wanted to see my girlfriend at the age of 17, I would go and see her on a park bench in Newport. Why is the government paying for them to have a flat all by themselves at all, never-mind whether it's one-bedroom or two-bedroom? And I got into a lot a of trouble because I suggested to the young man that perhaps he should go out and find a job himself. And he said there weren't any... I said, 'Well why don't you move to where the work is' and I immediately ran into a whole load of criticism for that. I even had someone email me here. He said, 'You're a Christian, you should be serving the Lord, one day you will stand by the Lord and account for this hardship'. And I wrote back and said, 'I read my bible, I don't see anywhere in the bible where it says 17 year olds should be given a flat but I see plenty of examples of people who have had to move for a better way of life, whether it was Abraham going off to the Promised Land or Moses or the disciples who toured all over Europe'. They all moved. I've taken the quote from the Press Association. Four Lib Dem MPs have tabled a Commons early day motion saying that the bedroom tax is a mistake and that, unless there are extensive changes - including a wider range of exemptions, and the removal of penalties from people who do not have the option of moving to a smaller home - it should be abolished. You can read the text of the motion here. Broadly it reflects the motion passed at the Lib Dem conference in September. The four Lib Dem MPs are: Greg Mulholland, Ian Swales, Adrian Sanders and Roger Williams. Caroline Lucas, the Green MP, used an intervention at the start of the bedroom tax debate to condemn the policy. She has sent out a news release saying that 1,270 people in Brighton and Hove are affected by the policy, of whom 300 are now behind with their rent. Of those 300 people, 205 are disabled. The Green-led council in Brighton and Hove has a policy of not evicting people who fall behind with their rent because of the bedroom tax, she says. This is a brutal policy, and the government simply doesn’t care that some of the most vulnerable in our society – many of whom have disabilities – are being hit hard. It’s worse than Victorian that the poor are being targeted by this tax, especially when the richest are benefiting from tax cuts. And it’s horrifying that such a high proportion of those affected are disabled. The policy risks pushing people who are already desperate into the hands of pay day loan sharks. The bedroom tax is a cruel and counterproductive measure from a government that seems completely indifferent to the impact of its policies. They say that people can get money from the discretionary housing payments fund but that just isn’t enough. It has to help people affected by all the welfare cuts. Brighton and Hove City Council’s DHP allocation for this year is just over £1m. That’s a drop in the ocean compared to the total reduction in benefit expenditure resulting from all the government’s cuts, which is over £13m. Citizens Advice does not get a vote in this evening's division on the bedroom tax. But it has issued a statement supporting the Labour position and saying the policy is a mistake. This is from Gillian Guy, its chief executive. The Government’s solution to spiraling Housing Benefit costs is simply creating more problems. Thousands are being pushed into arrears, 96 per cent of people affected have no alternative smaller homes to move into and some housing associations say they are being forced to demolish homes whilst 1.8 million languish on waiting lists. The number of issues of rent arrears that Citizens Advice Bureaux deal with is rising fast. The sky-high cost of housing is a huge strain on our clients’ budgets and is pushing many into the arms of payday lenders. Ministers are right to seek value for money, but the rushed nature of these changes and the lack of alternative housing means many will be caught in a catch-22 and unable to avoid the extra cost. The fundamental problem with the high cost of Housing Benefit is the lack of houses, not the families who live in them. This upside-down approach to policy does nothing to tackle the chronic housing shortage we face in the UK. For many disabled people and their families, having a second bedroom is not a luxury but an indispensible necessity. The under-occupancy penalty risks forcing a small number of families to bear the burden of years of under-investment in our housing stock. And here's what the Labour MP Stephen Pound said about his brother being a victim of the bedroom tax in the debate. There is a young man who lives in Earls Court who is in total renal failure. May I tell you that this man's spare bedroom is a dialysis unit. He has been told that he now has to pay the bedroom tax. He is very happy with the efforts of his MP, not of my political persuasion, to attempt to free him from the chains of the bedroom tax, but my brother faces losing his home of 20 years for being a kidney patient. Do you not agree with me that this is beyond disgrace? The Lib Dem MP Andrew George is also voting against the government on the bedroom tax, as George Eaton reports at the Staggers. Here's an extract from Rachel Reeves' opening speech. I've taken the quote from the Press Association. It's a shame that the pensions minister [Steve Webb] does not listen to his own party who only in September at the Liberal Democrat party conference voted overwhelmingly against the bedroom tax, saying that it is 'discriminating against the most vulnerable in society' and noted that the government had shown 'a lack of appreciation of the housing requirements of children and adults with disabilities and care needs'. But I am afraid that's what you get with the Liberal Democrats. They say one thing at their conference and when they are out on the doorsteps but they vote another way in here when it really counts. When they could make a difference they turn the other way. So while the work and pensions secretary [Iain Duncan Smith] scuttles off to Paris, he gets his Liberal Democrat pensions minister to defend a policy that isn't even part of his brief and is in contradiction with his own party's policy. I say shame on him and shame on his party. Tim Farron, the Lib Dem president, is set to vote against the government on the bedroom tax tonight, I'm told. A friend of Farron's tells me: "Party conference in Glasgow expressed its will very strongly against the bedroom tax and so Tim is listening to party members and will probably be voting against the government tonight. Tim is the voice of the party members, they have expressed their view and Tim wants to make sure that their voice is heard." Here's the motion on the bedroom tax that the Lib Dems passed at their party conference (pdf). It criticised the policy, and called for an immediate review of its impact. Rachel Reeves v Steve Webb - Verdict: Labour MPs have been claiming that Iain Duncan Smith stayed away today because he was too embarrassed to defend his bedroom tax policy. Having just heard the opening of the debate, another possibility occurs; perhaps he just realised that Steve Webb would do a better job. There is virtually nothing in the welfare portfolio harder to defend than the bedroom tax, and Duncan Smith (who is easily riled) would have probably ended up sounding shouty and belligerent if he had been here to respond to Rachel Reeves. Webb, though, was good. Defending sound policies is fairly easily, but the real test in the Commons comes when you are asked to defend the indefensible, and Webb managed about as well anyone could in the circumstances. But ultimately he was on a hopeless mission. Reeves hammered away at the injustices and inconsistencies of the bedroom with brisk, machine-gun efficiency, but it was the Labour interventions, and the stories they contained, that really sunk the government. (The Tory interventions were particularly poor.) The problems with the bedroom tax have been extensively documented already, but this was still an unusually moving opening to a debate. Webb tells MPs that, if someone comes to see them with a hard case, they should ask the council if it has spent all the money available for discretionary payments. And, if it has, they should ask it if it has asked the government for more money. Labour has nothing to say on the deficit, he says again. And that's it. His speech is over. Robert Flello, the Labour MP, says there are just no smaller homes available in his constituency. Webb says he is not talking about empty homes. He is talking about trying to get tenants to swap with other social housing tenants. Simon Hughes, the Lib Dem deputy leader, says he supported the Lib Dem motion on the bedroom tax passed at the party conference. Will the government exempt people who have applied to swap homes but are still waiting for a smaller property to become available? Webb says the Lib Dem motion on the bedroom tax was better than Labour. In response to a question about Manchester, he says Manchester returned £595,000 last year which it received for discretionary housing payments (DHP) but did not use. If Manchester is short of money this year, it can apply for more money, he says. Webb says he cannot commit the government to more money for remote rural communities. But Danny Alexander, the chief secretary to the Treasury, tends to be quite sensitive to the needs of communities like this, he says. Webb says Reeves did not mention the need to address the deficit. The welfare budget is the biggest in Whitehall. And housing benefit for social tenants is an area you have to look at. So spare rooms are an obvious issue to consider. Webb says there is an issue about adapted accommodation. The government could have tried writing down complex rules. But instead it decided it would be easier to allocate the discretionary money it would need to spend to help people with adapted homes and just give it to councils. Webb says last year, when the government gave councils money to make discretionary payments to help people with housing payments, Labour said it was not enough. But at the end of the year councils returned £10m. Webb says last year the government allocated £60m. This year it has trebled the sum, taking it up to £180m. And councils have the option of asking for more. But so far councils have not been asking for more, he says. Charles Kennedy, the former Lib Dem leader, says he cannot support the bedroom tax. Will the government considering exempting small communities. Webb says Kennedy has been lobbying on behalf of remote rural communities. The government has allocated an extra £5m to help these communities, he says. Labour's Emily Thornberry says, if the policy is designed to address over-crowding, why doesn't it apply to pensioners? Webb says he is not sure if Thornberry wants it to apply to pensioners. Thornberry says he is just probing, to find out what the purpose of the policy is. Webb says at least Labour is acknowledging over-crowding is a problem. Steve Webb, the Lib Dem pensions minister, is responding for the government. Iain Duncan Smith is at a conference on youth unemployment. Labour may not care about youth unemployment, but the government does. Webb says Rachel Reeves did not mention the concerns of tenants in over-crowded housing. He says he wants to take the start of Labour's motion and changing one one. That this House regrets the pernicious effect on vulnerable and in many cases disabled people of deductions being made from housing benefit paid to working age tenants in the private [not social, as the motion says] housing sector deemed to have an excess number of bedrooms in their homes. Webb says Labour's position would be consistent if it was opposed to people being penalised for having a spare room in public sector housing and private sector housing. But Labour introduced measures to cut housing benefit for people with a spare room in private housing. If people in private housing have to pay more for a room to store a dialysis machine, why shouldn't the same apply to people in social housing. Labour's Sheila Gilmore says this is a bogus point. Labour, when it introduced the private housing rule, did not introduce it without warning. Webb says Labour also talked about imposing the same rules on tenants in the public sector (ie, what the government has done). He quotes from a speech Malcolm Wicks gave when he was a minister. Reeves says it is time for the Commons to make a stand. MPs should stand with Danny Alexander's father on this, she says. Di Alexander says this policy is particularly unfair. And Danny Alexander should listen to the Lib Dem conference, which condemned the scheme. But that's what you get with the Lib Dems, she says. They say one thing in one place, and another in another. Labour would reverse it. It would fund this by: cutting down on bogus self-employment in the construction industry; reversing the tax cut for hedge funds, and cancelling the Treasury's shares-for-rights scheme, she says. And that's it. Her speech is over. Labour's Stephen Pound says his brother faces losing his home of 20 years because he is a kidney patient. He has a spare room which he uses for a dialysis machine. But he is not covered by an exemption, he says. Reeves mentions research from the University of York suggestion the savings will be £160m less than anticipated. There is a story about this research here. And here's the full report (pdf). Reeves says there is a contradiction at the heart of the policy. The government says the policy is designed to get people to move, so they free up large homes for larger families. Yet its projected savings figure assumes that families do not move, she says. So what is the purpose of this policy: to free up space, or to save money? John Hemming, a Lib Dem MP, says Labour introduced similar rules for the private sector. And the Labour government actually talked about introducing these principles for public sector housing, he says. When did Labour change its mind? Reeves says it will be interesting to see if Hemming votes with Labour, because the Lib Dem conference condemned the policy. If she becomes work and pension secretary, she will abolish the bedroom tax on day one, she says. Labour's Huw Irranca-Davies says this is a stupid policy. In south Wales, housing associations have three-bedroom homes lying empty because of the bedroom tax. And housing association arrears are going up. Reeves agrees. Labour's Madeleine Moon says people on dialysis moved into a home with a spare room to store their dialysis equipment. That means they do not have to go to hospital. It is not a spare room. It's a hospital room. Reeves says Moon's is making a good point. Margot James, a Conservative, says many of the cases cited by Reeves are covered by exemptions. Labour penalised claimants on housing benefit in private housing with spare rooms. So why is it unfair if the government imposes the same rules for public sector housing. Reeves says Labour's measure was not retrospective, whereas the government's is. Reeves says the policy is not just cruel; it is unworkable. Some 660,000 households are affected. Many have conditions that mean they need a second bedroom. And many are in poverty, he says. Labour's Robert Flello mentions a constituent who need a second bedroom for when his children come to visit. That is a condition for their being able to visit. The bedroom tax will prevent this, he says. Reeves agrees. This is an anti-family policy, as well as an anti-disabled policy, she says. Tobias Ellwood, a Conservative, asks what Reeves would say to people in overcrowded social housing who see other social housing tenants with spare rooms. Reeves says the government should build more homes. Back in the chamber, Reeves says many of the so-called spare bedrooms are rooms used by carers. Iain Duncan Smith is causing "hardship and heartbreak". Labour's Andrew Gwynne says in his constituency the discretionary budget allocated to the council to allow it to help people affected by the policy is not remotely large enough to meet the need. Reeves says she is not surprised. In Leeds, where she is an MP, two thirds of the discretionary budget has already been spent, she says. On the subject of the impact of the bedroom tax on the disabled, Demos sent me this earlier today. The government’s controversial ‘bedroom tax’ is costing disabled people over £200 million a year, according to analysis by the think-tank Demos. Researchers found 420,000 disabled people are currently being affected by the cut in what Coalition politicians are keen to label a ‘spare room subsidy’. The figures come from Demos analysis into the cumulative impact of the Government’s welfare reforms. It revealed an astonishing £28.3bn being taken out of disabled people’s pockets by 2018 – the date of the next spending review – due to a multitude of cuts including Disability Living Allowance (DLA), Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) and housing benefit. Caroline Lucas, the Green MP, says this is a "despicable" policy having a particular effect on disabled people. Reeves says she is absolutely right. Rachel Reeves, the shadow work and pensions secretary, is opening the debate. She says she is glad to see so many Labour MPs in the chamber for the debate. And disappointed to see so few Tories in the chamber. And she is disappointed that Iain Duncan Smith is not here. He is at a conference in Paris. But, she say, she is glad to see that one of the cabinet's strongest Eurosceptics has discovered an interest in Europe. Duncan Smith should have been here to hear about the impact of his policies, she says. The bedroom tax has had a particular impact on more than 400,000 disabled people, she says. Here's the Labour motion that MPs are debating. That this House regrets the pernicious effect on vulnerable and in many cases disabled people of deductions being made from housing benefit paid to working age tenants in the social housing sector deemed to have an excess number of bedrooms in their homes; calls on the Government to end these deductions with immediate effect; furthermore calls for any cost of ending them to be covered by reversing tax cuts which will benefit the wealthiest and promote avoidance, and addressing the tax loss from disguised employment in construction; and further calls on the Government to use the funding set aside for discretionary housing payments to deal with under-occupation by funding local authorities so that they are better able to help people with the cost of moving to suitable accommodation. And here's the government amendment. Line 1, leave out from ‘House’ to end and add ‘notes the substantial structural deficit which was inherited from the previous Government and the need to get the nation’s finances back into shape; further notes the need to bring expenditure on housing benefit under control; further notes that the proposed reversal of this policy would cost the Exchequer around half a billion pounds a year; regrets any exaggeration and misrepresentation of the effects of the policy; recognises the inequality of allowing social tenants to receive benefit for a spare bedroom whilst denying this opportunity to private tenants; supports the Government’s action to deal with this unfairness whilst protecting vulnerable groups such as pensioners and providing substantial funding through Discretionary Housing Payments to local authorities to support other tenants who would otherwise be adversely affected; further notes the Government’s continuing commitment to monitor the effects of the policy and the use of Discretionary Housing Payments; and welcomes the potential beneficial impact of this policy on those living in overcrowded accommodation and the 2.1 million families on waiting lists.’. The Commons debate on the bedroom tax will start shortly. Here's a short bedroom tax reading list. • An article by Iain Duncan Smith for ConservativeHome defending the bedroom tax (although, of course, he does not call it that.) The public can see how ridiculous it is that the benefit system was funding extra bedrooms, while hundreds of thousands of families are squeezed into cramped, overcrowded accommodation that is too small for their needs. Latest ONS figures show that around 375,000 households across England and Wales are living in overcrowded homes in the social rented sector, a fact hardly ever referred to by Labour who left us with this problem. And it is also clearly unfair to treat people differently in the social and private rented sectors. • A Daily Mirror article citing 25 reasons why the bedroom tax should go. • A post for Political Scrapbook explaining why Duncan Smith is not speaking in the debate. (He's at a conference in Paris.) Labour's Andy Slaughter uses a point of order to complain about journalists being briefed about the contents of Sir Bruce Keogh's report today (on an embargoed basis), when MPs will not see it until tomorrow. John Bercow points out that he did grant an urgent question on this. The statement is now over. The debate on the bedroom tax will start shortly. There's a 10-minute rule bill first. Following a point of order, John Bercow has just criticised the government for announcing a one-day delay in the timing of the autumn statement last night on Twitter, and not via a statement to MPs. Hunt says Labour cannot have it both ways; it cannot claim to be in favour of fiscal responsibility, but then complain about every cut. Labour's John Woodcock says ambulance response times have increased in 11 out of 12 trusts. Why is that? Hunt says, just as there is more pressure on A&E services, there is more pressure on ambulance services too. The government is trying to address this. Labour's Bill Esterson asks Hunt why he doesn't invest the NHS underspend in social care. Hunt says the government has already increased social care funding. But it is hard to invest an underspend because you cannot predict what that will be, he says. Labour's Virendra Sharma asks about the future of the A&E department at Ealing hospital. Hunt says an A&E unit at Ealing will stay. But the shape and size of it may change in the light of tomorrow's announcement. Labour's Kevin Brennan asks Hunt to apologise for calling people who visit A&E regularly "frequent fliers". (See 12.51pm.) Hunt says Brennan's question is "ridiculous". Labour's Andrew Slaughter asks why Hunt announced a few weeks ago that 45% A&E departments in West London would close. Hunt says services are being improved in the area. Labour's Jenny Chapman says it is harder to get a GP appointment in her area. A police superintendent has complained to her about his officers having to take patients to hospital because there are no ambulances. Hunt says that Chapman is the first Labour MP to acknowledge that it is hard to see a GP. The government is determined to address the problems created by the GP contract, he says. Here's a Guardian story about research that Jeremy Hunt cited earlier (see 12.54pm) about concentrating stroke services in specialist centres saving lives. Here is the full text of Jeremy Hunt's response to the urgent question. Mr Speaker, in January this year the Board of NHS England launched a review of urgent and emergency care in England. Urgent and Emergency Care covers a range of areas including: A&E departments, NHS 111 centres and other emergency telephone services, ambulances, minor injury units, and urgent care centres. The review is being led by Professor Sir Bruce Keogh, NHS England’s Medical Director. A report on Phase 1 of the review is being published tomorrow and it is embargoed until then. This is an NHS England report. NHS England is an independent body, accountable to me through the Mandate. The report being published tomorrow is a preliminary one, setting out initial thinking. A final version will be published in the new year. Sir Bruce has said that he will outline initial proposals and recommendations for the future of urgent and emergency care services in England. These have been informed by an engagement exercise that took place between June and August this year. These proposals will be further consulted upon through a number of channels including commissioning guidance and demonstrator sites. Spring 2014 will see another progress report. Decisions on changing services are taken at a local level by commissioners and providers, in consultation with all interested parties. That is exactly as it should be, Mr Speaker, as only then can the system be responsive to local needs. It is vital to ensure that both urgent and emergency care and the wider health and care system remain sustainable and readily understandable for patients. A&E performance levels have largely been maintained thanks to the expertise and dedication of NHS staff. A&E departments see 95% of patients within four hours and this figure hasn’t dropped below the 95% target since the end of April. But urgent and emergency care is falling behind the public’s needs and expectations. The number of people going to A&E departments has risen historically, not least because of an ageing population. A million more people are coming through the doors than in 2010. Winter inevitably further challenges the system, which is why we are supporting the most under-pressure A&Es with £250 million. Planning has started earlier than ever before this year, and the NHS has been extremely focused on preparing for additional pressure. Mr Speaker, we will look at Sir Bruce’s report extremely carefully. Reform of the urgent and emergency care system may take years to complete, but that does not mean that it is not achievable. We are exceptionally fortunate in this country to have in the NHS one of the world’s great institutions. Patients need to know that it will continue to be there for them whenever they need it. Labour's Graham Jones says 36,000 people used his local walk-in centre. Why does Hunt think it will be sensible to close it down? Hunt says Jones question highlights the problem. Labour opened walk-in centres in many places to solve the problems caused by their GP contract. Hunt says the government will address this. But where walk-in centres are valuable, they will be kept. Hunt says stroke survival rates in London have improved because the number of stroke centres has been cut from over 30 to eight (allowing patients to receive more specialist treatment). Hunt says an increasing number of people are coming from "Labour-run Wales" to seek NHS treatment in England. That's because they know where they can get better treatment. Hunt says some A&E patients are "frequent fliers". They keep coming back because there is nowhere else to go. Tomorrow's announcement will help to address this, he says. Hunt says the most senior A&E doctor in the country says between 15 and 30% of A&E patients could be treated elsewhere. Labour's Kevin Barron says competition law is causing chaos in the NHS. The government was warned about this. Hunt says many of the competition problems come from the Enterprise Act 2002. David Tredinnick, a Conservative, asks how many lives will be saved through the consolidation of services in London. Hunt says the measures in London will save hundreds of lives a year. Sir Bruce Keogh will say more about this tomorrow. Hunt is responding to Burnham. Real complacency was refusing to hold an inquiry into Mid-Staffs, he says. He says the A&E review is there to clear up the mess left by Labour. Walk-in centres were introduced because Labour messed up the GP contract, he says. The government will take difficult decisions. They are decisions Labour ducked. Burnham talked about a recruitment crisis. But there are 300 more A&E consultants than there were under Labour. And 2,000 more people are seen every four hours in A&E every day than under Labour. Under Burnham, the government made "so many wrong decisions about A&E", he says. Andy Burnham is responding. Rarely has the Commons been treated to such a disrespectful and complacent statement, he says. A&E services are in crisis already. David Cameron has taken personal control of the NHS crisis. And journalists have been given a private briefing this morning. They should not be given a briefing on material not available to MPs, he says.
